V.

Petitioner,

FEDERICO  PENA,  Secretary  of  Department  of
Transportation;  THOMAS  D.  LARSEN,  Administrator
of  the  Federal  Highway  Administration;  LOUIS  N.

Petition  For  Writ  Of  Certiorari
To  The  United  States  Court  Of  Appeals
For  The  Tenth  Circuit
¢

PETITION  FOR  WRIT  OF  CERTIORARI
¢

WILLIAM  Perry  PENDLEY*
*Counsel  of  Record

Attorneys  for  Petitioner

ISSUES  PRESENTED

1.  Whether  a congressional  race-based  set  aside  pro-
gram  for  awarding  highway  construction  contracts  sur-
vives  an  as  applied  constitutional  challenge  when  that
program  seeks  to  remedy  alleged  broad-based  societal
discrimination,  rather  than  clearly  identifiable  discrimi-
nation  perpetuated  by the  governmental  entity  seeking  to
remedy  the  discrimination?

2.  Whether  “strict  scrutiny,”  as  opposed  to
“a
lenient  standard,  resembling  intermediate  scrutiny,”  is
the  proper  standard  of review  for  determining  the  consti-
tutionality  of  a  race-based,  program  adopted  by  Con-
gress?

“

3.  Whether  the  Fifth  Amendment  requires  a  federal
agency,  in  implementing  a  federal  race-based,  set  aside
program  and  when  exceeding  the  goals  adopted  by Con-
gress,  to  conduct  the  inquiry  set  forth  in  City of Richmond
v.  J.A.  Croson,  Co.,  488  U.S.  469  (1989)?

ii

LIST  OF  PARTIES

Adarand  Constructors,  Inc.  (“Adarand”)  was  Plaintiff
in  the  United  States  District  Court  for  the  District  of
Colorado  and  Appellant  before  the  United  States  Court  of
Appeals  for  the  Tenth  Circuit.  Adarand  is  a  corporation
organized  under  the  laws  of  the  State  of  Coloiado.
Adarand  has  no  parent  or  subsidiary  corporations.

The  following  were  Defendants  in  the  United  States
District  Court  for  the  District  of Colorado  and  Appellees
before  the  United  States  Court  of  Appeals  for  the  Tenth
Circuit:  Samuel  K.  Skinner,  in  his  official  capacity  as
Secretary  of  the  Department  of Transportation  (Mr.  Skin-
ner  has  since  been  replaced  in  his  official  capacity  by
Federico  Pena);  Robert  E.  Farris,  in his  official  capacity  as
Administrator  of  the  Federal  Highway  Administration
(Mr.  Farris  has  since  been  replaced  in  his  official  capacity
by Thomas  D.  Larsen);  Louis  N.  MacDonald,  in  his  offi-
cial  capacity  as  Administrator  of  Region  VIII  of  the  Fed-
eral  Highway  Administration;  and  Jerry  Budwig,  in  his
official  capacity  as  Division  Engineer  of  the  Central  Fed-
eral  Lands  Highway  Division.

PETITION  FOR  WRIT  OF  CERTIORARI

SJ

OPINIONS  BELOW

Review  is sought  of the,  as  yet  unpublished,  opinion
of the  Tenth  Circuit  Court  of Appeals,  dated  February  16,
1994,  appearing  at  pages  App.  1 to  App.  26 of the  Appen-
dix.  The  opinion  and  order  of  the  District  Court,  dated
April  21,  1992,  Adarand  Constructors,  Inc.  v.  Skinner,  790  F.

1

Supp.  240  (D.Colo.  1992),  appear  at  pages  App.  26  to
App.  38  of  the  Appendix.

JURISDICTION

Petitioner  seeks  review  of the  opinion  and  judgment
issued  by the  Tenth  Circuit  Court  of Appeals  on  February
16,  1994.  The  Supreme  Court  has  jurisdiction  to  review
this  opinion  pursuant  to  28  U.S.C.  §1254(1).

¢

CONSTITUTIONAL  PROVISIONS  INVOLVED

The  Fifth  and  Fourteenth  Amendments  to  the  U.S.
Constitution  are  at  issue  in  this  Petition  for  Writ  of Cer-

tiorari.

STATUTE  INVOLVED

The  statute  involved  in  this  appeal  is  §502  of  the

Small  Business  Act,  15  U.S.C.  §644(g).  App.  39  to  40.

¢

STATEMENT  OF  THE  CASE

On  August  10,  1990,  Adarand  Constructors,  Inc.  filed
an  action  in  the  United  States  District  Court  for  the
District  of  Colorado  challenging  the  use,  by  the  Central
Federal  Lands  Highway  Division  (CFLHD),  of a  contract
provision  referred  to  as  the  “Subcontracting  Compensa-
tion  Clause”  (SCC).  The  express  purpose  of the  SCC  is  to

induce  prime  contractors  on  federal  highway  projects  to
hire  subcontractors  based  upon  the  race  or  gender  of the
owners  rather  than  the  amount  of the  bids  of those  sub-
contractors.  The  SCC  authorizes  bonus  payments  to  the
prime  contractors  as  the  mechanism  to  induce  the  award
of  subcontracts  to  minorities  and  women.!

This  case  presents  a  challenge  to  the  Small  Business
Act  of  1953,  as  amended  (SBA),  15  U.S.C.  §631  et  seq.,
which  provides  the  sole  source  of statutory  authority  for
the  SCC  program.?  Adarand  asserts  that  the SCC  program
adopted  in accordance  with  the  SBA,  violates  the  xight  of
equal  protection  guaranteed  by  the  Fourteenth  Amend-
ment,  as  incorporated  by the  Fifth  Amendment.  Adarand
further  asserts  that  the  SCC  program  violates  the  privi-
leges  and  immunities  secured  by 42  U.S.C.  §1983  and  42
U.S.C.  §2000(d).3

1  The  size  of  the  bonus  offered  under  the  SCC  program
equals  10%  of the  final  amount  of the  Disadvantaged  Business
Enterprise  (“DBE”)  subcontracts,  not  to  exceed  2%  of  the  final
prime  contract  amount  (1.5%  if the  prime  contractor  only  con-
tracts  with  one  DBE  subcontractor).  Moreover,  the  CFLHD
requires  prospective  prime  contractors  to  include  a  bid  item
amount  to  be  awarded  to  the  prime  contractor  under  the  SCC
program  regardless  of whether  the  prime  contractors  intend  to
collect  that  money.  Thus,  a  prime  contractor  cannot  gain  an
advantage  over  other  prime  contractors  by agreeing  to  forego
the  benefits  available  under  the  SCC.

2  A second  statute,  the Surface  Transportation  and  Uniform
Relocation  Assistance  Act  of  1987  (STURAA),  Pub.  L.  100-17,
101  Stat.  132  (1987),  is  implicated  only  in  that  it provided  the
funding  for  the  construction  project  at  issue  in  this  case,  the
West  Dolores  Project.  Adarand  does  not  challenge  the  constitu-
tionality  of STURAA  in  this  case.

3  Different  from  other  cases  recently  presented  to  this
Court  to resolve  these  and  other  concerns  regarding  the issue  of

Cross  Motions  For  Summary  Judgment  were  filed  on
April  24,  1991.  On  April  21,  1992,  the  District  Court
issued  an  opinion  and  order  denying  Adarand’s  Motion
For  Summary  Judgment  and  granting  the  United  States’
Motion  For  Summary  Judgment.

On  August  5,  1992,  after  obtaining  a  timely  Motion
For  Extension  Of  Time  To  File  Appeal,  Adarand  filed  a
Notice  Of  Appeal  with  the  Tenth  Circuit  Court  of
Appeals.  The  matter  was  decided  in  an  opinion  dated
February  16,  1994.  This  Petition  for  Writ  of Certiorari
follows.

STATEMENT  OF  FACTS

Adarand  is  a  highway  construction  company.  Randy
Pech,  who  is  a  white  male,  is  part  owner  and  serves  as
general  manager  of  Adarand.  In  this  capacity,  he  is
responsible  for  the  company’s  day-to-day  operations.

The  CFLHD  is  an  agency  within  the  United  States
Department  of  Transportation  (DOT).  Its  function  is  to
supervise  the  construction  and  maintenance  of highways
on  federally-owned  lands  within  its  geographic  region.4

the  use  of  race  in  federal  decision  making,  Adarand  has  not
stipulated  that  the underlying  statute  is constitutional.  Adarand
has  challenged  the  statute  and  the  SCC  program  upon  which  it
is based  both  facially  and  as  applied.

*  The  geographic  region  of the  CFLHD  includes  all  or  part
of the  following  states:  Colorado,  Nebraska,  North  Dakota,  Cal-
ifornia,  Wyoming,  Utah,  Arizona,  New  Mexico,  Texas,  South
Dakota,  Nevada,  Kansas,  and  Hawaii.

On  August  10,  1989,  the  CFLHD  issued a solicitation
for  bids  for  the  construction  of  a  4.7  mile  section  of
highway  to  be  located  in  the  San  Juan  National  Forest
(the  West  Dolores  Project)  in  southern  Colorado.  Section
108  of  the  contract  for  the  West  Dolores  Project  is  the
“Subcontracting  Compensation  Clause.”  The  SCC  Pays  a
monetary  bonus  to all prime  contractors  who  hire  “disad-
vantaged”  subcontractors  to  perform  work  constituting
more  than  10%  of  the  overall  contract  amount.

On  September  15,  1989,  the  CFLHD  awarded  the
prime  contract  for  the  West  Dolores  Project  to  Mountain
Gravel  &  Construction  Company  (Mountain  Gravel).
Shortly  thereafter,  Adarand  submitted  the  lowest  bid  for
the  guardrail  portion  of  the  project  to  Mountain  Gravel.
However,  Mountain  Gravel  awarded  the  subcontract  to  a
certified  DBE,  the  Gonzales  Construction  Company.  The
sole  reason  for  the  decision  of Mountain  Gravel  to  award
the  subcontract  to  the  Gonzales  Construction  Company,
rather  than  to  Adarand,  was  to  obtain  the  $30,000.00
bonus  payment  from  the  CFLHD.

The  CFLHD  highway  contracting  program  has,  as  its
statutory  basis,  the  SBA,  which  applies  to  all  federal
procurement  activities  and  requires  the  President  to  set
an  annual  “goal”  of  awarding  not  less  than  5%  of  all
contracts  to  small  businesses  owned  and  controlled  by
“socially  and  economically  disadvantaged  individuals.”
15  U.S.C.  §644(g)(1).  However,  the  SBA  also  provides:
Notwithstanding  the  Government-wide  goal,
each  agency  shall  have  an  annual  goal  that  pre-
sents,  for  that  agency,  the  maximum  practicable
Opportunity  for  .
.  small  business  concerns

.

owned  and  controlled  by socially  and  economi-
cally  disadvantaged  individuals  to  participate  in
the  performance  of contracts  let by such  agency.

Id.  The  term  “socially  and  economically  disadvantaged
individuals”  is  also  defined  by  the  statute.°

S

5  As  used  in  this  contract,  the  term  “small  business
concern”  shall  mean  a  small  business  as  defined  pur-
suant  to  section  3 of the  Small  Business  Act  and  rele-
vant  regulations  promulgated  pursuant  thereto.  The
term  “small  business  concern  owned  and  controlled
by socially  and  economically  disadvantaged  individ-
uals”  shall  mean  a  small  business  concern  -

(i)  which  is at least  51 per centum  owned  by one
or  more  socially  and  economically  disadvantaged
individuals;  or,  in the case  of any  publicly  owned
business,  at  least  51  per  centum  of  the  stock  of
which  is owned  by one  or  more  socially  and  eco-
nomically  disadvantaged  individuals;  and
(ii)  whose  management  and  daily  business
operations  are  controlled  by one  or  more  of such
individuals.

The  contractor  shall  presume  that  socially  and  eco-
nomically  disadvantaged  individuals  include  Black
Americans,  Hispanic  Americans,  Native  Americans,
Asian  Pacific  Americans,  and  other  minorities,  or  any
other  individual  found  to  be  disadvantaged  by  the
Administration  pursuant  to  section  8(a)  of  the  Small
Business  Act.

15 U.S.C.  §637(d)(3)(C).  Businesses  owned  or  operated  by white
males  are  not  presumed  to  be  “disadvantaged.”

REASONS  FOR  GRANTING  THE  WRIT
I.  A  TRUE,  DIRECT,  AND  IRRECONCILABLE  CON-
FLICT  EXISTS  BETWEEN  THE  DECISION  BELOW
AND  DECISIONS  RENDERED  BY  THIS  HONOR-
ABLE  COURT.

A.  THE  DECISION  OF  THE  TENTH  CIRCUIT
COURT  OF  APPEALS,  THAT  THE  PROPER
STANDARD  OF  REVIEW  OF  A  RACE-
BASED  CONGRESSIONALLY-MANDATED
PROGRAM  IS  “INTERMEDIATE  SCRUTINY,”
IS  IN  DIRECT  CONFLICT  WITH  DECISIONS
OF  THIS  HONORABLE  COURT.

The  Tenth  Circuit  Court  of  Appeals  applied  an
improper  standard  of  review  to  the  race-based  program
challenged  herein:

[I]f  Congress  has  expressly  mandated  a race-
conscious  program,  a  court  must  apply  a  lenient
standard,  resembling  intermediate  scrutiny,  in
assessing  the  program’s  constitutionality.

App.  15.  The  Court  reached  its  conclusion  by combining
the  opinion  authored  by  Chief  Justice  Burger  with  the
concurring  opinion  authored  by  Justice  Marshall.®  This
holding  of  the  Tenth  Circuit  is  in  conflict  with  the  deci-
sions  of  this  Honorable  Court.  A  “lenient  standard,
resembling  intermediate  scrutiny”  is not  the  standard  set
forth  by a  majority  of the Justices  in the  Fullilove  decision,

©  “A  majority  of  the  [Fullilove  v.  Klutznick,  448  U.S.  448
(1980)] Court agreed  that  strict  scrutiny  is not  the proper  test  for
evaluating  a race-conscious  remedial  program  authorized  by an
act  of Congress.”  App.  15,  fn.  10.

- oe

vf

7

nor  is  it  the  standard  set  forth  in previous  or  later  deci-
sions  of  this  Court.”

Although  there  is  some  question  regarding  whether
Chief  Justice  Burger’s  opinion  was  based  upon a “strict
scrutiny”  standard,’  he clearly  required  use  of a  standard
higher  than  intermediate  scrutiny:

We  recognize  the  need  for  careful  judicial  evalua-
tion  to  assure  that  any  congressional  program
that  employs  racial  or  ethnic  criteria  to  accom-
plish  the  objective  of  remedying  the  present
effects  of past  discrimination  is  narrowly  tailored
to  the  achievement  of  that  goal.

Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at  480.  (Emphasis  added.)  Furthermore
Chief  Justice  Burger  wrote:

7  The concurring  opinion  authored  by Justice  Marshall  sup-
ports  an  “intermediate  scrutiny”  standard  for  the  Fullilove  fac-
tual  situation  because  Justice  Marshall  rejected  both  “strict
scrutiny”  and  “the  minimally  rigorous  rational-basis  standard
of  review.”  Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at  519.  The  constitutional  test  is
met,  wrote  Justice  Marshall,  when  the  program  is “designed  to
further  remedial  purposes  [which]  serve  important  governmen-
tal  objectives  and  are  substantially  related  to  achievement  of
those  objectives.”  Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at 519.  But  Justice  Marshall
was  joined  by only  two  other  Justices,  i.e.  Justice  Brennan  and
Justice  Blackmun.

8  “The  principal  opinion  in  Fullilove,  written  by Chief  Jus-
tice  Burger,  did  not  employ  ‘strict  scrutiny’...  .
”  Croson,  488
U.S.  at  487.  Although  this  part  of the  decision  was  authored  by
Justice  O’Connor,  it should  be noted  that  the  language  from  this
portion  of  the  Croson  decision  did  not  draw  support  from  a
majority  of the  Court.

Any  preference  based  on  racial  or  ethnic  criteria
must  necessarily  receive  a  most  searching  exam-
ination  to  make  sure  that  it  does  not  conflict
with  constitutional  guarantees.

Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at  491.  (Emphasis  added.)  “Careful
judicial  evaluation,”  “narrow  tailor[ing]”  and  “most
searching  examination”  indicate  a  level  of scrutiny  much
closer  to  “strict  scrutiny”  than  to  “intermediate  scrutiny.”

Chief  Justice  Burger  set  forth  a  two  step  analysis  for
reviewing  the  Public  Works  Employment  Act  (PWEA)
program  reviewed  in  Fullilove.  However,  there  is nothing
in  that  analysis  from  which  to  infer  that  he  intended  any
standard  other  than  strict  scrutiny.  In  fact,  just  the  oppo-
site  is  true  as  shown  by the  language  quoted  above  and
by  the  second  step  of  Chief  Justice  Burger’s  analysis:

the  limited  use  of racial  and  ethnic  criteria  in the
context  presented,  is a  constitutionally  permiss-
ible  means  for  achieving  the  congressional  objec-
tives  and  does  not  violate  the  equal  protection
component  of  the  Due  Process  Clause  of  the
Fifth  Amendment.

Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at  473.  (Emphasis  in  original.)

This  Honorable  Court  has  always  used  “the  most
rigid  scrutiny”  in  considering  “violation{s]  [of]  the  equal
protection  component  of  the  Due  Process  Clause  of  the
Fifth  Amendment.”  Korematsu  v.  United  States,  323  U.S.
214,  216  (1944).°  “Most  rigid  scrutiny”  is “strict  scrutiny.”

9  “All  legal  restrictions  which  curtail  the  civil  rights  of a
single  racial  group  are  immediately  suspect.  It is not  to say  that
all  such  restrictions  are  unconstitutional.  It is to  say  that  courts

ilove,  recognized  that  the  “conventional”  test  is  “strict
scrutiny.”  Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at  519.

The  standard  of  review  articulated  by  Chief  Justice
Burger  was  not,  contrary  to  the  holding  of  the  Tenth
Circuit,  “a  lenient  standard,  resembling  intermediate
scrutiny,”  but  rather  the  “conventional”  “strict  scrutiny”
standard.'!°  Therefore,  the  Tenth  Circuit’s  opinion  is  in

must  subject  them  to  the  most  rigid  scrutiny.”  Korematsu  v.  United
States,  323  U.S.  at  216.  (Emphasis  added.)  See  also  University  of
California  Regents  v.  Bakke,  438  U.S.  265,  299  (1978)  (when  deci-
sion  making  touches  upon  an  individual’s  race  or  ethnic  back-
ground,  he is entitled  to a judicial  determination  that  the burden
he  is asked  to  bear  because  of his  race  or  ethnic  background  is
precisely  tailored  to  serve  a  compelling  governmental  interest);
Virginia  v.  Loving,  388  U.S.  1,  11  (1967)  (the  Equal  Protection
Clause  demands  that  racial  classifications  be  subjected  to  the
“most  rigid  scrutiny”);  and  Bolling  v.  Sharpe,  347  U.S.  497,  500
(1954)  (“[c]lassifications  based  solely  upon  race  must  be  scruti-
nized  with  particular  care  since  they  are  contrary  to  our  tradi-
tions  and  hence  constitutionally  suspect”).

10  The  conclusion  that  Chief  Justice  Burger’s  standard  of
review  is  the  conventional  standard  or  strict  scrutiny  is  bol-
stered  by the  concurring  opinion  of Justice  Powell.

Although  I would  place  greater  emphasis  than  the
Chief  Justice  on  the  need  to  articulate  judicial  stan-
dards  of  review  in  conventional  terms,  |  view  his
opinion  announcing  the  judgment  as  substantially  in
accord  with  my  own  views.  Accordingly,  I join  that
opinion  and  write  separately  to apply  the  analysis  set
forth  by my  opinion  in  University  of California  Regents
v.  Bakke,  438  U.S.  265  (1978).

Only  four  Justices  in  Metro  Broadcasting  agreed  “that
race-conscious  classifications  adopted  by  Congress  to
address  racial  and  ethnic  discrimination  are  subject  to  a
different  standard  than  such  classifications  prescribed  by
state  and  local  governments”  and  that  “intermediate
scrutiny”  is  the  proper  standard  of  review.  Four  Justices
do  not  constitute  a  majority  holding."

Justice  Stevens’  concurring  opinion  can  not  be  cited
as  a  basis  for  weakened  scrutiny  since  he  demands  that
the  reasons  for  racial  classifications  be  “clearly  identified
and  unquestionably  legitimate.”  Metro  Broadcasting  v.
FCC,  497  U.S.  at  601,  citing  his  dissenting  opinion  in
Fullilove.  In  his  dissenting  opinion  in  Fullilove,  Justice
Stevens  wrote  “[uJnless  Congress  clearly  articulates  the
need  and  basis  for  a  racial  classification,  and  also  tailors
the  classification  to  its  justification,  the  Court  should  not
uphold  this  kind  of  statute.”  Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at  545
(Justice  Stevens  dissenting).'2  Since  Justice  Stevens  dis-
sented  in  Fullilove  and  Justice  Marshall  concurred,  it can-
not  be  argued  that  Justice  Stevens’  view  of  the  proper

11  Those  four  justices  were  Brennan,  White,  Marshall  and

he  joined  in  the  decision,  but  through  which  he  set  forth  a
different  standard  of  review  from  the  standard  set  forth  by
Justice  Brennan.  Justice  Stevens’  standard  of  review  is  much
closer  to  “strict  scrutiny”  than  to  “intermediate  scrutiny.”  Metro
Broadcasting  v.  FCC,  497  U.S.  at  601-602  (concurring  opinion  of
Justice  Stevens).

12  Congress  has  done  neither.  It  has  not  “clearly  articu-
late[d]  the  need  and  basis  for  [the]  racial  classification”  and  has
not  “tailor[ed]  the  classification  to  its justification.”  “The  Court
should  not  uphold  this  .
.  statute.”  Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at  545
(Justice  Stevens  dissenting).

.

standard  of review  is the  same  as  that  set  forth  by Justice
Marshall  in  Fullilove.

While  Justice  Stevens  listed  several  situations  where
race-based  measures  are  “unquestionably  legitimate,”  the
list  does  not  include  highway  construction  or  government
contracts.  Metro  Broadcasting,  497  U.S.  at 601-602.13  Justice
Stevens’  failure  to  include  contracting  as  an  “unques-
tionably  legitimate”  race-based  measure  and  his  dissent
in  Fullilove  appear  to  manifest  his  view  that  contracting
requires  a  different  level  of  scrutiny  than,  for  example,
public  broadcasting,  and  that  congressionally-mandated
public  works  laws  are  subject  to a  more  rigorous  standard
of  review,  i.e.  strict  scrutiny.

As  this  Court  has  held  on  numerous  occasions,  strict
scrutiny  is  the  only  standard  of  review  to  be  applied  to
race-based  programs  like  the  one  at  issue  in  this  case,
even  if the  program  is  mandated  by Congress.  Since  the
decision  of  the  Tenth  Circuit  is  in  direct,  intolerable  and
irreconcilable  conflict  with  the  decisions  of  this  Honor-
able  Court  regarding  the  proper  standard  of  review  for
race-based  programs,  it is imperative  that  this  Honorable
Court  grant  this  Writ  of Certiorari  to  resolve  the  conflict.

13  That  list  is “extremely  narrow”  and  includes  “broadcast
diversity”;  “an  integrated  police  force”;  “a  public  school  fac-
ulty”;  and  “the  student  body  of  a  professional  school.”  Metro
Broadcasting,  497  U.S.  at  601-602.

14

B.  THE  DECISION  OF  THE  TENTH  CIRCUIT
COURT  OF  APPEALS,  REGARDING  THE
“PRINCIPAL  LESSON  OF  FULLILOVE,”  IS  IN
DIRECT  CONFLICT  WITH  THIS  COURT’S
DECISION  IN  FULLILOVE.

The  Tenth  Circuit  held:

The  splintered  Fullilove  Court  did  not  uphold
the  challenged  federal  program  because  a  speci-
fic  percentage  set-aside  was  mandated  by Con-
gress,  but  rather  because  the  program,  in spite of
the  racial  and  ethnic  preference,  satisfied  equal
protection  requirements.  448  U.S.  at  480  (Opin-
ion  of Burger,  C.J.)  In reaffirming  Fullilove  as  the
proper  standard  for  assessing  the  constitu-
tionality  of benign  race-conscious  measures,  the
Court  in  Metro  Broadcasting  noted  that,  notwith-
standing  the  “’great  weight’”  given  to  the  deci-
sions  of  Congress,  even  congressionally-
mandated  benign  race-conscious  measures  must
satisfy  equal  protection  requirements.  110  S.  Ct.
at  3011  (citation  omitted).

App.  21-22.  (Emphasis  in  original.)  The  Tenth  Circuit
opinion  is in  error  and  is in  direct  conflict  with  decisions
of  this  Honorable  Court.

1.  THE  STATUTE  IN  QUESTION  CONTAINS
NO  WAIVER  PROVISION  AS  REQUIRED
BY  THE  DECISIONS  OF  THIS  COURT.

In  Fullilove,  in  response  to a  facial  challenge  of a  race-
based,  set-aside  program,  this  Court  upheld  the  constitu-
tionality  of  that  federal  program.  However,  the  federal
program  was  upheld,  in large  part,  because  of the  flexible
nature  of  the  program,  specifically  a  “waiver”  provision

15

ca if
we

allowing  states  to  opt  out  of  the -race-based,  set-aside
federal  program  “where  application  of the  program  is not
feasible.”  Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at  468;  see  Croson,  488  U.S.  at
488-489 .14

This  Court  has  established  the  need  for  a  waiver
provision  if a  race-based  program  is to  withstand  consti-
tutional  scrutiny.  As  noted  in  Croson,  it was  the  presence
of  the  waiver  provision  which  ensured  the  constitu-
tionality  of  the  PWEA  when  challenged,  facially,  in  Full-
ilove..5  Thus,  the  waiver  provision  does  not  exist  for
administrative  tidiness  and  nicety,  but  to  ensure  that  the
only  basis  that  permits  resort  to  a  race-based  standard  -
that  is,  to  remedy  prior  state-sponsored  discrimination  -
is  not  circumvented.  This  Court  held  that  the  waiver
provision  was  to  be  used  to  prevent  the  unconstitutional
application  of  the  race-based  remedy  set  forth  in  the
PWEA.'6

There  is  no  waiver  or  similar  provision  in  the  Small
Business  Act  (15  U.S.C.  §644(g))  or  the  SCC.  As  noted  by
the  Tenth  Circuit:

The  Small  Business  Act  provides  the  statutory
authority  for  agencies  to  establish  specific  utili-
zation  goals  for disadvantaged  small  businesses.
15  U.S.C.  §644(g).  In  compliance  with  section
502  of  the  Small  Business  Act,  the  heads  of  the
various  federal  agencies,  including  the  DOT,

14  “[A]n  amorphous  claim  that  there  has  been  past discrim-
ination  in  a  particular  industry  cannot  justify  the  use  of  an
unyielding  racial  quota.”  Croson,  488  U.S.  at  499.

15  Croson,  488  U.S.  at 489.
16  Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at  481-482.

16

17

must  esiablish  annual  goals  for  small  business
participation  in  federal  procurement  contracts.
Id.  §644(g)(2).  These  goals  must  present  the
“maximum  practicable  opportunity”  for  small
business  concerns,  including  those  “owned  and
controlled  by  socially  and  economically  disad-
vantaged  individuals.”  Id.  §644(g)(1).

App.  5-6.  (Emphasis  added.)  The  continued  use  of  the
word  “shall”  throughout  the  statute  and  the  Tenth  Cir-
cuit’s  use  of  the  word  “must”  underscores  the  inflex-
ibility  of this  race-based  program.  There  is no  language  in
the  SBA  or  the  SCC  which  can  be  construed  as  a  waiver
provision,  let  alone  specific  language  which  meets  the
standard  required  by Fullilove.'”

Since  the  statute  in  question  contains  no  “waiver”
provision,  the  decision  of the  Tenth  Circuit  that  this  pro-
gram  passes  constitutional  muster  is  in  direct  conflict
with  the  decisions  of  this  Honorable  Court.  Therefore,
this  Petition  should  be  granted  to  remedy  the  conflict.

17  The  Tenth  Circuit  states:  “Because  Adarand  stipulates
that  section  502  satisfies  the  evidentiary  requirements  of  Full-
ilove,  the  only  question  to  be  resolved  is whether  the  SCC  pro-
gram  implemented  by  the  CLFHD  is  narrowly  tailored  to
achieve  the  remedial  purpose  of section  502.”  App.  22.  This  is a
mischaracterization  of Adarand’s  Reply  Brief.  The  Brief  states:
“ Adarand  concedes, for the sake of discussion,  that  the evidence  is
sufficient  to  satisfy  the  evidentiary  requirements  of  Fullilove.
However  this  concession  is  not  enough  to  save  the  Federal
Defendants’  argument.”  App.  43.  (Emphasis  added.)  By adding
the  language  “for  the  sake  of discussion,”  Adarand  was  stating
that  for purposes  of the discussion  it could  be assumed  that  the
This is much  differ-
evidence  met  the  requirements  of Fullilove.
ent  from  a  stipulation.  Adarand  simply  did  not  stipulate  as
indicated  by the  opinion  of the  Tenth  Circuit.

ae
ee

2.  THE  STATUTE  IN  QUESTION  IS  NOT
“NARROWLY  TAILORED”  AS  REQUIRED
BY  DECISIONS  OF  THIS  COURT.

To  pass  constitutional  scrutiny,  the  governmental
body  using  the  race-based  remedy  must  show  a  “compel-
ling  governmental  interest.”  In  addition,  the  use  of  the
limited  race-based  program  must  be  “narrowly  tailored”
to  meet  the  “compelling  governmental  interest”  thus
identified.  In  spite  of  the  decisions  of  this  Court,  the
Tenth  Circuit  held  that  the  “significant  governmental  pur-
pose  of  providing  subcontracting  opportunities  for  small
disadvantaged  business  enterprises”  meets  the  test.  App.
23.  (Emphasis  added.)  There  are  several  problems  with
this  holding.  First,  “significant  governmental  purpose”  is
not  what  the  government  must  show.  The  proper  stan-
dard  to be met,  according  to  the  decisions  of this  Court  is
a  “compelling  governmental  interest.”  There  is  no  “com-
pelling  governmental  interest”  identified  for  the  use  of a
race-based  program  in  the  SBA.'8

Furthermore,  the  statute  in  question  is  not  narrowly
tailored.  Narrow  tailoring  requires  that  the  race-
based  remedy  “will  not  last  longer  than  the  discriminatory
effects  it is designed  to  eliminate.”  Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at  523
(Powell,  concurring).  In other  words,  the  CFLHD  must,  as  a
result  of  the  Fifth  and  Fourteenth  Amendments,  determine
whether  the means chosen to effectuate  a clearly identified

.

“[A] generalized  assertion  that  there  has  been  past  dis-
crimination  in  an  entire  industry  provides  no  guidance  for  a
legislative  body  to  determine  the  precise  scope  of the  injury  it
aay o — It ‘has  no  logical  stopping  point.’  ”  Croson,  488

S.a

18

19

compelling  governmental  interest  are  “narrowly  tailored  to
the  achievement  of that  goal,”  that  is, “limited  in  scope  and
duration.”  Wygant  v.  Jackson  Board  of Education,  et al., 476  U.S.
267,  285  (1986).

In  finding  the  PWEA  program  challenged  in  Fullilove
constitutional,  Chief  Justice  Burger  focused  on  the  fact
that  the  program  under  review  was  “a  short-term  mea-
sure  to  alleviate  the  problem  of national  unemployment.”
Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at  457.19  Without  a  statutory  provision
establishing  a  limited  time  period  for  the  use  of the  race-
based  remedy,  the  program  cannot  be  said  to be  narrowly
tailored.

The  statute  challenged  in  this  Petition  has  been  in
place  since  1968  and  shows  signs  of lasting  forever.7°  The

19  “The  MBE  provision  may  be  viewed  as a pilot  project,
appropriately  limited  in extent  and  duration.  ..  .”  Fullilove,  448
U.S.  at  489  (Opinion  of  Burger,  C.J.)  “The  .
.  set-aside  is not  a
permanent  part  of federal  contracting  requirements.  As  soon  as
the  PWEA  program  concludes,  this  set-aside  program  ends.  The
temporary  nature  of  this  remedy  ensures  that  a  race-conscious
program  will  not  last  longer  that  the  discriminatory  effects  it is
designed  to  eliminate.”  Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at  513  (Concurring
opinion  of Powell,  J).

.

20  Not  only does  that  program  appear  to be here  to stay,  it is
also  a  “monster”  program.  The  statute  requires  a  minimum  of
5%  for  minority  participation  in all  of the  federal  government's
procurement  activities  and  demands  that  the  level  of participa-
tion  be  established  at  “the  maximum  practicable  opportunity”
for  DBEs,  which  could  represent  up  to  100%  of the  total  federal
government  procurement  contracts.  Even  5%  has  to  represent
billions  of dollars  which  are  awarded  based  on  race.  Obviously,
this  program  is  in  conflict  with  this  Court’s  opposition  to  race-
based  programs  which  are  “ageless  in  their  reach  into  the  past,
and  timeless  in  their  ability  to  affect  the  future.”  Wygant,  476

law  contains  no  automatic  sunset  provision  and  there  is
no  reason  to  believe  that  the  matter  will  be  revisited  by
Congress  in  the  near  future,  as  indicated  by  the  Tenth
Circuit:

[T]he  SCC  program  is  “appropriately  limited  in
extent  and  duration”  because  federal  procure-
ment  and  construction  contracting  practices  are
subject  to  regular  “reassessment  and  reevalua-
tion  by Congress.”

App.  23.  If  this  statement  is  true  then  every  action  of
Congress  is  “appropriately  limited  in  extent  and  dura-
tion.”  This  does  not  provide  the  protection  this  Court  has
held  that  the  Constitution  requires.

A  second  aspect  of  narrow  tailoring  is  that  the  pro-
gram  must  be  designed  to  remedy  clearly  identifiable
discrimination.?)  Yet,  since  there  are  no  findings  that
identify  the  discrimination  this  program  seeks  to  rem-
edy,”*  it suffers  from  the  same  problem  Justice  O’Connor
identified  in  Croson:

U.S.  at 276.  Mr.  Budwig:  “I’m  not  aware  that  [the Government’s
DBE  program’s]  end  is in  sight.”  App.  46-47.

21  “This  Court  never  has  held  that  societal  discrimination
alone  is  sufficient  to  justify  a  racial  classification.  Rather,  the
court  has  insisted  upon  some  showing  of  prior  discrimination
by the  governmental  unit  involved  before  allowing  limited  use
of racial  classifications  in order  to  remedy  such  discrimination.”
Wygant,  476  U.S.  at  274.  (Emphasis  added.)

22  This  is the  a problem  recognized  by Justice  Powell  in his

concurring  opinion  in Fullilove:

Although  the  set-aside  is pegged  at  a  reasonable  fig-
ure,  its effect  might be unfair  if it were  applied  rigidly

20

21

[I]t  is  almost  impossible  to  assess  whether  the
Richmond  Plan  is  narrowly  tailored  to  remedy
prior  discrimination  since  it  is  not  linked  to
identified  discrimination  in  any  way.

Croson,  488  U.S.  at  498.

Failure  of  this  race-based  program  to  provide  the
necessary  flexibility,  i.e.  waiver,  and  a  time  limit  for  its
use,  proves  that  the  program  at  issue  is  not  narrowly
tailored.

If  the  program  is  not  narrowly  tailored  it  is  uncon-
stitutional  under  the  strict  scrutiny  standard  required  by
the  decisions  of  this  Honorable  Court.  For  these  reasons
this  Petition  must  be  granted.

C.  THE  HOLDING  OF  THE  TENTH  CIRCUIT
COURT  OF  APPEALS  THAT  CONGRESS
“CAN  ENGAGE  MORE  FREELY  IN  AFFIRMA-
TIVE  ACTION  THAN  STATES  AND
LOCALITIES”  IS  IN  CONFLICT  WITH  DECI-
SIONS  OF  THIS  COURT.

The  Tenth  Circuit  held:

The  lesson  that  we  glean  from  Fullilove  and
Croson  is  that  the  federal  government,  acting

in  areas  of  the  country  where  minority  group  mem-
bers  constitute  a  small  percentage  of the  population.
Fullilove,  448  U.S.  514  (Justice  Powell,  concurring  opinion).  Col-
orado,  the  location  of the contract  at issue  in this  case  fits  within
the  areas  of the  country  described  by Justice  Powell.  Defendant
Budwig  testified  he  didn’t  believe  there  had  ever  been  any
discrimination  in  the  highway  construction  industry  in  Colo-
rado.  App.  45-46.

under  congressional  authority,  can  engage  more
freely  in  affirmative  action  than  states  and
localities.

App.  18.  This  statement  is  in  direct,  true  and  irreconcil-
able  conflict  with  the  holdings  of  this  Court:

In  view  of  our  decision  that  the  Constitution
prcehibits  the  states  from  maintaining  racially
segregated  public  schools,  it would  be  unthink-
able  that  the  same  Constitution  would  impose  a
lesser  duty  on  the  Federal  Government.

Bolling  v.  Sharpe,  347  U.S.  at  500.  The  holding  of the  Tenth
Circuit  with  regard  to  the  relationship  of Fullilove,  Croson,
and  Metro  Broadcasting  is  in  error.  Since  none  of  the
opinions  written  in  Fullilove  was  a  plurality,  let  alone  a
majority  opinion,  Fullilove  cannot  be  read  to  overrule
previous  Supreme  Court  precedence.

Although  Metro  Broadcasting  had  a  plurality,  it  did
not  have  the  necessary  majority  to  overrule  prior
Supreme  Court  precedence.  Nor  was  Bolling  v.  Sharpe,
addressed  by  Justice  Brennan  in  the  Metro  Broadcasting
case.  This  Honorable  Court  would  not  overrule  such  an
important  constitutional  precedent  by implication.

Finally,  the  portion  of  the  Croson  decision  which  is
cited  in  Metro  Broadcasting  for  the  proposition  that  Con-
gress  is treated  differently  by the  Constitution  than  other
governmental  bodies,  was  not  a  plurality  opinion  nor  a
majority  opinion.  There  are  no  majority  Supreme  Court
decisions  that  support  the  holding  of the  Tenth  Circuit  in
the  case  at bar.  The  decision  of the  Tenth  Circuit  is clearly
in  conflict  with  Bolling  v.  Sharpe.

22

23

To  resolve  this  true  and  direct  conflict  with  Bolling  v.

Sharpe,  this  Petition  must  be  granted.

II.  THE  DECISION  OF  THE  TENTH  CIRCUIT  COURT
OF  APPEALS  RAISES  IMPORTANT  CONSTITU-
TIONAL  ISSUES.

This  is an  important  challenge  to  the  constitutionality
of  a  race-based,  set-aside  program  adopted  by  Con-
gress.  It  asks  unanswered  questions  as  to  the  exact
relationship  among  the  decisions  of this  Court  in  Fullilove
v.  Klutznick,  448  U.S.  448  (1980);  City  of Richmond  v.  J.A.
Croson,  Co.,  488  U.S.  469  (1989)  and  Metro  Broadcasting  v.
FCC,  497  U.S.  547,  564  (1990).

While  Fullilove  upheld,  in  strictly  a  facial  challenge,
the  ability  of  Congress  to  adopt  race-based,  set-aside
programs;  while  Croson  struck  down a race-based,  set-
aside  program  adopted  by a city;  and  while  Metro  Broad-
casting,  appears  to  have  established  an  intermediate  stan-
dard  of  review  for  congressionally-mandated  acts;
unanswered  are  the  questions  regarding  an  “as  applied”
challenge  posed  by the  instant  Petition,  which  Chief  Jus-
tice  Burger  stated  in  Fullilove  would  have  to  await  future
challenges.?4  This  is  that  “future  case[  ].”

23  “Under  our  Constitution,  any  official  action  that  treats  a
person  differently  on  account  of  his  race  or  ethnic  origin  is
inherently  suspect  and  presumptively  invalid.”  Fullilove,  448
U.S.  at  523,  (Justice  Stewart,  dissenting  opinion).

24  “We  are  not  presented  here  with  a  challenge  involving  a
specific  award  of a  construction  contract  or  the  denial  of a  waiver
request;  such  questions  of specific  application  must  await  future
cases.”  Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at  486.  (Emphasis  added.)

This  case  presents  the  unanswered  questions:  (1)
Whether,  in  a  facial  and  as  applied  constitutional  chal-
lenge,  a  law  that  is  an  equal  protection  violation  when
enacted  by a state  is transformed  into  an  equal  protection
guarantee  when  enacted  by  Congress?25  (2)  Whether
“strict  scrutiny”  as  opposed  to  “a  lenient  standard,
resembling  intermediate  scrutiny”  is the  proper  standard
of  review  for  determining  the  constitutionality  of a  race-
based  program  adopted  by  Congress?  (3)  Whether  the
Fifth  Amendment  requires  a  federal  agency,  in  imple-
menting  a  federal  race-based,  set  aside  program,  to  con-
duct  the  inquiry  set  forth  in  City  of Richmond  v.  J.A.
Croson,  Co.,  488  U.S.  469  (1989),  especially  when  the  level
of  minority  participation  set  by  the  agency  far  exceeds
the  level  of  participation  required  by Congress?  At  the
time  of  the  West  Dolores  contract  award,  the  CFLHD’s
goal  was  set  at  12%  to  15%  of  its  annual  construction
budget,  that  is, 7%  to  10%  higher  than  set  by Congress  in
the  SBA.  App.  49.

Croson  commands  that  a  governmental  unit,  even  if
implementing  a  congressionally-mandated  program,  con-
duct  an  inquiry  in  order  to  ascertain  that  the

25  In  stating  a  concern  with  the  holding  of  the  Court  in
Fullilove,  Justice  Kennedy,  in  a  concurring  opinion  in  Croson,
stated:  “The  process  by which  a  law  that  is an  equal  protection
violation  when  enacted  by a  State  becomes  transformed  to  an
equal  protection  guarantee  when  enacted  by Congress  poses  a
difficult  proposition  for  me;  but  as  it  is  not  before  us,  any
reconsideration  of  that  issue  must  await  some  further  case.”
Croson,  488  U.S.  at  518.

24

implementation  of  a  race-based  program  above  that  set
by Congress  meets  the  requirements  of the Constitution.”¢

While  the  SBA  may  require  the  CFLHD  to  implement
a  race-based  remedy,  when  the CFLHD  exceeds  that  level,
it  must  do  so  in  a  constitutional  manner.  The  Fifth  and
Fourteenth  Amendments  to  the  U.S.  Constitution  compel
the  CFLHD  to  make  the  decision  to  raise  the  level  at
which  the  race-based  quota  is set  in  a  constitutional  man-
ner.  The  constitutional  method  is set  forth  in  Croson.  The
failure  of the  CFLHD  to  determine  whether  12  to  15%  as
opposed  to  5%  was  constitutionally  permissible  renders
the  CFLHD  program  unconstitutional.

This  Court  should  grant  certiorari  in  order  to  end  the
Constitutional  abuse  now  sanctioned  by  the  decision
below.

25

CONCLUSION

For  all  of the  above  reasons,  this  Court  should  grant
the  petition  of  Adarand  in  this  matter  and  review  the
erroneous  decision  of the  United  States  Court  of Appeals
for  the  Tenth  Circuit.

26  As  this  Court  made  clear  in  Croson,  a  state  must:  (1)
search  for  and  find  a  history  of state-sponsored  discrimination;
(2)  assert  the  compelling  state  interest  sought  to  be  served  by
the  race-based  remedy;  and  (3)  narrowly  tailor  the  race-based
remedy  both  as  to:  (a)  the  beneficiary  of  the  racial  bias,  that  is,
applying  the  remedy  to one  who  has  been  a victim  of such  state-
sponsored  discrimination;  and  (b)  the  duration  of  the  race-
based  remedy.  Croson,  488  U.S.  469.

App.  2

App.  3

Lisa  J.  Stark,  Attorney,  Civil  Rights  Division,  United
States  Department  of  Justice  (James  P.  Turner,  Acting

Before  HOLLOWAY,  GARTH*  and  McKAY,  Circuit
Judges.

GARTH,  Circuit  Judge.

This  appeal  involving  a  reverse  discrimination  chal-
lenge  requires  us  to  review  the  constitutionality  of  a
federal  program  designed  to  provide  contract  awards  for
disadvantaged  business  enterprises.

Appellant  Adarand  Constructors,  Inc.  (“Adarand”)
appeals  an  order  of  the  district  court  granting  summary
judgment  in  favor  of the  Government  appellees  [hereinaf-
ter  “Government” ]. 790  F. Supp.  240.  On  appeal,  Adarand
raises  three  arguments.  First,  it  argues  that  the  district
court  erred  in  holding  that  the  proper  standard  to  be
applied  is  found  in  Fullilove  v.  Klutznick,  448  U.S.  448
(1980),  rather  than  in  City  of Richmond  v.  ].A.  Croson,  Co.,
488  U.S.  469  (1989).

*  The  Honorable  Leonard  I.  Garth,  Senior  United  States
Circuit  Judge,  United  States  Court  of  Appeals  for  the  Third
Circuit,  sitting  by designation.

Second,  relying  on  Croson,  Adarand  asserts  that  the
Central  Federal  Lands  Highway  Division  (CFLHD)  must
make  specific  findings  of  past  discrimination  in  order  to
justify  its  reliance  on  the  disadvantaged  business  enter-
prise  (DBE)  program  which  furnishes  the  necessary  crite-
ria  for  the  federal  agency’s  implementation  of  a  race-
conscious  subcontracting  compensation  clause  (“the  SCC
program”).

Third,  Adarand  contends  that  section  502  of  the
Small  Business  Act,  15  U.S.C.  § 644(g),  which  provides
the  statutory  authorization  for  the  challenged  SCC  pro-
gram,  is  unconstitutional  as  applied  because  it  imper-
missibly  delegates  to  federal  agencies  the  authority  to
develop  minority-participation  goals  and  the  means  for
achieving  those  goals.

Our  jurisdiction  is  invoked  under  28  U.S.C.  § 1291,
and  we  affirmed  the  district  court’s  judgment  of April  21,
1992  for  the  Government.  We  do  so,  however,  on  grounds
different  from  those  relied  upon  by  the  district  court.!

I.

Adarand,  a  highway  construction  company  run  by a
white  male,  sought  declaratory  and  permanent  injunctive
relief  against  officials  of the  federal  Department  of Trans-
portation  (DOT)  and  other  DOT  officials  on  the  grounds
that  the  SCC  program  utilized  by the  CFLHD  violates  the

!  Helvering  v.  Gowran,  302  U.S.  238,  245  (1937)  (“[T]he  rule  is
settled  that  if the  decision  below  is correct,  it must  be  affirmed,
although  the  district  court  relied  upon  a  wrong  ground  or  gave
a  wrong  reason”).

ee

App.  4

equal  protection  guarantees  of  the  Fifth  and  Fourteenth
Amendments  and  the  privileges  and  immunities  guaran-
teed  by 42  U.S.C.  § 1983  and  42  U.S.C.  § 2000d  (Title  VI).?
Adarand’s  complaint  erroneously  alleged  that  the  statu-
tory  authorization  for  the  SCC  program  was  provided  by
two  transportation  assistance  acts:  the  Surface  Transpor-
tation  Assistance  Act  of 1982,  Pub.  L.  No.  97-424,  § 105(f),
96  Stat.  2097,  2100  (STAA),  and  its  successor,  the  Surface
Transportation  and  Uniform  Relocation  Assistance  Act  of
1987,  Pub.  L.  No.  100-17,  §  106(c),  101  Stat.  132,  145
(STURAA).  Both  Adarand  and  the  Government  filed
cross  motions  for  summary  judgment  with  the  district
court.  The  district  court  also  mistakenly  determined  that
the  challenged  program  was  authorized  by  STAA  and
STURAA.  For  purposes  of this  appeal,  both  parties  stipu-
late  that  the  SCC  program  is authorized  by section  502  of
the  Small  Business  Act,  15  U.S.C.  § 644(g),  and  that  the
constitutionality  of  STAA  and  STURAA  are  not  at  issue.

agencies  referred  to  within  this  opinion,  we  list  the  following
acronyms:

A.

The  Small  Business  Act  provides  the  statutory
authority  for  agencies  to  establish  specific  utilization
goals  for  disadvantaged  small  businesses.  15  U.S.C.
§ 644(g).  In  compliance  with  section  502  of  the  Small
Business  Act,  the  heads  of  the  various  federal  agencies,
including  the  DOT,  must  establish  annual  goals  for  small
business  participation  in  federal  procurement  contracts.3
Id.  § 644(g)(2).  These  goals  must  present  the  “maximum

*  Section  502  of the  Small  Business  Act  provides:

The  President  shall  annually  establish  Govern-
ment-wide  goals  for  procurement  contracts  awarded
to  small  business  concerns  and  small!  business  con-
cerns  owned  and  controlled  by socially  and  economi-
cally  disadvantaged  individuals.  The  Government-
wide  goal  for  participation  by  small  business  con-
tracts  shall  be  established  at  not  less  than  20 percent
of the total  value  of all prime  contract  awards  for each
fiscal  year.  The  Government-wide  goal  for  participa-
tion  by small  business  concerns  owned  and  controlled
by socially  and  economically  disadvantaged  individ-
uals  shall  be  established  at  not  less  than  5 percerit  of
the  total  value  of  all  prime  contract  and  subcontract
awards.  Notwithstanding  the  Government-wide  goal,
each  agency  shall  have  an  annual  goal  that  presents,  for
that  agency,  the  maximum  practicable  opportunity  for
small  business  concerns  and  small  business  concerns
owned  and  controlled  by socially  and  economically  disad-
vantaged  individuals  to  participate  in  the  performance  of
contracts  let  by such  agency.  The  Administration  and
the  Administrator  of  the  Office  of  Federal  Procure-
ment  Policy  shall,  when  exercising  their  authority
pursuant  to paragraph  (2),  insure  that  the  cumulative
annual  prime  contract  goals  for  all  agencies  meet  or
exceed  the  annual  Government-wide  prime  contract

App.  6

practicable  opportunity”  for  small  business  concerns,
including  those  “owned  and  controlled  by  socially  and
economically  disadvantaged  individuals.”  Id.  § 644(g)(1).

The  SCC  program  was  implemented  in  1979  by  the
Federal  Lands  Highway  Program  (FLHP)  of  the  Federal
Highway  Administration  (FHWA),  an  agency  within  the
DOT,  to  satisfy  the  agency  goals  requirement  of the  Small
Business  Act.  CFLHD  and  the  other  two  regional  offices
of  FLHP  utilize  the  SCC  program  as  a  means  of  meeting
their  respective  apportioned  shares  of  the  DOT  goal  for
disadvantaged  small  business  participation.  Under  the
SCC  program,  a  Subcontracting  Compensation  Clause  is
included  in  small-value  contracts  or  where  the  prime
contractor  is  a  small  business.  Prime  contractors  whose

goal  established  by  the  President  pursuant  to  this
paragraph.
