IN  THE
Supreme  Court of the United  States

OCTOBER  TERM,  1994

No.  93-1841

ADARAND  CONSTRUCTORS,  INC.,

Vv.

Petitioner,

FEDERICO  PENA,
SECRETARY  OF  TRANSPORTATION,  et  ai.,

Respondents.

On  Writ  Of Certiorari  To  The
United  States  Court  Of Appeals
For  The  Tenth  Circuit

BRIEF  AMICUS  CURIAE  OF
NATIONAL  ASSOCIATION  FOR  THE
ADVANCEMENT  OF  COLORED  PEOPLE
IN  SUPPORT  OF  RESPONDENTS

INTEREST  OF  AMICUS  CURIAE

Pursuant  to  Rule  37.3  of  this  Court,  the  National
Association  For  The  Advancement  Of  Colored  People
(NAACP)  respectfully  submits  this  brief  amicus  curiae  in
support  of Respondents.  Written  consent  to  the  filing  of
by counsel  for all parties.  Copies
this brief has  been granted

-2-

of  the  letters  of  consent  have  been  lodged  with  the  Clerk
of  the  Court.

The  NAACP  is  the  oldest  and  largest  civil  rights
organization  in  America,  with  over  500,000  members  in
some  | ,800 branches  throughout  the country.  The  NAACP
appears  frequently  in this  Court  representing  the  interests
of blacks  and  other  minorities  and  often  files  briefs  amicus
curiae  in  cases  of special  importance  to  Black  Americans.

This  case  is  of  particular  concern  to  the  NAACP
because  it involves  consideration  of a statutory  protection
which  Congress  has  extended  to  the  minority  construction
industry.  Over  the years,  the  NAACP  has expended  a large
proportion  of its  resources  attempting  to open  the  Nation's
construction  industry  to black  workers  and contractors.  We
have  filed  suit  against  construction  labor  unions  and
contractors  as  well  as  public  agencies  dispensing  funds  for
public  construction  projects  where  blacks  and  others  were
excluded  from  employment  opportunities.  We  have  persis-
tently  urged  Congress  and  the  executive  branch  to  create
programs  which  foster  opportunities  for  minorities  to
compete  fairly  in the  building  construction  industry.

The  instant  case raises  a challenge  to a statutory  vehicle
which  holds  substantial  promise  for  facilitating  the  entry
of  blacks  into  the  construction  industry  and  to  the  very
authority  of Congress  to  enact  such  remedial!  legislation.
If Petitioners  were  successful  in this  challenge,  blacks  and
other  minorities  who  wish  to  participate  in  the  Nation's
construction  industry  would  be  harmed.

Because  this  case  raises  questions  of  paramount
significance  to  the  public  interest,  we  believe  that  our
perspective  will  complement  the  brief  of Respondents  and
assist  the  Court  in the  resolution  of these  issues.

alte

SUMMARY  OF  ARGUMENT

We had thought the question  before  the Court,  whether
the Court should  review  federal  minority set-aside  programs
under  strict  scrutiny  or  heightened  scrutiny,  to  have  been
well-established.
In  Fullilove  v.  Klutznick,  448  U.S.  448
(1979),  and  more  recently  in  City  of Richmond  v.  J.A.
Croson  Co. ,  488 U.S.  469 (1989),  and Metro Broadcasting,
Inc.  v.  FCC,  497 U.S.  547  (1990),  the Court  has recognized
that  it must  grant  some  measure  of deference  to Congress’
policy  choices  in the  exercise  of its  enumerated  powers  --
in particular,  its powers  under  Section  5 of the  Fourteenth
Furthermore,  the  Court  has  held  that  the
Amendment.
constraints  of the Equal Protection  Clause  on Congress  itself
are  not  the  same  constraints  as  the  Clause  demands  of the
states.  Thus,  heightened scrutiny is the appropriate standard
of  review  for  remedial  statutes  enacted  by Congress.

In  our  view,  whatever  uncertainty  concerning  the
proper  standard  of review  existed  prior to Croson  and Metro
Broadcasting , see  United States  v.  Paradise,  480 U.S.  149,
166-67  n.17  (1987);  Sheet  Metal  Workers  v.  EEOC,  478  U.S.
421, 480 (1986),  has been  resolved.  But  in any  event,  even
were  the  Court  to apply  strict  scrutiny,  the  question  of the
“formulas  of  analysis"  employed  by  the  Court  may  be
irrelevant,  as  the  socially  and  economically  disadvantaged
business  enterprise  preference  program  created  under  the
Small  Business  Act  “would  survive  judicial  review  under
either  ‘test.’"  Fullilove,  448 U.S.  at 492 (opinion of Burger,
C.J.);  see  also  Paradise,  480  U.S.  at  166-67  (opinion  of
Brennan,  J.);  Sheet  Metal  Workers,  478  U.S.  at  480.

s@e

ofits

I.

THE  COURT’S  PRIOR  DECISIONS  COR-
RECTLY  ESTABLISH  THAT  FEDERAL
MINORITY  PREFERENCE  PROGRAMS  ARE
SUBJECT  TO  HEIGHTENED  SCRUTINY.

A.  Congress  has  plenary  authority  to enact  set-asides
such  as  Section  502  of the  Small  Business  Act,  15  U.S.C.
§ 644(g).  It may  claim  its  authority  from  “an  amalgam  of
its  specifically  delegated  powers."  Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at
473 (opinion  of Burger,  C.J.).  Most prominent  among  these
are  the  “broad  remedial  powers  of Congress"  (id.  at  483)
to  “determin{e]  whether  and  what  legislation  is needed  to
secure  the  guarantees  of  the  Fourteenth  Amendment."
Katzenbach  v.  Morgan,  384  U.S.  641,  651  (1966).

It is the special  province  of Congress  “to enforce  equal
protection guarantees,”  a vested  “competence and authority”
shared  with  “no  [other]  organ  of  government,  state  or
federal."  Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at 483.  Thus,  “Congress  ...
has  a specific  constitutional  mandate  to enforce  the dictates
of the  Fourteenth  Amendment."  Croson,  488  U.S.  at 490.

That  Congress  has  been  vested  with  the  power  to
enforce  Section  | of the  Fourteenth  Amendment  suggests
that  Congress  enjoys powers  in preference  to the states.  See
Croson,  488  U.S.  at  490-92.  As  the  Seventh  Circuit  has
recognized,  “[(t}he  joint  lesson  of Fullilove  and  Croson  is
that the federal  government can,  by virtue  of the enforcement
clause  of the  Fourteenth  Amendment,  engage  in affirmative
action  with  a  freer  hand  than  states  and  municipalities  can
do."  Milwaukee  County  Pavers  Ass'n  v.  Fielder,  922  F.2d
414,  423-24  (7th Cir.)  (Posner,  J.),  cert.  denied,  500  U.S.
954  (1991).

Because  of Congress’  express  power  to  enforce  the
Fourteenth  Amendment,  this Court should  respect  the policy
choices  made  by Congress  in the  exercise  of its discretion.
Metro  Broadcasting,  497  U.S.  at  563.  The  federal  courts

stand  as  sentinels  to secure  congressional  compliance  with
the  minimal  guarantees  of the  Equal  Protection  Clause,  but
must  recognize  the equal,  and perhaps  even  superior,  claim
of Congress  to insist  on state  conduct  beyond those  minimal
dictates.  Katzenbach,  384  U.S.  at 651  & n.10.  Similarly,
the Court  should  accord  even  greater  latitude  to congressio-
nal  judgment  of the  need  for  federal  remedial  programs.

While  in  our  view  Congress  may  claim  sufficient
avf>ority  in Section  5 for the provision of the Small  Business
Act  under  review  here,  Congress  need  not  rest  its authority
on  Section  5 alone.  The  grant  program  is an  exercise  of
Congress’  authority  under the Spending Clause,  U.S.  Const.
art.  I, § 8, cl.  1, and  the  Commerce  Clause,  U.S.  Const.
art.  I,  § 8.  cl.  3.
Under  the  Spending  Clause  Congress
may  “further broad policy objectives  by conditioning  receipt
of  federal  moneys  upon  compliance  by the  recipient  with
federal  statutory  and administrative  directives."  Fullilove,
448  U.S.  at  474.  Through  its  spending  power,  Congress
may  encourage  what  it may  not  compel,  South  Dakota  v.
Dole,  483  U.S.  203  (1987),  and  may  discourage  what  it
cannot  outright  forbid.
Harris  v.  McRae,  448  U.S.  297
(1980).  And  through  its commerce  power,  Congress  might
regulate  directly  “the  practices  of  prime  contractors  on
federally  funded  public  works  projects."  Fullilove,  448  U.S.
at 475  (citing Katzenbach  v.  McClung,  379 U.S.  294 (1964);
Heart  of Atlanta  Motel,  Inc.  v.  United  States,  379  U.S.  241
(1964)).

B.

Because  of  the  deference  due  Congress  in  the
exercise  of  its  enumerated  powers,  the  Court  has  insisted
that  federal  racial  preference  programs  satisfy  only  height-
In  Fullilove,  this  Court  considered  a
ened  scrutiny.
provision  of the  Local  Public  Works  Capital  Development
and  Investment  Act  of  1976,  as  amended  by  the  Public
Works  Employment  Act  of  1977,  which  required  a  ten
percent  Minority  Business  Enterprise  (MBE)  participation
in local public works projects  funded  through  federal  grants.

sin

“=

The  Court  noted  that  the  MBE  requirement  in  the  Public
Works  Act  was  part  of the  “ongoing  efforts  toward  deliver-
ance"  of  “equality  of economic  opportunity"  by the  Small
Business  Association.  448  U.S.  at 463  (opinion  of Burger,
C.J.).  Three  members  of  the  Court  upheld  the  set-aside
through  “close  examination"  (id.  at 472)  and  “careful  judicial
evaluation"  (id.  at 480)  in order  "to assure  that any congres-
sional  program  that  employs  racial  or  ethnic  criteria  to
accomplish  the  objective  of remedying  the  present  effects
of past discrimination  is narrowly  tailored  to the  achievement
of  that  goal."
Those  justices  emphasized  that  the
inquiry  into  Congress’  powers  was  of "limited  scope"  (ibid. ).

Jbid.

Three  additional  members  of the  Court  made  explicit
their  view  that  the  exacting  scrutiny  with  which  the  Court
judged  invidious  discrimination  was  "inapposite  to  racial
classifications  that  provide  benefits  to  minorities  for  the
purpose  of  remedying  the  present  effects  of  past  racial
discrimination."  448  U.S.  at 518  (Marshall,  J., concurring
Accordingly,  “the  proper  inquiry  is
in  the  judgment).
whether  racial  classifications  designed  to  further  remedial
purposes  serve  important  governmental  objectives  and  are
substantially  related  to  achievement  of those  objectives."
Id.  at  519.

Any  lingering  doubts  about  the  standard  of review  for
congressional  remedial  efforts  was  put  to  rest  in  Metro
Broadcasting.  In  that  case  the  Court  considered  an  equal
protection  challenge  to  the  Federal  Communications
Commission’s  minority  preference  policy.  A majority  there
stated  unambiguously  that  “benign  race-conscious  measures
mandated  by  Congress  --  even  if those  measures  are  not
‘remedial’  in  the  sense  of  being  designed  to  compensate
victims  of past  governmental  or  societal  discrimination  --
are  constitutionally  permissible  to the extent  that they serve
important  governmental  objectives  within  the  power  of
Congress  and  are  substantially  related  to  achievement  of
those  objectives."  497  U.S.  at  564-65  (footnote  omitted).

The  Court  made  plain  in  Metro  Broadcasting  that  it
“appl[ied]  that standard"  recognized  in Fullilove.  Id.  at 564.
See  “Joint  Statement,  Constitutional  Scholars’  Statement  on
Affirmative  Action  After  City of Richmond  v.  J.A.  Croson
Co.,"  98  Yale  L.J.  1711  (1989).

Nothing  in  the  Court’s  opinion  in  Croson  is  to  the
contrary.  The  majority  in that  case  found  that  the  Court’s
“treatment  of  an  exercise  of  congressional  power  in
Fullilove"  was  not  dispositive  of  treatment  afforded  an
exercise  of  municipal  power.
Croson,  488  U.S.  at  491.
While  a  plurality  of  the  Court  suggested,  as  had  Justice
Powell  in  Fullilove  (448  U.S.  at  507  (Powell,  J.,  concur-
ring);  see  also  Wygant  v.  Jackson  Board  of Education,  476
U.S.  267,  273  (1986)  (Powell,  J., concurring);  Regents  of
the  University  of California  v.  Bakke,  438  U.S.  265,  305
(1978)  (Powell,  J., concurring))  that  strict  scrutiny  should
govern  all  cases  of racial  discrimination  (488  U.S.  at  494
(opinion  of O’Connor,  J.);  see  also  id.  at  520  (Scalia,  J.,
concurring  in  the  judgment)),  the  plurality  very  carefully
pointed  out  that  the  Court  could  not  accord  the  City  of
Richmond  the  same  level  of deference  as  it had  accorded
“That
Congress  in  Fullilove.
Congress  may  identify  and  redress  the effects  of society-wide
discrimination  does  not  mean  that,  a fortiori,  the States  and
their  political  subdivisions  are  free  to  decide  that  such
remedies  are  appropriate.
Section  1  of  the  Fourteenth
Amendment  is an explicit  constraint  on  state  power,  and the
States  must  undertake  any  remedial  efforts  in  accordance
with that provision.”  488 U.S.  at 490 (opinion  of O'Connor,
J.)  (emphasis  in  original).  Applying  heightened  scrutiny
to federal  remedial  programs  recognizes  both  that Congress
has  primary  responsibility  for  remedial  programs  and  that
the  Equal  Protection  Clause  is expressly  a constraint  on  the
states.

As  the  plurality  stated:

C.

That  Congress  should  possess  the  authority  to
remedy  broad-based  societal  discrimination  is consistent  with

the  conceptual  underpinnings  of our  structure  of government,
whose  roots  can  be traced  to the  ideas  of Locke  and  Hobbes.
See  G.  Mace,  Locke,  Hobbes,  And  The  Federalist  Papers,
In  their  view,  the  natural  equality  which  all
7-8  (1979).
people  possess  by right  is inevitably  disrupted  by dynamics
beyond  their  control.  When  free  individuals  agree  to  be
bound  by the  laws  of a  government,  it is with  the  under-
standing  that  the  government  will,  among  other  things,  act
to  remedy  unjust  inequalities.  This  necessarily  entails  the
proposition  that  the  government  has  the  power  to  take
measures  sufficient  to achieve  that  end..  The  pre-eminence
accorded  our  national  legislature  --  in the  sense  of its duty
to  act  for  the  general  welfare  --  identifies  it as  the  body
endowed  with  a unique  and  particular  power  to confront  the
problems  of  society  as  a  whole.  When  Congress  acts  to
remedy  the  evils  of  discrimination,  it  does  so  with  an
authority  not  present  in statehouses  or town  halls.  Accord-
ingly,  it is appropriate  that Congress  should  be given greater
deference  than  state  and  local  governments  in securing  the
guarantees  of the  Equal  Protection  Clause.

Il.  EVEN  IF  THE  COURT  WERE  TO  APPLY
STRICT  SCRUTINY,  THE SMALL  BUSINESS
ACT DISADVANTAGED  BUSINESS  ENTER-
PRISE  PREFERENCE  IS  NARROWLY  TAI-
LORED  TO A COMPELLING  GOVERNMEN-
TAL  INTEREST.

The  compelling  rationale  for  the  MBE  program  in
Fullilove  --  what  the  plurality  referred  to  as  the  “ongoing
efforts  directed  toward  deliverance  of  the  century-old
promise  of equality  of economic  opportunity,"  448  U.S.  at
463  --  had  its  origins  in  the  same  section  of  the  Small
Business  Act at issue  here,  Section  8(a),  15 U.S.C.  § 637(a).
See  Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at 463-64.  That  section  authorizes
the  SBA,  as  “necessary  or  appropriate”  to  let  subcontracts
to  “socially  and  economically  disadvantaged  small  business

-9-

concerns.”  15  U.S.C.  § 637(a)(1)(B).  See  also  15 U.S.C.
§ 644(g).

15  U.S.C.  § 637(d)(3)(A)  further  provides:

It  is  the  policy  of  the  United  States  that  small
business  concerns  and  small  business  concerns
owned  and  controlled  by socially  and economical-
ly  disadvantaged  individuals  shall  have  the
maximum  practicable  opportunity  to  participate
in the performance  of contracts  let by any Federal
agency....

The  House  Subcommittee  on  SBA  Oversight  and  Minority
Enterprise  has  explained  the  need  for  such  authority:

The  effects  of  past  inequities  stemming  from
racial  prejudice  have  not  remained  in the  past.
The Congress  has recognized  the reality that past
discriminatory  practices  have,  to  some  degree,
adversely  affected  our  present  economic  system.

...  The  presumption  must  be  made  that  past  dis-
criminatory  systems  have  resulted  in  present
economic  inequalities.
In  order  to  right  this
situation,  the  Congress  has  formulated  certain
remedial  programs  designed  to  uplift  those
socially  or  economically  disadvantaged  persons
to a level  where  they may  effectively  participate
in  the  business  mainstream  of our  economy.

448  U.S.  at 465-66  (quoting  H.R.  Rep.  No.  468, 94th Cong,
ist  Sess.  1-2  (1975)).
In  a  subsequent  report  the  House
Committee  on  Small  Business  found:

[W]e  more  often  encounter  a  business  system
which  is racially  neutral  on  its face,  but  because
of past  overt  social  and economic  discrimination
is  presently  operating,  in  effect,  to  perpetuate
these  past  inequities.  Minorities,  until  recently,

-10-

x.  *

have  not  participated  to  any  measurable  extent,
in our  total  business  system  generally,  or  in the
construction  industry,  in  particular.

Id.  at  466  n.48  (quoting  H.R.  Rep.  No.  1791,  95th  Cong,
Ist  Sess.  182  (1977)).

Furthermore,  the  Small  Business  Act’s  DBE  program
is less  restrictive,  and therefore  less coercive,  than the MBE
program  at  issue  in Fullilove.  The  Public  Works  Employ-
ment  Act  required  a ten  percent  set-aside  for  MBE’s.  See
448  U.S.  at 454.  By contrast  the  Department  of Transporta-
tion,  the  federal  contracting  agency  here,  does  not  require
contractors  participating  in  the  Federal  Lands  Highway
Program  (as  was  Adarand  Constructors)  to  hire  DBE’s.
Rather  they  are  encouraged  to  do  so  through  an  additional
incentive  payment.  DOT  indirectly  subsidizes  the  hiring
of DBE’s,  payments  which  Congress  could  plainly  authorize
DOT  to  make  directly  to  DBE’s.

The  DBE  program  is not overinclusive.  As implement-
ed  by  DOT,  the  DBE  program  does  not  rely  on  broad
definitions  or  stereotypes  of what  constitutes  a Disadvantaged
Business  Enterprise,  but  is narrowly  tailored  to the driving
need  described  by  Congress  to  “uplift  ...
socially  or
economically  disadvantaged  persons,”  including  minorities.
Congress  defined  a  “socially  and  economically  disadvan-
taged"  small  business  as  one  of which  at  least  51  percent
is  owned  by  “socially  and  economically  disadvantaged
individuals  includ{ing]  Black  Americans,  Hispanic  Ameri-
cans,  Native  Americans,  Asian  Pacific  Americans,  and other
minorities,  or  any  other  individual  found  to  be  disadvan-
taged.”  15  U.S.C.  § 637(d)(3)(C)  (emphasis  added).

The DOT  regulations  implementing  this section  further
define  DBE’s  to  make  clear  that  those  within  this  list  “are
presumed  to  be  both  socially  and  economically  disadvan-
taged,”  but  that  “[t}his  presumption  is  rebuttable."

49  C.F.R.  Part  23,  Subpart  D,  App.  C.  The  regulations
explain  that  "[t}his  means  that  ...  a third  party  may  present
evidence  that the firm’s  owners  are  not truly socially  and/or
economically  disadvantaged,  even  though they are  members
of one  of the  presumptive  groups."  The  classification  of
a business  as  a DBE  cannot  be overinclusive  for the  simple
reason  that  the  presumption  is always  subject  to  challenge.
The  statute  but  provides  for  an  inference  of  fact,  not  a
presumption  of  law.  See  NLRB  v.  Curtin  Matheson,  494
In
U.S.  775,  814-15  (1990)  (Scalia,  J.,  dissenting).
Fullilove,  the  plurality  found  it  “significant”  that  the
“administrative  scheme  [of MBE  classification]  provides  for
waiver  and exemption,”  (448 U.S.  at 487 (opinion  of Burger,
C.J.)),  and  the  DBE  scheme  here  provides  for even  greater
flexibility.

For  similar  reasons  the  DBE  classification  is  not
underinclusive.
The  same  DOT  regulations  provide  that
"[i]Jndividuals  who  are  not  presumed  to  be  socially  and
economically  disadvantaged  by virtue  of membership  in one
of these  groups  may,  nevertheless,  be  found  to  be  socially
and  economically  disadvantaged  on  a case-by-case  basis."
49 C.F.R.  Part 23, Subpart  D, App.  C.  Social  disadvantage
must  “stem  from  his  or  her  color;  national  origin;  gender;
physical  handicap;  long-term  residence  in  an  environment
isolated  from  the  mainstream  of American  society;  or  other
similar  cause  beyond  the  individual’s  control."  The  social
disadvantage  must  be “chronic,  longstanding,  and substantial,
not  fleeting  or  insignificant"  and  should  be  linked  to
"impairment  of business  opportunities."

Ibid.

All  of these  factors  suggest  that  no  one  is presumptively
excluded  from  participation  in the DBE  program  on  the basis
of race,  color  or  national  origin, just as  no  one  conclusively
qualifies  for  the  DBE  program  on  the  basis  of race,  color,
or  national  origin.  In this  respect  the  DBE  program  is far
less  underinclusive  than  the  MBE  program  upheld  in
Fullilove.  See  448  U.S.  at  485-86.

-  12-

Finally,  the  DBE  program  implemented  by DOT  suffers
from  none  of the flaws  of the Richmond  program  in Croson.
The  DBE  program  is narrowly  tailored  to remedy  the effects
of  prior  discrimination  as  found  by Congress.  Like  “the
congressional  scheme  upheld  in Fullilove,"  which  “allowed
for  a  waiver  of  the  set-aside  provision  where  an  MBE’s
higher  price  was  not  attributable  to  the  effects  of  past
discrimination”  (Croson,  488  U.S.  at 508),  the  DBE  program
does  not  impose  an  absolute  preference  for  persons  based
solely  on  their  race  or  color.  Unlike  the  Richmond  scheme
under  which  “a  successful  black,  Hispanic,  or  Oriental
entrepreneur  from  anywhere  in  the  country  enjoy[ed]  an
absolute  preference  over  other citizens  based  solely  on  their
race,"  (id.  at  508),  the  DBE  program  is  race-neutral.
Although  it plainly  encourages  minority  participation  in the
DBE  program  by granting  minorities  the  rebuttable  presump-
tion  that  they are  socially  and  economically  disadvantaged,
Congress  has  carefully  and  adequately  explained,  as  this
Court  concluded  in Fullilove,  the  basis  for that  presumption
of  fact.

7"

-

CONCLUSION

For  the  foregoing  reasons,  the judgment  of the  court

of appeals  should  be  affirmed.

Respectfully  submitted,

NATIONAL  MINORITY  SUPPLIER

IN  SUPPORT  OF  RESPONDENTS

INTEREST  OF  THE  AMICUS  CURIAE
The  Minority  Business  Enterprise  Legal  Defense  and
Education  Fund,  Inc.  (“MBELDEF”)  is  a  nonprofit
corporation  founded  in 1980  by former  Maryland Con-
gressman  Parren  J.  Mitchell.  The  primary  purpose  of
MBELDEF  is  to  promote  legally  defensible  minority
business  opportunity  programs.  MBELDEF
is comprised
of  over  2,000  minority  businesses  nationwide,  many  of
which,  in their efforts  to become  successful  commercial

2
enterprises,  have  benefited  from  federal,  state,  and  local
minority  business  opportunity  programs.
Additionally,
MBEL  DEF  has  provided  numerous  state  and  local  govern-
ments  with  legal  guidance  in  the  adoption  of  such  pro-
grams  and  has  participated  in  significant  litigation  con-
cerning  these  programs.

The  National  Minority  Supplier  Development  Council,
Inc.  (“NMSDC")  is  a  nonprofit  corporation  founded  in
1969  that  seeks  to  provide  minority  business  with  access
to  purchasing  opportunities  in  the  public  and  private
sectors.  Currently,  NMSDC  has  46  regional  offices  and
a  national  office  that  coordinates  these  efforts  on  a  local
and  national  level.

The  National  Black  Chamber  of  Commerce,  Inc.
(“NBCC”)  is  a  nonprofit  corporation,  dedicated  to  the
economic  empowerment  of  African  American  communi-
ties  through  development  of  African  American  businesses,
particularly  manufacturing  and  construction.  NBCC  has
80 affiliated  chapters  throughout  the  nation.

The  National  Association  of  Minority  Contractors
(“NAMC”)  is  a  nonprofit  corporation  founded  in  1969.
During  its  25  years  of  service,  NAMC’s  goals  have  been
to  provide  education  and  training  to  minovity  contractors,
to  promote  the  economic  and  legal  interest  of  minority
construction  firms,  and  to  bring  equitable  procurement
and  business  opport»nities  to its membership.

These  Amici  Curiae  have  a  significant  interest  in  this
Court’s  review  of  the  substantial  constitutional
issues
presented  by  this  challenge  to  programs  that  benefit  mi-
nority  business  enterprises.’

INTRODUCTION  AND  SUMMARY  OF  ARGUMENT

This  case  raises  issues  of  great  social  and  jurispru-
dential  importance.  Petitioner  challenges  the  federal  gov-
ernment’s  power  to  encourage  the  awarding  of  federal
contracts  to  businesses  owned  by  socially  and  economi-

‘The  parties  have  consented  to  the  filing  of  this  brief.  Their

letters  of  consent  are on file  with  the Clerk  of  the Court.

3
cally  disadvantaged  persons.  Specifically,  petitioner  argues
that  the  equal  protection  component  of  the  Fifth  Amend-
ment  invalidates  longstanding  federal  efforts  to  assist  dis-
advantaged  persons  because  these  programs  incorporate
rebuttable  evidentiary  presumptions  that  members  of  par-
ticular  racial  and  ethnic  groups  suffer  such  disadvantage.
Even  such  limited,  remedial  consideration  of  race,  peti-
tioner  urges,  is  generally  forbidden  to  the  federal  gov-
ernment.

These  important  issues  should  not  be  addressed  in  this
case.
First,  and  quite  remarkably,  petitioner  has  never
identified  which  set  of  federal  statutes  and  regulations  it
is  attacking.  At  least  three  different  sets  of  federal  laws
grant  contracting  preferences  to  businesses  owned  by
socially  and  economically  disadvantaged  persons  and  are
incorporated  into  the  contract  clause  at  issue  here.  The
record  does  not  reveal  which  program  is  at  issue,  and
any one  of the three  might  have  led to the challenged
preference.  These  programs  differ  in  important  respects,
and  those  differences  may  make  a critical  difference  in
the  constitutional  analysis.  Furthermore,  because  of  this
confusion,  the Court  would  either  have to affirm  or in-
validate  all  the  programs,  whether  or  not  they  are  placed
at  issue  by  the  facts,  thus  rendering  what  may  well  be
an  advisory  opinion.

Second,  the  record  does  not  indicate  whether  a  pre-
sumption  based  on  race  is  even  at  issue.  Businesses  can
qualify
as disadvantaged  without  any reliance
on a racial
presumption.  The record  does  not establish  whether  the
subcontractor  at  issue qualified  by virtue  of a  racial  pre-
sumption  or  by direct  proof  of  disadvantage.  Resolution
of the question  presented
might  well result
in an advisory
opinion  for this  reason  as  well.

If  this  Court  reaches  the merits,  the decision  below
should  be  affirmed.  Strict  equal  protection  scrutiny  should
not  apply  here.  Congress  has  substantial  leeway—more
leeway  than  is  afforded  the  States—to  make  limited  use
of  racial  considerations  to  remedy  disadvantages  result-

4

ing  from  race  discrimination.
The  federal  programs
at  issue  do  not  grant  preferences  on  the  basis  of  race.
They  grant  unobjectionable  preferences  on  the  basis  of
social  and  economic  disadvantage:  both  minority  and
non-minority  individuals  may  qualify  for  statutory  prefer-
ences.  Each  program  uses  race  as  a  basis  for  a  rebuttable
presumption  of  disadvantage,  but  where  that  presumption
is  questioned,  each  Program  also  places  minority  appli-
cants  for  disadvantaged  status  on  equal  footing  with  non-
minority  applicants  by  requiring  the  same  proof  of  eco-
nomic  disadvantage  from  each.  Moreover,  the  contract
Clause  at  issue  does  not  obligate  private  contractors  to
employ  socially  and  economically  disadvantaged  subcon-
tractors.
It  merely  provides  compensation  to  those  who
choose  to  do  so.
Thus,  racial  considerations  play  a
carefully  confined  role  in  the  overall  scheme,  and  pose
none  of  the  risks  that  have  required  the  application  of
Strict  scrutiny  in other  contexts.

and  the  record  before  Congress  provides  substantial  evi-
dence  from  which  Congress  could  reasonably  conclude

U.S.  469  (1989),  confirm  the  continuing  pattern  of  dis-
crimination  at  the  state  and  local  levels  in  all  regions  of
the  country—and  thus  reinforce  the  continuing  need  for
contracting  preferences  for  the  socially  and  economically
disadvantaged.

5

ARGUMENT
I.  THE  RECORD  IN  THIS  CASE  DOES  NOT  PRO-
VIDE  AN  ADEQUATE  BASIS  FOR  THIS  COURT'S
REVIEW.

Petitioner  purports  to challenge  the  use  of racial  prefer-
ences  in  the  “Federal  Construction  Procurement  Pro-
gram.”  Petitioner's  Brief  (“Br.”)  at  5.  No  such  “pro-
gram”  exists.  The  specific  contract  provision  at  issue  in
this  case  references  at  least  three  different  federal  regula-
tory  schemes  that  provide  preferences  for  subcontractors
under  three  different  sets  of  criteria  that  measure  social
and  economic  disadvantage.  Petitioner  never  specifically
identifies  which  regulatory  scheme  it is attacking,  and  the
record  does not establish  which one applied  here.  Indeed,
the record does not clearly establish  that a  racial prefer-
ence  is  even  at  issue.  Subcontractors  may  qualify  for  a
Statutory  preference  without  any  reliance  on a racial  pre-
sumption,  and the record  does  not establish  whether  the
subcontractor  in this  case  did  so.

A. The Record  Does  Not Establish  Which  Of At  Least
Three  Different  Federal  Regulatory  Programs  Is
At  Issue.

The contract  between the federal  government  and the

prime contractor  at  issue contains  a standard  provision—
the  Subcontracting  Compensation  Clause  (“SCC”  )—pro-
viding  monetary  compensation  if  the  prime  contractor
awards  subcontracts
in excess  of 10%  of the prime con-
tract  amount  to  “small  business  concerns  owned  and
controlled  by  socially  and  economically  disadvantaged
individuals.”
(J.A.  24-25).  Under  the  SCC,  a  sub-

? DOT’s  Federal  Highway  Administration  (“FHWA”)  requires
the use of  the SCC  in contracts  for  Construction  of  Roads  and
Bridges  on  Federal  Highway  Projects.  See  Defendants’  Brief  in
Support  of  their  Motion  for  Summary  Judgment,  Exhibit  1,  at
I-1  and  I-24.
The  Central  Federal  Lands  Highway  Division
(“CFLHD”),  a  regional  office  of  FHWA,  contracted  with  Mountain
Gravel  and  Construction  Company  (“Mountain  Gravel”)  for  the
federally  funded  construction  of  a  stretch  of  highway  in  Colorado.

as  defined  under  the  FAR  Contract  Clause  52.219-8  entitled

Gonzales  Construction  Company  (“Gonzales”)  received
a  subcontract  from  Mountain  Gravel  for  which  Adsrand
Constructors  (“Adarand”)  was  the  low  bidder  because
Gonzales  had  SCC  DBE  status.  But  nothing  in the  record
establishes  how  Gonzales  qualified.  In  particular,  it does
not  show  whether  Gonzales  provided  proof  of certification
by the  SBA, a city,  a  county,  a  municipal  authority,  the
State  of  Colorado,  or  some  other  state,  or  whether  he
qualified  through  participation  in the  general  Section  8(d)

* Petitioner  acknowledges

that  CFLHD  at  least  accepts  certifi-
cation  of  disadvantaged  status  based  on  some  of  the  differing
standards  of  these  several  regulatory  schemes.  See  Br.  at  8  n.7.
In  fact,  CFLHD  has  a  statutory  and  contractual  obligation  to
comply  with  Section  8(d),  and  it  has  a  contractual  obligation  to
allow  certification  under  category  (4)  above.  An  additional  difi-
culty  exists,  however,  as  to  the  applicability  of  Section  8(d).
Although  the  contract  permits  and  the  statute  requires  its  applica-
bility,  the  record  contains  conflicting  indications  as  to  whether
CFLHD  in  fact  accepts  self-certification  under  the  SCC.  It  is  our
understanding  that  a  contractor  may  qualify  by  way  of  8(d),  but
the  issue  is not  entirely  clear  in this  case.

8

Subcontracting  Program.  Thus,  it  is  unclear  which—if
any—of  the  three  federal  preference  programs  incorpo-
rated  into  the  SCC  is  at  issue  here.  Indeed,  petitioner
cites  indiscriminately  to  conflicting  regulations  from  differ-
ent  schemes.*

The  federal  preference  programs  are  similar  in  im-
portant  respects.
In  each,  a  small  business  must  be  at
least  51%  owned  and  controlled  by  socially  and  eco-
nomically  disadvantaged  individuals.’  The  definitions  of
social  and  economic  disadvantage  are  also  similar:

Socially  disadvantaged  individuals  are  those  who
have  been  subjected  to  racial  or  ethnic  prejudice  or
cultural  bias  because  of  their  identity  as  a  member
of  a  group  without  regard  to  their  individual
qualities.
[JEconomically  disadvantaged  individuals  are  those
socially  disadvantaged  individuals  whose  ability  to
compete  in  the  free  enterprise  system  has  been  im-
paired  due  to  diminished  capital  and  credit  oppor-
tunities  as  compared  to  others  in  the  same  business
area  who  are  not  socially  disadvantaged.*

The  three  federal  programs  also  afford  members  of
particular  racial  and  ethnic  groups  some  presumption  of

(citing  FAR  regulations  correctly  and  Section  8(a)  regulations

disadvantage.®  Alternatively,  disadvantaged  status  may  be
granted  “on  a  case-by-case  basis,  [to]  individuals  who  are
not  members  of  [a  named  minority  group],”  49  C.F.R.
§ 23.62,  such  as  disabled  Vietnam  veterans,  Appalachian
white  males,  Hasidic  Jews,  or  any  other  individuals  who
demonstrate  that  they  are  socially  and  economically  dis-
advantaged.  49  C.F.R.  Pt.  23,  Subpt.  D.,  App.  A  (analy-
sis  of  § 23.62).  In  each  federal  program,  applicants
whose  presumptive  status  is  questioned"  must  offer  the
same  proof  of  economic  disadvantage  as  applicants  who
are  not  members  of  named  minority  groups.
See,  e.g.,
49  C.F.R.  § 23.69;  FAR  § 19.703(a)(2).  In  the  ulti-
mate  determination  of  economic  disadvantage,  therefore,
each  of  the  three  programs  treats  minority  and  non-
minority  applicants  identically.  See  13  C.F.R.  § 124.106
(a);  13  C.F.R.  § 124.106(b);  49  C.F.R.  Pt.  23,  Subpt.
D, App.  C.

These  federal  programs  differ,  however,  in  three  ways
that  may  have a critical  bearing  on  this  Court’s  constitu-
tional  analysis:  (1)  the  basis  for  individual  identification
as  a  member  of  a  named  minority  group;  (2)  the  scope
of  the  presumption  of  disadvantaged  status  based  on
named  minority  group  membership;  and  (3)  the  proce-
dures  available  for  challenging  presumptions  of  disadvan-
taged  status.

of  eligibility.
See  13  C.F.R.  § 124.311(g).  SBA
will  conduct  a  review  of  eligibility  “upon  receipt  of
specific  and  credible  information”  that  a  business  no
longer  meets  the  requirements  for  economically  dis-
advantaged  status.  13 C.F.R.  § 124.111(c).™

These  differences  in  the  three  programs  may  be  critical
to  this  Court’s  constitutional  analysis.  A  program  that
allows a self-certification  of disadvantaged  status  poses  dif-
ferent  questions  than  a  program  that  requires  government
certification.  A  presumption  of  both  social  and  economic
disadvantage  may  be  more  overinclusive  than  is  a  pro-
gram  that  even  initially  requires  proof  of  economic  dis-
advantage.  A  program  that  allows  any  third  party  to
In  determining  economic  disadvantage,  SBA  makes  a  detailed
assessment  of  factors  in  three  categories:  “The  personal  financial!
condition  of  the  individual(s)  claiming  disadvantaged  status,  in-
cluding  that  individual’s  access  to  credit  and  capital;  the  financial
condition  of  the  applicant  concern;  and  the  applicant  concern’s
access  to  credit,  capital  and  markets.”  13  C.F.R.  § 124.106(a)  (2).
Section  8(a)  is  further  narrowed  by  a  number  of  unique  re-
strictions,  ¢.g.,  a  small  business  concern’s  tenure  in  the  Section
8(a)  Program  is  subject  to  annual  eligibility  review  and  a  fixed
term  of  years.  See  18  C.F.R.  $$ 124.110,  124.111.
Section  8(a)
eligibility  tenure  is not open-ended.

‘12
challenge  an  individual's  presumptive  status  may  be  more
narrowly  drawn  than  one  that  allows  only  the  government
to  do  so.  The  applicable  constitutional  analysis  requires
careful  consideration  of  the  way  race  is  used,  as  well  as
whether  the  government's  objectives  could  be  met  with
more  narrowly  drawn  regulations.  Proper  consideration
of  the  questions  presented  thus  requires  careful  attention
to  the  precise  structure  of  the  challenged  government  pro-
gram.  See  infra  Point  II.B.2.

B.  The  Deficiencies  In  The  Record  Preclude  Review  Of

The  Questions  Presented.

Petitioner  has  not  asked  this  Court  to  strike  down  a
particular  federal  statute  or  regulatory  scheme;  it  has
asked  the  Court  to  invalidate  the  concept  of  racial  prefer-
ences  in  contracting.  Nothing  in  the  record,  including  the
opinion  of  the  Court  of  Appeals,  makes  clear  which  of
the  different  regulatory  schemes  discussed  above  is at  issue
in  this  case.  The  pleadings  and  the  lower  court  decisions
indiscriminately  refer  to  the  Federal  Acquisition  Regula-
tions,  regulations  implementing  STURAA  and  regulations
implementing  the  Small  Business  Act,  as  if they  contained
identical  regulatory  schemes.

Having  failed  to  identify  the  federal  program  specifi-
cally  at  issue,  petitioner  is effectively  asking  this  Court  to
answer  an  abstract  question  about  whether  race  may  play
a  role  in  subcontracting  decisions  involving  federal  con-
tract  awards.
But  such  a  question  should  not—indeed
cannot—be  answered  in  the  abstract.  As  demonstrated,
differences  in  the  various  programs  may  be  important  to
the  Court’s  constitutional  analysis,  particularly  with  re-
spect  to  the  “narrow  tailoring”  inquiry.  More  funda-
mentally,  because  any  one  of  the  programs  might  be  at
issue,  this  Court  can  definitely  resolve  the  case  only  by
affirming  or  invalidating  all  the  programs.  That  would
necessarily  result  in  an  advisory  opinion  with  respect  to
the  programs  that  Gonzales  did  not  use  to  qualify  for
the  subcontract  in  issue.  Even  more  fundamentally,  the
record  does  not  indicate  whether  Gonzales  qualified  for
disadvantaged  status  by  virtue  of  a  presumption,  or

13
whether  he  proved  disadvantage  without  benefit  of  a  pre-
sumption.”  Thus,  it is unclear  whether  a  racial  presump-
tion  is even  at  issue.

This  record  plainly  lacks  the  “reasonable  clarity  and
definiteness”  required  for  adjudication  of  a  constitutional
question  of  this  importance.  Rescue  Army  vy.  Municipal
Court  of Los  Angeles,  331  U.S.  549,  568,  576  (1947);
see  also  California  Bankers  Ass'n  v.  Shultz,  416  U.S.  21,
56  (1974).  At  the  most  basic  level,  there  may  be  no  case
or  controversy  respecting  the  use  of  race.  The  record
contains  no  finding  or  even  allegation  that  Gonzales  re-
ceived  the  benefit  of  a  race-based  presumption  in  qualify-
ing for  the  award  at  issue.  Absent  proof  that  a  racial  pre-
sumption  is  at  issue,  a  ruling  in  this  case  would  be  ad-
visory  in  nature.  See  Flast  v.  Cohen,  392  US.  83,  96
(1968).  See  also  Poe  v.  Ullman,  367  USS.  497,  501
(1961).  Even  if  jurisdiction  were  technically  present,
review  would  be  inappropriate  because  the  record  does
not  indicate  which  federal  program  is  et  issue,  and  this
Court  therefore  lacks  a  concrete  context  for  deciding  the
questions  presented.  Such  prematurity  and  abstractness
present  “  ‘insuperable  obstacles’  to  the  exercise  of  this}
Court’s  jurisdiction,  even  though  that  jurisdiction  is tech
nically  present.”  Socialist  Labor  Party  v.  Gilligan,  406
U.S.  583,  588  (1972)  (quoting  Rescue  Army,  331  U.S.
at  574).  This  record  simply  does  not  “adequately  pre-
sent”  the  issues  raised  by  petitioner.  McClanahan  v.
Morauer  &  Hartzell,  Inc.,  404  U.S.  16,  16  (1971  ).

_--

For  these  reasons,  the  writ  of  certiorari  should  be  dis-
missed  as  improvidently  granted.  See  Commonwealth  of
'’®  Our  review  of  the  record  indicates  that  the  entire  factual  basis
of petitioner’s  claim  that  a  racial  presumption  operated  is  contained
in  paragraphs  10  and  11  of  the  Declaration  of  Starr  Profit,  Moun-
tain  Gravel’s  Chief  Estimator.  See  Plaintiff’s  Motion  for  Summary
Judgment,  Exhibit  E, ©" 10 and  11,  cited  in,  Memorandum  of  Points
and  Authorities  in  Opposition  to  Defendants’  Motion  for  Summary
Judgment  at  10.  Those  paragraphs  contain  a  general  assertion
that  Gonzales  was  a  DBE.  They  do  not  identify  the  program  under
which  he  qualified.  Nor  do  they  indicate  whether  he  qualified  for
that  program  by  use  of  a  race-based  presumption  as  opposed  to  by
presenting  direct  proof  of  disadvantage.

14
Massachusetts  v.  Painten,  389  US.  560,  561  (1968):
Williams  v.  Zuckert,  371  U.S.  531,  533  (1963).
SUBCONTRACTING

II.  THE

COMPENSATION
CLAUSE  AND  THE  REBUTTABLE  PRESUMP-
TIONS  COMPORT  WITH  THE  REQUIREMENTS
OF  THE  EQUAL  PROTECTION  COMPONENT  OF
THE  FIFTH  AMENDMENT.
A.  This  Court  Should  Apply  Intermediate  Scrutiny
In  Reviewing  The  Subcontracting  Compensation
Clause.

If  this  Court  reaches  the  merits,  it  should  decline  peti-
tioner’s  invitation  to  apply  strict  scrutiny  to  the  challenged
programs.  For  three  reasons,  the  analysis  set  forth  in
Croson,  488  U.S.  469,  has  no  application  here.

First,  the  programs  challenged  here  differ  in  critical
respects  from  programs  of  the  kind  considered  in  Croson.
In  Croson,  the  Court  held  that  a  local  minority  set-aside
program  should  be  subjected  to  strict  scrutiny  because
it employed  a  “rigid  rule  erecting  race  as  the  sole  criterion
in  an  aspect  of  public  decisionmaking.”  488  U.S.  at  493
(emphasis  added).  Cf.  Shaw  y.  Reno,  113  S.  Ct.  2816
(1993).  The  federal  programs  incorporated  into  the  SCC
do  not  set  aside  government  contracting  opportunities  for
a  group  defined  by  race  or  ethnicity.  Eligibility  depends
on  social  and  economic  disadvantage.  The  programs  ex-
clude  individuals  in racial  and  ethnic  minority  groups  who
cannot  meet  the  criteria  for  disadvantage,  and  include
individuals  not  in such  groups  if they can  meet  the  criteria.
To  this  extent,  the  programs  at  issue  do  not  even  raise  a
substantial  constitutional  issue.
As  Justice  Scalia  ex-
plained  in his separate  opinion  in Croson,

nothing  prevents  [government]  from  according  a  con-
tracting  preference  to  identified  victims  of discrimina-
tion.  While  most  of  the  beneficiaries  may  be  black,
neither  the  beneficiaries  nor  those  disadvantaged  by
the  preference  would  be  identified  on  the  basis  of
their  race.

488  U.S.  at  526.  See  also  Fullilove,  488  U.S.  at  535
(Stevens,  J., concurring).

15
To  be  sure,  race  is  relevant  in  these  federal  programs.
Although  applicants  who  are  not  members  of  minority
groups  can  qualify  for  contracting  preferences  without
the  presumption,  an  applicant’s  race  may  trigger  a  pre-
sumption  of  social  and/or  economic  disadvantage.
See
supra  Point  L.A.  Those  presumptions,  however,  are  re-
buttable.  Indeed,  proof  required  for  an  ultimate  determi-
nation  of  economic  disadvantage  is  identical  for  both
minority  and  non-minority  applicants:  where  a  minority
applicant’s  presumptive  status  is  questioned,  he  or  she
will  be  required  to  make  the  same  showing  of  economic
disadvantage  as  will  a  non-minority  applicant.  See  supra
Point  I1.A.  Furthermore,  the  SCC  does  not  reyuire  prime
contractors  to  engage  disadvantaged  subcontractors,  but
merely  provides  additional  compensation  to  those  prime
contractors  who  choose  to do so.

Nothing  in  this  Court’s  equal  protection  decisions  re-
quires  the  application  of  strict  scrutiny  to  this  limited
use  of  race  as  a  rebuttable  presumption  of  disadvantage.
In  this  critical  respect,  the  federal  programs  under  chal-
lenge  here  differ  from  those  at  issue  in  Croson,  Fullilove
and  Metro  Broadcasting—all  of  which  granted  preferences
exclusively  on  the  basis  of  race  or  ethnicity.
Fullilove,
448  U.S.  at  453-54;  Croson,  488  U.S.  at  713:  Metro
Broadcasting  Inc.  v.  FCC,  497  U.S.  547,  557-58  (1990).
Second,  where  Congress  has  authorized  the  use  of  a
race-conscious  classification
in  a  program  designed  to
remedy  the  effects  of  racial  and  ethnic  discrimination,  this
Court  has  applied  intermediate  scrutiny  to  assess  the  con-
stitutional  validity  of  agency  rules  implementing  the  de-
tails  of  that  program.  See  Metro  Broadcasting,  497  U.S.
at  463,  565,  566."*  Indeed,  in  Metro  Broadcasting,  this
Court  cited  Croson  as  reaffirming  “the  lesson  of  Fullilove
that  race-conscious  classifications  adopted  by  Congress
to  address  racial  and  ethnic  discrimination  are  subject  to

*®  Congressional  power  to  authorize  such  programs  is  rooted  in
the  enforcement  power  of  Section  5  of  the  Fourteenth  Amendment
and  in  Congress’  Spending  and  Commerce  Clause  Powers.
See
Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at 473-75.

16
a  different  standard  than  such  classifications  prescribed
by  state  and  local  governments.”  Jd.  at  565.

the  President

Congress  has  instructed

to  adopt  a
government-wide  goal  for  participation  of  socially  and
economically  disadvantaged  businesses  in  government  con-
tracting  of  not  less  than  5%  of  the  total  contracting  for
each  fiscal  year,  and  has  instructed  each  federal  agency
to  adopt  goals  to  give  those  businesses  the  “maximum
practicable  opportunity”  to  participate  in  contracts  and
subcontracts  let  by  the  agency.
15  U.S.C.  § 644(g)(1).
As  one  means  of  helping  it meet  this  statutory  obligation,
DOT’s  Federal  Highway  Administration  (“FHWA”)  spec-
ifies  the  use  of  the  SCC  in  contracts  for  Construction  of
Roads  and  Bridges  on  Federal  Highway  Projects.
See
Defendants’  Brief  in  Support  of their  Motion  for  Summary
Judgment,  Exhibit  i,  at  I-1  and  I-24.  Intermediate  scru-
tiny  applies  to  this  provision  because  “when  a  program
employing  a  benign  racial  classification  is  adopted  by  an
administrative  agency  at  the  explicit  direction  of Congress,
[it  is]  “bound  to  approach  [its]  task  with  appropriate  def-
erence  to  the  Congress.’”  Metro  Broadcasting,  497  U.S.
at  563.

.

Third,  “Congress  has  considerable  latitude,  presenting
special  concerns  for  judicial  review,  when  it  exercises  its
‘unique  remedial  powers  .
.  under  § 5  of  the  Fourteenth
Amendment.’”  Metro  Broadcasting,  497  U.S.  at  605
(O'Connor,  J.,  dissenting).  That  power  clearly  operates
where  Congress  requires  the  States  to  take  measures  to
remedy  past  discrimination.
See  id.  (citing  Fullilove).
If Gonzales  obtained  SCC  DBE  eligibility  by virtue  of  its
status  as
a  STURAA  DBE/MBE,  then  it  is  a  State’s
action  in  certifying  Gonzales  pursuant  to  STURAA  that
is  at  issue.  Through  STURAA,  Congress  requires  States
to  follow  certain  procedures  in  determining  the  eligibility
of  small  businesses  for  preferences.  STURAA,  Pub.  L.
No.  100-17,  § 106(c)(4),  101  Stat.  132,  146.  This  case
is  therefore  indistinguishable  from  Fullilove  insofar  as  it
examines  a  presumption  contained  in  STURAA  and  ap-
plied  by  a  State.  Moreover,  Sections  8(a)  and  8(d)

17
ameliorate  the  effects  of  past  discrimination  by state  and
local  governments,  see  infra  note  22  and  accompanying
text,  thereby  resting  on  Congress’  Section  5 power  to  cure
state  and  local  violations  of  the  Equal  Protection  Clause.
Whether  Congress  chooses  a  federal  or  state  agency  as  the
instrument  for  administration  of  its  remedial  programs
should  not  be  dispositive  of  the  applicable  standard  of
scrutiny.  Congress’  Section  5 authority  under  the  Four-
teenth  Amendment  gives  Congress  unique  power  that  is
subject  only  to  intermediate  scrutiny.

B.  Federal  Contracting  Programs  For  Socially  And
Economically  Disadvantaged  Individuals  Satisfy
Any  Applicable  Standard  Of  Equal  Protection
Review.

Petitioner’s  constitutional  challenge  should  be  rejected
irrespective  of  what  standard  of  review  this  Court  applies.
First,  the  compelling  nature  of Congress’  purpose,  i.e.,  to
remedy  the  effects  of  a  pattern  of  discrimination  in  fed-
eral  contracting,  is  evident  from  the  legislative  history  of
the  Small  Business  Act  and  from  nationwide  disparity
Studies  conducted  in  the  wake  of  this  Court’s  decision  in
Croson.  Second,  Congress’  use  of  race  is  narrowly  tail-
ored  to  avoid  the  risk  of  stereotyping  because  it is neither
underinclusive  nor  overinclusive:  race  is  neither  a  neces-
sary  nor  a sufficient  condition  for  securing  the  preferred
status.  Moreover,  legislative  history  and  agency  data  es-
tablish  a  clear  nexus  between  the  chosen  policy  and  the
Congressional  objectives.

1.  Congress  had  ample  evidence  upon  which  to
base  its  findings  of discrimination  in government
contracting.

The  SCC  and  the  eligibility  provisions  incorporated
therein  are  “justified  as  a  remedy  that  serves  the  com-
pelling  governmental  interest  in  eradicating  the  continu-
ing  effects  of  past  discrimination  identified  by  Congress.”
Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at  496  (Powell,  J.,  concurring).  Be-
cause  Congress  has  the  authority  to  prohibit  discrimina-
tory  practices  and  “prescribe  remedies  to  eradicate  their

18
continuing  effects,”  id.  at  502,  the  issue  of  whether  Con-
gress’  asserted
interest  is  compelling  must  focus  on
whether  Congress  has  made  sufficient  findings  to  support
its  determination  of  discrimination.  Jd.  In  Fullilove,  this
Court  examined  the  adequacy  of  Congress’  findings  of
extensive  discrimination  against  minority  businesses  in
government  contracting  in  reviewing  the  mandatory  race-
based  preference  that  accompanied  federal  money  desig-
nated  for  use  in  local  public  works  projects.  Id.  at  453,
502.  In  that  examination,  the  Court  looked  to  the  legis-
lative  history  of  the  Small  Business  Act.  /d.  at  463-67,
502-06.
Applying  strict  scrutiny,  Justice  Powell  con-
cluded  that:

A  court  must  accept  as  established  the  conclusion
that  purposeful  discrimination  contributed  signifi-
cantly  to  the  small  percentage  of  federal  contracting
funds  that  minority  business  enterprises  have  re-
ceived.
.  Although  the  discriminatory  activities
were  not  identified  with  the  exactitude  expected  in
judicial  or  administrative  adjudication,  it  must  be
remembered  that  Congress  may  paint  with  a  much
broader  brush  than  may  this  Court.

..

Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at  506  (internal  quotation  omitted).
As  of  1977,  then,  this  Court  had  determined  that  Con-
gress  “had  abundant  historical  basis  from  which  it  could
conclude  that  traditional  procurement  practices,  when
applied  to  minority  businesses,  could  perpetuate  the  ef-
fects  of  prior  discrimination.”  Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at  478
(Burger,  C.J.).  This  conclusion  applies  with  even  greater
force  to  this  case  because  the  Small  Business  Act  is  di-
rectly  at  issue  here.

—*

Congress  continued  to  collect  evidence  of  discrimination
in  federal  contracting  against  disadvantaged  and  minority
business.

v.  J.  A.  Croson:  Impact  Response:  Hearing  before  the  Subcommit-

Testimony  was  presented  at  these  hearings  by  minority  business
owners  from  diverse  regions  of  the  country  in  various  categories
of  business  including  construction,  goods,  and  services.  Witness
testimony  uniformly  reflected  ongoing  marketplace  discrimination
and  heightened  barriers  and  denial  of  market  access  as  a  direct
result  of  the  dismantling  of  affirmative  action  remedies  in  the  wake
of  the  Croson  decision.

at  492.%  The  thousands  of  specific  anecdotes  from  mi-

23  In  the  rare  case  where  no  availability/utilization  disparity  was
discovered,  the  authors  attributed  their  findings  to  very  low  avail-

24

parity  studies  portray  a  serious  pattern  of  discrimination
across  all  regions  of  this  nation  and  across  all  categories
of  industry.  Manifestations  of  discrimination  have  typi-
cally  included  such  forms  as  denials  of  opportunities  to
bid,  unfair  denials  of  contract  awards,  double  standards
in  performance,  unequal  access  to  financing,  and  price
discrimination  by suppliers.

The  disparity  study  commissioned  by  the  City  of
Denver,  Colorado  is  particularly  comprehensive.
See
Denver  Study,  supra  note  22.  The  Denver  study  exam-
ined  all  facets  of  the  construction  industry  and  analyzes
disparities  for  each  minority  sought  to  be  protected  by
the  City’s  program.  The  study  focused  on  the  develop-
ment  and  compilation  of  potential  evidence  of  discrim-
ination  in  the  Denver  construction  and  professional  design
industries.
/d.  at  I-2  to  I-4.  Not  only  did  the  study  find
significant  statistical  disparities  on  projects  where  no  MBE
goals  applied,  it  also  found  that  minority  construction
firm  availability  was  suppressed,  given  the  number  of  mi-
norities  working  in  the  industry.  Jd.  at  VI-4,  VIII-2  and
App.  C.  The  study  concluded:

Overall,  there  is  strong  evidence

of  discrimi-
nation  against  MBEs  and  WBEs  in  the  construction
industry...  .

Evidence  of  discrimination  was  found  for  four
groups  within  the  minority  construction  and  profes-
sional  design  industries.  There  was  substantial  evi-
dence  of discrimination  against  Hispanics,  somewhat
less  evidence  against  Blacks  and  less  against  Asians
and  Native  American  Indians.  ...  Because  of  the
pervasiveness  of  racial  discrimination,  and  because
of the  evidence  of  discrimination  against  each  minor-
ity  group,  we  conclude  there  exists  race-based  dis-
crimination  against  all  four  minority  groups  in  both
construction  and  professional  design.

Id.  at VIII-8.

ability  of  minority  businesses,  also  caused  by  systemic  discrimina-
tion.  See,  e.g.,  St.  Petersburg  Study,  supra  note  22.

25
Similarly,  a  1993  study  in  Ashville,  North  Carolina
found  evidence  of  discrimination  in  contracting  and  pro-
curement  activities.
Ashville  Study,  supra  note  22,  at
xix.  During  the  eight  years  examined  in construction  con-
tracting,  white  male-owned  businesses  received  99.1%  of
the  $52,235,352.00  expended  on  construction  contracts  and
subcontracts.  Id.  at  xi.  Moreover,  the  study  concluded
that  utilization  of  African  American  firms  averaged  only
0.34%  of  construction  dollars  available,  though  African
American  firms  constituted  2.28%  of  available  construc-
tion  firms.  /d.

In  Hayward,  California,  a  1993  study  of  contracting
in that  city found  the  following  dollar  utilization  of MBEs
as  a  percentage  of  availability  for  prime  contracting:
African  Americans—2%;  Asian  Americans—7%:;  His-
panic  Americans—8%;  and  Women—18%.  Hayward
Study,  supra  note  22,  at  Tbl.  A,  1.  The  disparities  in
Phoenix,  Arizona  are  similar.  In  1987,  the  average  black-
owned  firm  received  $55,195  in  revenues  by contracting
with  the  Phoenix  city  government,  while  the  average  ma-
jority  firm  received  $181,230.  Phoenix  Study,  supra  note
22,  at  VII-2.  Census  data  from  1987  showed  that  minor-
ity businesses  represented  5.80%  of the  market.  However,
in  the  period  between  1987  and  1992,  minority-owned
businesses  received  only  1.91%  of  the  City’s  purchase
dollars,  while  majority-owned  businesses  received  96.25%.
Id.  at V-3.

A  study  in  New  Haven,  Connecticut  found  a  “long  en-
trenched  and  widespread  pattern  of  racial  and  gender  dis-
crimination  in  the  construction  industry  in  New  Haven.”
New  Haven  Study,  supra  note  22,  at  28.  In  1987,  blacks
owned  6%,  hispanics  0.5%,  and  women  3%  of  the  con-
struction  firms  in  New  Haven.  However,  based  on  Equal
Employment  Opportunities  Commission  Data  for  Con-
necticut  showing  percentages  of construction  employees  in
the  State,  in the  absence  of discriminatory  barriers,  blacks
should  own  10%,  hispanics  5%  and  women  6%  of  the
construction  firms  in the area.  /d.  at  19.

26

In  New  York  City,  a  study  calculating  disparity  ratios
for  each  minority  group  that  receives  contracting  prefer-
ences  found  that  M/WBEs  received  a  substantially  smaller
share  of  the  dollars  spent  by New  York  City  in  1989  and
1990  than  would  be  expected  given  the  availability  of
M/WBEs  in  the  geographic  market  from  which  the  City
procures  goods  and  services.  New  York  City  Study,  supra
note  22,  at  x.  For  example,  African  Americans  received
only  17  cents  of  every  dollar  they  should  have  received
based  on  their  availability  as  prime  contractors  and  only
68  cents  of  every  dollar  as  subcontractors.  Id.  The  study
also  found  that  discrimination  is  especially  “virulent”  in
the  construction  industry,  and  that  African  Americans  en-
counter  the  most  frequent  and  severe  discrimination.  /d.
at  xi.

This  significant  and  persuasive  evidence  of  a  national
pattern  or  practice  of  discrimination  in  government  con-
tracting  supports  both  the  adoption  and  the  continued  use
of the  SCC  and  the  federal  programs  incorporated  therein.
Congress  perceived  a  compelling  governmental  interest
and  enacted  legislation  to  remedy  the  systemic  disadvan-
tages  experienced  by  minority  business.
The  evidence
available  in  the  legislative  history  and  subsequent  empiri-
cal  studies  establishes  that  the  need  for  federal  action
remains  compelling.
See  generally  Turner  Broadcasting
System,  Inc.  v.  FCC,  114  S.  Ct.  at  2472  (acts  of  Con-
gress  must  be  upheld  if  justified  by  either  “evidence  on
which  Congress  relied,  or  .
.  additional  evidence”  validat-
ing Congress’  judgment).

.

2.  Congressional  authorization  of DOT’s  use  of  the
SCC  and  of  the  federal  programs  incorporated
therein  is necessary  to  accomplish  and  narrowly
tailored  to further  the  compelling  governmental
interest.

Narrow  tailoring  ensures  “that  the  means  chosen  ‘fit’
[the]  compelling  goal  so  closely  that  there  is  little  or  no
possibility  that  the  motive  for  the  classification  was  il-
legitimate  racial  prejudice  or  stereotype.”  Metro  Broad-
casting,  497  U.S.  at  617  (O’Connor,  J., dissenting)  (quot-

27
ing  Croson,  488  U.S.  at  493).  In  Metro  Broadcasting,
Justice  O’Connor  expressed  concern  that  the  direct  link
drawn  by  the  FCC  between  race  and  distinct  viewpoints
demonstrated  that  the  means  chosen  to  promote  broadcast
diversity  were  based  on  stereotyping.  Metro  Broadcasting,
497  U.S.  at  617.  In  this  case,  however,  race  is  neither  a
necessary  nor  a sufficient  condition  of  securing  the  pref-
/d.  The  SCC  and  the  federal  programs  do  not
erence.
equate  a  particular  race  with  disadvantage,  but  instead
acknowledge  that  individuals  of  any  race  may  experience
prejudice  or  bias  that  may  result  in  social  and  economic
disadvantage.
.  The  SCC  and  the  federal  programs’  preference  eligibil-
ity  provisions  are  narrowly  tailored.  Disadvantage  is  the
basis  for  classification:
race  does  not  serve  as  its  proxy.
See  Metro  Broadcasting,  497  U.S.  at  621  (O’Connor,  J.,
dissenting).  Both  minority  and  non-minority  individuals
may  establish  eligibility  for  the  preference.  Members  of
named  minority  groups  have  the  benefit  of  a  rebuttable
presumption  of  disadvantage,  but  if  that  status  is  ques-
tioned,  they  are  subject  to  the  same  requirements  of  proof
of  economic  disadvantage  as  are  non-named  minority
group  members.  See  supra  Point  I.A.  Non-disadvantaged
and  large  minority  businesses  are  excluded  from  this  pref-
erence,  and  small  disadvantaged  non-minority  businesses
can  receive  its benefits.”

Although  “the  Government  cannot  employ  race  classifi-
cations  that  unduly  burden  individuals  who  are  not  mem-
bers  of  the  favored  racial  and  ethnic  groups,”  Metro
Broadcasting,  497  U.S.  at  630  (O’Connor,  J., dissenting) ;
497  U.S.  at  597-98,  the  burden  suffered  by non-minorities
in  this  case  is limited  to  the  requirement  that  they  prove
their  disadvantaged  status  without  the  benefit  of  the  evi-
dentiary  presumption  to  that  effect.  No  specialized  market
has  been  created  and  reserved  for  minority-owned  busi-
nesses,  and  the  opportunity  to  compete  for  subcontracts

*4  Adarand  apparently  never  challenged  the  disadvantaged  status

of  Gonzales.

28

falling  within  the  SCC  does  not  “depend[]  entirely  upon
race  or  ethnicity.”  Jd.  at  630.%  Even  the  scope  of  the
market  that  has  been  targeted  for  the  benefit  of  disadvan-
taged  businesses  is fluid:  only  goals,  not  quotas  are  to  be
set  by DOT  for  participation  of disadvantaged  business  in
DOT  contracting  and  subcontracting;*  and  only  an  in-
centive  payment  based  on  voluntary  use  of  disadvantaged
businesses  by  prime  contractors  is  included  in  the  SCC.
See  497  U.S.  at  630-31.

The  Small  Business  Act  sets  a  nationwide  goal  for  dis-
advantaged  business  participation  in  federal  contracting
of  “no  less  than  5%.”  15  U.S.C.  § 644(g).  DOT  is  re-
quired  to  set  goals  for  itself  that  present  the  “maximum
practicable  opportunity”  for  disadvantaged  business  par-
ticipation  in  DOT  contracting.
/d.  The  figure  that  is
operational  in  this  case,  however,  is  the  10%  goal  in  the
SCC:  contractors  may  choose  to  use  disadvantaged  busi-
nesses  for  subcontracting  in  an  amount  of  at  least  10%
of  the  total  value  of  the  contract  in  order  to  make  them-
selves  eligible  for  additional  compensation.”  A  goal  of

251f  a  market  has  been  reserved  for  a  class,  it  is  a  class  of
disadvantaged  businesses.  A  classification  of  that  nature  only  need
survive  this  Court’s  rational  basis  scrutiny.

Even  that  market  has  not  been  reserved,  however.  It  is  not  true
that  all  dollars  spent  by  the  Federal  government  in  fulfillment
of  federal  contracting  goals  are  restricted  to  race-based  contractor
recipients.  Under  SBA’s  Section  8(a)  program,  the  Section  8(d)
program,  and  STURAA,  small  disadvantaged  businesses  are  per-
mitted  to  subcontract  or  joint  venture  with  nondisadvantaged
firms,  provided  the  disadvantaged  business  is  not  “fronting”  for
the  nondisadvantaged  firm.  See,  e.g.,  138  C.F.R.  §§ 124.314,  124.321.
Just  as  a  firm  such  as  Mountain  Gravel  awarded  a  subcontract  to
Gonzales,  firms  such  as  Gonzales  subcontract  to  firms  like  Adarand.
Disadvantaged  business  awards  are  not  a  closed  fund  in  which  non-
disadvantaged  businesses  do  not  participate.

26  See  15 U.S.C.  § 644(g).

27The  12-15%  goal  for  disadvantaged  business  participation
that  CFLHD  set  for  itself  to  help  DOT  meet  its  overall  goals  is
simply  not  operative  in  this  case.
It  has  no  effect  on  the  SCC:
DOT’s  FHWA  distributes  the  specifications  for  contracts  relating

29

10%  is reasonable  and  narrowly  drawn.  A  10%  set-aside
was  upheld  in  Fullilove  in  part  because  it  included  a
waiver  provision  that  prevented  rigid  application  of  the
set-aside  in areas  of the  country  where  minority  population
might  be  low.  448  U.S.  at  513-14.%  The  preference  in
this  case  is  even  more  narrowly  drawn  because  it  is  not
a  set-aside,  but  is,  instead,  a  voluntary  option  that  con-
tractors  may  take  or  leave  as  they  please.

Moreover,  the  nexus  between  the  chosen  policy  and
the  asserted  interest  is  demonstrated  here  by  the  direct
relationship  between  preference  programs  for  disadvan-
taged  businesses  and  the  level  of  utilization  of  those  busi-
nesses.  In addition  to the  legislative  history  and  the Croson
disparity  studies,  federal  agency  data  suggests  that  dis-
advantaged  business  participation  rates  would  have  re-
mained  inequitably  low  in  the  absence  of  an  8(a)  pro
gram.  Statistics  published  by  the  Department  of  Defense
show  that  dollars  awarded  to  8(a)  businesses  increased
from  $9  million  in  1969  to  nearly  $2.8  billion  in  1993.
Department  of  Defense,  Small  Disadvantaged  Business
(SDB)  Program  History.  From  1969  to  1993,  the  per-
centage  of  DOD  total  contracting  dollars  going  to  all
SDBs  has  increased  from  0%  to  5.3%.  Id.  These  statis-
tics  demonstrate  the  effectiveness  of  and  continuing  need
for  programs  ot  ameliorate  the  effects  of  past  discrim-
ination.  Without  them,  those  effects  will  continue  to  be
felt.”

to  highway  construction  and  CFLHD  simply  incorporates  that
specified  provision  in  its  contracts.

28  The  10%  figure  is  one  that  Congress  has  clearly  considered
appropriate  as  a  goal  for  disadvantaged  business  participation  in
DOT  projects.  In  STURAA,  Congress  legislated  that  figure  as  the
goal  recipients  of  DOT  highway  funds  should  meet.  STURAA,
Pub.  L.  No.  100-17,  § 106(c)  (1),  101  Stat.  132,  145.

2°  Indeed,  as  noted  in  at  least  one  disparity  study,  absent  such
programs,  disparity  will  remain:  “African  American  owned  MBEs
received  none  of  the  91  subcontracts  issued  by  Alameda  County
prime  contractors  who  had  received  awards  during  the  period  when
the  M/WBE  program  was  suspended.”
Aiameda  Study,  supra
note  22,  at  ix-x.  Although  the  use  of  the  SCC  is  not  explicitly

30

Finally,  as  Justice  Powell  noted  in  Fullilove,  in  enact-
ing  the  preference  at  issue  there  and  in  the  Small  Busi-
ness  Act,  Congress

knew  that  other  remedies  had  failed  to  ameliorate  the
effects  of  racial  discrimination  in  the  construction
industry.  Although  the  problem  had  been  addressed
by  antidiscrimination  legislation,  executive  action  to
remedy  employment  discrimination  in  the  construc-
tion  industry,  and  federal  aid  to  minority  businesses,
the  fact  remained  that  minority  contractors  were
receiving  less  than  1%  of federal  contracts.

Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at  511  (citing  123  Cong.  Rec.  7156
(1977)  (remarks  of  Sen.  Brooke)).  For  these  reasons,
the  SCC  and  federal  eligibility  provisions  incorporated
therein  are  narrowly  drawn  so  as  to  survive  even  this
Court’s  strictest  scrutiny.

CONCLUSION

For  the  reasons  stated  herein,  this  Court  should  either
dismiss  the  writ  of  certiorari  as  improvidently  granted  or
affirm  the  judgment  of the  Court  of Appeals.

Respectfully  submitted,

limited  in  duration,  the  continuing  need  for  these  programs  justi-
fies  their  operation.  STURAA  is,  of  course,  merely  a  fixed  term
appropriations  statute,  and  the  funding  at  issue  in  this  case  ex-
pired  in  1991.  See  STURAA  Pub.  L.  No.  100-17,  § 106(a)  (8).
Section  8(a)  eligibility  is  limited  to  a  fixed  term  of  years.  See
supra  note  14.

IN THE

Supreme  Court  of the United  States

OCTOBER  TERM,  1994

ADARAND  CONSTRUCTORS,  INC.,

Petitioner,

FEDERICO  PENA,  et al,

Respondents.

On  Writ  of Certiorari  To  The  United  States
Court  of Appeals  for  the  Tenth  Circuit

BRIEF  AMICUS  CURIAE  OF  THE  NAACP  LEGAL
DEFENSE  AND  EDUCATIONAL  FUND,  INC.,
IN  SUPPORT  OF  RESPONDENTS

INTEREST  OF  AMICUS'

The  NAACP  Legal  Defense  and  Educational  Fund,
Inc.,  is a  non-profit  corporation  formed  to  assist  African-
Americans  to  secure  their  constitutional  and  civil  rights  by
The  Subcontracting  Compensation
means  of  litigation.
Clause  at  issue  in  this  case  was  adopted  in  part  to

'

Copies  of letters  from  the  parties  consenting  to  the  filing of

this  brief  have  been  filed  with  the  Clerk.

2

3

encourage  federal  contractors  to  subcontract  with  firms
owned  by African-Americans  who  have  been  the  victims  of
racial  discrimination.
Amicus  has  previously  filed  briefs
with  this  Court,  on  behalf  of  parties  or  amici,  in  a  wide
variety  of cases  involving  race-conscious  affirmative  action.
We  believe  our  views  on  the  circumstances  presented  by this
case  may  be  of assistance  to  the  Court.

SUMMARY  OF  ARGUMENT

The  petitioner  in  this  case  seeks  only  prospective
injunctive  relief  with  regard  to  certain  possible  future
applications  of  the  Subcontracting  Compensation  Clause.
In  order  to  establish  standing  to  obtain  such  injunctive
relief,  petitioner  must  demonstrate  that  it faces  reasonably
certain  and  immediate  injury  if the  injunction  is  denied.
Lujan  v.  Defenders  of Wildlife,  504  U.S.  __  (1992);  Los
Angeles  v.  Lyons,  461  U.S.  95 (1983);  O’Shea  v.  Littleton,  414
U.S.  488  (1974).  Petitioner  faces  no  such  imminent  injury.

(1)

Although  the  SCC  has been  in use  since  1979,
and  petitioner  has  been  in  business  since  1976,  petitioner
can  point  to  only  one  contract  which  it allegedly  ever  lost
because  of the  SCC.

(2)

The  SCC  is  utilized  only  in  the  relatively
in  which  FHWA  itself  directly
uncommon  instances
contracts  for  the  construction  of  a  highway.
In  1990  the
FHWA  let  in  Colorado  only  one  construction  contract
involving  guardrail  work,  the  particular  work  engaged  in by
petitioner.

(3)

Not  all  such  FHWA  direct  contracts  contain

an  SCC.

(4)  — In  some  instances  the  SCC  is of no  practical
importance  because  subcontracting  with  DBEs,  which  the

SCC  merely  encourages,  is  required  by  other  contract
provisions  not  challenged  in this  action.

(5)

Petitioner  can  identify  only  six  instances
bid  on  any

between
subcontracting  work  for  an  FHWA  direct  contract.

1976  and  1990  when  it  ever

(6)

Petitioner  does  not  challenge  all applications
of the  SCC.  It raises  no  constitutional  objection  to  the  use
of  the  SCC  where  the  owners  of  a  DBE  are  in  fact
disadvantaged,  or  where  a firm  qualified  as  a DBE  solely
because  it was  owned  and  managed  by women.  Petitioner
challenges  the  constitutionality  of  the  SCC  only  in  those
cases,  if any,  in  which  the  SCC  results  in  the  award  of a
subcontract  to  a  firm  whose  owners  and  managers  are  not
disadvantaged,  but  qualified  for  DBE  status  solely  because
of the rebuttable  presumption  that  minority  owner  operators
of small  firms  were  disadvantaged  by past  discrimination.
Whether  and  when  petitioner  will  be  injured  in  the  future
by any  such  application  of an  SCC  are  too  speculative  to
confer  the  requisite  standing.

ARGUMENT

I. INTRODUCTION

Petitioner  urges  this  Court  to  decide  in  this  case
fundamental  constitutional  issues  of far-reaching  importance
regarding  the  ability  of  the  Congress,  and  of  federal
executive  agencies,  to engage  in any  form  of race-conscious
action.  Petitioner’s  brief  sets  forth  an  elaborately  detailed
formulation  of constitutional  principles which  petitioner  asks
Noticeably  absent  from  both  the
this  Court  to  adopt.
petition  and  that  brief,  however,  is any  account  of  how  a
decision  by  this  Court  on  these  complex  issues  would
actually  affect  the  petitioner  itself.

4

5

In  the  proceedings  below,  the  respondents  directly
challenged  petitioner’s  standing  to litigate  the constitutional
questions  now  presented  to  this  Court.
The  issue  of
standing  must  be  addressed  before  the  Court  undertakes  to
consider  the  substantive  constitutional  questions  raised  by
the  briefs;  in the  absence  of standing,  this  appeal  would  not
present  the  "case  or controversy"  required  by Article  III, and
the  federal  courts  would  lack  subject  matter  jurisdiction.  It
is particularly  important  that  standing  problems  be carefully
assessed  before  resolving  such  substantive  constitutional
questions,  since  constitutional  issues  are  to  be  decided  on
the  merits  only where  clearly  necessary.

Since  the  initial  filing of the  complaint  in this  action,
both  the  litigation  in  the  courts  below  and  a  substantial
body  of discovery  have  sharpened,  clarified  and  ultimately

limited  the  scope  of  petitioner’s  claim.
posture,  petitioner’s  claim  has  been  narrowed  in  three
important  respects.

provision  of the  FAR  Contract  states:

"  "Woman-owned  small  business’  ...  means  small

by women  who  are  United  States  citizens  and  who
also  control  and  operate  the  business.”

Answer,  Exhibit  B, p.  F-16;  48 C.F.R.  52.219-13.  The  treatment  of
women-owned  and operated  firms  under  the  SCC  is thus  significantly
different  than  the  treatment  of minority  owned  and  operated  firms.
All  women-owned  small  business  are  classified  as  DBEs,  whereas
minority  owners  are  only  accorded  a  rebuttable  presumption  of
disadvantage.  J.App.  24.

Third,  the  relief  sought  by  petitioner  is  expressly
limited  to the  utilization  of SCCs  in future federal  contracts.
The  instant  litigation  was  apparently  triggered  by  the
rejection  in 1989  of petitioner’s  bid  to  supply  guardrails  for
a  highway  project  known  as  the  West  Dolores  Project.
Petitioner  alleged  that  the  guardrail  subcontract  was  given
instead  to  a DBE  which  had  submitted  a  bid  higher  than
that  of the petitioner.  Petitioner’s  1990  complaint,  however,
did  not  ask  that  petitioner  be  awarded  the  disputed
subcontract,  or  request  damages  or  any  other  form  of
monetary  relief  in  connection  with  the  West  Dolores
Project.
Perhaps  coincidentally,  the  complaint  did  not
allege  that  the  particular  DBE  which  won  the  guardrail
contract  for  the  West  Dolores  Project  had  qualified  as  a
DBE  because
of  the  disputed  minority-disadvantage
presumption,  or  even  that  that  DBE  was  minority-owned.

Because  the  complaint  seeks  solely  prospective
injunctive  relief,  petitioner  has  standing  only  if it  faces  a
real  and  reasonably  immediate  injury  if use  of the  disputed
minority-  disadvantage  presumption  is not  enjoined.  The
Although
record  makes  clear  that  that  is  not  the  case.

An  official  of Adarand  suggested  that  because  of the  SCC
the  firm  had  also  lost  a  subcontract  to  a DBE  in connection  with  a
highway  construction  project  at  McClure  Pass.  Deposition  of Steven
Goeglein,  p. 21.  Adarand’s  own  records  revealed,  however,  that  the
guardrail  subcontract  on  the  McClure  Pass  project  was  actually  won
by a non-DBE  firm  which  underbid  Adarand.  Plaintiff's  Answers  to
Defendant’s  Interrogatories,  Attachment  3,  Project  FH  15-1;  id.,
Attachment  1, Project  FH  15-1;  Defendants’  Responses  to Plaintiff's

injured  in the  past  by a challenged  practice.  Unless  such  a
plaintiff  also  faces  injury  in  the  proximate  future  from  a
repetition  of  that  practice,  he  or  she  would  lack  a
substantial  stake  in whether  continued  use  of the  practice  is
enjoined.  Absent  such  an  impending  injury,  issuance  of the
injunction  would  confer  no  palpable  benefit  on  the  plaintiff,
and  would  provide  no  redress  for  any  imminent  harm.

The  Court  has  on  repeated  occasions  invoked  this
aspect  of  standing  jurisprudence  to  deny  declaratory  or
prospective  injunctive  relief.  In Golden  v.  Zwickler,  394 U.S.
103  (1969),  the  plaintiff,  a former  member  of Congress,  had
been  convicted  under  a  New  York  law  forbidding  the
circulation  of anonymous  political  pamphlets;  he sued  for a
declaratory  judgment  that  the  statute  was  unconstitutional.
Although  the  plaintiff  alleged  that  he  intended  to  distribute
similar  leaflets  in subsequent  elections,  394  U.S.  at  106,  this
Court  concluded  that  the  plaintiff  lacked  standing  to  seek
such  relief  because  there  was  not  a  "real  [or]  immediate"
prospect  that  the  plaintiff  would  run  for  office  again  in the
near  future,  and  thus  it was  "wholly  conjectural  that  another
occasion  might  arise"  when  the  plaintiff  might  again  be
prosecuted.  394  U.S.  at  109.
