In  O'Shea  v.  Littleton,  414  U.S.  488  (1974),  the
to
plaintiffs  claimed
unreasonably  high  bail  and  unusually  harsh  sentences  as
part  of a  scheme  of intentional  racial  discrimination  on  the
part  of police  and  judicial  officials.  In O’Shea,  as  here,  the
plaintiffs  sought  no  damages  as a result  of past  actions,  but
requested  purely  prospective  relief.
In  holding  that  the
plaintiffs  lacked  standing  to  seek  such  relief,  the  Court
insisted  that  the  "threat  of  injury  must  be  both  ‘real  and
immediate’,  not  ‘conjectural’  or  ‘hypothetical’."  414  U.S.  at
494.
The  plaintiffs  had  "not  pointed  to  any  imminent
prosecutions  contemplated  against  any  of  their  number."

Although
acknowledging  that  Lyons  could  maintain  an  action  for
damages,  the  Court  dismissed  for lack  of standing  the  count
of Lyons’ complaint  that  sought  injunctive  relief.  The  Court

prospective  relief only if he could  demonstrate  the  existence
of  an  “immediate,”  461  U.S.  at  105,  107,  110-11,  and
"realistic  threat,"  461  U.S.  at  106  n.  7, that  he  would  again
be  subject  to  such  abuse  by  the  police.
The  Court
emphasized  that  no  further  such  incidents  had  occurred  in
the  five  months  following  the  only known  occasion  on  which
Lyons  had  been  assaulted.  461  U.S.  at  108.

This  Court  most  recently  applied  these  principles  in
Lujan  v.  Defenders  of Wildlife,  504  U.S.__,  119  L. Ed.  2d
351  (1992).  The  plaintiffs  in  that  case  sought  to  enjoin
certain  actions  by the  Secretary  of the  Interior  which  they
alleged  would  imperil  the  Nile  crocodile  in  Egypt  and  the
Asian  Elephant  in  Sri  Lanka.  The  plaintiff  organization
based  its claim  of standing  on  affidavits  from  two  members,
both  residents  of the  United  States,  who  stated  that  they

119  L.  Ed.  2d  at  3677  (Emphasis  in original).  The  Court
stressed  that  a  plaintiff's  burden  is particularly  heavy  if the
likelihood  of  injury  "depends  on  the  unfettered  choices

12

See  id.  at  367  n.  2:
"Although  imminence  is  concededly  a  somewhat  elastic
concept,  it cannot  be stretched  beyond  its purpose,  which  is to insure
that  the  alleged  injury  is not  too  speculative  for Article  III purposes  -
-  that  the  injury  is ‘certainly  impending  ....’  ...
It has  been  stretched
beyond  the  breaking  point  where,  as  here,  the  plaintiff  alleges  only
an  injury  at  some  indefinite  future  time,  and  the  acts  necessary  to
make  the  injury  happen  are  at  least  partly  within  the  plaintiff's  own
control.
In  such  circumstances  we  have  insisted  that  the  injury
proceed  with  a  high  degree  of  immediacy,  so  as  to  reduce  the
possibility  of deciding  a case  in which  no  injury would  have  occurred
at  all."  (Emphasis  in original).

made  by independent  actors  not  before  the  courts,"  119  L.
Ed.  2d  at  365,  or  turns  on  circumstances  “at  least  partly
within  the  plaintiff's  own  control."  119  L. Ed.  2d at  367  n.
2.  Both  of those  complicating  considerations  are  present  in
the  instant  case.

The  circumstances  of this  case,  as  set  out  below,  are
readily  distinguishable  from  those  in  Northeastern  Florida
Chapter  of AGC  v.  City  of Jacksonville,  124  L.  Ed.2d  586
(1993).  First,  the plaintiff in Northeastern  was  an  association
of  240  contractors  and  subcontractors;  counsel  for  the
plaintiffs  there  conceded  that  the  standing  question  would
have  been  very  different  if he had  represented  only a single
firm."*  Second,  the  plaintiffs  in Northeastern  alleged  that
they  "regularly  bid  on"’*  the  contracts  actually  affected  by
the  disputed  set-aside;  here  petitioner  had  bid  on  only one
such  subcontract  in  eleven  years.  Third,  the  ordinance  at
issue  in Northeastern  applied  to  all  purchases  made  by the
City  of Jacksonville;  here  the  SCC  is found  on  average  in
less  than  one  contract  per  year  per  state,  and  in  many  of
even  those  limited  instances  the  SCC  is for practical  reasons
devoid  of  operational  significance.
Fourth,  the  set-aside
provision  in  Northeastem  precluded  non-MBE  firms  from
even  bidding  on  many  contracts.
The  SCC  has  no  such
effect;  petitioner  in  fact  bid  for  the  subcontract  in  the
disputed  West  Dolores  Project."*  Petitioner’s  claim  is not

Finally,  this  case,  unlike  Northeastern,  is  largely
concerned  with  the  actions  of agencies  and  individuals  who
simply  are  not  before  the  Court.  See  Lujan  v.  Defenders  of
Wildlife,  119  L.  Ed.2d  at  365.  Petitioner  objects  not  to  the
SCC  incentive  for  subcontracting  with  DBEs,  but  to  the
manner  in which  the  list  of eligible  DBEs  is complied.  The
list  of  eligible  DBE’s,  however,  is  prepared  not  by  the.

addition,  what  impact  if any  an  SCC  may  have  on  petitioner
will  depend  on  the  independent  actions  of  future  general
contractors,  who  are  free  to  disregard  the  SCC  incentive
where  it exists,  and  to  prefer  a DBE  bidder  even  if no  SCC
is applicable.  See  Simon  v.  Eastern  Kentucky  Welfare  Rights
Organization,  426  U.S.  26  (1976).

(3)

Not  every  direct-bid  contract  let by FHWA
contains  an  SCC.  In some  instances,  pursuant  to section
8(a)  of the  Small  Business  Act,  a contract  is awarded  to
a  DBE  general  contractor.”  In  such  cases  the  general
contract  contains  no  subcontracting  compensation  clause,
and  the  general  contractor  has  no  financial  incentive  to
award  guardrail  or  any  other  subcontracts  toa  DBE.  As
a practical  matter,  most  DBEs  who  work  on  FHWA
direct  bid contracts  do so  as  general  contractors,  pursuant
to  section  8(a),  the  validity  of which  petitioner  does  not
here  challenge.”  On  the  one  occasion  in  which  it bid
for  the  guardrail  subcontract  work  for  a  section  8(a)
contract,  Adarand  won  the  subcontract.*'
The  SCC  is
also  omitted  from  certain  non-section  8(a)  contracts.”

In other  instances,  although  a contract  does
contain  an  SCC,  the  compensation  provided  by the  SCC
is  of  no  practical  significance  because  the  general
contractor  is required  by other  contact  terms  to  submit
and  adhere  to  a  specific  plan  for  subcontracting  with
DBEs.  Whenever  a  large  general  contractor  receives  a
direct-construction  contract  worth  in excess  of $1 million,
the  FHWA  requires  that  contractor  to  submit  an
"The

DBEs.®  In  the  case  of  the  West  Dolores  Project,  the
general  contractor  was  too  small  to  be  subject  to  this
“subcontracting  plan"  requirement;”  the  SCC  was  thus
of operational  significance.  But  a  larger  contractor  with
a  subcontracting  plan  would  be  under  an  independent
obligation  to  subcontract  to  DBEs  the  amount  of work
specified  in  its  subcontracting  plan,  and  thus  would  not
ordinarily  be  affected  by the  deletion  or  modification  of

Even  in  the  case  of  a  contractor  whose
contract  contains  an  SCC  but  no  subcontracting  plan,  the
SCC  at  times  will  have  no  actual  impact  on  the
contractor’s  behavior.  The  compensation  provided  by the
SCC  is  not  contingent  on  the  contractor  extending  any
special  bidding  preference  to  a DBE;  if lowest  bidder  for
a subcontract  of the  requisite  size  happens  to  be a  DBE,
the  contractor  receives  the  specified  additional  payment,
even  though  the  contractor  would  have  awarded  the
subcontract  to  the  low-bidding  DBE  regardless  of  the

possibility  that  petitioner  will  lose  one  contract  every
eleven  years  falls  far short  of imminent  injury.

IV.

TO  THE  MINORITY-DISADVANTAGE

.

Dismissal  of the  instant  action  for  lack  of standing
will  not,  of course,  leave  petitioner  without  a  remedy  if at
some  point  in  the  future  it  actually  faces  loss  of  a
subcontract  because  of the  disputed  minority-disadvantage
presumption.

arguendo,  that  that  circumstance  would  preclude  petitioner

general  manager.
would  be  very  different  if  petitioner,  having  itself  been
placed  on  the  DBE  list  by virtue  of the  gender-based  rule,
were  then  to  challenge  use  of  the  mere  presumption  of

It  is, of course,  impossible  to  foresee  the  extent  to
which  these  or  other  possibly  significant  developments  might
transpire  between  now  and  the  date,  if  any,  on  which
petitioner  actually  faces  loss  of a  subcontract  by reason  of
the  minority-disadvantage  presumption.  Even  if some  form

were  to  occur  in  the  interim.

Id.  at  10  ("Q.  What  portion  of  Adarand  do  you  own  now?
None.").

A.

“’

The  employment  of  a  man  as  general  manager  would  not
necessarily  preclude  classification  of  Adarand  as  a  woman-owned
business.  FAR  Contract  Clause  52.219-13  requires  only that  women
“exercis[e]  the  power  to  make  policy  decisions”  and  are  "actively
involved  in  the  day-to-day  management  of  the  business.”  Answer,
Exhibit  B,  p.  F-16.  On  the  other  hand,  a  firm  which  is in  reality
owned  and  operated  by a  non-disadvantaged  man  could  not  qualify
as  a DBE  merely  by conferring  purely  nominal  ownership  and  job
titles  on  women  or  disadvantaged  individuals.

SUPREME  COURT  OF  THE  UNITED  STATES

THE  UNITED  STATES  COURT  OF  APPEALS

BRIEF  AMICUS  CURIAE  OF  THE
LATIN  AMERICAN  MANAGEMENT  ASSOCIATION
IN  SUPPORT  OF  RESPONDENTS

INTEREST  OF  AMICUS  CURIAE

The

Latin

American

Management

Association
(““LAMA”’)  is  a  non-profit  organization  located  in Wash-
ington,  D.C.,  the  mission  of  which  is  to  advance  the
interests  of  Hispanic-owned  businesses  and  other  small,
minority-owned  firms  in  the  federal  and  commercial  con-
tracting  arenas.  Many  of  LAMA’s  members  participate

]

2

3

in  federal  contracting  programs  designed  to  promote  the
development  of  firms  owned  by  socially  and  econo-
mically  disadvantaged  individuals,  including  the  Small
Business  Administration’s  section  8(a) program.  The  con-
tinued  viability  of  many  disadvantaged  businesses  rests
on  the  technical,  managerial  and  contractual  assistance
made  available  through  these  programs.

The  letters  of  Petitioner  and  Respondents  consenting
to  the  filing  of  this  brief  amicus  curiae  have  been  filed
with  the  Clerk  of  the  Court  pursuant  to  Rule  37.3,
Rules  of the  Supreme  Court  of the  United  States.  Amicus
files  this  brief  on  the  merits  to  support  the  position  of
the  Respondents  to  this  action.

SUMMARY  OF  ARGUMENT

Acts  of  Congress  have  been  accorded  great  weight  by
this  Court  in  recognition  of  the  unique  powers  of  the
National  Legislature.  Because  Petitioner  challenges  racial
and  ethnic  classifications  created  by federal  statutes,  the
less  exacting  standard  of  scrutiny  described  in  Fullilove
v.  Klutzmck,  448  U.S.  448  (1980),  should  be  applied.
The  challenged  statutes  were  passed  by Congress  in  fur-
therance  of  important  governmental  objectives,  which
are  to  remedy  past  discrimination  and  foster  the  develop-
ment  of  businesses  owned  by socially  and  economically
disadvantaged  individuals  who  have  historically  been  the
victims  of discrimination.

The  Small  Business  Act’s  eligibility  criteria,  as  incorpor-
ated  in  the  STURAA  program,  are  narrowly  tailored  to
provide  business  development  opportunities  to  firms
owned  and  controlled  by  socially  and  economically  dis-
advantaged  individuals.
These  criteria  are  not  underin-
clusive  in  that  they  do  not  ipso  facto  exclude  any  indi-
vidual  or  group  from  eligibility.  Also,  these  criteria  are

not  overinclusive  because  they  do not  allow  participation
by  socially  disadvantaged  persons  who  have  experienced
relative  economic  success.

ARGUMENT

Petitioner  asks  the  Court  to  consider  the  constitution-
ality  of  an  Act  of Congress,  “the  gravest  and  most  deli-
cate  duty  that  this  Court  is called  on  to  perform.”  Fulli-
love  v.  Klutznick,  448  U.S.  448,  472  (1980)  (quoting
Blodgett  v.  Holden,  275  U.S.  142,  148  (1927)).  After  all,
it  is  Congress  that  is “charged  by the  Constitution  with
the  power  to  ‘provide  for  the  .
.  general  Welfare  of the
United  States’  and  ‘to  enforce,  by  appropriate  legisla-
tion,’  the  equal  protection  guarantees  of the  Fourteenth
Amendment.”  Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at  472.

.

Petitioner’s  challenge  arises  out  of the  Central  Federal
Lands  Highway  Division’s  Subcontracting  Compensation
Clause  program,  which  is funded  by section  106(a)(8)  of
the  Surface  Transportation  and  Uniform  Relocation
Assistance  Act  of  1987,  Pub.  L.  No.  100-17,  101  Stat.
132,  145  (1987)  (hereinafter  “STURAA”  or  “STURAA
program”).
Section  106(c)  of  STURAA  establishes,  as
a  general  rule,  that  not  less  than  ten  percent  of  the
STURAA  program’s  appropriated  funds  be  expended
with  small  businesses  “owned  and  controlled  by socially
and  economically  disadvantaged  individuals,”’  which  are
referred
to  as  “Disadvantaged  Business  Enterprises”
(“DBEs”).!  This  provision  is authorized  by section  502

1 Although  the  STURAA  program  makes  reference  to  “dis-
advantaged  business  enterprises,”  other  similar  programs  make
reference  to  “‘small  and  disadvantaged  businesses.’’
Both  terms,
however,  refer  to  small  businesses  that  are  owned  and  controlled
by individuals  who  are  “socially  and  economically  disadvantaged,”
as defined  in the Small  Business  Act.

4

5

of  the  Small  Business  Act,  15  U.S.C.  §644(g),  which
directs  feder=!  agencies  to  achieve  ai  least  five  percent
participati:n  by  small  and  disadvantaged  businesses  in
federal  contracting  programs.

Section  106(c)(2)(B)  of  STURAA  defines  “socially
and  economically  disadvantaged  inviduals”  by reference
to  the  Small  Business  Act,  15  U.S.C.  §637(d).  It  is the
“presumption”  contained  in  section  8(d)  of  the  Small
Business  Act  which  is at  the  very  core  of this  case:

Americans,
Asian

disadvantaged
Hispanic

The  contractor  shall  presume  that  socially  and
include
economically
Black
Native
Americans,
Pacific  Americans  and  other
minorities,  or  any  other  individual  found  to  be
disadvantaged  by  the  [Small  Business]  Adminis-
tration  pursuant  to  section  8(a)  of  the  Small  Busi-
ness  Act.

individuals
Americans,

15  U.S.C.  §637(d)(3)(C).?

However,  the  term  “socially  disadvantaged  individuals”

does  not  exclude  any  individuals  from  qualifying:

Socially  disadvantaged  individuals  are  those  who
have  been  subjected  to  racial  or  ethnic  prejudice
or  cultural  bias  because  of  their  identity  as  a mem-
ber  of  a  group  without  regard  to  their  individual
qualities.

15  U.S.C.  §637(a)(5).

The  term  “economically  disadvantaged  individuals”

is defined  as  follows:

Economically  disadvantaged  individuals  are  those
socially  disadvantaged  individuals  whose  ability  to

2 Section  8(d)  refers  to  “the  contractor”  because  it  was
intended  to  impose  requirements  on  prime  contractors  in  their
relationships  with  subcontractors  owned  by  socially  and  eco:  >-
mically  disadvantaged  individuals.  See  15  \).S.C.  §637(d).

compete  in  the  free  enterprise  system  has  been
impaired  due  to  diminished  capital  and  credit  oppor-
tunities  as  compared  to  others  in  the  same  business
area  who  are  not  socially  disadvantaged.

15 U.S.C.  §637(a)(6)(A).

Petitioner  contends  that  the  STURAA  program’s  auth-
orizing  statute,  section  502  of  the  Small  Business  Act,
as  well  as  the  “presumption”  incorporated  into  the
STURAA  program  by  reference,  create  unconstitutional
racial  and  ethnic  classifications.  The  precepts  on  which
this  position  rest  are  flawed.

I.
FULLILOVE  AND  METRO  BROADCASTING  APPLY  TO
THE  STATUTES  CHALLENGED  IN  THIS  CASE.

Petitioner  seeks  to  heighten  the  judicial  scrutiny  of
federal  programs  established  by Congress  in  which  racial
or  ethnic  criteria  are  employed  for  remedial  purposes.
Implicit  in  this  request  is that  this  Court  ignore  Fullilove,
which  established  the  applicable  standard  of review  under
such  circumstances,  in  favor  of  the  standard  in  City  of
Richmond  v.  J.A.  Croson  Co.,  488  U.S.  469  (1989).

The  Small  Business  Act’s  small  and  disadvantaged  busi-
ness  goal  and  the  section  8(d)  “presumption”  were
designed  by  Congress  to  eliminate  the  present  effects  of
past  discrimination  and  to  foster  the  development  of
businesses  owned  by  socially  and  economically  disad-
vantaged  individuals.  In such  an  effort:

Congress  has  necessary  latitude  to  try  new  tech-
niques  such  as  the  limited  use  of racial  and  ethnic
criteria  to  accomplish  remedial  objectives;  this  is
especially  so  in  programs  where  voluntary  coopera-
tion  with  remedial  measures  is  induced  by  placing
That  the  pro-
conditions  on  federal  expenditures.

6

gram  may  press  the  outer  limits  of  congressional
authority  affords  no  basis  for striking  it down.

Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at  490.

The  “great  weight”  accorded  to  congressional  action  is
in  recognition  that  “in  no  organ  of government,  state  or
federal,  does  there  repose  a more  comprehensive  remedial
power  than  in  the  Congress,  expressly  charged  by  the
Constitution  with  competence  and  authority  to  enforce
equal  protection  guarantees.”  Jd.  at  484.  Accordingly,
the  level  of  scrutiny  articulated  in  Fullilove  is  appro-
priate.

Federal  programs  designed  to  remedy  discrimination
are  properly  subject  to  a lesser  degree  of scrutiny  because
Congress  has  broad  remedial  powers  under  the Commerce
Clause,  Spending  Clause  and  Civil  War  Amendments.
Metro  Broadcasting,  Inc.  v.  FCC,  497  U.S.  547,  563
(1990)  (citing  Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at  473-78  (opinion  of
Burger,  C.J.));  see  also  Fullilove,  at  499-500,  508-09
(Powell,  J., concurring).  This  is in  contrast  to  state  and
local  programs  which  must  undergo  more  rigorous
scrutiny  under  Croson.  The  reason  for this  dichotomy  is
that  “the  struggle  for  racial  justice  has historically  been  a
struggle  by the  national  society  against  oppression  in the
individual  States.”
Croson,  488  U.S.  at  522  (Scalia, J.,
concurring).
Congress,  as  the  National  Legislature  with
the  institutional  competence  and  authority  to  enforce
equal  protection  guarantees,  should  therefore  be  subject
to  a  less  exacting  standard  of  review  than  state  and  local
governments.
Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at  490  (opinion  of
Burger,  C.J.);  id.  at  498  (Powell,  J., concurring); see also
Cleland  v.  National  College  of Business,  435  U.S.  213,
221  (1978)  (“the  District  Court’s  error  was  ..
its  fail-
ure  to  give  appropriate  deference  to  Congress’  judg-
memt  ....

)

.

7

Although  the  challenged  statutory  provisions  were
designed  to  correct  past  inequities,  as  discussed  below,
they  were  also  intended  to  foster  the  development  of
small  and  disadvantaged  businesses.
Therefore,  to  the
extent  that  the  provisions  in  question  are  benign  meas-
ures,  they  serve  an  important  governmental  objective  and
withstand  constitutional  scrutiny.
As  the  Court  stated
in Metro  Broadcasting:

[B]enign  race-conscious  measures  mandated  by
Congress  —  even  if those  measures  are  not  ‘reme-
dial’  in  the  sense  of  being  designed  to  compensate
victims  of  past  governmental  or  societal  discrimin-
ation  —
are  constitutionally  permissible  to  the
extent  that  they  serve  important  governmental
objectives  within  the  power  of  Congress  and  are
substantially  related  to  achievement  of those  objec-
tives.

497  U.S.  at  564.

In  sum,  the  Court  of  Appeals  for  the  Tenth  Circuit
properly  applied  the  standards  articulated  in  Fullilove
and  Metro  Broadcasting.  Accordingly,  the  statutes  chal-
lenged  in  this  case  are  subject  to a leas  exacting  level  of
scrutiny,  and  the  important  governmental  objectives  —
to  provide  members  of historically  victimized  groups  with
the  ability  to  develop  and  expand  their businesses  so  that
they  are  able  to  compete  in  the  mainstream  economy  —
withstand  constitutional  scrutiny.

Il.
THE  CHALLENGED  STATUTES  SERVE  IMPORTANT
GOVERNMENTAL  OBJECTIVES.

The  Small  Business  Act’s  small  and  disadvantaged  busi-
ness  goal  and  the  section  8(d)  “presumption”  serve
important  governmental  objectives  that  have  been  clearly
articulated  by  Congress.  As  such,  these  statutes  do  not

8

9

violate  the  equal  protection  component  of  the  Due
Process  Clause  of the  Fifth  Amendment.

In  1978,  Congress  passed  the  Small  Business  Invest-
ment  Act  of  1978,  Pub.  L.  No.  95-507  (“1978  Act”),
which,  among  other  things,  established  the  section  8(d)
subcontracting  program  and  codified  existing  regula-
tions  regarding  the  section  8(a)  program,  15  U.S.C.
§637(a).>
As  discussed  above,  the  purposes  of  these
programs  are  twofold.
First,  Congress  sought  to  rem-
edy  past  discrimination  against  minority-owned  firms
and  other  socially  and  economically  disadvantaged
individuals.
Second,  the  programs  were  intended  to
offer  socially  and  economically  disadvantaged  individ-
uals  the  opportunity  to  participate  fully in the  free  enter-
prise  system  through  contracting  opportunities  with  the
federal  government.  Congress  determined  that  by assist-
ing  these  individuals  in  obtaining  social  and  economic
equality  the  Nation  as  a  whole  would  also  benefit
through  an  improved  functioning  economy  and  more

5 Although  the  eligibility  requirements  for  the  §8(a)  pro-
gram  mirror  the  requirements  for  the  STURAA  program,  the  8(a)
program  operates  under  a  different  format.  To  gain entry  into  the
§8(a)  program,  an  individu»,  must  submit  a  detailed  application
to  the  Small  Business  Administration  (“SBA”)  which  is supported
by  evidence  that  the  individual  is “socially  and  economically  dis-
advantaged.”
If  the  SBA  determines  that  the  concern  is eligible
to  participate  in  the  program,  it  is  admitted  for  a  fixed  rogram
period.
After  the  expiration  of  that  period,  the  concern  ““grad-
uates”  from  the  program.
With  respect  to  contractual  assistance
under  the  program,  federal  procuring  agencies  are  authorized  to
set-aside  procurement  opportunities  for  8(a)  program  participants.
Depending  on  the  value  of the  prospective  contract,  award  is made
either  on a sole-source  basis,  or  through  competition  among  eligible
8(a)  firms.  The  ensuing  8(a)  contract  is “tripartite”  in nature,  with
the  SBA  serving  as  the  prime  contractor,  and  the  8(a)  firm serving
as  the  SBA’s  subcontractor.

competition  in  the  free  enterprise  system.  See  15  U.S.C.
§631(f)(1)(A)-(G).

The  legislative  history  of the  1978  Act  reveals  that,  in
addition  to  fostering  the  development  of  disadvantaged
businesses,  Congress  specifically  sought  to  redress  “past
and  present  discrimination.”  H.R.  Conf.  Rep. No.  1714,
95th  Cong.,  2d.  Sess.  21-22,  reprinted  in  1978  U.S.  Code
Cong.  &  Admin.  News  3879,  3881-82.  Congress  recog-
nized  that  “historic  past  discrimination”  had  hindered
the  efforts  of  many  groups  to  “participate  in  the  free
enterprise  system.”  S.  Rep.  No.  1070,  95th  Cong.,  2d
Sess.  15,  reprinted  in  1978  U.S.  Code  Cong.  &  Admin.
News  3835,  3850.  Indeed,  Congress  found  that, in many
cases,  business  owners  were  tarred  by their  identification
as  members  of minority  groups:

[M] any  persons  are  socially  disadvantaged  because
of their  identification  as  a member  of certain  groups
including,  but  not  limited  to,  Black  Americans,
Hispanic  Americans,  Native  Americans  and  other
minorities  that  have  suffered  the  effects  of discrim-
inatory  practices  or  similar  invidious  circurnstances
over  which  they have  no  control.

H.R.  Conf.  Rep.  No.  1714,  95th  Cong.,  2d.  Sess.  21-22,
reprinted  in  1978  U.S.  Code  Cong.  & Admin.  News  3879,
3881-82.*

As  co-sponsor  of  the  Senate  bill,  Senator  John  Glenn
made  clear  that  the  purpose  of  the  1978  Act  was  not
only  to  correct  the  inequalities  created  by past  discrimin-

*As  discussed  infra,  in  1978,  Congress  made  clear  that  the
effects  of  discrimination  extended  beyond  racial  and  ethnic  lines
and  included  “other  Americans”  who  may  have  been  subject  to
cultural  bias.
H.R.  Conf.  Rep.  No.  1714,  95th  Cong.,  2d.  Sess.
21-22,  reprinted  in  1978  U.S.  Code  Cong.  &  Admin.  News  3879,
3881-82.

10

ation,  but  also  to  promote  increasing  levels  of participa-
tion  by  disadvantaged  businesses  so  that  they  could
compete  in the  economic  mainstream:

[T]) here  is still  a  long  way  to  go to  move  minorities
into  the  Nation’s  economic  mainstream.  Minorities
only  own  4 percent  of  the  Nation’s  businesses  and
account  for  less  than  1  percent  of  total  business
receipts.  The  administration  has  pledged  to  triple
Federal  procurements  to  minorities  by  1979  and  I
strongly  support  that  objective.  I hope  that  we  will
see  committed  and  aggressive  action  by the  Federal
agencies  whose  responsibility  it is to  carry  out  that
pledge.

124  Cong.  Rec.  29,641  (1978).

Importantly,  Congress  passed  the  1978  Act  approxi-
mately  one  year  after  it passed  the  Public  Works  Employ-
ment  Act  of  1977  (“PWEA”),  Pub.  L.  No.  95-28,  91
Stat.  116,  codified  at  42  U.S.C.  §6701  et seg.  As noted
in  Fullilove,  the  Court  may  look  beyond  the  legislative
history  and  examine  the  total  contemporary  record  of
congressional  action  dealing  with  the  problems  of  dis-
crimination  against  socially  and  economically  disadvan-
taged  business  enterprises.
Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at  503
(Powell,  J.,  concurring).>
When  Congress  enacted  the
PWEA,  there  was  “abundant  evidence  from  which  it
could  conclude  that  minority  businesses  had  been  denied
effective  participation  in public  contracting  opportunities
by procurement  practices  that  perpetuated  the  effects  of
prior  discrimination.”
Jd.  at  477-78.  In  particular,  the

5 Similarly,  in  Metro  Broadcasting,  the  Court  noted  that,
where  “Congress  has  legislated  repeatedly  in  an  area  of  national
concern,  its  Members  gain  experience  that  may  reduce  the  need  for
fresh  hearings  or  prolonged  debate  when  Congress  again  considers
action  in  that  area.”
497  U.S.  at  572  (quoting  Fullilove,
448  U.S.  at  502-03  (Powell, J., concurring)).

11

Fullilove  Court  noted  that  the  following  evidence  was
available  to  Congress:

Congress  had  before  it, among  other  data,  evidence
of a  long  history  of marked  disparity  in the  percent-
age of public  contracts  awarded  to  minority  business
enterprises.  This  disparity  was  considered  to  result
not  from  any  lack  of capable  and qualified  minority
businesses,  but  from  the  existence  and  maintenance
of  barriers  to  competitive  access  which  had  their
roots  in  racial  and  ethnic  discrimination,  and  which
continue  today,  even  absent  any  intentional  discrim-
ination  or  any  other  unlawful  conduct.

Id.  at  478.

When  Congress  passed  the  1978  Act  the  following
year,  Congress  had  available  to  it  the  same  evidence  of
“historic  past  discrimination”  in  public  contracting.
S.  Rep.  No.  1070,  95th  Cong.,  2d  Sess.  15,  reprinted
in  1978  U.S.  Code  Cong.  &  Admin.  News  3855,  3850.
It  would  have  been  entirely  duplicative  for  Congress
to  convene  hearings  to  compile  the  same  evidence  of
“historic  past  discrimination”  in public  contracting.

Thus,  Congress  passed  the  1978  Act  and  established
the  8(d)  ‘‘presumption”  in  recognition  of its finding  that
certain  racial  and  ethnic  groups  had  historically  suffered
from  the  effects  of discrimination.  The  objectives  of the
statute  were  supported  by  substantial  evidence  of  past
discrimination  in  public  contracting.
At  that  time,
Congress  had  “abundant  historical  basis  from  which  it
could  conclude  that  traditional  procurement  practices,
when  applied  to  minority  businesses,  could  perpetuate
the  effects  of  prior  discrimination,”  Fullilove,  448  U.S.
at  478,  and  that  “many  persons  are  socially  disadvan-
taged  because  of  their  identification  as  a  member  of cer-

>———_—_

tain  groups  .
.  that  have  suffered  the  effects  of discrim-
inatory  practices  or  similar  invidious  circumstances  over
which  they  have  no  control.”©
H.R.  Conf.  Rep.  No.
1714,  95th  Cong.,  2d.  Sess.  21-22,  reprinted  in  1978
U.S.  Code  Cong.  &  Admin.  News  3879,  3881-82.  Con-
gress  acted  well  within  its  power  when  it authorized  the
section  8(d)  “presumption.”

Unfortunately,  over  the  course

of  the  following
decade,  many  of  the  legitimate  objectives  sought  to  be
achieved  by  the  1978  Act  were  not  reached.’  Small  and

’To illustrate,  on July 6, 1988,  hearings  were  held  before  the
Government  Activities  and  Transportation  Subcommittee  of  the
House  Committee  on  Government  Operations  to  review  the  Gen-
eral  Services  Administration’s  (“GSA”)  implementation  of Public
Law  95-507.  Specifically,  the  Subcommittee  reviewed  compliance
with  the  requirement  that,  generally,  federal  contracts  in  excess
of  $500,000  ($1  million  for  construction  contracts)  include  a sub-
contracting  plan  with  specific  contracting  goals  for  small  and
disadvantaged  businesses.
Based  on  information  provided  by  the
GSA,  the  Subcommittee  determined  that  589  contracts  were
awarded  by  the  GSA  in  1987  to  prime  contractors  for  a  total  of
$3.4  billion.  However,  of  that  total,  minority-owned  small  busi-
nesses  received  a  paltry  .006  percent  in  subcontracts.
Subcon-
[footnote  continued]

VJ

13

disadvantaged  business  participation  did  not  rise  to  the
levels  deemed  necessary  by  Congress  to  correct  past
inequalities.  Because  these  businesses  were  not  provided
with  the  necessary  assistance  to  allow  them  to  compete
meaningfully  in  the  free  market  economy,  the  effects  of
past  discrimination  lingered  and  the  need  to  revamp  the
Small  Business  Act  was  dire.  Accordingly,  in response  to
perceived  failures  of  the  1978  Act,  Congress  passed  the
Business  Development  Reform  Act  of  1988,  Pub.  L. No.
100-656,  102  Stat.  3853  (1988)  (“1988  Act”).

The  objective  of  the  1988  Act  was  to  strengthen  the
1978  Act  by changing  the 8(a) program,  and  encouraging
all  federal  agencies  to  strive  toward  increasing  small  and
disadvantaged  business  participation.
As  the  Chairman
of  the  House  Committee  on Small  Business,  John LaFalce,
stated  during  floor  debates,  the 8(a) program  is “intended
to  be  the  most  significant  effort  of the  Federal  Govern-
ment  to  reduce  the  effects  of  discrimination  on  entre-
preneurial  endeavors.”
133  Cong.  Rec.  33,315  (1987)
(statement  of Congressman  LaF alce).

The  Senate  Small

Business  Committee’s  ranking
Republican,  Senator  Lowell  Weicker,  echoed  the  concern
that  the  effects  of discrimination  persisted  in federal  con-
tracting.  Senator  Weicker  also  reiterated  the importance
of continuing  these  programs  for the  purpose  of achieving
fair  and  proportionate  small  and  disadvantaged  business
representation:

But  I will  again  say,  and  the  few  who  might  have
doubts  about  the  need  to  continue  to  provide  such
business  opportunities  as  this  program  offers,  there

tracting  with  Small  and  Disadvantaged  Businesses:  GSA  Oversight:

14

15

is  still  much  to  be  done  if we  are  ever  to  meet  the
challenge  of  opening  doors  to  the  minority  business
community  to  enter  and  rightfully  participate  in
this  Nation’s  rich  economy.  There  are  more  than  17
million  small  businesses  in this  country,  yet  less  than
1  million  are  owned  and  controlled  by  minorities
and  more  than  90  percent  of  those  are  still  single
owner,  mom  and  pop  operations.

134  Cong.  Rec.  31,492  (1988).

To  overcome  these  shortcomings,  Congress  included  a
provision  in  the  1988  Act  which  established  a  goal  to
award  small  and  disadvantaged  businesses  at  least  five
percent  of  the  total  value  of all  prime  contracts  and  sub-
contracts  during  each  fiscal  year.  Pub.  L.  No.  100-656,
$502,  102  Stat.  3887,  codified  at  15 U.S.C.  §644(g)(1).
To  monitor  compliance  with  the  goal,  Congress  estab-
lished  reporting  requirements.
Pub.  L.  No.  100-656  at
§§503  and  504,  codified  at  15  U.S.C.  §644(h)  and
$636  note,  respectively.
A  Commission  on  Minority
Business  Development  was  also  established  to  study  the
efficacy  of  all  federal  programs  intended  to  promote
minority  business  development.  Jd.  at  §505,  codified  at
15  U.S.C.  §636  note.

In  short,  the  1988  Act  was  passed  in  response  to  a
recognition  by Congress  that  the  1978  Act  had  failed  to
eliminate  the  effects  of  discrimination  in  federal  con-
tracting.
There  was  little  need  to  engage  in  additional
fact-finding  since  the  efforts  undertaken  in  1978  had
been  largely  unsuccessful  and  small  and  disadvantaged
businesses  continued  to  struggle  in  their  efforts  to  over-
come  the  effects  of  past  discrimination.
Because  Con-
gress  realized  that  it  could  not  achieve  its  objectives
without  the  wholehearted  assistance  of  all  federal  agen-
cies,  a  government-wide  goal  was  established.
Drawing

from  the  total  contemporary  record  of  congressional
action  dealing  with  the  effects  of discrimination  in  fed-
eral  contracting,  this  Court  should  find  that  the  statutory
provisions  challenged  by  the  Petitioner  are  substantially
related  to  important  and  well-considered  governmental
objectives  —  i.e.,  remedying  past  discrimination  and  pro-
moting  the  development  of businesses  owned  by socially
and  economically  disadvantaged  individuals.

Ill.
THE  SMALL  BUSINESS  ACT’S  ELIGIBILITY  CRITERIA
ARE  NARROWLY  TAILORED  TO  ALLEVIATE  THE
EFFECTS  OF  PAST  DISCRIMINATION.

The  Small  Business  Act’s  eligibility  criteria,  as  incor-
porated  by  reference  into  the  STURAA  program,  are
narrowly  tailored  to  ensure  that  only  those  persons
intended  by  Congress  to  benefit  from  small  and  disad-
vantaged  business  programs  are  permitted  to  partici-
pate.
In  particular,  these  eligibility  criteria  do  not
exclude  any  person  from  consideration  as  a  “socially”
or  “economically”  disadvantaged  individual.  Rather,  any
individual  who  can  demonstrate  that  he  or  she  has  been
victimized  by  racial,  ethnic  or  cultural  bias  will  satisfy
these  eligibility  standards.  Additionally,  individuals  who
have  attained  a  relative  degree  of  economic  success  are
ineligible
to  participate
even  though  they  may  be
“socially”  disadvantaged.
Because  the  Small  Business
Act’s  eligibility  criteria  are  neither  overinclusive  nor
underinclusive,  they withstand  constitutional  scrutiny.

As  noted  above,  the  STURAA  program  grants  eligi-
bility  to  businesses  which  are  owned  and  controlled  by
“socially  and  economically  disadvantaged  individuals.’”®

S Under the  STURAA  program,  subcontractors  must  annually
certify  their  “socially  and  economically  disadvantaged”  status.  49
[footnote  continued]

16

For  the  definition  of  this  term,  the  STURAA  program
adopts  the  same  standard  as  that  which  is contained  in
the  Small  Business  Act,  15  U.S.C.  §637(a)  and  (d).  See
Pub.  L.  No.  100-17,  §106(c),  101  Stat.  146  (1987).
The  Small  Business  Act  describes  eligibility  criteria  for
the  section  8(a)  and  8(d)  programs,  we  well  as  other  fed-
eral  programs  created  pursuant  to  congressional  man-
date  to  eliminate  the  effects  of discrimination  in  federal
contracting.”  As  noted  above,  section  8(d) presumes  that
certain  racial  and  ethnic  groups,  including  Black  Amer-
icans,  Hispanic  Americans,  and  Native  Americans,  are
both  socially  and  economically  disadvantaged.  15  U.S.C.
§637(d)(3)(C)."°

A.  Social  Disadvantage
The  Small  Business  Act  defines  “socially  disadvan-
taged”  individuals  as  those  “who  have  been  subjected  to
racial  or  ethnic  prejudice  or  cultural  bias  .
.  without
regard  to  their  individual  qualities.”
15  U.S.C.  §637-
(a)(5).
Importantly,  this  definition  does  not  grant  a
“preference,”  as  Petitioner  maintains,  to  members  of
any  particular  racial  or  ethnic  group.
Rather,  as  dis-
cussed  below,  any  person  who  can  make  the  requisite
showing  that  he  or  she  has  been  subjected  to  bias  may
qualify  as  “socially  disadvantaged.”  Moreover,  any  group

.

certification  is subject  to  challenge.

of  Defense,  which  incorporates  the  Small  Business  Act's  definition
of “socially  and  economically  disadvantaged  individuals.”

sumed  to  be  “economically”  disadvantaged.

may  gain  entitlement  to  the  “presumption”  described  in

The  flexibility  within  these  eligibility  criteria  is  con-
sistent  with  congressional  intent.
Indeed,  Congress
sought  to  keep  the  doors  to small  and  disadvantaged  bus-
iness  programs  open  even  to  white  individuals,  provided
they could  make  the  requisite  showing:

[B]ecause  of present  and  past  discrimination  many
minorities  have  suffered  social  disadvantagement...  .
However,  the Conferees  realize  that  other  Americans
may  also  suffer  from  social  disadvantagement
because  of  cultural  bias.
For  example,  a  poor
Appalachian  white  person  who  has  never  had  the
opportunity  for  a  quality  education  or  the  ability
to  expand  his  or  her cultural  horizons,  may  similarly
be found socially  disadvantaged  ...

.

H.R.  Conf.  Rep.  No.  1714,  95th  Cong.,  2d  Sess.  21-22,
reprinted  in  1978  U.S.  Code  Cong.  &  Admin.  News
3879,  3882.  (Emphasis  added).

Additionally,  regulations  implementing  the  section
8(d)  program  make  clear  that  any  individual  may  qual-
ify  as  “socially  disadvantaged”  if  he  or  she  can  prove
social  disadvantage  by  clear  and  convincing  evidence.
13 C.F.R.  §124.105(c)."

Moreover,  although  Congress  listed  certain  groups  that
are  “presumed”  to  be  socially  disadvantaged,  the  list  is
non-exclusive.  Any  “identifiable  group,”  as  a whole,  may
gain  entitlement  to  the  same  “presumption.”  The  SBA
has  established
detailed  procedures  for  such  group
recognition:

llin  the  case  at  bar,  Petitioner  was  not  ipso  facto  excluded

from  consideration  as  a “socially  disadvantaged”  firm.

18

19

Socially  disadvantaged  group  inclusion  —

(1) General.  Upon  an  adequate  preliminary  showing
to  SBA  by  representatives  of  an  identifiable  group
that  the  group  has  suffered  chronic  racial  or  ethnic
prejudice  or  cultural  bias,  and  upon  the  request  of
the  representatives  of  the  group  that  SBA  do  so,
SBA  shall  publish  in  the  FEDERAL  REGISTER
a  notice  of  its  receipt  of a  request  that  it consider
a  group  not  specifically  named  in  paragraph  (b)(1)
of  this  section  to  have  members  which  are  socially
disadvantaged  because  of  their  identification  as
members  of  the  group  for  the  purpose  of eligibility
for  the  8(a)  program.
The  notice  shall  adequately
identify  the  group  making  the  request,  and  if a hear-
ing  is  requested  on  the  matter  and  such  request  is
granted,  the  time,  date  and  location  at  which  such
hearing  is  to  be  held.  All  information  submitted  to
a  request  should  be  addressed  to  the
support
—_
AA/MSB&COD.

(2) Standards  to  be  applied.  In determining  whether
a  group  has  made  an  adequate  preliminary  showing
that  it has  suffered  chronic  racial  or  ethnic  prejudice
or  cultural  bias  for  the  purposes  of  this  regulation,
SBA  shall  determine:

(i) Whether  the  group  has  suffered  the  effects  of
prejudice,  bias,  or  discriminatory  practices;

(ii) Whether  such  conditions  have  resulted  in
economic  deprivation  for  the  group  of  the  type
which  Congress  has  found  exists  for  the  groups
named  in the  Small  Business  Act; and

conditions

have  produced
(iii) Whether  such
impediments  in  the  business  world  for  members
of  the  group  over  which  they  have  no  control  and
which  are  not  common  to  all small  business  own-
ers.
If it is demonstrated  to  SBA  by a particular
group  that  it satisfies  the  above  criteria,  SBA  will

publish  the  notice  described  in  paragraph  (d)(1)
of this  section.

13 C.F.R.  §124.105(d).  (Emphasis  added).

Thus,  consistent  with  congressional  intent,  the  “pre-
sumption”  can  be  applied  to  any  group  of  individuals
that  has  been  victimized.  The  aforementioned  proced-
ures  comply  with  fundamental  due  process  requirements
in  that  they  provide  for  public  notice  and  an  opportun-
ity to  present  evidence  at a hearing.  Therefore,  the  “pre-
sumption”  is not  an  arbitrary  or  underinclusive  standard,
as  has been  suggested.  Rather,  it is available  to any  group
that  can  make  the  requisite  showing  of bias.

Furthermore,  the  presumption  that  any  individual
member  of a designated  group  is “socially  disadvantaged”
is  rebuttable.  Under  the  STURAA  program  (as well  as
other  small  and  disadvantaged  business  programs),  a sub-
contractor’s  certification  that  it is owned  and  controlled
by a “socially  disadvantaged”  individual  may  be protested
by  interested  parties.  See  49  C.F.R.  §23.69;  13  C.F.R.
§124.603.  Therefore,  rather  than  mandating  that  mem-
bers  of  designated  groups  be  considered  “socially  disad-
vantaged,”  the  “presumption”  shifts  the  burden  of proof
to  those  who  choose  to  challenge  an  individual’s  claim
of socially  disadvantaged  status.

Indeed,  Congress  has  stated  that  membership  in  a
designated  group  does  not  guarantee  one’s  eligibility  as
“socially  disadvantaged:”

[1]n  many,  but  not  all,  cases  status  as  a  minority
can  be  directly  and  unequivocally  correlated  with
social  disadvantagement  ...

.

H.R.  Conf.  Rep.  No.  1714,  95th  Cong.,  2d  Sess.  21-22,
reprinted  in  1978  U.S.  Code  Cong.  &  Admin.  News
3879,  3882.
(Emphasis  added).  Also,  SBA  regulations

a ee  a ae  Ly

20

provide  that  “in  the  absence  of evidence  to  the  con-
trary,  the  following  individuals  are  presumed  to be socially
disadvantaged:  Black  Americans; Hispanic Americans...  .”
13  C.F.R.  §124.105(b).
(Emphasis  added).  Thus,  an
individual’s  membership  in  a  designated  group  does  not
ipso facto  lead  to  a finding  of social  disadvantage.

For  example,  in  Maralex  Trucking,  Inc.,  No.  386,
Docket  Nol  MSBE-91-6-25-14  (SBA  Office  of  Hearings
and  Appeals,  Dec.  5,  1991),  a  Black-American  appli-
cant  for  the  SBA’s  section  8(a)  program  certified  in
her  application  that,  although  she  had  suffered  limited
employment  opportunities  as  a  result  of sexual  discrim-
ination,  she  had  not  suffered  unemployment  or  limited
educational  opportunities  as  a  result  of  racial  discrim-
ination.
The  SBA  refused  to  admit  the  applicant  into
the  8(a)  program.
In  remanding  the  matter  following
appeal,
the  SBA’s  Office  of  Hearings  and  Appeals
(“OHA”)  noted  that  the  applicant’s  statement  that
she  had  not  suffered  social  disadvantage  as  a  result  of
racial  discrimination:

[T] aken  alone,  might  be  considered  to  be ‘evidence
to  the  contrary,’  as  that  term  is used  in  §124.105(b)
of  the  regulations,  and  thus  a sufficient  reason  to
deny  her  social  disadvantage  on  the  basis  of  her
being a Black-American.

Decision  at  p.  14  n.7.

In  sum,  any  individual  may  qualify  as  “socially  disad-
vantaged”  provided  the  requisite  showing  is  made.
In
addition,  although  members  of  certain  groups  are  “pre-
sumed”  to  be  “socially  disadvantaged,”  any  “‘identifi-
able  group”  may  gain  entitlement  to  the  “presumption”
if  its  claim  is  supported  by  sufficient  evidence  of  past
discrimination.
Finally,  the  “presumption”  that  an
individual  member  of  a  designated  group  is  “socially

21

disadvantaged”  is  rebuttable  and  may  be  disproved  by
interested  parties.

B.  Economic  Disadvantage
Under  the  Small  Business  Act’s  eligibility  criteria,
social  disadvantage  alone  will  not  suffice.  An  individual
must  also  show  that  he  or  she  has  suffered  “economic
disadvantage.”
The  “economic  disadvantage”  require-
ment  is  a  legitimate  color-blind  criterion  which  limits
eligible  participants  in  small  and  disadvantaged  busi-
ness  programs,  including  the  STURAA  program,  to
those  who  have  felt  the  economic  effects  of  discrimin-
ation.
This  economic  criterion  further  demonstrates
that  the  Small  Business  Act’s  eligibility  requirements
are  not  overinclusive,  but  rather,  narrowly  tailored  to
achieve  legitimate  objectives.

SBA  regulations  define  “economic  disadvantage”  as

follows:

individuals

[E]conomically
disadvantaged
are
socially  disadvantaged  individuals  whose  abil::v  to
compete  in  the  free  enterprise  system  has  been
impaired  due  to  diminished  capital  and  credit
opportunities  as  compared  to  others  in  the  same  or
similar  line  of  business  who  are  not  socially  disad-
vantaged,  and  such  diminished  opportunities  have
precluded  or  are  likely  to  preclude  such  individuals
from  successfully  competing  in the open  market.  In
determining  economic  disadvantage  for  purposes  of
8(a)  program  eligibility,  SBA  shall  compare  the
applicant  concern’s  business  and  financial  profile
with  profiles  of  businesses  in  the  same  or  similar
line  of business  which  are  not  owned  and  controlled
by socially  and  economically  disadvantaged  individ-
uals.

(ii) This  program  is  not  intended  to  assist  con-
cerns  owned  and  controlled  by  socially  disadvan-
taged  individuals  who  have  accumulated  substantial

22
wealth,  who  have  unlimited  growth  potential  or  who
have  not  experienced  or  have  overcome  impedi-
ments  to  obtaining  access  to  financing,  markets  and
resources.

13  C.F.R.  §124.106(a)(1).  See  also  15  U.S.C.  §637(a)-
(6)(A)."?

No  individual,  regardless  of his  or  her race  or  ethnicity,
is  eligible  for  the  STURAA  program  (or other  small  and
disadvantaged  business  programs)  if that  individual  has
advanced  in  the  business  world  and  overcome  the  impedi-
ments  caused  by his  or  her  status  as  a  socially  disadvan-
taged  individual.
Thus,  the  assertion  by amicus  curiae
Atlantic  Legal  Foundation  that  members  of  the  desig-
nated  groups  are  presumed  to  be  economically  disad-
vantaged  even  if  they  are  “wealthy”  (see  Brief  at  16)  is
inaccurate.
See  Autek  Systems  Corp.  v.  United  States,
835  F.  Supp.  13,  15  (D.D.C.  1993)  (upholding  SBA’s
rejection  of 8(a) applicant  due  to  owner’s  high income).

Although  subcontractors  in  the  STURAA  program
need  only  certify  as  to  their  DBE  status,  this  certifica-
tion  may  be  protested  by  interested  parties,  including
competing  subcontractors.
49  C.F.R.  §§23.51  and
23.69(a)(1).
In  that  event,  an  investigation  is  under-
taken  to  determine  if  the  subcontractor  is,  in  fact,
“economically  disadvantaged.”  49 C.F.R.  §23.69(b).

The  protest  procedures  in  the  STURAA  program  are
similar  to  the  protest  procedures  created  pursuant  to  the
Small  Business  Act.
In  considering  a  protest  which

121, determining  “economic  disadvantage’’  for  purposes  of
the  §8(d)  subcontracting  program  and  the Department  of Defense’s
Small  and  Disadvantaged  Business  (“SDB’’)  program,  the  SBA
applies  the  same  criteria  used  in  determining  disadvantaged  status
under  the  §8(a)  program.
13  C.F.R.  §124.106(a).  However,  in
reviewing  eligibility  under  the  §8(d)  and  SDB  programs,  the  SBA
applies  those  criteria  in a less  restrictive  manner.  13 C.F.R.  § 124.-
106(b).

23

alleges  that  a  firm  is  not  owned  and  controlled  by  an
“economically  disadvantaged”  individual,  the  SBA  con-
siders  factors  relating  both  to  the  financial  condition  of
the  individual  claiming  disadvantaged  status  and  the
financial  condition  of  the  business.
In  so  doing,  the
SBA  examines  three  areas:

(1) the  personal  financial  condition  of  the  indi-
vidual  claiming  disadvantaged  status,  including
that  individual’s  access  to  credit  and  capital;
(2) the  financial  condition  of the  business;  and
(3) the  business’  access  to  credit,  capital  and

markets. !*

A  finding  of non-disadvantaged  status  not  only renders
the  firm  ineligible  for  award  in  the  procurement  at  issue,
but  such  a  finding  also  renders  the  firm  ineligible  to
compete  in  the  future,  absent  a  change  in  its  financial
condition.  13 C.F.R.  §124.609(h)(2).

Regarding  the  first  factor,  a  review  of  the  owner’s
individual  financial  condition  is  conducted  in  order  to
exclude  socially  disadvantaged  individuals  who  have
“accumulated  substantial  wealth,  who  have  unlimited
growth  potential  or  who  have  not  experienced  or  have
overcome  impediments  to  obtaining  access  to  financing,
markets  and  resources.”
13  C.F.R.  § 124.106(a)(1)/(ii).
The  SBA  considers  all  forms  of personal  income,  includ-
ing  the  individual’s  gross  salaries,  wages,  remuneration,
fees,  allowances,  dividends,  commissions  and  receipts

13 specifically,  the  SBA  examines:  (1)  business  tax  returns
for  the  last  two  completed  fiscal  years;  (2)  personal  tax  returns
for  the  last  two  years  for all officers,  directors  and  individuals  own-
ing  at  least  five  percent  of the  entity;  (3) business  financial  state-
ments  for  the  last  two  completed  fiscal  years;  (4) Articles  of Incor-
poration  and  Bylaws;  and  (5)  such  other  information  as  may  be
required.  13 C.F.R.  § 124.608.

24

of all  kinds  and  descriptions.  See  Super Solutions  Corp.,
No.  461,  Docket  No.  MSBE-93-12-28-19,  at  p.  2  (SBA
Office  of Hearings  and  Appeals,  April  12,  1994).

With  regard  to  the  second  factor,  the  SBA  must
“compare  the  applicant  concern’s  business  and  financial
profile  with  profiles  of businesses  in  the  same  or  similar
line  of  business  which  are  not  owned  and  controlled  by
socially  and  economically  disadvantaged  individuals.”  13
C.F.R.  §124.106(a)(2)(ii).  The  SBA  reviews  the  assets,
revenues,  pre-tax  profit,  working  capital  and  net  worth
of  the  business,  including  the  value  of  the  investments
in  the  business  held  by the  individual  claiming  disadvan-
taged  status.  /d.

Finally,  in  considering  the  concern’s  access  to  credit
and  capital,  the  SBA  evaluates  its  access  to  long-term
and  working  capital  financing,  equipment  trade  credit,
raw  materials  and  supplier  trade  credit,  and  bonding
capability.  13  C.F.R.  §124.106(a)(5)(iii).

Despite  the  view  of  the  Court  of  Appeals  for  the
Seventh  Circuit  in  Milwaukee  County  Pavers  Ass’n  v.
Fiedler,  922  F.2d  419,  422  (7th  Cir.),  cert.  denied,  500
U.S.  954  (1991),  that  the  rebuttable  presumption  of
disadvantaged  status  is  “likely  to  be  decisive,”  the  pro-
test  procedures  described  above  have  been  employed
frequently  and  with  great  effect. !*

For  example,  in  a  single  federal  procurement  offered
by the  Department  of  the  Navy,  four  of nine  offerors  in

4The majority  of reported  protests  arise  out  of the Depart-
ment  of  Defense’s  SDB  program,  § 1207  of  the  National  Defense
Authorization  Act  for  Fiscal  Year  1987,  10  U.S.C.  § 2323.  Like
the  STURAA  program,  this  program  relies  on  the  Small  Business
Act’s  eligibility  requirements.

25

the  competitive  range  were  found  by  the  SBA  to  be
non-disadvantaged,  and  therefore,  ineligible  for  award.
See  Integrated  Systems  Analysts,  Inc.,  GSBCA  No.
10750-P,  91-1  BCA  423,477  (CCH)  (1990).
Indeed,
in  Integrated  Systems  Analysts,  the  General  Services
Administration  Board  of  Contract  Appeals,  in  summar-
izing  the  results  of  protests  filed  during  the  prior  three
years,  noted  that  the  SBA  had  found  “a  hundred  or
more”  firms  to  be  ineligible  and  “in  excess  of forty”  to
be  eligible
for  participation  in  the  Department  of
Defense’s  SDB  program.  91-1  BCA  at p.  117,776.

Inc.,

Solicitation

In  Computer  Dynamics,

No.
N00039-88-R-0221  (SBA  MSB&COD,  Nov.  16,  1989),
the  SBA  found  that  the  protested  business  was  not
“economically  disadvantaged”  despite  the  fact  that  its
owner  was  “‘socially  disadvantaged.”
In  declaring  the
company  ineligible,  the  SBA  found  the  owner’s  personal
financial  condition,  the  company’s  business  financial
condition,  and  the  company’s  access  to credit  and  capital
to  be  above  national  averages.  The  SBA  concluded  that
the  company’s  owner  had  “accumulated  substantial
wealth  and  [had]  overcome  the  impediments  to  obtain-
ing access  to  financing,  markets  and  resources.”  Decision
at  p.  4.  See  also  Systems  Management  American  Corp.,
Inc.,  Solicitation  No.  N00039-88-R-0221  (SBA  Office  of
Program  Eligibility,  July  11,  1990)  (denying  eligibility
to  business  owned  by  “socially  disadvantaged”  individ-
ual);  DeLeon  Technical  Services,  Inc.,  Solicitation  No.
F05611-92-B-2010  (SBA  Office  of  Program  Eligibility,
Jan.  13,  1993)  (finding  “socially  disadvantaged”  owner’s
wealth  was  “not  indicative  of an  individual  who  is econ-
omically  disadvantaged”).  Cf. Apex  Roofing  Co.,  Solic-
itation  No.  F29651-91-B-0025  (SBA  Office  of  Program
Eligibility,  Sept.  27,  1991)  and  Hirsch  &  Co.,  Solicita-

26

27

tion  No.  N68711-91-D-0007  (SBA  Office  of  Program
Eligibility,  March  21,  1991)  (denying  eligibility
to
“socially  disadvantaged”  owners  unable  to  certify  that
their  ability  to  compete  in  the  free  enterprise  system
had  been  impaired).

In  short,  claims  of  “economic  disadvantage’’  fre-
quently  are  challenged  and  often  disproved.  Unquestion-
ably,  the  SBA  closely  monitors  eligibility  in  accordance
with  definitive  requirements.
Accordingly,  the  Small
Business  Act’s  eligibility  criteria  are  narrowly  tailored  and
strictly  enforced  to  achieve  the  legitimate  objectives  of
redressing  the  effects  of past  discrimination  and  fostering
the  development  of small  and  disadvantaged  businesses.

C.  Section  502  is Not  “‘Limitless”’

Contrary  to  Petitioner’s  assertions,  section  502  of  the
Small  Business  Act,  15  U.S.C.  §644(g),  is  not  “poten-
tially  limitless”  in  extent  and  duration.  Rather,  the Court
of  Appeals  for  the  Tenth  Circuit  correctly  noted  that  fed-
eral  procurement  and  contracting  practices  are  subject  to
“regular  ‘reassessment  and  reevaluation  by  Congress.’  ”’
Adarand  Constructors,  Inc.  v.  Pena,  16  F.3d  1537,  1546
(10th  Cir.  1994)  (citations  omitted).  Indeed,  the  Small
Business  Act  requires  the  SBA  submit  to  the  Presi-
dent  a  detailed  annual  report  analyzing  the  extent  of
federal  contracting  with  small  and  disadvantaged  busi-
nesses.
15  U.S.C.  §644(h)(2).  The  President,  in turn,  is
required  to  include  such  information  in  an  annual  report
to  Congress.  Jd.  at  §644(h)(3).

Through  the  reauthorization  and  appropriation  pro-
cesses,  Congress  exercises  periodic  oversight  of  the  sec-
tion  502  small  and  disadvantaged  business  goal.  In addi-
tion,  outside  of  these  processes,  Congress  may  enact

legislation  which  impacts  on  the  goal.  For  example,  as
part  of the  Federal  Acquisition  Streamlining  Act  of 1994,
Pub.  L.  No.  103-355,  §7102,  Congress  recently  created
an  additional  tool  to  assist  federal  agencies  in  meeting
their  individual  small  and  disadvantaged  business  goals
established  pursuant  to  section  502.!°
The  passage  of
this  legislation  demonstrates  ongoing  congressional  over-
sight  and  commitment  to  eliminating  the  effects  of dis-
crimination  in  federal  contracting.  Accordingly,  section
502  is  not  “limitless,”  but  rather,  is subject  to  periodic
review  and  evaluation  by Congress.

CONCLUSION

For  the  reasons  set  forth  above,  Amicus  Curiae  respect-
fully  urges  this  Court  to  affirm  the  decision  of the  United
States  Court  of Appeals  for the Tenth  Circuit.

Dated:  December  5, 1994

Respectfully  submitted,

PAMELA  j. MAZZA

(Attorney  of Record)
ANDREW  P. HALLOWELL
ANTONIO  R.  FRANCO
FRANK  C. GULIN

on  small  and disadvantaged  business  contracting  in any industry
share of small  and dis-
category  thet is bearing  a  disproportionate
advantaged  business  contracts.  See  Pub. L. No.  103-355,  §7102(b)-
(2)(D).
Thus,  Congress  is  continuing  to  carefully  scrutinize  the
impact  of  small  and  disadvantaged  business  programs  on  non-
disadvantaged  firms.

>The strict scrutiny  standard  requires  that courts strike down
legislation  if not "necessary"  to achieve  a  “compelling”  or  "overriding"
government  purpose.  City of Richmond  v.  J.A.  Croson  Company.,  488
U.S.  469  (1989).  Under  the  intermediate  scrutiny  standard,  a  court
must  uphold  legislation  so long as it is “substantially  related"  to an
“important”  government  purpose.  Jd.

protect  the  civil  liberties  of the  newly  freed  slaves,  the

a

The  affirmative  right  to  societal  participation  is
included  in  the  liberties  guaranteed  by  the  Fourteenth
Amendment  which  may  be  enforced  by  Congress.
Slaughter-House  Cases,  21  L.  Ed.  at  407;  see  also  City
of Richmond  v.  J.A.  Croson  Company,  488  U.S.  469,
511  (1989)  (STEVENS,  J.,  concurring)  ("A  central
purpose  of the  Fourteenth  Amendment  is to  further  the
national  goal  of equal  opportunity  for  all  our  citizens.").
The  Fourteenth  Amendment  contains
“a  necessary
implication  of  a  positive  immunity  or  right,  most
valuable  to the  colored  race."  Strauder  v.  West  Virginia,
This  positive  right
100  U.S.  303,  307-308  (1880).
includes  the  right  to  be free  from  "legal  discriminations,
implying  inferiority  in  civil
society,  lessening  the
security  of their  enjoyment  of the  rights  which  others
enjoy."
Jd.  The  right  to  societal  participation  is  also
evidenced  by the  subsequent  enactment  of the  Fifteenth
Amendment  which  secured  for  the  newly  freed  slaves  the

See
right  to  participate  in  the  democratic  process.
Slaughter-House  Cases,  16  Wall.  36,  21  L.  Ed.  394,  407
(1873).
Accordingly,  since  the  Thirteenth  Amendment
guaranteed  that  no  man  could  be  the  property  of another
and  the  Fifteenth  Amendment  secured  participation  in the
democratic  process,  the Fourteenth  Amendment  necessarily
protects  those  rights incident  to societal  participation.”

The  propriety  of the  right  to  societal  participation
has  seldom  been  doubted,  and  only  once  by the  Court.
See  Plessy  v.  Ferguson,  163  U.S.  537  (1896),  overruled
by Brown  v.  Board  of Education  of Topeka,  Shawnee
County,  Kansas,  347  U.S.  483  (1954)  (separation  of
socially  incompatible  races  is constitutional).  When  the
Court  overruled  Plessy's  “separate  but  equal"  doctrine  in
Brown,  it reaffirmed  the  Fourteenth  Amendment  night  to
societal  participation.
In  Plessy,  the  Court  determined
that  the  Fourteenth  Amendment  did  not  assure  African
Americans  the right to participate  in American  society:

James  Madison  wrote:

>From  hindsight,  the  need  for  the  affirmative  guarantee  of the
Fourteenth  Amendment  can  be  explained  by  the  problem  of  the
“When  a  majority  is
majority  faction.
included  in a  faction,  the  form  of popular  government.  .  .enables  it
to sacrifice  to its ruling passion  or  interest,  both  the public  good and
the  rights of other  citizens.”  THE  FEDERALIST  No.  10, 20  (James
Madison)  (Washington  Square  Press  ed.  1964).
Since  minorities,
by  definition,  are  not  within  the  majority  class,  the  affirmative
guarantee  to  societal  participation  remains  necessary  to  guard
against  factionist  tendencies.

Further,

in  Brown

specifically
addressed  the  nght  to  societal  participation.  The  Brown
Court  noted  that  the  children  on  whose  behalf  the  suits
were  brought  were  given equal  treatment  under  the law,*
but  held  that  equality  of  treatment  was  insufficient:
"Our  decision,  therefore,  cannot  tum  on  merely  a
comparison  of these  tangible  factors...  .  We  must  look
instead  to  the  effect  of  segregation  itself  upon  public
education."  Brown,  347  U.S.  at  492.  The  Court  found
that  mere  equality  of treatment  socially  stigmatized  the

“"Here.  .  there are findings  below that the Negro and white schools
involved  have been  equalized,  or are being equalized,  with respect  to
[all] ‘tangible  factors."  Brown,  347  U.S. at 492  (omitting  footnotes).

black  children:  "To  separate  them  from  others  of similar
age  and  qualifications  solely  because  of  their  race
generates  a feeling  of inferiority  as  to  their  status  in the
community  that  may  affect  their  hearts  and  minds  in  a
way  unlikely  ever  to be undone."  /d.  at 494.

488  U.S.  at  493.

The  affirmative  right  to  societal  participation,
however,  does  not  extend  to the majority  race.  The  nght
turns  on  recognition  of  society's  treatment  of  insular
In  City  of Richmond,  Justice
minorities  as  inferior.
O'Connor  stated,  "Classifications  based  on  race  carry  a
danger  of  stigmatic  harm."
While
Justice  O'Connor  clearly  intended  her  statement  to  cover
all races,  in fact,  the  majority  races  have  no  need  to  fear
stigmatism.  As stated  by the dissenting  Justice  Harlan  in
Plessy,  "Sixty  millions  of whites  are  in no  danger  from
the presence  here  of eight millions  of blacks."
163  U.S.
at 560  (Harlan,  J., dissenting).  Indeed,  in his classic  line
that  “our  constitution  is  color  blind,"  Justice  Harlan
cached
of
legislation  which  would  maintain  the  supremacy  of the
white  race  over  the inferior  black  race:

Constitution's

in  terms

blindness

the

The  white  race  deems  itself  to  be  the
dominant  race  in this  country.  And  so  it is,
in prestige,  in achievements,  in education,
in wealth,  and in peer.  So, I doubt  not,  it
will  continue  to  be  for  all  time,  if  it
remains  true  to its great  heritage,  and holds
fast  to  the  principles  of  constitutional
liberty.  But  in view  of the  constitution,  in

is  color-blind,  and  neither
constitution
knows  nor  tolerates  classes  among  citizens.

Plessy,  163  U.S.  at 559  (Harlan,  J., dissenting).°

While  today  Justice  Harlan's  statements  may  raise

been  upon  protecting  racial  and  ethnic

(1989),  IS LEGALLY  AND  FACTUALLY

Petitioner  is mistaken  in its determination  that  the
holding  in  City  of Richmond  v.  J.A.  Croson  Company,
488  U.S.  469  (1989)  applies  to  its  challenge.  Beyond
the  fact  that  City  of Richmend  dealt  with  a  local
affirmative  action  program,  which  deserves  considerably
less  deference  than  a  federal  program,’  the  issues

'?  Upon concluding,  in City of Richmond,  that  “a  sound distinction
between  federal  and state action  based on race"  was required  by the
Civil  War Amendments,  Justice  Scalia  stated:

[I]t is one thing to permit  racially  based conduct  by the
Federal  Government  —  whose  legislative  powers
conceming  matters  of race were explicitly  enhanced
by the Fourteenth  Amendment.  .
~  and quite another
to permit  it by the precise  entities  against  whose
conduct  in  matters  of  race  that  Amendment  was
specifically  directed."

.

City of Richmond,  488  U.S.  at 521-22  (SCALIA,  J., concurring).

V.  THE  COURT  SHOULD  NOT  OVERRULE
PRECEDENT  ESTABLISHING  REDUCED
SCRUTINY

Corsonant  with  the  determination  that  section  5
of  the  Fourteenth  Amendment  gives  Congress  the
affirmative  power  to  legislate  on  behalf  of  those
historically  subject  to  racial  antagonism  are  the  Court's
determinations  in  Bolling  v.  Sharpe,  347  U.S.  497
(1954),  and  Metro  Broadcasting,
v.  Federal
Communications  Commission,  497  U.S.  547  (1990),  that
racial  classifications  by the  federal  government  receive  a
standard  of review  less  stringent  than  that  applied  to  the
States.  According  to  the  holding  in Planned  Parenthood
of Souteastern  Pennsylvania  v.  Casey,  112  S.  Ct.  2791,
2808-2816  (1992),  concerns  for  judicial  integrity  and
respect  for  a  National  rule  of law  require  adherence  to
these  prior,  workable  decisions  pursuant  to  the  doctrine
of stare  decisis.  Id.

Inc.

“Certainly,  Congress  can take notice  of  matters  of  general
knowledge  to the same, or greater,  extent as the judiciary.  See Muller v.
Oregon,  207  U.S.  412, 421  (1908)  ("We take judicial cognizance of all
matters  of general knowledge.").

22

severe  enough  to  violate

The  first  time  the  Court  applied  the  equal
protection  component  of the  Fourteenth  Amendment  to
the  federal  government,  in Bolling,  the  Court  applied  a
lesser  standard  of review  than  strict  scrutiny.  In holding
that  segregated  schooling  in  the  District  of Columbia  is
discrimination
the  Fifth
Amendzuent's  Due  Process  Clause,  the  Court  stated:
"Segregation  in  public  education  is  not  reasonably
related  to  any  proper  governmental  objective...  ."
Bolling,  347  U.S.  at  500.  Since  this  standard  of review
for  racial  classifications  by the  federal  government  has
never  been  overruled,  the  Court  should  adhere  to  its
determination.

Further,  the  Court,  for  all  intents  and  purposes,
reaffirmed  the  Bolling  standard  of  review  in  Metro
Broadcasting.  Four  years  ago,  in Metro  Broadcasting,  a
five  Justice  majority  applied  intermediate  scrutiny  to  a
federal  racial  classification  in  federal  communications
licensing.  In upholding  the  federal  classification,  Justice
Brennan  stated:  “We  hold  that  benign  race  conscious
measures  mandated  by Congress.  .  .are  constitutionally
permissible  to  the  extent  that  they  serve  important
governmental  objectives.  .  .and  are  substantially  related
to  achievement  of those  purposes."  Metro  Broadcasting,
497  U.S.  at 564-65."°

'S Here,  Congress  and  the  President  determined  the  need  for
societal  equality  in contracting  opportunities.  See,  supra,  page  13,
14  & note  7.  Thus,  the  concern  expressed  by the  dissent  in Metro
Broadcasting  over  the majorities’  “benign  race  conscious”  language

that  the  use

Due to the proximity  in time  between  the  decision
in Metro  Broadcasting  and  the  instant  action,  the  fears
embodied  by the  doctrine  of stare  decisis  are  magnified.
In  her  discussion  of  the  doctrine,  in  Casey,  Justice
O'Connor  examined  the  overruling  of Lochner  v.  New
York,  198  U.S.  45  (1905),  by  West  Coast  Hotel  Co.  v.
Parrish,  300  U.S.  379  (1937),  and  the  overruling  of
Plessy  v.  Ferguson,  163  U.S.  537  (1896)  by Brown  vy.
Board  of  Education  of  Topeka,  Shawnee  County,
Kansas,  347  U.S.  483  (1954).
Casey,  112  S.  Ct.  at
2812-813  (O'CONNOR,  J.,  for  the  Court).

O'Connor  found,  among  other  factors,
that  in  the

remains  inapplicable  in the  instant  action.  See  Metro  Broadcasting,

distance  in  time  between  the  overruled  cases  to  their

the  texture  of  federal  acquisition  and  licensing  law

The  judgment  of  the  United  States  Court  of

Appeals  for the  Tenth  Circuit  should  be affirmed.

SUMMARY  OF  THE  ARGUMENT

and

owned

businesses,

Congress,  through  the  Small  Business  Act,  requires
the  Executive  Branch  to  use  its  purchasing  power  to  foster
including  small
the  development  of  small
by  socially
businesses
and
controlled
See  generally  15
economically  disadvantaged  persons.
U.S.C.  §631  (Declaration  of  Policy)(the  "Act").
The  Act
requires  annual,  government-wide  goals  to  be  set  for
purchases  of  supplies  and  services  from  small  business
enterprises  and  small  disadvantaged  business  enterprises
(DBEs),  and  sets  a floor  for the  DBE  procurement  goal.  The
statutory  definition  of DBE  includes,  but  is not  limited  to  a
racial  component.  Not  exclusively  race-based,  the  eligibility
standards  includes  persons  who  have  been  subjected  to
“ethnic  prejudice  or  cultural  bias."

Amici  argue  that  Congress  has  broad  powers  to  enact
such  legislation  and  to  delegate  authority  to  the  Executive
Branch  to  implement  its  will;  and  that  in  delegating  such
authority,  the  Act  is unambiguous  about  its  intent  to  create

constitutionally  permissible  "goals  and  opportunities"  for
small  business  enterprises  and  socially  and  economically
disadvantaged  business  enterprises.  Amici  argue  that  the use
of a  remedial  expedient,  the  Subcontracting  Compensation
Clause  ("SCC"),  by Respondent  to  achieve  the  purposes  of
the  Small  Business  Act,  as  amended,  and  supplemented  by
essential
acts  and  procurement  policies,
comport  with  the  will  of Congress  and  do  not  violate  the
nondelegation  rule.  All  government  actions  were  within  the
scope  of its authority,  and lawful.

appropriation

Further,  Amici  argue  that  the  SCC,  a  mere  remedial
expedient  inviting  a  private  act  for  a  valuable  government
privilege,  does  not  compel  any  private  act  for  the  valuable
government  privilege.  We  emphasize  that  under  the  SCC,
no  government  action
is  unbound,  no  governmental
obligation  vests,  no  liability  cf  the  government  or  prime
contractor  is  created  until  the  prime  contractor  is  selected
and  thereafter  exercises  its  sole  discretion  to  accept  the
invitation  of offer  set  forth  by the  terms  of the  SCC.  Amici
submit  that  the  selection  of the  DBE  by Mountain  Gravel  &
Construction  Company  in the exercise  of its discretion  under
the  SCC,  objected  to  by Petitioner  was  a private  act,  not  a
cognizable  action  for  the  valuable  government  privilege
under  the  Equal  Protection  Clause  of the  Fifth  Amendment
to the U.S.  Constitution.

the

that

argue

Amici

Alternatively,

remedial
expedient,  SCC,  as  narrowly  tailored  by its  own  terms  and
the  policies  of the  Executive  Branch  is as  permissible  as  the
set-aside  program  upheld  by  the  Court  in  Fullilove  v.
Klutznick,’  and  its  progeny.2  We  submit  that  the  proper
scope  of  judicial  review  adopted  in  Fullilove  is  the
controlling  precedent  in  this  case,  if  the  constitutional
guarantee  claimed  by Petitioner  is a cognizable  state  action.

' 488 U.S.  448  (1980).

? See,  e.g.,  Metro  Broadcasting  v.  FCC,  497  U.S.  547 (1990).

The  SCC  is just what  it explains  itself to be:  a clause
of  a  federal  procurement  contract  which  invites  offers,
subject  to  acceptance,’  for  monetary  compensation  "for
awarding  subcontracts  to  small  business  concerns  owned  and
controlled  by  socially  and  economically  disadvantaged
individuals."  The  invitation  for  offer  is made  as  part  of the
procurement  process  which  lets  contracts  under  a  bidding
procedure  established  by the  Federal  Acquisition  Regulation
System  ("FAR"),’  and  implements  the  acquisition-related
section  of various  congressional  statutes,  including  the Small
_  Business  Act.°  Drawing  on  the  opinion  of the  court  below,
on  September  15,  1989,  a  division  of  the  Department  of
Transportation  Central  Federal  Lands  Highway  Division
("CFLHD"),  awarded  a  $1-million-plus  prime  contract  to  a
small  business  contractor  (Mountain  Gravel  &  Construction
Company),  for a federal  highway  project  in Colorado  known
as  the  West  Dolores  project.’  Amici  understand  that
Mountain  Gravel  was  bound  to  fulfill  the  mandatory  terms
of the  contract  which  had  been  awarded  to  it.  However,  the
SCC  remained  in  a  state  of  equipoise,  as  a  continuing
contractual  incentive,  which,  according  to  its  terms  would
either  vest,  or  be rejected  solely  within  the  sole  discretion  of
Mountain  Gravel.  Hence,  acceptance  of  the  terms  of  the

>  RESTATEMENT  (Seconp)  Or  Cowrracts,  §24,  defines  an  offer  as  "a
manifestation  of willingness  to enter  into a bargain  so  made  as  to justify
another  person  in understanding  that  his assent  to that bargain  is invited
and  will  conclude  it.”  An  invitation  to  offer  in  government  contracts
need not be accepted,  but the power of acceptance  permits  the offeree  to
transform  the offer  into a contractual  obligation.  See  Arrnur  L. Coram,
Offer  and Acceptance  and  Some  of The  Resulting  Legal  Relations,  26
Yale  L.J.  169,  181-182,  199-200  (1917).

continuing  offer  incentive  was  voluntary  and  premised  on  a

responded  to  the  government's  invitation  to  offer.  Mountain
Gravel,  acting  on  the  continuing  offer  under  the  SCC,

Court  claiming  that  the  above  described  optional  incentive

I.  THE  SCC  AND  DBE  ELIGIBILITY  |

REQUIREMENTS  COMPORT  WITH  THE
SMALL  BUSINESS  ACT  AND,  AS
IMPLEMENTED,  VIOLATE  NEITHER  THE
NONDELEGATION  RULE,  NOR  IS BEYOND
THE  SCOPE  OF  AUTHORITY  RULE  AND  IS
OTHERWISE  CONSTITUTIONALLY
PERMISSIBLE

The judicial  principle  undergirding  the nondelegation
rule  permits  Congress  to  delegate,  by  statute,
or
amendment,  the  authority  to  implement  the  will  of

*  id.

*  The  record  gives  no  indication  of the  racial  composition  Adarand’s
ownership,  although  its  general  manager  is  a  white  male  and  its
stockholders  include  the general  manager's  wife  and  two  other  women."

!° 4darand,  16 F.3d  1537, cert.  granted,  63 U.S.L.W.  3255  (U.S.  Sept.
26,  1994)(No.  93-1841).

Congress.  The  nondelegation  rule"  is an  old  rule  and  an
important  one  because  the  people  depend  on  this  rule  to
assure  that  Congress  does  its  work.
Hence,  when
Congress  delegates  its authority  to  a  legislative  agency,”
a judicial  unit,"’  or  to  an  arm  of the  Executive  Branch,"
the  text  of  the  organic  or  amending  act  is  required  to
establish  general  policy  or  standards  to  guide  the  delegate
in the  creation  of tools  to  fil!  in the  blanks  to  enable  the
delegate  to  fulfill  the  will  of Congress  in an  efficient  and
effective  manner,  as  an  administrative  function.’*

This  process  enables  Congress,  in  an  exceedingly
complex  nation,  in  which  there  exist  ongoing  conflicting
interests,  to  carry  out,  to  fund,  correct,  amend  or  repeal,  its
actions  on  behalf  of  the  people."®  The  delegation  process
permits  needed  elasticity--as  guided  by Congress--by  federal
agencies  to  implement,  correct,  create,  amend  or  repeal
administrative  actions,  or  to  use  appropriated  funds  in  an
exceedingly  complex  nation  toward  the  resolution
of
conflicting
and  the
incidental  funding  process  is  a  means  by  which  Congress
reinforces  its responsibilities  incident  to  representation,'’  one

interests.  The  delegation

process
