BRIEF  FOR  AMICUS  CURIAE
NATIONAL  ASSOCIATION  OF MINORITY
BUSINESSES,  IN SUPPORT  OF
RESPONDENT

INTEREST  OF AMICUS  CURIAE'

The National  Association  of Minority  Businesses  (NAMB)
respectfully  moves  the Court  for leave to file,  in support  of the
Respondents,  the annexed  brief  amicus  curiae  on the merits  of the
appeal  in this case by the Petitioner,  Adarand  Constructors,  Inc.
NAMB  obtained  the consent  of the United States,  Solicitor  General
and of Mr.  William  Perry  Pendley  of Mountain  States  Legal
Foundation,  counsel  for Petitioner.
I

NAMB  is an incorporated  501(C)(6),  voluntary, association
located  in  Arlington,  Virginia.  NAMB  members  are  minority
owned  businesses  nationwide.  NAMB  maintains  a  membership
totalling  150 companies,  of which most or all are members  of one
minority  community  or another.  NAMB  is the premiere,  national-
scale minority  association  in the United  States  which  provides
business  assistance  and information  regarding  federal  government
procurement.  It maintains  members  from  all fifty states  and the
Virgin  Islands.  Its purpose  is to further  the objectives  of minority

networking  opportunities  for  these  groups.
Particularly,  at  the
heart  of NAMB  is its service  of gathering  information  and
providing  assistance,  to its members,  regarding  the various  federal
socially
federal
contracting  programs.
Many  members  engage  in  government
contracting,  both on the basis  of federal  and state socially  and

and  economically

disadvantaged

business

'The parties  have consented  to the submission  of this brief.  Their letters

of consent  have been filed with the Clerk of the Court.

vi

economically  disadvantaged  business  contracting  programs.

Every  member  of NAMB,  and  thus  NAMB  itself,  will  be
impacted  by the  outcome  of this  case,  hence,  the  membership  and
the  organization  maintain  an  actual  and  substantial  interest  in this
Despite  its  interest,  NAMB  did  not  participate  in  the
matter.
proceedings  in the court  below  solely  because  it was  unaware  that
such  proceedings  were  in progress  until  it was  too  late  to intervene
therein.

ll

federal

business

disadvantaged

The  questions  presented  by  the  appeal  in  this  case  are
vitally  important  to NAMB,  its members  and affiliates.  The appeal
questions,  particularly,  Department  of Transportation  socially  and
economically
contracting
However,  this  case  impacts  generally  all  federal
regulations.
government  socially  and  economically  disadvantaged  business
contracting  programs.  The  appeal  seeks  a  determination,  among
other  things,  that  the  socially  and  economically  disadvantaged
business  federal  contracting  program  in question  has  exceeded  its
objectives  and  otherwise  cannot  be justified  when  subject  to  the
appropriate  standard.  Moreover,  that  standard  has  been  placed  in
question.  In the  broadest  sense,  then,  the  effect  of racism  in this
country  is  under  review.
Particularly,  how  and  to  what  extent
racism  has  harmed  minorities  is questioned.

The potential  ramifications  of this appeal  are broad,  and
their effect  wide-ranging.  Conceivably,  the practical  effect  may  be
a  re-examination  of and  fundamental  change  to  all  socially  and
economically  disadvantaged  business  federal  contracting  programs.
Perhaps,  these programs  will  be removed  and new ones recreated.
Perhaps,  they will just be removed  or modified.  In any event, the
possibilities  are  numerous,  although  one  thing  is  clear;  any
outcome  other  than  one in favor of the Respondent  is  certain  to
negatively  affect  NAMB  members.

disadvantaged  business  federal  contracting  that  is questioned  by
this appeal,  and in most other federal  contracting  programs.  The
association  itself  operates  to  further  enhance  participation  in such
programs.  The  impact  of an  outcome  favoring  the  Petitioner  will
be a  removal  of or decrease  in the primary  source  of income  for
many  NAMB  members,  causing  a  significant  and  substantial
decrease  in  membership.
Likewise,  without  the  benefit  of  the
continued  existence  of these  programs  in their  present  form,  which
is a  conceivable  ramification  of a  successful  appeal  herein,  the
heart  of NAMB  will  be viscerated;  its purpose  minimized,  and  its
raison  d'etre  permanently  and  substantially  decreased.

Ill

Finally,  the  Respondents  in this  case  are  not  in the  same
position  as  is NAMB  to speak  for the  whole  of minority  members
around  the  Country  with  regard  to  the  important  problems  of
statutory  construction  before  the  Court  on  this  appeal.  Similarly,
other  entities  which  have  successfully  sought  to  file  briefs  amicus
curiae  in  this  appeal  are  not  in  the  same  position  as  NAMB.
NAMB  is,  perhaps,  the  only  national-level  entity  capable  of
representing  all  forms  of minorities;  particularly,  those  interested
in engaging  in socially  and  economically  disadvantaged  business
federal  contracting  programs.  Groups  such  as  the  American  Civil
Liberties  Union  (ACLU),  for  example,  although  representing  the
needs  of minorities  generally,  do not  provide direct  assistance  with
regard to federal  socially  and economically  disadvantaged  business
federal  contracting  programs  such as the one questioned  by this
appeal.

Wherefore,  NAMB  respectfully  moves  the Court  for leave
to file the brief  annexed  hereto  on the merits  of the questions
by the appeal.
raised

NAMB  members  have  a  substantial  business  interest  in
participation  in and access  to the  system  governed  by the
economically
Department

of  Transportation

socially

and

vii

viii

INTRODUCTION  AND  SUMMARY  OF ARGUMENT

The National  Association  of Minority  Businesses  (NAMB),
in support  of Respondent,  hereby  asserts  its position  with regard  to
the legal issues  presented  in this appeal.  Particularly,  NAMB  takes
issue  with the Petitioner's  position on  several  points.  First, NAMB
challenges  Petitioner's  argument  that  the federal  regulations  under
impose  an
examination
of  social
automatic
Second,  it is  NAMB's  position  that  maintaining
disadvantage.
diversity  in federal  goverment  contracting  and  promoting  use  of
historically  under-utilized  business  concerns  furthers  both  a
substantial  and a compelling  government  interest,  and otherwise  is
narrowly  tailored  to  satisfy  that  interest.
Finally,  although  the
interest  in maintaining  diversity  in federal  government  contracting
and the use  of historically  under-utilized  business  concems  satisfies
both  strict  and  intermediate  levels  of  constitutional  scrutiny,
intermediate  scrutiny  is the  appropriate  standard  of review.

presumption

I

In pursuit  of this  appeal,  Petitioner  claims  that  there  exists
a  presumption  of  social  disadvantage  under  the  Small  Business
Administration  and  Department  of Transportation  regulations,  and
that  that  presumption  is automatically  and  erroneously  made,  and
has caused  Petitioner  harm.  Petiticner’s  claim  is defective  in that
no such  presumption,  based  on the  language  of the cited
regulations,  13 C.F.R.  124.105(b)  and  48 C.F.R.  52.219-8,  can  be
reasonably  found.  Those  sections  read  together,  in pertinent  part,
mandate  the  satisfaction  of  an  express  condition  before  a
presumption  will  be made.

Significantly,  before  a minority  contractor  may  access  the
program,  such  minority  member  must  separately  qualify  for  the
program,  as  both  socially  and  economically  disadvantaged,  after
submitting  an  extensive  set  of  application  materials,  including
financial  materials  and certifications  to the effect  that they have  in
fact  been  disadvantaged.  13 C.F.R.  124.105(b)(2).

If  the  condition  is  not  satisfied  after  review  of  the
The

application  materials,  then  no  presumption  is  permitted.

operative  language  of  13  C.F.R.  124.105(b)  reveals  this  express
"In  the  absence  of  evidence  to  the
condition  as  follows:
contrary,  the  following  individuals  are  presumed  to  be  socially
disadvantaged."  (emphasis  added).  Clearly,  the  plain  meaning  of
this  express  language  imposes  a condition  that  if and only  if there
is no  evidence  showing  the  applicant  has  not  been  disadvantaged
in the  application  materials  or  elsewhere,  can  it then  be presumed
that that applicant  has in fact been disadvantaged  and gain  access
to the  program.  Stated  another  way,  if evidence  in the  application
materials  or  elsewhere  is presented  which  contradicts  a  claim  of
disadvantage,  then  the  minority  contractor  will  not  be  granted
access  to  the  program.
No  automatic  presumption  exists  as
Petitioner  claims.

In short,  after  submitting  application  materials  to evidence
a  claim
that  they  have  been  socially  and  economically
disadvantaged,  a  minority  contractor  may  only  then  satisfy  the
requisite  condition,  and  at  that  point  be  considered  adequately
disadvantaged  to  participate  in the  program.

il

Moreover,  maintaining  diversity  in  federal  government
contracting  furthers  both  a substantial  and  compelling  government
interest,  and  as  such  satisfies  the  requisite  legal  standard.  In fact,
the promotion  of racial diversity  in the area of broadcasting  has
already  been  established  as  furthering  an  important  governmental
interest.  See  Metro  Broadcasting,  Inc.  v.  F.C.C.,  497  U.S.  547,
110  S.Ct. 2997,  111  L.Ed.2d
445 (1990).  Logic and reason  dictate
that  the  Court  follow  Metro  and  apply  that  rationale  to the  area  of
government  contracting.

Likewise,  the race-based  contracting  programs  are  narrowly
tailored  to surpass  both the intermediate  and strict scrutiny  tests.
They additionally  cannot  be more  narrowly  tailored  than  at present
without  adversely  affecting  the  highly  important  governmental
interests  that  they  have  been  designed  to  further,  and  in  fact  do
further.

ARGUMENT

I.

A.

THE  PETITIONER’S
CLAIM  THAT  THERE  IS  A
PRESUMPTION  OF  SOCIAL  DISADVANTAGE  IS
MISLEADING

The  Personal  Eligibility  Statement  Required  Of  8(a)
Applicants  Demonstrates  That  No  Presumption  Of Social
Disadvantage  Exists

Petitioners  claim  that  "a member  of a listed  minority  group
obtains  DBE  [disadvantaged  business  enterprise]  status  merely  by
proving  that individual's  membership  in one  or more  of the
“presume[d]"  disadvantaged  minorities."?
In  support  of  this
contention,  Petitioners  rely on  13 C.F.R.  §124.105(b)  which  states:

"In  the  absence  of evidence  to  the  contrary,  the  following
individuals  are  presumed  to  be  socially  disadvantaged:
Black  Americans;  Hispanic  Americans;  Native  Americans
Native
Eskimos,
(American
Hawaiians)..."

Indians,

Aleuts,

or

Petitioners  further  rely  on  48  C.F.R.  §52.219-8,  which  states  in
relevant  part:

that

shall

socially

presume

Contractor

and
"The
economically  disadvantaged  individuals  include  Black
Americans,  Hispanic  Americans,  Native Americans, Asian-
Pacific  Americans,  Subcontinent  Asian  Americans,  and
other  minorities,  or any  other  individual  found  to be
disadvantaged  by the  Administration  pursuant  to  section
8(a)  of the  Small  Business  Act.”

*Petitioner's  brief  at p. 23.

Petitioner's  contention  that  this  presumption  allows
members  of the  enumerated  minority  groups  to  obtain  DBE  status
merely  by  virtue  of  showing  membership  in  such  groups  is not
correct.  In order to obtain  DBE status,  one must  do much  more
than  simply  claim  membership  in an  enumerated  minority  group,
and  there  is  no  presumption  of  DBE  status  based  merely  upon
membership  in one  of the  enumerated  minority  groups.

Petitioner  misconstrues  the process  required  to obtain  DBE
status.  The  regulations  relied  upon  by the  Petitioner  do not  fully
illuminate  the  “social  disadvantage”  issue.
A  prospective
applicant  must  certify  under  penalty  of  law  that  he  or  she  is
socially  disadvantaged.  It is the  meaning  of the  certification  that
is important.

To  certify  that  one  is  socially  disadvantaged,  one  is
alleging  under  penalty  of  law  that  they  have  been  subjected  to
racial  or  ethnic  prejudice  or  cultural  bias  because  of  their
identification  [by others]  as  members  of groups  without  respect  to
their  individual  qualities.’  Simply  to be a member  of a particular
racial  or  ethnic  group  does  not  make  one  eligible  for  "social
disadvantage”  status.  The  “presumption”  discussed  in  13  C.F.R.
124.105(b)  and relied  upon  by Petitioner  is one  of post-certification
eligibility  for  socially  disadvantaged  status,  not  final  DBE
eligibility  or  status  as  socially  disadvantaged.

To allege that one  is socially  disadvantaged  is to swear  and
aver  to the fact that one  has personally  experienced  racial  or ethnic
prejudice  and/or  cultural  bias.
The  regulation  relied  upon  by
Petitioner  merely  illustrates  the  respect  given  to  an  applicant's
certification  that  he  or  she  is socially  disadvantaged.  It is not  a
determination  of DBE  status  or  “social  disadvantage”  status.

Petitioner's  reliance  on  48 C.F.R.  52.219-8  is misplaced  in
that  this  regulation  does  not  discuss  eligibility
for  social
disadvantage  status.  Rather,  it speaks  to the respect  that must be

given  by  private  contractors  to  the  federal  certification  and
eligibility  process.  In stating  that the contractor  shall  presume  that
certain  groups  of individuals  are  socially  disadvantaged,  including
those  certified  by  the  administration  and  the  Small  Business
Administration’s  8(a)  program,  the  regulation  merely  denies  the
private  contractor  the  ability  to  unilaterally  bar  socially  and
economically  disadvantaged  individuals  from  participation  in the
Additionally,  it directs
programs  established  for  their  benefit.
private  contractors  to respect  the policing  process  already
established  under  the  regulations  promulgated  by the  Department
of Transportation  and  the  Small  Business  Administration.‘  The
subject  regulation  makes  clear  that  private  contractors  have  no
authority  to upset the procedural  process  set forth in the regulations
and that they  must respect  the certification  process.

13  C.F.R.  124.105(b)  begins  with  the  phrase  “In  the
absence  of evidence  to  the  contrary..."  Such  language  is at  odds
with  the  notion  that  a presumption  of DBE  status  exists  by virtue
of membership  in an  enumerated  minority  group  without  more  and
Petitioner’s  assertions  as  such  are  false.  The  language  of  this
statute  clearly  requires  more than  mere  membership  in the minority
group.  The  statute  does  not  read  “The  following  individuals  are
presumed  to  be  socially  disadvantaged..."
Because  the  statute
requires  more, it cannot  be said that a  presumption  of DBE status
exists  by virtue  of membership  in one of the enumerated  minority
groups.
requires  respect  for  an  individual's
certification  of social  disadvantage.  The  statutory  language  cited
by the Petitioner  is taken  out of context  and cannot  be read without
including  other  sections  which  effectively  set  forth  the  DBE
certification  process.  The  certification  process  not  only  includes
a  finding  of  social  disadvantage,  but,  additionally,  economic
disadvantage  must  naturally  flow  therefrom.
Moreover,  other
elements  such  as  business  size,  revenue,  and  duration  of time  in

The  statute

713 CFR.  124.105(a).

“49 C.F.R.  23.69  and  13 C.F.R.  124.101,  et.  seq.

5

business  are  determining  factors.’

In  order  to  obtain  eligibility  under  § 8(a)  of  the  Small
Business  Act, an  individual  or concern  applicant  must  complete  an
8(a)  Personal  Eligibility  Statement.
The  first  part  of  this
application  requests  information  related  to  social  disadvantage.
Question  2 in this  section  asks  an  applicant  why they are  claiming
social  disadvantage.  This question  allows  an  applicant  to state  that
they are  claiming  social  disadvantage  because  of their identification
[by others]  as  a member  of one  of the  minority  groups  enumerated
in  13  C.F.R.  124.105(b).
This  does  not  depend  simply  upon
membership  in a minority  group,  but hinges  on  the  fact  that  others
have  identified  the  applicant  as  a member  of a minority  group  and
have  discriminated  against  the  applicant  because  of this  fact.  13
C.F.R.  124.105(a)  states:

"Socially  disadvantaged  individuals  are  those  who  have
been  subjected  to racial  or ethnic  prejudice  or cultural  bias
because  of their  identities  as  members  of groups  without
regard
social
disadvantage  must  stem  from  circumstances  beyond  their
control.”

qualities.  | The

individual

their

to

When  an  applicant  claims  social  disadvantage  under  question  two
of the  Personal  Eligibility  Statement,  they  are  certifying  that  they
have  experienced  racial  or  cultural  bias  as  an  individual.  Being  a
to obtain
of a racial  minority  group alone  will not suffice
member
eligibility.  A person  who has not experienced  specific  instances  of
racial,  ethnic  or  cultural  discrimination  or  bias  would  not  qualify
as  socially  disadvantaged  regardless  of their  race  or  ethnicity.

The  Personal  Eligibility  Statement  asks  applicants  to
further  enumerate  the types of prejudice  they have  suffered
and to
certify  that  such  prejudice  has  impaired  the  applicant's  ability  to

*See  generally  13 C.F.R.  §§124.101  -  124.109.

6

a

—

compete  in  the free  enterprise  system  due to the applicant's
diminished  capital  and  credit  opportunities  as  compared  to  others
in the same business  area who are not socially  disadvantaged.  This
detailed  inquiry and certification  process determines  an individual's
ability  to  participate  in  the  program,  and  to  be  certified  socially
and economically  disadvantaged.  It  is not enough
that  an
individual  merely  be  a  member  of a minority  group.  One  must
certify  that  they have experienced  personal  discrimination  and  bias
and that as a result  of that discrimination  and bias, they have  been
denied  access  to credit  and capital.  This is hardly  a presumption.
If an applicant  responds  affirmatively  that he or she has
been denied  access  to credit  and capital  because  of discriminatory
practices,  the  applicant  is required  to  document  such  impairment
with  specific  examples.  Moreover,  each  applicant  must  sign  and
certify,  under  penalty  of ten years  imprisonment  or  fine  of up  to
$500,000,  that  all  information  in  the  application  is  true  and
complete.  Socially  disadvantaged  status,  in other  words,  must  be
certified  and  proved.  This  extensive,  in depth,  application  process
evidences  that  there  is  no  presumption  of  DBE  status  merely
because  an  individual  is  a  member  of  an  enumerated  minority
group.

This  language  indicates  that  merely  because  an  appiicant  says  he

An applicant  may be required  to prove these allegations.  If one  is
required  to prove their status,  there can be no presumption  of
status.  Furthermore,
if one is required to certify  under penalty  of

presumption  of  socially  disadvantaged  status.
A  presumption
means  there  is nothing  further  to prove.

C.

Both  Small  Business  Administration  And  Department  of
Transportation  Regulations  Demonstrate  That  Specific
Proof  Of Social  Disadvantage  Is Required

*13 C.F.R.  124.106(a)iXii).

10

there

First,

The Petitioner  argues that only the specified  racial and
ethnic  classes  of persons  can be socially disadvantaged.  Petitioner
argues further  that only the specified  racial minorities  are subject
to a presumption
of disadvantaged  status.  These  arguments  are
misleading.
is  no  presumption  of  socially
disadvantaged  status.  Social disadvantage
must be proven,  and
must  be proven  on an individual  case by case basis.  Furthermore,
Petitioner's  assertion flies in the face of the plain language  of the
regulations  they attempt  to construe  with respect  to the subject
procurement  eligibility.  DBE  status  in  the  subject  procurement
program  is governed  by either  one of two  groups  of federal
regulations.  The first group  of regulations  that  determines
eligibility  for DBE status  is the Small  Business  Act's  8(a) program
eligibility  requirements.''  The  second  set  of federal  regulations
are the regulations  promulgated  by the Secretary  of the Department
of  Transportation."*
An  individual  or  concern  applicant  may
obtain  socially  disadvantaged  status  under  either set of federal
regulations  for purposes of participation in the subject procurement.
As  explained  previously,  under  the  8(a)  eligibility
requirements,
social disadvantage  is not limited
to members  of the
enumerated  racial  or ethnic  groups.  Eligibility  for the program  is
not foreclosed  to anyone,  provided  they meet the enumerated
criteria.'’  Not only is the program  open to members  of all racial
or ethnic  groups,  but moreover,  there is an appeal  procedure  for
added review should an applicant
be denied  eligibility
to participate

"13 C.F.R.  124.101-109.

"49 CFR.  23.51-69.

"13 C.F.R.  124.105(a)  states the general eligibility as follows:  “Socially
disadvantaged  individuals  are those who have been subjected to racial or ethnic
Prejudice  or cultural bias because  of their identification  as members  of groups
without regard  to their individual  qualities.”

11

in the  program."*  Nothing  in the regulations  stops  Petitioner  from
applying  for  8(a)  program  eligibility  and  proving  their  status  for
participation,  even  if Petitioner  is  not  a member  of one  of  the
enumerated  minority  groups.

Petitioner  mischaracterizes,  misstates  and  misleads  the
Court  with  respect  to  their  assertion  that  the  “enumerated”  racial
minorities  alone  are  eligible  for  socially  disadvantaged  status  for
the  purposes  of the subject  procurement  program.  The  regulations
promulgated  by the Office  of the Secretary  of Transportation  make
it clear  that anyone  who can  demonstrate  that they have  personally
suffered  social  disadvantage,  stemming  from  his  color,  national
origin,  gender,  physical  handicap  or  other  cause  beyond  that
individual's  control,  may  be eligible.'*  Although  racial  minorities
have  been  certified,  the  process  is not  closed  to  anyone  who  can
meet  the  eligibility  criteria.

No presumption  of socially  disadvantaged  status  can  exist

For  Department  of  Transportation  program
purposes,  there  is  no  presumption  of  eligibility.
Rather,  the

Even  that
presumption  may  be rebutted  by the  recipient  [state].  49  C.F.R.
§23.53(a)(1)  merely  allocates  the  burden  of proof to the  recipient
[state]  after  an  applicant  has  certified  to  socially  disadvantaged
status.  The  challenge  procedures  clearly  state  that  individual
instances  of bias and discrimination  must  be shown  in order to
obtain  DBE  status,  and  more  particularly,  socially  disadvantaged
status.  An  “individual  must  demonstrate  that  he or she has
personally
social  disadvantage,  not  merely  claim
membership  in a non-designated  group  which  could  be considered
socially  disadvantaged.""’  This  can  be  achieved  by setting  forth
with  particularity  the  instances  of  discrimination  to  which  the
applicant has been subjected. These  procedures  establish that there
is  NO presumption  of eligibility  and that  such  status  must  be
certified, and that certification  may be challenged.  The Department
of Transportation  DBE certification
process differs from that of the
Small  Business  Administration’s  8(a)  program  in  that  once
certification  by the  prospective  participant  [DBE]  to the  recipient
occurs,  under Department  of Transportation  regulation,  it is not
final.  It may be challenged  and overturned  by the recipient  of the
subject highway  funds,  and  must  be proved  again  to the  recipient
in accordance  with the challenge  procedures  set forth in 49 C.F.R.
23.69.

These  regulations  are  clear  in  that  there  is  no  presumption  of
-ligibility  for  socially  disadvantaged  status  simply  because  an
applicant  is an  “enumerated”  racial  minority.

A recipient  [state]  may not challenge  8(a) certification;  that
program  is regulated  solely  by the  Small  Business  Administration.
The  Federal  Department  of Transportation  regulations  direct  the
State  to  recognize  a  DBE’s  certification  of  social  disadvantage
under  49  C.F.R.  23.62.  Once  the  prospective  DBE  certifies  that
the  applicant  is  socially  disadvantaged  under  the  Department  of
Transportation  regulations,  that  certification,  not  merely  the

membership  of an  applicant  in  one  of the enumerated  minority
groups,  is  the act which creates a  rebuttable presumption  that the
certification  is proper.  This rebuttable  presumption  is discussed  at
49 C.F.R.  23.53  and  23.62.  A presumption  of social  disadvantage
is distinguishable  from  a  rebuttable  presumption  that  a  DBE’s
Department of Transportation  certification  of social  disadvantage
iS  proper.  The respect given to Department of Transportation
certification  is what creates  the presumption.  Moreover,  unlike  the
Small  Business  Administration  certification,  Department  of
Transportation  allows  policing  of its program  certification  by the
state.  Small  Business  Administration  has  its own  internal  appeals
and  enforcement  procedures.

|

There  is no  presumption  of social  disadvantage  simply  by
an  individual's  membership  in a racial  or  ethnic  minority  group.
Socially  disadvantaged  status  must  be  proved  and  certified
individually  on  a case  by case  basis  by those  individuals  who  seek
this  status.
Insufficient  evidence  of this  status  will  result  in  an
individual  not  being  deemed  socially  disadvantaged  under  either
group  of  federal  regulations.  The  only  presumption  created  in
either  of these  groups  of federal  regulations  is one  of accuracy
once  an  individual  seeking  socially  disadvantaged  status  under
either  group  of  regulations  so  certifies.
Meaning,  once  an
individual  certifies  socially  disadvantaged  status  to Department  of
Transportation  and that ce:tification  is acknowledged  with  respect
to  program  eligibility,  the  presumption  arises  that  the  information
relied  upon  in acknowledging  such  certification  is accurate.  The
recipient  is able  to use  this presumption  in determining  whether  it,
too, will grant certification.  There can be no presumption  of social
disadvantage  when an  individual  must  certify and prove  those  facts
which  would  create  eligibility.  A Presumption  does  not  require
proof.

A.

THE  SUBJECT  FEDERAL  CONTRACTING
PROGRAMS  ARE  CONSTITUTIONAL  AND  PASS
BOTH  INTERMEDIATE  AND  STRICT  SCRUTINY
ANALYSIS

Diversity  In Federal  Contracting  And  Business  Utilization
Is  Both  A  Substantial  And  Compelling  Governmental
Interest

In  Wygant  v.  Jackson  Board  of Education,  476  U.S.  267,
106  S.Ct.  1842, 90 L.Ed.2d  260 (1986),  this Court  determined  that
a  state  agency  seeking  to  impose  a  race-based  layoff  provision
rust  justify  that  action  by showing  such  action  is necessary  to  a
compelling  state  interest.  More  specificaily,  the  Court  stated  that
to  satisfy  this  heightened  level  of scrutiny,  the  proponent  of the
race-based  provision  must  meet  a two-pronged  test.  First,  it must
be  shown  that  the  provision  is  justified  by  a  compelling
governmental  interest."  The  second  prong  of  the  test  requires
that  the  challenged  provisions  have  been  narrowly  tailored  to
achieve  the  compelling  government  interest.'®

The  Federal  Construction  Procurement  Program  was
created  for  the  purpose  of  including  historically  under-utilized
businesses  in  government  contracts.”
Such  a  governmental

"Wygant  v.  Jackson  Board  of Education,  476  U.S.  267,  274.  citing

Palmore  v.  Sidoti,  466  U.S.  429,  432  (1984).

"Fullilove  v.  Klutznick,  448  U.S.  448,  480,  100  S.Ci.  2758,  65  L.Ed.2d

902  (1980).

15  U.S.C.  631  (e)2)  states:

[15  U.S.C.  §637(a)]  to  -

“It  is, therefore,  the  purpose  of section  8(a)

(A)  promote  the business  development  of small business  concerns
owned  and  controlled  by socially  and  economically  disadvantaged
individuals  so that such concerns  can compete  on an equal basis in
the American  economy;
(B) promote

the competitive  viability

of such concerns  in the

16

concern  is compelling.  It is at least as compelling as the purpose
of promoting  racial  diversity.  In University  of California  Regents
v.  Bakke,  438  U.S.  265,  311-315,  98 S.Ct.  2733,  57 L.Ed.2d  750
(1978),  this Court  found  racial  diversity  in higher education  to be
a compelling  government interest.  Simply because  this Court has
not  articulated  that  the  inclusion  of historically  under-represented
businesses  in government  contracting  is a compelling  government
interest does not  foreclose  that possibility.”’  Moreover,  Petitioner,
in  its brief, has conceded that this Court in the past has found s
compelling  governmental  interest exists where the purpose of a
challenged act is to remedy past discrimination.  At the very least,
the  articulated  purposes  of the  Federal  Construction  Procurement
Program  are  intended  to do just that.
In  establishing  a  compelling  governmental  interest  it  is
unnecessary  to identify  the specific  instances  of discrimination  to
be remedied.  In Justice  O’Connor’s  concurring  opinion  in Wygant,

marketplace by providing such available contract,  financial. technical,
and management assistance as may be necessary: and
(C) clarify and expand the program for the procurement by the
United States of articles, supplies, services, Materials,  and

"Wygant,  supra,  at 286 (O'Connor,  J., concurring).

715  U.S.C.  §631(e\1).

17

as  well  as  diversity  among  competing  concerns  in  the  national
economy.  These  findings  demonstrate  the basis  for the firm  belief
of Congress  that  remedial  action  was  required.  Every  participant
in the  program,  whether  certified  by  the  Department  of
Transportation  or  Small  Business  Administration  has certified  and
proven  they  have  individually  experienced  acts  of discrimination
and/or  bias,  and  that  these  acts  have  affected  their  ability  to act  in
the  business  world.  This  is more  than  mere  past discrimination,  it
is evidence  of present  and  ongoing  discrimination  and  bias.

In order  to withstand  constitutional  scrutiny  under  an
intermediate  standard,  it must  be shown  that  the  challenged  act  is
important  to  a substantial  governmental  interest.”  In light of the
atmosphere  of  ongoing  discrimination  and  bias,  the  interest  of
diversity  in  federal  contracting  is  more  than  important,  it  is
This  Court  addressed  the  issue  of  local  action
compelling.
adopting  minority  set-aside  programs  in  City  of Richmond  v.
Croson,  488  U.S.  469,  109  S.Ct.  706,  102  L.Ed.2d  854  (1989).
There  this  Court  held  that  the  appropriate  level  of scrutiny  to  be
applied  was  strict  scrutiny,  requiring  the  local  action  to  be
necessary  to  a  compelling  governmental  interest.
However,  in
Fullilove,  the  Court  declined  to  apply  the  strict  scrutiny  standard
to  a federal  public  works  act.  Finally,  in Metro  Broadcasting,  the
Court  confirmed  its holdings  in the prior decisions  and opined  that
Croson  is not  the appropriate  standard of review  for Congressional
acts.  Today,  Metro  Broadcasting  controls  the  level  of scrutiny  to
be  applied  to  the  federal  program  being  challenged.

The Petitioner  seems  to infer that if  Congress  deals with a

suspect  class  that  Congress  cannot  have  a  level  of review  lower
than strict scrutiny.  It is  well settled  that  Congress  has a  lower
of review  for its dealings  with suspect  classes  than do the
standard
States.  In Nyquist  v.  Mauclet,  432  U.S.  1, 53 L.Ed.2d  63 (1977),

“Metro  Broadcasting,  Inc.  v.  F .C.C.,  497  U.S.  $47,  110  S.Ct.  2997,  111
L.Ed.  2d 445  (1990),  Fullilove  v.  Klutznick,  448  U.S.  448,  100  S.Ct.  2758,  65
L.Ed.2d  902  (1980).

this Court ruled that in dealing with alienage, a  suspect class, the
Program  initiated by the state of New York was to be reviewed

Nyquist  Court  noted  that  in Matthews  vy.  Diaz,  426  U.S.  67,  96
S.Ct.  1883, 48 L.Ed.2d  478  (1976),  decided  the previous  year,  the
Court applied a relaxed scrutiny and upheld a federal statute which
based eligibility  for federal medical insurance  of aliens upon a
durational residency requirement.  The Court stated in relevant
part,  --Congress, as an aspect of its broad  power  over immigration
and  naturalization,  enjoys  rights  to distinguish  among  aliens  that
are not shared by the States."*  It is  not an aberration
that acts of
Congress are treated differently than acts of the States.  Thus, the
difference  between  Fullilove  vy.  Klutznick,  Metro  Broadcasting  v.
F.C.C.,  and  City  of Richmond  vy.  Croson  are  well  rooted  in
precedent established  by this Court.  In the case at bar,  Congress
enjoys  broad  powers  over  spending  and  commerce,  and  should  be
afforded  the  same  relaxed  scrutiny  as  in Matthews  v.  Diaz.
'
In  Metro  Broadcasting,  this  Court  found  that  racial
diversity  is  an  important  governmental  objective.
Like  the
minority businesses  in Metro  Broadcasting,  the minority  business
in the instant case is in competition with non-minority  businesses
which  have  historically  dominated  the  industry.  The  articulated
purposes  of  the  Federal  Construction  Procurement  Program  of
encouraging
full participation  by minorities in our free enterprise
system to reach a level of economic equality  and encouraging the
development  of small business concems are consistent with the
government  interest  of  racial  diversity  articulated  in  Metro
Broadcasting.
Racial  diversity  in  broadcasting  and  higher

“Matthews  v.  Diaz,  426  U.S.  67, 84-87.

19

education  are  no  less  important  than  racial  diversity  in industry.”
If  this  type  of  governmental  interest  is  important  enough  to
withstand  constitutional  muster  in the  broadcast  arena,  it is surely
sufficient  in  this  instance  as  well.
Racial  diversity  is  no  less
important  here.  That  Congress  specifically  articulated  its purpose
in enacting  the Federal Construction  Procurement  Program  as goals
of full  participation  and economic  equality  is synonymous  with  the
purposes  of racial  diversity.
Diversity  in  federal  contracting  is
both  a  substantial  and a  compelling  governmental  interest  and
survives  both  strict  and  intermediate  level  constitutional  scrutiny.

B.

The  Subject  Federal  Contracting  Programs  Are  Narrowly
Tailored  And  Pass
Intermediate  And  Strict
Constitutional  Scrutiny

Both

The second  prong  of an  intermediate  level  scrutiny  analysis
requires  the  government  action  to  be  substantially  related  to  the
achievement  of  its  important  governmental  interest.
In  deciding
this  factual  question,  the  Court  has  recognized  that  agency  and
Congressional
warrant
The
Congressional  findings  in  this  regard,  through  the  certification

great  weight.

fact-finding

thal racial diversity in higher education is such a  governmental  interest upon

*In Metro Broadcasting  the Court analyzed the deference  that should be

given to Congress  and the FCC in determining  whether minority  ownership

give “great weight to the decisions  of Congress and the experience  of the
Commission”,

have

which

individual's

government  contracts;  discrimination  by potential  clients;  exclusion

factors
business
development.”  All  of these  elements  of social  disadvantage  are
important to a substantial government interest, as well as necessary
to a  compelling  government  interest,  and are related  to the
enumerated  Congressional  purpose  of achieving  full  participation,
economic  equality, as well as racial diversity  in federal  contracting.
In order for a  challenged act to be narrowly  tailored  under
the strict scrutiny  standard,  the inquiry is  whether  a less restrictive
means could have been used to effectuate the purposes  of the
challenged act.” In tailoring an action so as to effectuate it in the
least restrictive way,  this Court has recognized  that it may
sometimes be necessary  to have innocent  persons bear some of the
burden.”  However,  in fashioning
a remedial  program  that affects
innocent persons, the means chosen should not be such that they
impose a  “disproportionate  harm on the interests, or unnecessarily
trammel  the  rights  of innocent  individuals  directly  and  adversely

715  U.S.C. §631 (eX!) sets forth the findings of Congress with respect to

the purposes of the development of the 8(a) program.

*49 C.F.R.  23.69,  Appendix  C, (A)2)iii).

*Wygant,  at  280 (footnote  6).

"id. at 280-281.

21

affected  by  a  plan's  racial  preference.”*'
It  is  permissible  to
burden  innocent  parties  in  a manner  which  does  not  unnecessarily
trammel  the interests  and rights of such parties,  the effect of which
is to  merely  foreclose  one  of many  options  of these  parties.

The  Federal  Construction  Procurement  Program,  while  it
does  affect  innocent  parties,  does not do so in an impermissible
manner.
The  program  gives  opportunities  to  socially  and
economically  disadvantaged,  as  well  as  historically  under-utilized,
It  does  not  directly  take  away  anything,  unduly
businesses.
burden,
or traverse  upon the rights  of innocent  parties.  In this  way
the Federal  Construction  Procurement  Program  can be analogized
to  remedial  hiring  goals.  This  Court  in  Wygant,  at  pp.  282-283,
has  stated,  “denial  of a  future  employment  opportunity  is not  as
intrusive  as  loss  of an  existing  job.”  In the  case  at  bar,  Petitioner
of receiving  the
has not shown  that  it  had any firm  expectation
subcontract.  Petitioner  was  one  of many  who  submitted  a bid  for
the  project,  uncertain  of whether  it would  be  hired.  Petitioner's
expectation  of being  hired  was  no  greater  than  that  of any  other
bidder.  Moreover,  Petitioner  was  not  deprived  of anything.  The
Federal  Construction  Procurement  Program  is narrowly  tailored  to
the  compelling  governmental  interest  of promoting  utilization  of
historically  under-utilized  business  concerns  and  encouraging
diversity  in the  competitive  market  for government  contracts.  As
the  decision  to  award  the  subcontract  to  Respondent  was a hiring
decision,  it may  be considered a less  intrusive  means  of achieving
the governmental  interest
at stake.  The  burden  of the Federal
Construction  Procurement  Program  is  sufficiently  distributed
among  a large population, all subcontract
bidders,  so as to make  its
methods  less  intrusive.

The Federal  Construction  Procurement  Program  is narrowly
tailored
in another  respect.  The program  is open to all socially  and
economically  disadvantaged  business  concerns  that are  able to mee!

Id.  at  287  (O'Connor,  J., concurring).

22

the program's  strict  eligibility  criteria.  13 C.F.R.  Ch.  | §$124.101
(a) states that in order to obtain eligibility to participate in the
program,  applicants  must  meet  all  the  eligibility  criteria”.
13
C.F.R.  Ch.  1  $124,101  (b)  goes  on  to  state  that  to  maintain
eligibility,  the same criteria  must continue  to be met.  In short, to
be eligible for the Federal  Construction  Procurement  Program, an
applicant  must qualify  as a small business concern,  the applicant
concern  must be at least  51%  owned  by a  U.S. citizen(s)  who
qualifies  as  socially  and economically  disadvantaged,  the concern
must be managed  and controlled  by socially  and economically
disadvantaged  individuals,  the concer  must  have  been  in business
in its industry  classification  for a minimum  nun der of years  (2),
the  concern  may  be  subject  to  individual  character  review,  and
certain types of businesses are per se excluded from the program.
13 C.F.R.  Ch.  1 §§124.102-124.109.

Eligibility

in  the  Federal  Construction  Procurement
Program  requires  much  more  than  simply  qualifying  as  a socially
and  economically  disadvantaged  business  concern.  The  socially
and  economically  disadvantaged  criteria  is further  divided.  Once
2 concern acquires  socially disadvantaged  Status, that status merely
qualifies  the  concern  under  one  of many  eligibility  requirements.
A showing  of social disadvantage  does not alleviate the need to
show economic  disadvantage,  among other requirements.  The
Federal  Construction  Procurement  Program  requirements  for
eligibility  are extensive and clearly show that Congress, in enacting
the program,  narrowly tailored  it  to comport  with the specific
purposes  of the act and compelling  governmental  interests.  The
Federal  Construction  Procurement  Program  is  not  broad  or
overreaching,  but  is narrowly  tailored  since  those  concerns  which
become  certified  under  Federal  Construction  Procurement  must
show  with particularity  their social and economic  disadvantage  and
must  swear  under  oath  to that  fact.

Part 23, Subpart D, Appendix C.

“see also Office of the Secretary of Transportation  regulations at 49  CFR
=

23

Moreover,  the  Federal  Construction  Procurement  Program
is limited  in extent  and duration.  13 C.F.R.  Ch.  1 §124.101(b)  sets
forth  the  requirements  for continuing  eligibility.  Those  provisions
require  concems  to  continue  meeting  the  initial
eligibility
requirements,  and  further  set  forth  procedures  by  which  to
terminate  a concern's  eligibility  to participate  in the program.  This
ensures  that once  certified,  a concern  may  not  take  advantage  of its
participation  in the  program  indefinitely.  The  act  goes  so  far as  to
encourage  private  policing  of its provisions  by the  general  public
to  ensure  that  individuals  or  concerns  which  are  not  eligible  will
13 C.F.R.  Ch.  1 §124.101(c).
be  so  identified.

In sum,  the  Federal  Construction  Procurement  Program  is
sufficient  to  withstand  constitutional  muster  under  the  strict
scrutiny  standard.  The  program  serves  a compelling  governmental
interest  and  is narrowly  tailored  to  achieve  those  interests.

N

SION
The  federal  contracting  program  in  question  is narrowly
tailored  to  achieve  a compelling  government  interest.  There  is no
presumption  of  social  disadvantage.
It  is  not  overinclusive,
overbroad,  or overreaching.  It does  not  go on  in perpetuity  and  its
duration  is sufficiently  limited.  The  proper  standard  of review  of
this  program  is intermediate  or  relaxed  scrutiny.  It need  only  be
demonstrated  that  the  program  in question  is substantially  related
to  an  important  government  interest.
Nevertheless,  the  subject
program  also  passes a strict  scrutiny  analysis  as  well,  since  it is
necessary  to  a  compelling  state  interest  and  narrowly  tailored  to
achieve  that  interest.

24

INTEREST  OF  THE  AMICI  CURIAE

Amici  states  implement  many  federal  programs
enacted  by  Congress  to  remedy  racial  discrimination,
including  the  highway  contracting  minority  incentives
program  challenged  by petitioner  in this case.  Since this
Court’s  decision  in  Fullilove  v.  Klutznick,  448  U.S.  448
(1980),  challenges  to such  Congressionally-enacted  remedial
programs  have  generally  been reviewed  by the lower  courts
under  a  standard  lower  than  the  strict  stutiny  which
petitioner  demands.  A ruling  by this  Court  imposing  strict
scrutiny  on  legislation  enacted  by Congress  to remedy  racial
discrimination  would  call  into  question  the  continuing
validity  of these  programs.

In addition,  many  states  have  enacted  race-conscious
Like  the  incentive
programs  to  remedy  discrimination.
program
at issue here, state remedial  programs  are based on
legislative  findings  concerning  problems  and  determinations
of  the  best  solutions.
Amici  believe  those  legislative
findings  should  be accorded  deference,  not  second-guessed
by courts.  Thus,  the constitutionality  of key state  programs
may be affected  by the standards  established  with respect  to
the power  of  Congress  to act in this  case.  See City  of
Richmond  v. J.A.  Croson  Co., 488 U.S.  469, 491-92  (1989).

Amici  believe  that the decision  of the court  of appeals
is correct,  and  submit  this  brief  to  assist  the  Court  in  its
resolution  of the  case.

SUMMARY  OF  ARGUMENT

This  case  involves  a Congressionally-mandated  race-
conscious  remedy  to  counter  the  continuing  effects  of past
racial  discrimination  in  federal  procurement  contracting.

2

3

Thus,  it does  not  present  the  specific  issue  decided  by this
Court’s  landmark  opinion  15  years  ago  in  Fullilove  v.
Klutznick,  448  U.S.  448  (1980):  Congressional  legislation
requiring  states  to  implement  a  race-conscious  remedy  for
the  continuing  effects  of past  discrimination  by states  is not
Nor  does  this  case  involve  the
subject  to  strict  scrutiny.
central  proposition  of this  Court’s  most  recent  “affirmative
action”  ruling  in  Metro  Broadcasting  v.  FCC,  110  S.Ct.
2997  (1990):  Race-conscious  federal  legislation  may  be
justified  by interests  that  are  not  remedial.

This case  nonetheless  is governed  by the  fundamental
tenet
that  guided  Fullilove  and  Metro  Broadcasting:
Congress,  as the national  legislature,  specifically  empowered
by  the  Reconstruction  Amendments  to  enforce  those
guarantees  of  racial  equality,  is  to  be  accorded  particular
deference  by  this  Court  when  Congress  enacts  legislation
designed  to  remove  the  present  results  of  past  racial
discrimination.  That  deference  was  constitutionally  correct
in  1980  and  1990  and  remains  so  today.

Even  if  this  Court  were  now  to  abandon  proper
deference  to Congress  in this area  and,  for the  first  time,  to
require  that  Congress  provide  a  “compelling  state  interest”
and adopt "narrowly  tailored”  race-conscious  measures,  basic
separation  of  powers  principles,  and  the  specific  powers
granted  to Congress  under  the Reconstruction  Amendments,
bar  the  Court  from  requiring  of Congress  the  same  type  of
factfinding  necessary  to  support  the  imposition  of a  race-
conscious  remedy  by  a  lower  federal  court  or  a  state
legislature.
Instead,  respect  for  Congress’  special  role  in
this  area  requires  greater  judicial  deference  to  Congress’

factfinding  in determining  the  need  for,  and  the  scope  of, a
race-conscious  remedy.

As discussed  below,  that  result  is strongly  supported
by  this  Court’s  seminal  decisions  reviewing  Congress’
actions  under  section  5  of  the  Fourteenth  Amendment.
Those  decisions  recognize  Congress’  substantive  power  to
identify  equal  protection  violations  as  well  as  its  broad
remedial  power  to address  them.  Moreover,  where,  as here,
the challenged  legislation  provides  a race-conscious  remedy
for past racial  discrimination  by the federal  government,  the
Thirteenth  Amendment  provides  a specific  textual  source  of
Congressional  power  to  identify  “badges  and  incidents  of
slavery”  and to address  them  with  remedial  power  equivalent
to  that  under  section  5 of the  Fourteenth  Amendment.

Deference  to  Congress’  factfinding  in  this  context
also  reflects  basic  constitutional  notions  of  the  tripartite
structure  of  the  federal  government.
The  institutional
characteristics  of  Congress  grant  that  branch  factfinding
capabilities  that  complement  or  improve  on  those  available
to
to  the  courts,  and  that  are
determinations
racial
discrimination  and  the  appropriate  remedy.

government’s

particularly

relevant

federal

the

of

This  Court  should  not  brush  aside  the  holding  of
Fullilove  and  the long-standing  principles  on  which  that case
rests  regarding  the  Constitutional  allocation  of  power
between  Congress  and the Court  to protect  the rights  of
For  the  reasons  set  out
"discrete  and  insular  minorities."
below,  amici  urge  the  Court  to  affirm  the  Tenth  Circuit’s
ruling  in this  case.

4

ARGUMENT

I.

Deference

The  Reconstruction  Amendments  Require
Congress’
Judicial
Determination  of the  Need  for  Legislation
to Remedy  Racial  Discrimination  by Private
Parties  or  the  Federal  Government  as  well
as  the  States.

to

A.

Fullilove  and  Metro  Broadcasting  Reject
Strict  Scrutiny  of Race-Conscious  Remedial
Legislation  Enacted  by Congress.

This  Court’s  decision  in  Fullilove  v.  Klutznick,  448
U.S.  448  (1980),  stands  for  the  proposition  that  strict
scrutiny,  as  defined  by petitioner,  is too  intrusive  a standard
for judicial  review  of an  act  of Congress  requiring  states  to
set  aside  a  percentage  of  federal  construction  funds  for
minority
racial
discrimination.
Metro  Broadcasting  v.  FCC,  110  S.Ct.
2997,  3008  (1990)  ("A majority  of the Court  in Fullilove  did
not  apply  strict  scrutiny  to  the  race-based  classification  at
issue.");  id.  at  3032  (O'Connor,  J., dissenting)  ("the  Court
correctly  observes  that  a  majority  did  not  apply  strict
scrutiny....").

a  remedy

businesses

past

for

as

The  principal  Fullilove  opinion,  written  by  Chief
Justice  Burger  and  joined  by Justices  White  and  Powell,
“recognize[s]  the  need  for  careful  judicial  evaluation  to
assure  that any congressional  program  that employs  racial  or
ethnic  criteria  to accomplish  the  objective  of remedying  the
present  effects  of past discrimination  is narrowly  tailored  to
the  achievement  of that  goal."  Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at 480.

5

Although  the Chief Justice  noted  that  the remedial  legislation
at  issue  in Fullilove  would  pass  even  strict  scrutiny,  id.  at
492,  his  test  does  not  limit  potential  governmental  interests
to  “compelling”  ones,  nor,  in finding  that  the  set-aside  was
"narrowly
tailored,”  does  his  opinion  impose  a  "least
restrictive  alternative”  test.  See id.  at 479-90;  compare  City
of Richmond  v.  J.A.  Croson  Co.  488  U.S.  469,  507-08
(1989)  (reviewing  city’s  minority  set-aside  under  a  least
restrictive  alternative  standard).

Justice  Marshall’s  opinion, joined by Justices  Brennan
and  Blackmun,  imposes  a standard  of review  that  is clearly
more  deferential  than  that  of Chief  Justice  Burger:  "[T]he
proper  inquiry  is whether  racial  classifications  designed  to
further  remedial  purposes  serve  important  governmental
objectives  and  are  substantially  related  to  achievement  of
those  objectives.”  Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at 519  (Marshall,  J.,
Moreover,  Justice  Marshall
concurring  in  the  judgment).
went  on  to  conclude  that  the  set-aside  in Fullilove  was  not
merely  "substantially  related,”  but  “carefully  tailored”  to the
“important  and congressionally  articulated  goal of remedying
the  present  effects  of past  discrimination."  /d.  at  521.'

Regardless  of the  precise  formulations  employed  in
articulating  the standard  of judicial  review,  in upholding  the

'  Justice  Powell  also wrote a  concurring  opinion,  stating  that “racial
classification  ...  is constitutionally  prohibited  unless  it is a necessary  means
of advancing  a  compelling  governmental  interest,"  Fullilove,  448 U.S.  at
496 (Powell,
J., concurring).  Yet he, like the Chief Justice,  made  clear
that he would not require  that Congressional  remedial  legislation  satisfy a
“least restrictive  means”  test.  /d. at 508.  Whether
one agrees  with
petitioner  that Justice  Powell  adopted  strict scrutiny  or not, it  remains  true
that a  majority  of the Court did not adopt  a strict scrutiny  test.

6

7

the

power

enforcement

set-aside  the  separate  opinions  of a  majority  of  the  Court
recognized  Congress’  power  to identify  racial  discrimination
and  to  implement  a  remedy  for  it,  and  the  corresponding
need  for  judicial  deference  to  Congress’  determinations  in
this  area.  The  Chief  Justice’s  opinion  begins  by noting  the
“deference”  due  to  Congress  as  a  "coequal  branch"  given
express
Fourteenth
under
Amendment.
Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at  472  (Burger,  C.J.).
Based  on  the  Court's  recognition  of Congress’  broad  power
under  the enforcement  clause  of the  Fourteenth  Amendment
in Katzenbach  v.  Morgan,  384  U.S.  641  (1966),  and  Oregon
v.  Mitchell,  400  U.S.  112  (1970),  and of Congress’  similarly
broad  power  under  the  enforcement  clause  of the  Fifteenth
Amendment  in South  Carolina  v.  Katzenbach,  383 U.S.  301
(1966),  the  Chief  Justice  concluded  that  Congress  may
identify,  and  adopt  remedial  legislation  to  counter,  the
effects  of past  discrimination  even  where  the  Court  would
not  find  a violation  of the  Equal  Protection  clause.  Inherent
in such  a view  of Congress’  power  is corresponding  judicial
deference  to  Congress’  determination  of a  “state  interest”
sufficient  to require  a race-conscious  remedy.  Fullilove,  448
U.S.  at 476-77  (Burger,  C.J.).  Similarly,  citing  Congress’
"broad  remedial  powers,”  id.  at 483,  the Chief Justice  stated
that  in  determining  whether  the  program  was  “narrowly
tailored,”  “doubts  must  be  resolved  in  support  of  the
congressional  judgment  that  this  limited  program  is  a
necessary  step....  "  Jd.  at  489.

Indeed,  the  Chief  Justice’s  refusal  to adopt  either  of
the  standards  of judicial  review  that  the  Court  had  just
articulated  in  Regents  of the  University  of California  v.
Bakke,  438 U.S.  265  (1978),  see  Fullilove,  448 U.S.  at 492,
and  his adoption  instead  of a standard  derived  from  Morgan,

Mitchell,  and  South  Carolina  v.  Katzenbach,  shows  that  he
viewed  remedial  legislation  by  Congress  as  demanding  a
distinct  and  more  deferential  standard  of judicial  review  than
remedial
action  by other  bodies.  He saw the  issues  posed  by
the Congressional  enactment  in Fullilove  as  more  like  those
posed by the Congressional  enactments  in Morgan  than  those
posed  by  the  Regents’  minority  preference  in  Bakke  --
because  Congress  has greater  powers  in the equal  protection
See  also  448  U.S.  at  499  (Powell,  J.,  concurring)
area.
("Unlike  the Regents  ...  Congress  properly  may  -  and  indeed
~  must  -  address  directly  the problems  of discrimination  in our
society.");  id.  at 502  (Powell,  J., concurring)  ("It is beyond
question  ...
that  Congress  has  the  authority  to  identify
unlawful  discriminatory  practices,  to prohibit those practices,
and  to  prescribe  remedies  to  eradicate  their  continuing
effects.”);  id.  at  520,  n.4  (Marshall,  J.,  concurring  in  the
judgment).

Four  years  ago,  in  Metro  Broadcasting,  the  Court
relied  on  Fullilove  to uphold  the FCC’s  minority  preference
in  allocating  broadcasting  licenses  in  the  interest  of  racial
diversity  in  broadcasting.
Echoing  Justice  Marshall’s
concurrence  in Fullilove,  the  Court  in Metro  Broadcasting
extended  the  deferential  Fullilove  standard  to  non-remedial
as  well  as  remedial  legislation.  "We  hold  that  benign  race-
conscious  measures  mandated  by Congress  -  even  if those
are not remedial  in the sense of being  designed  to
measures
compensate  victims  of  past  governmental  or  societal
discrimination  --  are constitutionally  permissible  to the extent
that they serve  important  governmental  objectives  within  the
power  of  Congress  and  are
to
achievement  of those  objectives."  Metro  Broadcasting,  110
S.Ct.  at  3008-09.

substantially

related

The  express  holding  of Metro  Broadcasting  squarely
answers  the  second  question  presented  in the instant  petition
for  certiorari  by  rejecting  the  strict  scrutiny  standard
proffered  by petitioner  in this case.  Indeed,  since  the federal
program  challenged  here  is remedial,  this  case  is an  even
stronger  instance  than  Metro  itself  for  application  of  a
standard  lower  than  strict  scrutiny.  Cf Metro  Broadcasting,
110  S.Ct.  at  3031  (O’Connor,  J.,  dissenting)  (Fullilove’s
lower  standard  “applies  at  most  only  to  congressional
measures
past
discrimination.").?

identified

remedy

seek

that

to

Dissenting  in Metro  Broadcasting,  Justice  O’Connor,
joined  by  Chief  Justice  Rehnquist  and  Justices  Scalia  and
Kennedy,  argued  that  judicial  deference  to  Congress’
determination  of a government  interest  warranting  particular
race-conscious  relief is strictly confined  to instances  in which
Congress  imposes  such  mandates  on  the  states,  and  does  not
extend  to  the  administration  of federal  programs  by federal
110  S.Ct.  at  3030-31
Officials.
Looking  to  the  text  of  the
(O’Connor,  J.,  dissenting).
Fourteenth  Amendment,  the dissent  argued  that all Congress
can  do  under  that  Amendment  is provide  oversight  to  the

Metro  Broadcasting,

?  Justice  O’Connor’s  dissent  in  Metro  Broadcasting  distinguished
Fullilove  on  three  grounds:  first,  that  it involved  a Congressional  mandate
to the states  pursuant  to Congress’  section  5 power;  second,  that  it
involved  remedial  legislation;  and third,  that the “substantially  related  to
important  government  interests"  test adopted  by the Metro Broadcasting
majority  was  not  adopted  by a majority  in Fullilove.  Metro  Broadcasting,
110  S.Ct.  at  3031-32  (O’Connor,  J.,  dissenting).  As  discussed  below,
those points  do not provide  a basis to distinguish  Fullilove  from the instant
case.

9

states’  actions  and prevent  the states  from  violating  the Equal
Protection  clause.  Id.

to

that

judicial

deference

But  the  majority  rejected  this  narrow  view  of  the
basis  for judicial  deference  to Congressional  determinations
in  the  area  of equal  protection.  Metro  Broadcasting,  110
S.Ct.  at  3008,  n.11.  Indeed,  the  holding  in  Fullilove
demonstrates
Congress’
determinations  when  enacting  remedial  legislation  to correct
racial  discrimination  extends  beyond  review  of  legislation
governing  state  action.  The  set-aside  program  at  issue  in
Fullilove  applied  not  only  to  states,  but  also  to  private
parties.
Accordingly,  Chief  Justice  Burger’s  opinion,
observing  that  Congress  enacted  the  minority  set-aside
requirement  under  “an amalgam  of its specifically  delegated
powers",  Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at 473  (Burger,  C.J.),  looks  to
section  5 of the  Fourteenth  Amendment  to  find  affirmative
of racial
to correct  the continuing  effects
power  in Congress
discrimination  in the procurement  practices  of state  and local
governments,  id.  at  476,  and  to  the  Commerce  Clause  for
the power  to correct  such  effects  in the practices  of prime
contractors  on  federally  funded  public  works  projects.
/d.
at  475-76,  citing  Heart  of Atlanta  Motel  v.  United  States,

B.

The  Reconstruction  Amendments  grant
Congress  substantive  power  to  identify
equal  protection  violations  as  well  as  power
to  remedy  them.

As  Fullilove  recognizes,  section  5 of the  Fourteenth
Amendment,  and  the  parallel  enforcement  provisions  of the
Thirteenth  and  Fourteenth  Amendments,  confer  not  only
remedial  but  substantive  power  on  Congress  to  identify
conduct  endangering  those  guarantees  of  equal  protection
even  where  the Court  has  not  done  so  and  without  regard  to
whether  the  Court  would  so  find.
The  Reconstruction
Amendments  thus  grant  Congress  the  power  to  go  beyond
the  Court  in  advancing  the  cause  of  “discrete  and  insular
minorities,"  and  require  complementary  deference  by  the
Court  to  Congress’  determinations  in this  area.

The  seminal  expression  of this  separation-of-powers
principle  is Katzenbach  v.  Morgan,  384  U.S.  641  (1966).
The  Court  there  upheld  section  4(e)  of  the  Voting  Rights
Act, which  prohibited  imposition  of literacy  requirements  for
voting  on  persons  educated  in a public  or  accredited  private
school  in which  the  language  of instruction  was  other  than
English.  Finding  that  section  4(e)  was  a proper  exercise  of
Congress’  power  under  section  5  of  the  Fourteenth

a threat  to equal  protection

Amendment,  the Court  held  that  Congress’  section  5 power
is  not  only  remedial,  but  substantive.
Congress  is  not
confined  to  remedying  violations  of  the  Equal  Protection
clause  identified  by  the  courts;  indeed,  Congress  may
identify
and act to remedy  it
"without  regard  to  whether  the  judiciary  would  find  [an
equal protection  violation]".  Jd.  at 649.  “Correctly  viewed,
§ 5  is  a  positive  grant  of  legislative  power  authorizing
Congress  to  exercise  its discretion  in  determining  whether
and what  legislation  is needed  to secure  the guarantees  of the
Fourteenth  Amendment."  Jd.  at 651.  Accord,  Croson,  488
U.S.  at 490  (O’Connor,  J.) ("Congress,  unlike  any  State  or
political  subdivision,  has a specific  constitutional  mandate  to
enforce  the  dictates  of  the  Fourteenth  Amendment.
The
power  to  ‘enforce’  may  at  times  also  include  the  power  to
define
threaten
situations  which  Congress  determines
principles  of equality  and  to adopt  prophylactic  rules  to deal
with  those  situations.").

The  Morgan  Court  held  that  the  proper  test  of  the
affirmative  reach  of Congress’  power  under  section  5 was
that  set  out  in McCulloch  v.  Maryland,  17  U.S.  (4 Wheat)
316  (1819),  to  describe  Congress’  powers  under  the
Necessary  and  Proper  Clause.  Morgan,  384  U.S.  at 650.  °
Morgan  noted  that  this  same  broad  test had been  held to
define  Congress’  power  under  similar  language  in  the
Fifteenth  and  Eighteenth  Amendments.  Morgan,  384  U.S.

*  "Let the end be legitimate,  let it  be within  the scope  of  the
constitution,  and all means  which are appropriate,  which  are plainly
adapted  to that end,  which are not prohibited,  but consist  with the letter
and spirit of the constitution,  are  constitutional.”  Morgan,  384 U.S.  at 650,
quoting McCulloch,  17 U.S.  at 421, and citing  Ex parte  Virginia,  100 U.S.
339,  345-46  (1880).

In  renowned  Footnote  Ten,  the  Court  held  that
at  651.
Congress’  power  to enforce  the  Equal  Protection  clause  is a
one-way  ratchet:  Congress  can  act  to  protect,  but  has  no
power  under  section  5 “to  restrict,  abrogate,  or  dilute,"  the
rights guaranteed  by the Fourteenth  Amendment.  Jd.  at 651,
n.10.

citing  id.  at  654-55  n.15.’
In  short,  Footnote  Ten  of
Morgan  is  to  Congress  as  Footnote  Four  of  Carolene
Products  is to  the  Court:  a  specific  recognition  of authority
and responsibility  to protect  the rights of those disadvantaged
in the  political  process.

The  holding  of Morgan  confirms  what  Footnote  Ten
says:  that  Congress’  power  to go beyond  the authority  of the
Court  is  limited  to  situations  where  Congress  acts  to
advantage  the  “discrete  and  insular  minorities"  that  are
inadequately  represented  in  the  political  process  and  were
therefore  the  focus  of  the  Court’s  concern  in  Carolene

Products.

power  of other  voters  in New  York.’  Yet  no  member  of the
Court  except  Justice  Douglas  was  concerned  about  this

Morgan  directly  supports  deference  to Congressional
findings  of racial  discrimination,  in  a way  markedly  similar
to  Fullilove.  The  Court  in  Morgan  deferred  to  Congress’
determination  that  the  legislation  was  needed  to  prevent
discrimination
at  652
(hypothesizing  various  sufficient  findings  Congress  might
have  made).  Indeed,  the  deference  inherent  in the Morgan
holding  is made  clear  by the  dissent’s  focus  on  the  lack  of
"factual  data"  in  the  legislative  record.  See  Morgan,  384
U.S.  at  669  (Harlan,  J., dissenting).*

in  public  services.

” Korematsu  v.  United  States,  323  U.S.  214  (1944),  is an  example  of
the Court applying  strict  scrutiny  to government  discrimination  against a
racial  minority  --  the internment  of American  citizens  of Japanese  ancestry.
Thus,  contrary  to petitioner's  assertions,  it does  not  support  strict  scrutiny
of remedial  legislation  enacted  by Congress.

*  The historical background  of the Reconstruction  Amendments  also
supports  judicial  deference  to  Congress  in  remedial  legislation.  As
commentators
have recognized,  the post Civil  War Congress *... desired
to secure the stated guarantees against a future unsympathetic  Congress as
well as the courts."  John E.  Nowak and Ronald  D.  Rotunda,  Constitutional
Law at 913.  “The history  will always  be unclear,  for the drafters  had no
reason  to anticipate  a  time  when  the Court  might be more liberal  in

Comat:
niin)

Congress  at this time was dominated  by the Radical  Republicans,  who
viewed  themselves  as the principal  protectors  of the recently freed slaves.
The only  Supreme  Court that the members  of Congress  knew  was a  Court
which had not applied  the Bill of Rights  to the States;  had not assisted  in
giving the protection  of the law to the abolitionists  in the South prior to the
War;  had  enforced  the fugitive  slave  laws;  and  had  invalidated

Fourteenth

Mitchell  arguably  narrows  the  congressional  power
and  corresponding  judicial  deference  in the equal  protection
area  recognized  in  Morgan  to  situations  where,  as  in  the
instant  case,  Congress  has  identified  a  threat  to  racial
equality  under  law and  has imposed  a remedy.  See Mitchell,
400  U.S.  at  146  (opinion  of Douglas,  J.)  (Morgan  noted
that  section  4(e)  could  be  sustained  as  remedying  racial

congressional  legislation  limiting  the  expansion  of  slavery  into  new
territories."  /bid.

discrimination),  id.  at  132-33  (opinion  of Black,  J.) (same).
But  Mitchell  confirms  Congress’  broad  substantive  powers
and  corresponding  judicial  deference  in  that  situation.
Indeed,  all  the  Justices  held  in  Mitchell  that  prohibiting
literacy  tests  was  a valid  exercise  of Congress’  power  under
the  enforcement  clauses  of  the  Fourteenth  or  Fifteenth
Amendment,  regardless  of  whether  they  viewed  federal
elections  as  a  federal  or  state  function.
The  Court
apparently  unanimously  believed  that the principle  of judicial
deference  where  Congress  identifies  and  acts  to  remedy
racial  discrimination  applies equally  to Congress’  imposition
of  a  remedy  on  the  federal  government  as  it  does  to
Congress’  imposition  of such  a  remedy  on  the  states.
See
Mitchell,  400  U.S.  at  117-18  (opinion  of  Harlan,  J.)
(summarizing  the  various  opinions).’

The  Thirteenth  Amendment  provides  a
specific  textual  mandate  requiring  judicial
deference  to  Congress’  determinations  in
legislating
the  continuing
to  eradicate
effects  of past  racial  discrimination.

As  this  Court  has  recognized  for over  a century,  the
enforcement  clause  of  the  Thirteenth  Amendment  “clothes

Congress  with  power  to  pass  all  laws  necessary  and  proper

Congress’  enactments  under  the  Thirteenth  Amendment  are
subject only to rational  review  by the Court.  Jones  v.  Alfred
E.  Mayer  Co.,  392  U.S.  409,  440  (1968)  ("Surely  Congress
has the power  under  the Thirteenth  Amendment  rationally  to
determine  what  are  the  badges  and  the  incidents  of slavery,
and the authority  to translate  that determination  into effective
Thus,  where,  as  here,  Congress  enacts
legislation.").
remedial  legislation  to  eradicate  continuing  effects  of past
racial  discrimination,  the Thirteenth  Amendment  provides  an
express  grant  of  power  to  Congress  at  least  equivalent  to
Congress’  power  under  section  5 and  requiring  at least  equal
deference  by the  Court.

In the Court's  first  opinion  addressing  the Thirteenth
Amendment,  the  Court  recognized  Congress’  power  to
eradicate  racial  discrimination  with  respect  to the exercise  of
those  "fundamental  rights  which  are  the  essence  of  civil
freedom,"  first  among  which  was  the  “right  to  make  and
Civil  Rights
enforce  contracts”  that  is at  issue  in this  case.
Cases,  109  U.S.  at  22.  The  Court  held,  however,  that  the
Thirteenth  Amendment  did  not  empower  Congress  to  bar
racial  discrimination  in public accommodation  as a “badge of
slavery",  on  the today-astonishing  ground  that at the time  the
Thirteenth  Amendment  was  adooted,  "free  colored  people"
did  not  experience  such  discrimination  as  an  “invasion  of
{their]  personal  status  as  a  freeman."  /d.  at 25."°

In his prescient  and  eloquent  dissent,  the  first  Justice
Harlan  argued  that  the  Thirteenth  Amendment  gave  to
Congress  power  to define  and  remedy  "badges  and  incidents
of slavery,”  Civil  Rights  Cases,  109  U.S.  at 36-37  (Harlan,
J., dissenting),  and  that where  Congress  legislates  under  that
power  to  protect  the rights  of racial  minorities,  the  Court
should  defer  to Congress’  action.  /bid;  see  also  id.  at 50-51
(judicial
Fifteenth
Amendment  authority).  Justice  Harlan’s  equally  enlightened
dissent  in Plessy  v.  Ferguson,  163 U.S.  537 (1896),  in which
the  Court  upheld  state  law  segregation  in public  transport,
makes  clear  that  he  saw  Congress’  Thirteenth  Amendment
power  to identify  "badges and  incidents  of s!avery”  as a one-
way  ratchet.
Congress  could  not  prevent  the  Court  from
identifying  and  removing  other  “badges  and  incidents,"
rather,  both  branches  were  entrusted  by the  Reconstruction
Amendments  with  the  responsibility  to  protect  the  rights  of
racial  minorities.  Plessy,  163  U.S.  at 554-55,  564  (Harlan,
J., dissenting).

similar

Almost  a  century  after  the  Civil  Rights  Cases,  in
Jones  v.  Alfred  H.  Mayer  Co.,  392  U.S.  409  (1968),  the
Court  adopted Justice  Harlan’s  view  of Congressional  power
under  the  Thirteenth  Amendment,  holding  that  it  gives
Congress  power  to identify  "badges and incidents  of slavery"
even  where  the  Court  has  not,  and  to  pass  legislation
“necessary  and proper”  to eliminate  them,  subject to rational
basis  review  by the Court.  392  U.S.  at 439-40.  The  Court
held  that  the scope  of Congress’  power  under  the Thirteenth
Amendment,  as  under  the  Fourteenth,  is  defined  by  the
McCulloch  test.  Id.  at 443-44.  Describing  the history of the
Thirteenth  Amendment,  and  the  Civil  Rights  Act  of  1866
which  it  authorized,  the  Court  held  that  the  legislative

20

21

history  confirms  the  plain  language  of the  Amendment  and
the  statute:  the  enacting  Congress  intended  that  they  reach
beyond  state  action  to  encompass  private  discrimination,  as
well  as  discrimination  by  states.  Jd.  at  422-  37,  see  also
General  Contractors  Ass’n,  Inc.  v.  Pennsylvania,  458  U.S.
375  (1982).

This Court  has recognized  Congress’  power  under  the
Thirteenth  Amendment  to require  federal  action  to eradicate
the  legacy  of  racial  discrimination,  a  power  as  broad  as
Congress’  power  to  require  state  action  to  eradicate  the
legacy  of  racial  discrimination  under  section  5  of  the
Fourteenth  Amendment.  In  Hurd  v.  Hodge,  334  U.S.  24
(1948),  argued  and  decided  together  with  Shelley  v.
Kraemer,  334  U.S.  1 (1948),  the  Court  held  that  Congress
had  authority  to  impose  on  the  District  of  Columbia,  a
federal  enclave,  the  same  equal  protection  requirements  as
on  the states,  Hurd,  334  U.S.  at 33, n.14,  and  that  Congress
could  prevent
racially
discriminatory  covenants  to the same  extent  that  it could  bar
See  also
state  courts  from  doing  so.
Jones  v.  Mayer,  392  U.S.  at  417-19.

courts  from  enforcing

See  id.  at  35-36.""

federal

Just  as  Congress  under  the  Fourteenth  Amendment
has one-way  power  to require  affirmative  action  by states,  so
Congress  under  the  Thirteenth  Amendment  has  one-way
power  to  go beyond  simply  mandating  strict  racial  equality

''  The Court based  its holding  in Shelley  on the Fourteenth  Amendment
and  on  Section  | of the  Civil  Rights  Act  of  1886,  as  amended  in  1870
pursuant  to Congress’  authority  under  section  5  of  the  Fourteenth
Amendment.  In Hurd,  the Court  relied  on Section  2 of  the same  Act
(enacted  under  the  Thirteenth  Amendment),  and  found  its  holding  in
Shelley  closely  analogous.  Hurd,  334  U.S.  at  33.

in private or federal  action  that affects  basic  rights,  including
the right to contract,  in the interest  of eradicating  "badges  or
incidents  of slavery."  As noted,  the text  of the Amendment
itself  grants  express  power  to  protect  the  rights  of  racial
minorities;  the  Amendment  is not  race-neutral,  nor  do  we
need a  Footnote  Ten to understand,  as the first Justice
Harlan  saw,  that  Congress’  power  thereunder  is  only  to
advance,  not oppress,  such  “discrete  and insular  minorities."
Modern  Thirteenth  Amendment  jurisprudence  includes  the
Proposition
the
prohibition  of  racial  discrimination  in  the  making  and
enforcing  of private contracts,  Runyon  v.  McCrary,  427 U.S.
160  (1976),  while  allowing  affirmative  action  by  private
employers,  United  Steelworkers  v.  Weber,  443  U.S.  193
(1979).
And  Congress’  Thirteenth  Amendment  power
extends  to create,  in 42  U.S.C.  §1985(3),  a  special  federal
remedy  for racial  minorities  against  “conspiratorial,  racially
discriminatory  private  action  aimed  at depriving  them  of the
basic  rights  that  the law  secures  to all  free  men."  Griffin  v.
Breckenridge,  403  U.S.  88,  105  (1971).

encompasses

Congress’

power

that

This  view  of Congress’  power  under  the  Thirteenth
Amendment  is  buttressed  by  the  Court’s  jurisprudence
describing Congress’  power  under the Fifteenth  Amendment.
Like  the  Thirteenth  Amendment,  the  text  of the  Fifteenth
contains  an  express  one-way  grant  of authority  to Congress
to protect  the voting rights of racial  minorities.  And  like the
Thirteenth  Amendment,  the  Fifteenth  is  binding  on  the
federal  government  as  well  as  on  the  states.  The  leading
modern  case on  the Fiftunth  Amendment,  South  Carolina  v.
Katzenbach,  383  U.S.  301  (1966),  incorporates  a vision  of
Congress’  substantive  and  remedial  authority  to  protect  the
voting  rights  of  racial
minorities  equivalent  to  that

22

23

enunciated  that  same  Term  in Morgan  for Congress’  powers
under  the  Fourteenth  Amendment.
South  Carolina  v.
Katzenbach,  383  U.S.  at  325-27.

“appropriate

Similarly,  in City of Rome  v.  United  States,  446  U.S.
156  (1980),  the  Court  held  that  “under  §2 of the  Fifteenth
Amendment,  Congress  may  prohibit  practices  that  ...  do not
violate  §1 of the  Amendment."  Jd.  at  177.  Subject  only  to
rational  basis  review,  Congress  may  determine  that  certain
voting  rights  provisions  are
methods  of
attacking  the  perpetuation  of  earlier,  purposeful  racial
discrimination,  regardless  of  whether  the  practices  they
prohibit  were  discriminatory  only  in effect."  Jd.  at  176-77.
In  certain  circumstances,  the  Court  has  upheld  the  use  of
racial  criteria  by a state  pursuant  to the Voting  Rights  Act  to
advance  the voting  rights of racial  minorities.  United Jewish
Organizations  of Williamsburgh,  Inc.  v.  Carey,  430 U.S.  144
Congress  can  thus  identify  threats  to  minority
(1977).

discrimination  by the federal  government  is
entitled  to deference  and  should  be upheld.

Indeed,  as  the  dissent  noted,  Congress  “clearly”  has  the
power  to adopt  "narrowly  tailored  race-conscious  measures
++»  tO  remedy  discrimination  in  the  [federal]  allocation  of

/d.,  see  also  id.  at  3036  ("The
remedial  interest  may  support  race  classifications  because
that interest  is necessarily  related  to past  racial

Thus,  in  the  context  of  remedial
legislation  enacted  by Congress,  it makes  little  difference
whether  the  Court  requires  a  "compelling"  or  merely  a
"substantial"  interest  -  remedying  past  racial  discrimination,
including  racial  discrimination  by the  federal  government,
meets  either  test.  Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at 492  (Burger,  C.J.)
(remedial  program  at  issue  would  meet  the  strict  scrutiny
standard  set  out  in  Bakke);  Wygant  v.  Jackson  Board  of
Education,  476  U.S.  267,  268  (1986)  (O’Connor,  J.,
concurring)  ("[A]s  regards  certain  state  interests  commonly
relied  upon  in formulating  affirmative  action  programs,  the
distinction  between  a  ‘compelling’  and  an  ‘important’
governmental  purpose  may  be a negligible  one.")

Similarly,  in  reviewing  the  fit  between  a  race-
conscious  remedy  chosen  by  Congress  and  the  racial
discrimination  which  Congress  sought  to  remedy,  Fullilove
establishes  a test which  does not depend  on whether the fit
is termed  “narrow”  or  "substantial":  Has Congress  "adopted
means  to  ensure  that  the  award  of a  particular  preference

Croson,  488  U.S.  at  488-89  (O’Connor,  J.).  See  generally
Wygant  v.  Jackson  Board  of Education,  476  U.S.  at  287
(O’Connor,  J., concurring).  ("[T]he  Court  is ...  in accord
in  believing  that  a  public  employer,  consistent  with  the
