In  a  tremendous  effort  to  extricate  itself  from  prece-
dent  and  principles  previously  established  in Constitutional
jurisprudence,  nearly  twenty  years  ago,  a  plurality  of  this
Court  incautiously  decided  that  the  Constitution  is  color-
blind.
Regents  of  the  Univ.  of  California  v.  Bakke,
438  U.S.  265  (1978).  In  Bakke,  Justice  Powell,  for  a
plurality,  seemed  to  suggest  that,  because  the  Constitution
is  colorblind,  strict  scrutiny  should  apply  to  all  racial
classifications  whether  benign  or  invidious.
Bakke,  438
U.S.  at  291-300  (Powell,  J.,  concurring).  There  is  no
doubt  that  questions  touching  upon  race  present  the  single
most  difficult  issue  confronting  the  Court.  Consequently,
the  need  to  advance  a  colorblind  principle  of  Constitu-
tional  law  is  not  only  appealing  for  its  structural  ele-
gance,’  but  because  it  appeals  to  the  best  of  our  demo-
cratic  ideals.  Yet,  “[a  colorblind  Constitution]  is  a  tra-
dition  without  a past.”  *°

The  application  of  strict  scrutiny  to  benign  racial  clas-
sifications  sanctioned  by Congress  is predicated  upon  the
postulate  that  the  Constitution  is  colorblind.  This  postu-
late,  which  was  first  suggested  by  Justice  John  Marshall

*  “Principled  decisions  are  those  that  rest  on  reasons  with  respect
to  all  the  issues  in  the  case,  reasons  that  in  their  generality  and
their  neutrality  transcend  any  immediate  result  that  is  involved.”
Compare  Herbert  Wechsler,  Toward  Neutral  Principles  of Constitu-
tional  Law,  73  Harv.  L.  Rev.  119  (1959)  with  Arthur  Miller  and
Ronald  F.  Howell,  The  Myth  of  Neutrality  in  Constitutional  Ad-
judication,  27  U.  Chi.  L.  Rev.  661,  676  (1960);  Addison  Mueller
and  Murrall  O.  Schwartz,  The  Principle  of  Neutral  Principles,  7
U.C.L.A.  L.  Rev.  571  (1960) ; Jan  Deutsch,  Neutrality,  Legitimacy,
and  the  Supreme  Court:  Some  Intersections  Between  Law  and
Political  Science,  20  Stan.  L.  Rev.  169  (1968);  Charles  E.  Clark,
A  Plea  for  the  Unprincipled  Decision,  49  Va.  L.  Rev.  660  (1968) ;
G.  Edward  White,  The  Evolution  of  Reasoned  Elaboration  Juris-
prudential;  Criticism  and  Social  Change,  59 Va.  L. Rev.  279  (1978)  ;
Gerald  Gunther,  The  Subtle  Vices  of  the  “Passive  Virtues”—A
Comment  on  Principle  and  Expediency  In  Judicial  Review,  64
Colum.  L.  Rev.  1  (1964);  and  Benjamin  Andrew  Zelermyer,  Benja-
min  N.  Cardozo:  A Directive  Force  in Legal  Science,  69 B.U.  L. Rev.
213,  219  (1989).

10  William  T.  Coleman,  Jr.,  Equality—Not  Yet,  N.Y.  Times,  Jul.

13,  1981,  at  Al5,  Col.  2.

9

Harlan  in  his  dissent  to  Plessy  v.  Ferguson,  163  U.S.  at
557,  is not only contrary  to the history  of this  Country,
but  is not reflected  in the Constitutional  jurisprudence
by this  Court.  See,  e.g.,  Dred  Scott  v.  Sanford,  60  US.
(19  Howard)  393  (1857);  Brown  v.  Board  of Educ.,  349
U.S.  294  (1955)  (Brown  II);  see  also  Gunnar  Myrdal,
An  American  Dilemma:  The  Negro  Problem  and  Mod-
ern  Democracy  (1944).
These  cases  established  Con-
stitutional  principles  that  were  consistently  influenced  by
the  color-line.  See,  e.g.,  Gong  Lum  v.  Rice,  275  U.S.  78
(1927)  (upholding  state  authority  to  exclude  child  of
Chinese  ancestry  from  “white”  schools  because  equal fa-
cilities  were  provided  for  non-whites).
Indeed,  it  was
because  of  a  history  of  Constitutional  interpretation  that
was  influenced  by  the  color-line  that  Justice  Black  in
Korematsu  v.  United  States,  323  U.S.  214  (1944)  and
Justice  Stone  in  United  States  v.  Carolene  Products  Co.,
304  U.S.  144  (1938),  recognized  that  racial  classifica-
tions  against  minorities  required  judicial  scrutiny.  Until
Bakke,  and  to  a  certain  extent  in  Defunis  v.  Odegaard,
416  U.S.  312  (1974)  (per  curiam),  racial  classifications
were  applied  to  exclude  racial  minorities  from  the  full
benefits  of society.  In  sum,  the  reason  that  racial  classifi-
cations  deserve  this  Court’s  most  searching  scrutiny  is
because  history  has  amply  demonstrated  that  political
majorities  will  exact  a  toll  on  racial  minorities."’  History,
however,  would  not  suggest  that  political  majorities
would  invidiously  discriminate  against  themselves.  James
L.  McAlister,  Comments:  A  pigment  of the  Imagination:
Looking  at  Affirmative  Action  through  Justice  Scalia’s
Colorblind  Rule,  77  Marq.  L.  Rev.  327,  351,  353  n.209,
citing  John  Ely,  The  Constitutionality  of  Reverse  Dis-
crimination,  41  U.  Chi.  L.  Rev.  723,  727,  735  (1974).

11  As James  Madison  observed,  when  contemplating  how  to  curb
the  excesses  at the  majority  for  this  new  country,  “[w]Je  have  seen
the mere  distinction  colour  made  in the most  enlightened  period  of
time,  a  ground  of  the  most  oppressive  dominion  ever  exercised  by
man  over  man.”  James  Madison,  June  6, 1787,  Notes  of Debates  in
the  Federal  Convention  of  1787,  76-77.

10

The  legacy  of  racism  persists  despite  the  efforts  of  the
law  to  dictate  otherwise.  When  the  Thirteenth,  Four-
teenth,  and  Fifteenth  Amendments  were  adopted,”  they
did  not,  with  the  blink  of  an  eye,  eliminate  racism.
When  Brown  v.  Board  of  Education,  347  U.S.  483
(1954),  reversed  the  “separate  but  equal”  principles  of
Plessy,  this  Court  did  not  destroy  the  notion  that  African
Americans  and  other  insular  and  discrete  minorities  were
inherently  inferior.  As  Alexis  de  Tocqueville  observed,
long  before  slavery  was  abolished,  “[a]  natural  prejudice
leads  a  man  to  scorn  anybody  who  has  been  his  inferior,
long  after  he  has  become  his equal;  the  real  inequality  .  . .
is  always  followed  by  an  imagined  inequality  rooted  in
mores.”  Alexis  de  Tocqueville,  Democracy  in  America
341  (G.  Lawrence  trans.,  J.  Mayer  ed.  1969);  see  also
William  T.  Coleman,  Jr.,  A  Bicentennial  Celebration  of
the  Constitution:  The  Third  Circuit  Judicial  Conference
in  Philadelphia:  Federalism,  The  Great  Vague  Clauses
and  Judicial  Supremacy:  Their  Constitutione’  Role  in  the
Liberty  of a  Free  People,  49  U.  Pitt.  L.  Rev.  699,  709
n.49  (1988)  (discussing  effects  of  racism).
In  other
words,  inequality  and  disadvantage  do  not  end  simply  be-
cause  this  Court  declares  that  the  Constitution  is  color-
blind.  As  the  great  jurist  J.  Skelly  Wright  observed,  “to
abruptly  retreat  ot  the  abstract  ground  of  high  principle
in  face  of  the  reality  is to  ignore  the  fact  that  this  nation
has  irrevocably  rejected  the  strict  colorblind  theory  of
government.”  J.  Skelly  Wright,  Colorblind  Theories  and
Color-Conscious  Remedies,  47  U.  Chi.  L.  Rev.  213,  220
(1979).

12  Justice  O’Connor  recognized  the  context  in  which  these  amend-

ments  were  enacted,  when  she  stated  that:

The  Civil  War  Amendments  themselves  worked  a  dramatic
change  in  the  balance  between  congressional  and  state  power
over  matters  of  race.  Speaking  of  the  Thirteenth  and  Four-
teenth  Amendments  in  Ex  parte  Virginia,  100  U.S.  339,  345,
25  L.  Ed.  676  (1880),  the  Court  stated:  ‘They  were  intended
to  be,  what  they  really  are,  limitations  of  the  powers-of  the
States  and  enlargement  of  the  Power  of  Congress.’
City  of Richmond  v.  J.A.  Croson  Co.,  488  U.S.  469,  490  (1989).

11

Bakke,  438  U.S.  at  319-20.

This  Court’s  decisions  in  Bakke,  Fullilove,  Wygant,
Croson,  and  Metro  Broadcasting  all  reflect  the  tension  of
a  genuine  attempt  to  grapple  with  the  race  problem
honestly  and  as  forthrightly  as  possible  given  the  con-
vergence  of  precedent  and  history  while  recognizing  that
“there  is little  in our  jurisprudence  to  date  that  will  guide
us  in  .  ..  [the  area  of  affirmative  action].”  Jd.  at  232.
In  Bakke,  a  tie  between  two  four-Justice  coalitions  was
broken  by  Justice  Powell  who  voted  to  invalidate  the
minority  admissions  quota  but  recognized  the  use  of  race
conscious  programs.
In
Fullilove,  the  Court  broke  into  three  groups  with  three
Justices  setting  the  prevailing  view  by voting  to  uphold  a
race  conscious  program.  Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at  453-92.
In  Wygant,  only  a  plurality  could  agree  on  the  reason  to
strike  down  a  race  based  plan  to  lay  off  public  school
teachers.  Wygant  v.  Jackson  Bd.  of Educ.,  476  U.S.  267,
269-84  (1986).  In  Croson,  “one  of  the  most  complex
scorecards  of  the  history  of  the  Court,”  was  decided
only  after  much  division  between  the  Justices.  In  Metro
Broadcasting,  this  Court  tried  to  utilize  principled  reason
when  it recognized  that  strict  scrutiny  was  not  the  proper
standard,  but  again  the  Court  spoke  through  a plurality
opinion.

Justice  Oliver  Wendell  Holmes  observed  in  his  book
The  Common  Law  that:  “the  life  of the  law  has  not  been
it has  been  experience.”  Oliver  W.  Holmes,  The
logic:
Common  Law  1-2  (1881).  The  application  of  strict
scrutiny  to  benign  racial  classifications,  particularly  when
enacted  or  sanctioned  by Congress,  does  not  truly  reflect
the  experience  of  the  law  on  this  issue.  The  experience
of  American  law  has  revealed  that  racial  classifications
have  been  used,  as recognized  by Justice  Stone  in Caro-
lene  Products  Co.,  304  U.S.  at  152  n.4,  by political  ma-
jorities  against  minorities.  As  such,  the  application  of
strict  scrutiny  in  the  context  of  Congressional  affirmative
action  programs  is without  historic  support.

18 Thomas  Ross,  The  Richmond  Narratives,  68  Tex.  L.  Rev.  381,

895  (1989).

<i

12

Indeed,  because  strict  scrutiny  was  developed  under
a  different  set  of  circumstances,  its  value  as  a  principle
of  equal  protection  doctrine  for  application  to  benign
racial  classification  is significantly  diminished,  particularly
at  the  Congressional  level.  Harvard  Law  Professor  Wechs-
ler  defined  a  principle  as  “one  that  rests  on  reasons  with
respect  to  all  the  issues  in  the  case,  reasons  that  in  their
generality  and  their  neutrality  transcend  any  immediate
result  that  is  involved.”  See  Herbert  Wechsler,  Toward
Neutral  Principles  of  Constitutional  Law,  73  Harv.  L.
Rev.  1,  19  (1959).  The  corruption  of  a  principle  occurs
when  ad  hoc  and  constant  defining  and  redefining  of  the
principle  occurs  within  a  relatively  short  period  in  history.
Id.  at  21-35;  see  also  Charles  A.  Reich,  Mr.  Justice  Black
and  the  Living  Constitution,  76  Harv.  L.  Rev.  673,  737
(1963).  Strict  scrutiny  was  developed  to  protect  minori-
ties.  Korematsu  v.  United  States,  323  U.S.  214  (1944);
United  States  v.  Carolene  Products  Co.,  304  U.S.  144
(1938).
Conceptually,  as  a  principle,  strict  scrutiny
makes  good  judicial  and  political  sense.  It reflects  histori-
cal  reality,  and  it  reflects  ideals  that  date  back  from
the  inception  of  this  Country.  See  The  Federalist,  Nos.
10,  51  (James  Madison)  (Sesquicentennial  ed.  1937);
36-47
Robert  A.  Burt,  The  Constitutional
(1992).  Given  that  strict  scrutiny  stands  on  such  firm
foundation,  it  proved  exceedingly  useful  in  “smok[{ing]
out”  the  illegitimate  uses  of  race  by  political  majorities.
Croson,  488  U.S.  at  506.  Taken  out  of  this  context,
however,  strict  scrutiny  loses  all  value  as  a  neutral  prin-
ciple  of  general  application,  particularly  as  it  relates  to
advancing  the idea  of a colorblind  Constitution.

Conflict,

The  application  of strict  scrutiny  as  a principle  of equal
protection  to  benign  racial  classifications  enacted  by Con-
gress  can  only  accomplish  three  purposes:
(i)  rob  the
idea  of  a  colorblind  Constitution  of  its  intrinsic  value;
(ii)  rob  the  application  of  exacting  judicial  scrutiny  of
racial  classifications  of its meaningful  value  within  the con-
text  of addressing  the  excesses  of political  majorities;  and
(iii)  perpetuate  the lingering  legacy  of a  color  conscious
Constitution.

13

B.  The  demands  of  federalism  preclude  the  applica-
tion  of strict  scrutiny  to Congressionally  sanctioned
benign  racial  classifications

This  Court’s  rejection  of  the  application  of  strict  scru-
tiny  in  Metro  Broadcasting  and  Fullilove  is an  expression
of  the  concepts  of  federalism  within  the  context  of  the
equal  protection  clause.  This  expression  is further  ampli-
fied  when  one  considers  the  Metro  Broadcasting  and  Ful-
lilove  decisions  in light  of this  Court’s  decisions  in Bakke,
Wygant,  and  Croson.  The  apparent  distinction  between
the  latter  three  cases  and  the  Metro  Broadcasting  and
Fullilove  cases  is that  this  Court  has  implicitly  recognized
there  must  be a stricter  equal  protection  review  of  state
and  local  action  than  that  of  Congress,  which  can  act  to
remedy  past  discrimination  pursuant  to  section  5  of  the
Fourteenth  Amendment.  See,  e.g.,  Katzenbach  v.  Morgan,
384  U.S.  641  (1966).  Justice  O’Connor  and  Justice
Powell  have  expressly  recognized  this distinction  in Croson
and  Bakke  respectfully.  Croson,  488  U.S.  at  490  (“Con-
gress,  unlike  any  other  state  or  political  subdivision,  has
a  specific  constitutional  mandate  to  enforce  the  dictates
of the Fourteenth  Amendment  .
.  to define  situations
which  Congress  determines  threaten  principles  of equality
and to adopt prophylactic  rules to deal with those  situa-
tions”);  Bakke,  438  U.S.  at  302  (“We  have  previously
recognized  the  special  competence  of  Congress  to  make
findings  with  respect  to  the  effects  of  identified  past  dis-
crimination  and  its  discretionary  authority  to  take  appro-
priate remedial  measures” ).

.

Directly  applicable  to  the  issues  before  the  Court,
Justice  Scalia,  in  his  concurring  opinion  in  Croson,  recog-
nized the concept  of federalism  in an equal  protection
analysis  of  benign  racial  classifications  drawn  by  Con-
gress.  In  Croson,  Scalia  said  that  a  “sound  distinction
between  federal  and  state  (or local)  action  based  on race
rest not only  upon  .
.  [the plain  language  of the first
and  fifth  sections  of  the  Fourteenth  Amendment?  but
upon  social  reality  and  governmental  theory.”  Croson,

.

14.

488  U.S.  at  522  (Scalia,  J.,  concurring).
Justice  Sca-
lia  further  advanced  a  view  wholly  compatible  with
James  Madison’s  view  by  stating  “that  racial  discrimina-
tion  against  any  group  finds  a  more  ready  expression  at
the  state  and  local  level  rather  than  at  the  federal  level.”
Id.  at  523.  This  view  was  clearly  articulated  by  James
Madison  in  The  Federalist.  The  Federalist,  Nos.  10,  51
(James  Madison)  (Sesquicentennial  ed.  1937).  At  the
heart  of the  Madisonian  principle  is an  understanding  that
federalism  would  help  avoid  one  of  the  central  problems
of  democracy—the  prospensity  of  popular  governments
to  resolve  conflicts  “not  according  to  the  rules  of  justice
and  the  rights  of  the  minor  party,  but  by  the  superior
force  of  an  interested  and  overbearing  majority.”  **  Thus,
Madison’s  grappling  with  the  issue  of  treatment  and  pro-
tection  of  the  minority  by the  majority  within  the  context
of  the  democratic  process”  has  great  meaning  for  an
equal  protection  analysis.

The  judgments  of  Congress,  as  opposed  to  those  of  a
local  or  a  state  legislature,  are  entitled  to  particular  def-
erence,  because  Congress  is  the  Constitutional  equal  of
this  Court.  Moreover,  as  a  large  and  popularly  elected
body,  Congress  mirrors  the  national  will—a  consideration
of  importance  in  a  country  founded  upon  the  sovereignty
of  the  people.  Judicial  restraint,  therefore,  in  the  review
of  federal  statutes  follows  naturally  from  the  realization
that  courts  “are  not  representative  bodies.  They  are  not
designed  to  be  a  good  reflex  of  a  democratic  society.”
Dennis  v.  United  States,  341  U.S.  494,  525  (1951)
(Frankfurter,  J., concurring);  see  Learned  Hand,  The  Bill
of Rights  11-18  (1958);  Alexander  M.  Bickel,  The  Least
Dangerous  Branch  (1962).

14  The  Federalist,  No.  10,  at  54  (James  Madison)  (Sesquicenten-

nial  ed.  1937).

5  The  Federalist,  Nos.  10,  51  (James  Madison)  (Sesquicenten-

nial  ed.  1937).

15

In  reviewing  civil  rights  legislation,

this  Court  has
often  recognized  “the  special  competence  of  Congress  to
make  findings  with  respect  to  the  effects  of  identified  past
discrimination  and  its  discretionary  authority  to  take  ap-
propriate  remedial  measures.”  Bakke,  438  U.S.  at  301
n.41  (Powell,  J.);  Jones  v.  Alfred  H.  Mayer  Co.,  392
U.S.  409,  440  (1968);  Katzenbach  v.  Morgan,  384  U.S.
at  648-653;  South  Carolina  v.  Katzenbach,  383  U.S.
301,  326-27  (1966);  Katzenbach  v.  McClung,  379  US.
294,  305  (1964).  This  Court  is  to  give  judicial  defer-
ence  to  the  edicts  of  the  political  democracy  absent
some  justifiable  reason  to  ignore  the  decisions  of  the
populace.  The  Court’s  basis  for  stepping  into  the  political
arena  to  make  adjustments  has  been  where  the  political
process  was  perverted  and  used  to  the  detriment  of  a
“discrete  and  insular”  minority  whom  was  unable  to  pro-
Carolene
tect  itself  from  the  tyranny  of  the  majority.
Products  Co.,  304  U.S.  at  152  n.4.

The  language  of  the  Fourteenth  Amendment  clearly
limited  the  authority  of  the  state  and  not  Congress—
an  implicit  recognition  of  the  Madisonian  concept  of
the  democratic  principles  of  this  Country.”  U.S.  Const.
amend.  XIV,  § I.  The  basis  for  failing  to  limit  Congress’
authority  seems  quite  reasonable,  since  the  federal  gov-
ernment,  which  is  answerable  to  a  large  constituency,
exerts  a  broader  spectrum  of power  than  a state,  and
thus  may  be  immune  to  more  discrete  and  parochial
interests  and  concerns."  This  Court  has  recognized  that
Congressionally  mandated  actions  deserve  greater  defer-
ence  because  such  acts  are  subject  to  the  equal  pro-

16  “Madison,  by arguing  that  a  large  republic  would  best  protect

and  smallness.”  Notes,  A Madisonian  Interpretation  of  the  Equal
Protection  Doctrine,  91  Yale  L.J.  1403,  1405  n.5  (1982)  (citations
omitted).

17 See  generally  The  Federalist,  No.  10,  at  60  (James  Madison)

(Sesquicentennial  ed.  1937).

tection  component  of  the  due  process  clause  of  the  Fifth

See  Hampton  v.  Mow  Sun  Wong,  426
U.S.  88,  100  (1976).  In  fact,  this  Court  has  required

century-old  promise  of equality  of  economic  opportunity.”

As  espoused  by  Justice  Holmes,  this  Court  has  ap-

of  the  “gravest  and  most  delicate  duty  that  this  Court  is

U.S.  594  (1927).  Following  that  edict,  this  Court  stated
“we  are  bound  to  approach  our  task  with  appropriate
deference  to  the  Congress,  a  co-equal  branch  charged  by
.
the  Constitution  with  the  power  to  ‘provide  for  the  .

.

The  application  of  strict  scrutiny  to  state  and  local
legislation  is consistent  with  the  Madisonian  concept  that
State  and  locally  sponsored  race  conscious  programs  may
require  some  heightened  scrutiny.  Regents  of  the  Univ.
of  California  v.  Bakke,  438  U.S.  265  (1978);  Fullilove
v.  Klutznick,  448  U.S.  448  (1980).  This  understanding
clearly  reflects  the  Court’s  divergent  treatment  between
the  two  governmental  entities.  Under  section  5  of  the
Fourteenth  Amendment,  Congressional  acts  mandated  by
the  Constitutionally  chartered  body  deserve  greater  defer-
ence  because  Congress  is  charged  with  the  enforcement
of  the  equal  protection  clause  as  applied  to  the  states.”
U.S.  Const.  amend.  XIV,  § 5.

18  Section  5 of the  Fourteenth  Amendment  states:  “The  Congress
shall  have  the  power  to  enforce,  by  appropriate  legislation,  the
provisions  of  this  article.”  U.S.  Const.  amend.  XIV,  § 5.

II.  IN  ANY  EVENT,  THE  APPLICATION  OF  STRICT
SCRUTINY  AS  DEVELOPED  BY  THIS  COURT
UNDER  CITY  OF  RICHMOND  v.  J.A.  CROSON  IS
IMPRACTICAL  FOR  A  NATIONAL  LEGISLATIVE
BODY  CONFRONTED  WITH  THE  NEED  TO
UTILIZE  BENIGN  RACIAL  CLASSIFICATIONS

A. Statistics  of the  type  demanded  by  Croson  are
incapable  of  comprehending  discrimination  on  the
scale  contemplated  by  national  racial  preferences
As  documented  by scholars,  history  is clear  -  discrimi-
nation  against  racial  minorities  in this  country  has  resulted
in vast  inequalities  which  remain  today.  See  Cornel  West,
Race  Matters  (1993);  Paul  M.  Sniderman  and  Thomas
Piazza,  The  Scar  of  Race  (1993).
Congress’  powers
through  section  5  of  the  Fourteenth  Amendment  author-
ize  this  body  to  enact  remedial  legislation  to  correct  the
evils  of invidious  discrimination  for  the  good  of  our  dem-
ocratic  society.  Thus,  deference  has  been  accorded  to
Congress to rectify  discrimination.

Affirmative  action  programs  are  most  often  designed
“to  remedy  the  present  effects  of  past  discrimination.”
Wygant,  476  U.S.  at  280  (quoting  Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at
Legislatures  enacting
480  (opinion  of  Burger,  C.J.)).
these  programs  seek  to  remedy  existing  disparities  such
that  they  recognize  that  a  system  grounded  in  preventive
measures  alone  cannot  overcome  the  pervasive  effects  of
Robert  Allen  Sedler,  Beyond
the  history  of  racism.”

19  By  implementing  affirmative  action  programs,  legislature  ap-
parently  recognize  that  “You  do  not  take  a  person  who,  for  years,
has  been  hobbled  by chains  and  liberate  him,  bringing  him  up to the
starting  line  of  a  race  and  then  say,  ‘You  are  free  to  compete  with
all  the  others,’  and  still  justly  believe  that  you  have  been  com-
pletely  fair.”  George  R.  LaUoue  and  John  Sullivan,  Esq.,  “But
For”  Discrimination:

Statistics  do  not  lend  themselves  to  comprehending  the
breadth  of  a  national  affirmative  action  program.  Appre-
ciating  the  limitations  of  statistics,  particularly  at  the  na-
tional  level,  requires  appreciating  the  scope  of the  problem
facing  minority-owned  businesses.
It  has,  for  some  time
now,  been  widely  recognized  that  minority  businesses  are
not  an  integral  part  of  America’s  free  enterprise  system.”
For  example,  African-American  owned  enterprises  con-
stitute  approximately  3%  of  all  U.S.  businesses  and  1%
of  all  U.S.  revenues.  Laura  Everson  and  Carl  T.  Hall,
How  Black  Firms  are  Fairing,  Minority  Businesses  Still
Struggling  To  Get  Into  Economic  Mainstream,  S.F.  Chron-
icle,  May  11,  1992,  at  Bl.  Indeed,  racial  discrimination
in  American  commerce  was  an  integral  part  of  the  sys-
tem  and  is  heavily  documented.  Booker  T.  Washington
and  W.E.B.  DuBois  were  early  comentators  on  the  issue.

DuBois,  The  Economic  Future  of  the  Negro  (1906)  at

One  cannot  lose  sight  of  the  fact  that  affirmative  ac-
tion  programs  are  designed  to  mitigate  and  rectify  the
discriminatory  evils  of  the  past  and  present  in  a  manner
that  increases  minority  participation  in  the  economy  of
our  democratic  society.  To  require  nationally  mecha-
nistic  statistical  analyses  from  Congress  is  Herculean.
Applying  Croson  to  the  statute  at  issue  would  in  essence
foreclose  many  Congressionally  mandated  affirmative  ac-
tion  programs.  Not  only  is  the  task  of  justification  and
statistical  vertification  of  discrimination  nearly  impossible
to  accomplish,  some sources  of covert racism  and discrimi-
nation  allude  capture  by statistics.  Indeed,  Petitioner  and
its  amici  have  spent  a  considerable  amount  of  argument
demonstrating  the  inherent  problems  of refining  statistics.
Furthermore,  it is evident  that  not  all  social  and  economic
disparities  can  be  identified  as  race-neutral.  Thus,  this
Court  should  recognize  the  purpose  of  Congress’  remedial
acts,  as  well  as  the  practical  limitations  of a  national  legis-
lative  body,  when  considering  the  application  of  strict
scrutiny.

B. The  scope  of statistics

are  im-
required  by Croson

practical  for  a  national  legislative  body

The  Constitution  does  not  require  that  a legislature  con-
duct  hearings,  build  a  record,  and  make  formal  findings
when  it  passes  a  law.  Perez  v.  United  States,  402  U.S.
146,  156-57  (1971);  Oregon  v.  Mitchell,  400  U.S.  112,
147  (1970)  (Douglas,  J., concurring  in  part  and  dissent-
ing in part);  Katzenbach  v.  McClung,  379  U.S.  294,  299
(1964);  Carmichael  v.  Southern  Coal  &  Coke  Co.,  301
U.S.  495  (1937);  Bi-Metallic  Investment  Co.  v.  State
Bd.  of Equalization,  239  U.S.  441  (1915).  As  Professor
Cox  has  noted,  “[t]he  Court  does  not  review  the  suffi-

20

21

ciency  of  the  evidence  in  the  record  to  support  congres-
sional  action  ..
.  and  the  practice  of  relying  upon  the
legislative  record  when  it  exists  should  not  be  taken
to  show  that  such  a  record  is  required.”  Archibald  Cox,
The  Supreme  Court,  1965  Term-Forward:  Constitutional
Adjudication  and  the  Promotion  of  Human  Rights,  80
Harv.  L.  Rev.  91,  105  (1966).

This  basic  separation  of  powers  principle  is  embodied
in  the  Constitution’s  grant  to  Congress  of  the  power  to
U.S.  Const.
“determine  the  Rules  of  its  Proceedings.”
art.  I,  § 5.  The  courts,  far  from  requiring  a  detailed  legis-
lative  history  or  findings,  have  “long  been  committed  both
to  the  presumption  that  facts  exist  which  sustain  congres-
sional  legislation  and  also  to  deference  to  congressional
judgment  upon  questions  of degree  and  proportion.”  Cox,
supra  21,  at  107.  The  relevant  cases  support  this  analysis;
they  uniformly  hold  that  a  statute  “will  not,  be  set  aside
if any  state  of facts  reasonably  may  be conceived  to  justify
it.”  McGowan  v.  Maryland,  366  U.S.  420,  426  (1961);
James  v.  Strange,  407  U.S.  128,  133  (1972).

Croson  recognized  that  Congress  is  assigned  the  duty
to  remedy  society-wide  discrimination.  Croson,  488  U.S.
at  490;  Coral  Constr.  Co.  v.  King  County,  941  F.2d
910,  925  (9th  Cir.  1991),  cert.  denied,
US.  —,
112  S.  Ct.  875  (1992);  Ellis  v.  Skinner,  961  F.2d  912
U.S.  —,  113
(10th  Cir.  1992),  cert.  denied,
S.  Ct.  374  (1992),  citing  Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at  478.  As
such,  Congress  is not  required  to  make  the  same  particu-
larized  findings  as  are  the  states  for  every  affirmative  ac-
tion  statute  that  it  passes.  Moreover,  Congress  is  not
required  to  make  specific  findings  of  past  discrimination
where  it  has  an  abundant  historical  basis  upon  which  to
Ellis  v.  Skinner,  961  F.2d  912
ground  its  legislation.
(10th  Cir.  1992),  citing  Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at  478.  “After
Congress  has  legislated  repeatedly  in  an  area  of  national
concern,  its Members  gain  experience  that  may  reduce  the
need  for  fresh  hearings  or  prolonged  debate  when  Con-
gress  again  considers  action  in  that  area.”  Fullilove,  448
U.S.  at  503  (Powell,  J.,  concurring).  Thus,  the  “joint

lesson  of Fullilove  and  Croson  is that  the  federal  govern-
ment  can,  by virtue  of the enforcement  clause  of the
Fourteenth  Amendment,  engage  in  affirmative  action  with
a  freer  hand  than  states  and  municipalities
can  do.”
Milwaukee  County  Pavers  Ass'n  v.  Fielder,  922  F.2d
419  (7th  Cir.),  cert.  denied,  500  U.S.  954  (1991);
Tennessee  Asphalt  Co.  v.  Farris,  942  F.2d  969  (6th  Cir.
1991).
Accordingly,  although  the  Court  may  require
Congress  to  demonstrate  historical  discrimination  against
minorities  in  order  to  justify  a  program  of  affirmative
action,  it  is  impractical  to  require  Congress  to  meet  the
strict  requirement  of  detailed  statistics  established  by
Croson.

Furthermore,  if  the  Court  requires  Congress  to  utilize
a  Croson  statistical  analysis  to justify  race conscious  legis-
lation,  many  Congressional  programs  would  never  come
into being,  because  of the difficulty  and costs involved  in
completing  the requisite  studies  necessary  for a  program
intended  to  reduce  the effects  of discrimination  on a  na-
tional  basis. Cf.  Long v. City  of Saginaw,  911  F.2d  1192
(6th  Cir.  1990)  (finding  of  unconstitutionality  because
defining  statistical  pool as “protective  services”  is  too
all  encompassing  and  fails  to  look  at  age,  education,
fitness,  and  other  attributes);  O’Donnell  Constr.  Co.  v.
District  of  Columbia,  963  F.2d  420  (D.C.  Cir.  1992)
(concluding  state  affirmative  act  unconstitutional);  Coral
Constr.  Co.  v.  King  County,  941  F.2d  910  (9th  Cir.
1991},  cert.  denied,  ——  US.  ——,  112  S.  Ct.  875
(1992)  (finding  county  set-aside  program  unconstitu-
tional);  Podberesky  v.  Kirwan,  No.  93-2527,  No.  93-
2585,  1994  U.S.  App.  Lexis  29943  (4th  Cir.  Oct.  27,
1994)
scholarship  program  un-
constitutional);  Hayes  v.  North  State  Law  Enforcement
Officers  Ass'n,  10  F.3d  207  (4th  Cir.  1993)  (concluding
city  promotion  policy  unconstitutional);  United  Black
Firefighters  Ass'n  v.  City  of Akron,  976  F.2d  999  (6th
Cir.  1992)  (finding  consent  decree  regarding  hiring  proce-
dures  unconstitutional);  Black  Fire  Fighters  Ass'n  of

(finding  university's

22

23

Dallas  v.  City  of  Dallas,  19  F.3d  992  (Sth  Cir.  1994)
(finding  consent  decree  regarding  promotion  plan  uncon-
stitutional);  Cunico  v.  Pueblo  School  Dist.  No.  60,  917
F.2d  431  (10th  Cir.  1990)  (finding  hiring  decision  un-
constitutional).

The  statistical  burden  Petitioner  and  its  amici  seeks
this  Court  to  place  on  Congress  is  senseless  and  insur-
mountable.  Petitioner  and  its amici  would  require  the  fed-
eral  government  to  map  each  political  subdivision  of  the
United  States  for  every  job  category  imaginable  with  re-
spect  to  historical  and  contemporary  discrimination.  Brief
of  Petitioner  at  34-41;  Brief  at  ALP  at  13-29;  Brief  of
PLF  at  24-28.  For  example,  the  Pacific  Legal  Founda-
tion  states  that:

Section  5  authority  is  lacking  in  this  case  because
neither  Colorado  nor  any  political  subdivision  within
Colorado  has  been  shown  to  have  engaged  in  dis-
crimination  in  the  highway  construction  industry.

If  Congress  is  required  to  act
Brief  of  PLF  at  27-28.
within  these  constraints,  it  will  be  rendered  impotent
given  that  the  task  of  Congress  to  comprehend  discrimi-
nation  on  a  national  scale  is  daunting,  and  would  result
in  hundreds  of  hearings  and  studies,  costing  millions  of
dollars.  See,  e.g.,  George  R.  LaNovu,  and  John  Sullivan,
Esq.,  “But  For’  Discrimination  How  Many  Minority-
Owned  Businesses  Would  There  Be?,  24  Colum.  Hum.
Rts.  L.  Rev.  93,  99  (1992-93)  (“By  March  1991,  29
state  and  local  jurisdictions  had  completed  some  sort  of
post-Croson  MBE  study  at  a  total  cost  over  $5,491,162.
The  Minority  Business  Enterprise  Legal  Defense  and  Edu-
cation  Fund,  Inc.  also  reported  that  another  37  studies
were  underway  at  costs  totalling  over  $7,029,929.”);
Martin  Katz,  The  Economics  of  Discrimination:
The
Three  Fallacies  of  Croson,  100  Yale  L.J.  1032  (1991);
James  E.  Jones,  The  Genesis  and  Present  Status  of  Af-
firmative  Action  in  Employment:  Economic,  Legal,  and
Political  Realities,  70  lowa  L.  Rev.  901,  933-39  (1985).

Furthermore,  Congress  does  not  function  in  a  manner
that  lends  itself  to  the  multiplicity  of  tasks  required  by
Croson.  Congress  would  need  to  hire  sociologists,  statis-
ticians,  and  other  professionals  to  undertake  the  statistical
analyses  necessary  to  comprehend  the  impact  of  discrim-
ination  on  particular  industries  on  a  national  level.  See
George  R.  LaNovu  and  John  Sullivan,  Esq.,  “But  For”
Discrimination  How  Many  Minority-Owned  Businesses
Would  There  Be?,  24  Colum.  Hum.  Rts.  L.  Rev.  93,  99
(1992-93);  Martin  Katz,  The  Economics  of  Discrimina-
tion:  The  Three  Fallacies  of Croson,  100  Yale  L.J.  1032
(1991);  James  E.  Jones,  The  Genesis  and  Present  Status
of Affirmative  Action  in  Employment:  Economic,  Legal,
and  Political  Realities,  70  lowa  L.  Rev.  901,  933-39
(1985).  Such  a  standard  is  not  only  impractical,  but  it
also  inappropriately  imposes  a  unique  and  undue  burden
on  federal  legislation  designed  to  remedy  the  effects
of  past  discrimination.  Given  the  egalitarian  goals  asso-
ciated  with  such  programs  along  with  the  greater  defer-
ence  provided  to  Congress,  it  defies  Constitutional  juris-
prudence  to  require  affirmative  action  programs  to  meet
a  higher  standard  of statistical  certitude  and  burden  than
any  other  legislation  passed  by Congress.

In  order  to  uphold  affirmative  action  programs,  Croson
requires  that  the  affected  industry  be  statistically  broken
down  into  its  smallest  segments  of  trade  and  profession
in order  to analyze  and decide  whether  there  is discrim-
ination  against  individuals  in  all  of  those  positions.  Cro-
son,  488  U.S.  at  501-02;  Hazelwood  School  Dist.,  433
U.S.  at  308.  Importantly,  the  Small  Business  Adminis-
tration’s  categorizing  of  U.S.  industry—the  Standard  In-
dustrial  Classification  (“SIC”)  codes—encompasses  seven
pages  of  single  space  delineation  in  the  Code  of  Federal
Register  of  various  job  categories  to  be  examined.
13
C.F.R.  § 121.601.  A  Croson  analysis  on  the  federal  level
would  require  Congress  to  investigate  the  discriminatory
practices  in  each  of  these  job  categories  for  programs
promoting  economic  diversity.  Such  a  requirement  is  an

24

impossible  task,  and  ensures  that  the  economy  will  be
unduly  dominated  by  majority-owned  established  busi-
nesses  at  the  expense  of  minority-owned  businesses.

Moreover,  to  require  a  mechanistic  statistical  analysis
of  Congress  as  a  prerequisite  to  upholding  its  legislation
is  inapposite  to  the  intent  of  the  Fourteenth  Amendment.
See  Fullilove  v.  Klutznick,  488  U.S.  488  (1988);  Metro
Broadcasting,  Inc.  v.  FCC,  497  U.S.  547  (1990).  The
goal  of section  502  is to  better  the  economy  of this  Coun-
try  by  freeing  up  more  resources  presently  kept  down
by  discrimination,  and  in  the  process  remedy  the  effects
of  discrimination.
15  U.S.C.  § 361.  By  the  imposition
of  major  expenses  on  a  national  entity  attempting  to
establish  such  a  program,  the  legislature  will  most  likely
conclude  that  the  most  cost-effective  measure  is  to  sim-
Ac-
ply  allow  racism  to  flourish,  an  intolerable  result.
cordingly,  the  proposal  to  require  Congress  to  meet  the
comprehensive  use  of  statistics  established  by  Croson
should  be rejected.

To  advance  a  democratic  society,  Congress  has  and
must  be  allowed  to  continue  to  ensure  that  all  citizens
are  integrated  into  this  society  politically,  socially,  and
economically.  The  Fourteenth  Amendment  provides  Con-
gress  with  such  power;  thus  this  Court  must  not  limit
Congress’  power  by  imposing  the  nearly  impossible—
national  studies  of discrimination  using  a  Croson  standard
of  statistically  vertification.  Accordingly,  it is impractical
and  against  sound  reasoning  to  impose  a  Croson  statistical
requirement  on  the United  States  Congress.

——— —_——

25

CONCLUSION

For  the  reasons  stated,  this  Court  should  affirm  the
Tenth  Circuit’s  interpretation  of  this  Court’s  decisions  in
Metro  Broadcasting  and Fullilove.

Respectfully  submitted,

BRIEF  AMICUS  CURIAE  OF
NATIONAL  COALITION  OF  MINORITY  BUSINESSES
IN  SUPPORT  OF  RESPONDENTS
°
INTEREST  OF  AMICUS  CURIAE

National  Coalition  of  Minority  Businesses  (NCMB)  is  a
nonprofit  organization  composed  of  minority-owned  busi-
nesses,  encompassing  a  wide  cross  section  of racial  and
ethnic  minorities,  and individual  leaders  of a  number  of
other  minority  trade  associations.  The members  of  NCMB
include  disadvantaged  businesses  that  participate  in or  have
graduated  from  the  Small  Business  Administration’s  § 8(a)
program,  as well  as other businesses  that have been  certified
as  disadvantaged  business  enterprises  (DBEs),  15  U.S.C.
§ 644(g)  (1988)  for purposes  of Section  502 of the Small
Business  Act  and/or  as  small  disadvantaged  businesses

1

|
|

|

|

/

)

a

2
(SDBs)  under  the  Department  of  Defense  and  other  federal
programs.

NCMB’s  purposes  include  promoting  the  interests  of
minority  businesses,  representing  the  interests  of  minor-
ity businesses  before  Congress  and  federal  agencies,  and
collecting  and  distributing  information  about  issues  of
concern  to  minority  businesses  to  members  of  industry
groups,  the press  and  the public.  Members  of NCMB  have
repeatedly  testified  before  Congress  regarding  federal
procurement  statutes  such  as  the  Small  Business  Admin-
istration  SDB,  DBE  and  minority  business  enterprise
(MBE)  programs  which  are  designed  to  open  oppor-
tunities  to  minority  businesses  previously  foreclosed
from  participation  due  to  bias  or  other  institutional  bar-
riers.

It is important  to  understand  the  historical  context  of
the  statute  that  is  at  issue  here,  as  well  as  of  a  host  of
related  statutes  that  have  been  enacted  to  redress  the
problem  of  discriminatory  exclusion  from  government
procurements.  The  core  concept  of  providing  assistance
to  businesses  owned  or  controlled  by “socially  and  eco-
nomically  disadvantaged”  persons  dates  back  to  1969,
when  President  Nixon  issued  an  executive  order  direct-
ing the  Small  Business  Administration  to  use  its authority
under  § 8(a)  of  the  Small  Business  Act  to  assist  small
business  concerns  owned  and  controlled  by such  disad-
vantaged  individuals.!  President  Nixon  issued  this  execu-
tive  order,  rather  than  awaiting  congressional  action,
because  of  the  urgent  need  to  address  the  urban  unrest
and  other  serious  problems  resulting  from  discrimination
against  and  disparate  treatment  of  minorities.  He  recog-
nized  the  undeniable  historical  fact  that  minorities  had
been  unlawfully  denied  access  to  participation  in  the  free

*  Exec.  Order  No.  11458,  3 C.F.R.  779  (1966-1970  Comp.);
Exec.  Order  No.  11518,  3 C.E.R.  907  (1966-1970  Comp.);  Exec.
Order  No.  11625,  3 C.F.R.  616  (1971-1975  Comp.).

3
enterprise  system  and  indeed  had  been  deprived  of  all
basic  civil  rights.

The  Congress  and  President  Johnson  acknowledged
the  continuing  and  disgraceful  existence  of de jure  racial
discrimination,  which  they  sought  to  rectify  by enacting
the  major  civil  rights  legislation  of  the  1960s.  President
Nixon  realized  that  even  this  landmark  legislation  was
not  enough  to  remove  the  effects  of hundreds  of years  of
oppression  and  discrimination  against  racial  minorities,
nor  to  solve  the  ongoing  economic  problems  of  racial
bigotry,  division  and  unrest.  Thus,  it  was  essential  to
bring  minority  groups  into  the  economic  mainstream  by
providing  real  equality  of  opportunity  to  participate  in
the  free  enterprise  system.  Since  the  federal  government
is  the  nation’s  largest  purchaser  of  goods  and  services,
President  Nixon  sought  to  use  this  market  to  nuture
“black  capitalism”  (as  he  called  it),  to  provide  minorities
with  a  “business  stake  in  America,”  and  to  fulfill  the
unrealized  goals  of  the  civil  rights  legislation.

In  the  1970s  Congress  undertook  a  lengthy  review  of
the  administrative  program  created  by  this  executive
order  and  recognized  the  need  to improve  and  strengthen
this  program.  Thus,  for  example,  the  minority  set-aside
provisions  of  the  Public  Works  Employment  Act  of  1977,
42  U.S.C.  § 6705  (1988)  which  were  upheld  by this  Court
in  Fullilove  v.  Klutznick,  448  U.S.  448  (1980),  were
expressly  linked  by Congress  to  the  ongoing  § 8(a)  pro-
gram  and  were  motivated  in  part  by Congress’  concern
over  the  limited  effectiveness  of that  program  in  signifi-
cantly  increasing  minority  business  participation  in  gov-
ernment  procurements.  See  id.  at  463-67.

Similarly,  in  1978,  when  Congress  codified  and
strengthened  the  Small  Business  Administration’s  pro-
gram  by,  among  other  things,  enacting  the  challenged
provisions  of Section  502  of the  Small  Business  Act,  Con-
gress  recognized  that  minorities  continued  to lack  oppor-
tunity  to  participate  fully  in the  free  enterprise  system  or
to  secure  federal  government  contracts.  Congress’  well-

ee

4
supported  findings  in  this  regard  are  set  forth  in  the
Small  Business  Act  itself.  See  15  U.S.C.  § 631(f)(1).

Throughout  the  1980s  and  early  1990s  these  federal
programs  to  remedy  the  effects  of discrimination  and  to
bring  minority  groups  into  the  economic  mainstream
have  been  the  subject  of  intense  and  continuing  scrutiny
by Congress,  by the  Executive  Branch  and  by the  public
at  large,  in  each  instance  resulting  in  new  and  additional
efiorts  to  remedy  the  problem.  Although  these  federal
programs  have  resulted  in significant  gains  for disadvan-
taged  businesses,  the  evidence  clearly  shows,  and  every
Congress  and  Administration  since  the  late  1960s  has
correctly  recognized,  that  the  need  for  these  programs
has  not  ended  because  minorities  continue  to  suffer  the
effects  of  systematic  and  institutional  racial  discrimina-
tion.  As  recently  as  September  16,  1994,  President  Clinton
recognized,  by Executive  Order  No.  12928,  the  continuing
need  “to  enforce  vigorously  the  letter  and  spirit  of public
laws  that  promote  increased  participation  in  Federal  pro-
curement  by Small  Businesses  Owned  and  Controlled  by
Socially  and  Economically  Disadvantage.)  Individuals.”2
The  federal  programs  in question  are  a  matter  of vital
importance  to  the  members  of NCMB  and  to  the  minority
business  community  at  large,  as  well  as  to  the  minority
community  and  the  nation  as  a  whole.  For  example,
during  fiscal  year  1993,  the  8(a)  program  had  4,848  partic-
ipating  business  concerns.  These  8(a)  firms  employed
105,800  individuals  and  won  $4.3  billion  worth  of federal
contracts,  but  even  more  important,  the  federal  program-
matic  support  has  served  as  a  catalyst  to  enable  such
firms  to  compete  in  the  commercial  market  and  earn  57%
(or  $7.3  billion)  of  their  revenues  outside  the  8(a)

2  Exec.  Order  No.  12928,  Promoting  Procurement  with
Small  Business  Owned  and  Controlled  by Socially  and  Econom-
ically  Disadvantaged  Individuals,  Historically  Black  Colleges
and  Universities,  and  Minority  Universities  (Sept.  16,  1994).

5
program.  The  existence  and  success  of  these  and  other
disadvantaged  businesses  that  benefit  from  statutes  such
as  Section  502  is a  matter  of vital  economic  importance  to
the  minority  community  as  a  whole.  These  businesses
have  played  a  major  role  in providing  direct  and  indirect
employment  opportunities  for  minorities,  and  curtail-
ment  of necessary  federal  programs  would  therefore  have
a  devastating  impact  on  a  major  segment  of the  minority
community  and  the  economic  health  of numerous  diverse
communities  throughout  the  nation.

Since  the  arguments  raised  by Petitioner  and  its  sup-
porting  amici  threaten  the  very  existence  of  vital  and
necessary  programs  such  as  Section  502  of  the  Small
Business  Act,  the  issues  in  this  case  are  of  great  signifi-
cance  to  NCMB.3

SUMMARY  OF  ARGUMENT

The  courts  below  properly  held  that  Section  502  of
the  Small  Business  Act,  15  U.S.C.  § 644(g)  (1988),  which
provides  for  the establishment  of annual  goals  for  federal
procurement  from  disadvantaged  business  enterprises
(DBEs),  is clearly  constitutional.  This  Court  has  long  rec-
ognized  that  Congress  has  the  power  to  identify  and
redress  the  effects  of past  or  present  racial  discrimination
on  public  contracting,  and  that  Congress’  decision  to  use
racial  or  ethnic  criteria  in  achieving  these  remedial  pur-
poses  is  entitled  to  substantial  deference.  Fullilove  v.
Klutznick,  448  U.S.  448  (1980).  The  use  of  race  and  eth-
nicity  here  simply  as  rebuttable  factors  in  determining
whether  an  individual  is  socially  disadvantaged  within
the  meaning  of Section  502  is in  fact  a  considerably  more
limited  use  of racial  and  ethnic  criteria  than  that  upheld
as  an  appropriate  remedial  measure  in  Fullilove.  Indeed,

3  Pursuant  to  Rule  37  of  the  Rules  of  this  Court,  letters
reflecting  the  written  consent  of the  parties  to the  filing  of this
brief  have  been  filed  with  the  Clerk  of the  Court.

6
one  of the  dissenting  Justices  in Fullilove  expressly  distin-
guished  the  category  of  Minority  Business  Enterprises
(MBEs)  in  that  case  (in  which  categorization  was  based
solely  on  race)  from  the  more  limited  and  acceptable  use
of race  as  but  one  factor  in defining  DBEs  under  the Small
Business  Act.  See  Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at  335  n.6  (Stevens,  Re
dissenting).

When  Congress  enacted  Section  502  in  1978,  Con-
gress  had  ample  evidence  that  racial  minorities  continued
to  lack  equal  opportunity  to  compete  for  federal  govern-
ment  contracts  due  to  barriers  erected  by  decades  of
severe  and  odious  racial  discrimination.  Much  of  this
evidence  was  discussed  in  Fullilove  itself,  and  led  Justice
Powell  to  note  that  “a  court  must  accept  as  established
the conclusion  that  purposeful  discrimination  contributed
significantly  to  the  small  percentage  of  federal  contract-
ing  funds  that  minority  business  enterprises  have
received.”  Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at  506  (Powell,  J.,  concur-
ring).

In  the  years  following  the  enactment  of Section  502,
Congress  has  continued  to  revisit  the  issue  of  racial  dis-
crimination  and  disadvantaged  business  enterprises.  In
1988,  Congress  amended  Section  502  to provide  minimum
government-wide  goals  for  participation  by  small  busi-
nesses  and  DBEs.  Moreover,  Congress  has  conducted
extensive  and  repeated  oversight  hearings  on  issues
related  to  DBEs,  considering  the  views  of  both  propo-
nents  and  opponents  of  the  DBE  provisions  of  the  Small
Business  Act.  These  facts  should  alleviate  any  concerns
that  Congress  has  given  merely  “perfunctory  consider-
ation”  to  the  DBE  provisions  challenged  here.  See  Full-
ilove,  448  U.S.  at  550  (Stevens,  J., dissenting).

In  short,  this  Court  should  reaffirm  Fullilove  and
uphold  the challenged  DBE  provisions  of the  Small  Busi-
ness  Act.  Failure  to do so  would  cause  enormous  damage

7

to  thousands  of  DBE  companies  (and  hundreds  of  thou-
sands  of  their  employees)  that  have  made  major  invest-
ments  of  time  and  money  in  reliance  upon  the  statutory
promise  that  DBEs  will  receive  the  “maximum  practicable
opportunity”  to  participate  in  federal  contract  awards.
Fullilove  was  correctly  decided  and  should  be  reaffirmed
based  on  settled  principles  of  stare  decisis  and  the  con-
tinuing  need  for  remedial  measures.

ARGUMENT
I.  CONGRESS  HAS  THE  AUTHORITY  TO  IDENTIFY
AND  REDRESS  THE  EFFECTS  OF  SOCIETY-WIDE
DISCRIMINATION  WITH  RESPECT  TO  FEDERAL
PROCUREMENT
In  Fullilove,  this  Court  upheld  a  10%  set-aside  for
MBEs  which  was  contained  in  Section  103(f)(2)  of  the
Public  Works  Employment  Act  (PWEA),  Pub.  L. 95-28,  91
Stat.  116,  42  U.S.C.  §§ 6701-6736  (1988).  The  PWEA  pro-
vided  federal  funds  for  local  public  works  projects  and
required  that  the  state  and  local  governments  receiving
these  funds  expend  at  least  10%  on  goods  and  services  of
MBEs.  Unlike  the  DBEs  at issue  in this  case  (where  race  is
only  one  factor  in  defining  the  class  of  disadvantaged
persons),  MBEs  were  defined  simply  as  businesses  owned
by members  of  specified  minority  groups.

The  principal  opinion  in  Fullilove,  written  by Chief
Justice  Burger,  first  asked  “whether  the  objectives  of  this
litigation  are  within  the  power  of Congress.”  448  U.S.  at
473  (emphasis  in  original).  The  Chief  Justice  noted  that
the  PWEA  imposed  obligations  on  prime  contractors,  as
well  as  state  and  local  governments,  because  “when  the
procurement  practices  of the grantee  involve  the award  of
a  prime  contract  to  a  general  or  prime  contractor,  the
obligations  to assure  minority  participation  devolve  upon
the  private  contracting  party.”  Id.  at  474.  The  principal

Se crete en

8
opinion  found  that  “[i]nsofar  as  the  MBE  program  per-
tains  to  the  actions  of private  prime  contractors,  the Con-
gress  could  have  achieved  its  objectives  under  the
Commerce  Clause”  because  “[t]he  legislative  history  of
the  MBE  provision  shows  that  there  was a rational  basis
for  Congress  to  conclude  that  the  subcontracting  prac-
tices  of prime  contractors  could  perpetuate  the  prevailing
impaired  access  by  minority  businesses  to  public  con-
tracting  opportunities,  and  that  this  inequity  has  an  effect
on  interstate  commerce.”  Id.  at  475-76.

With  respect  to  state  and  local  governments,  how-
ever,  the  principal  opinion  declined  to  rely  on  the  Com-
merce  Clause  as  the  source  of  Congress’  authority.  See
Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at 476  (noting  that  “there  are  limitations
on  the  reach  of  the  Commerce  Power  to  regulate  the
actions  of  state  and  local  governments”).  Instead,  the
principal  opinion  concluded  that  “[iJnsofar  as  the  MBE
program  pertains  to  the  actions  of  state  and  local
grantees,  Congress  could  have  achieved  its  objectives  by
use  of  its  power  under  § 5  of  the  Fourteenth  Amend-
ment.”  Id.  at  478.  The  Chief  Justice  stated:

Although  the  [PWEA]  recites  no  preambulary
“findings”  on  the  subject,  we  are  satisfied  that
Congress  had  abundant  historical  basis  from
which  it could  conclude  that  traditional  procure-
ment  practices,  when  applied  to  minority  busi-
nesses,  could  perpetuate  the  effects  of  prior
discrimination.  Accordingly,  Congress  reason-
ably  determined  that  the  prospective  elimina-
tion  of  these  barriers  to  minority  firm  access  to
public  contracting  opportunities  generated  by
the  1977  Act  was  appropriate  to  ensure  that
those  businesses  were  not  denied  equal  oppor-
tunity  to  participate  in  federal  grants  to  state
and  local  governments,  which  is  one  aspect  of
the  equal  protection  of  the  laws.

Id.

Se

.

9

Petitioner  and  its  supporting  amici  argue  that  Full-
ilove  is inapplicable  here  because  it “addressed  Congres-
sional  power  under  § 5 of  the  Fourteenth  Amendment,
which  is not  at issue  in this  case.”  Pet.  Br.  at 32.  Thus,  one
amicus  points  out  that  “[w]hile  the  MBE  program  in
Fullilove  concerned  the  ability  of a  state  to  receive  federal
funds,  the  DBE  program  under  review  here  concerns
incentive  payments  to  individuals  who  employ  certified
DBE’s.”  Brief  Amicus  Curiae  of Pacific  Legal  Foundation
in  Support  of  Petitioner  at  18  (emphasis  in  original).

These  attempts  to  distinguish  Fullilove  are  meritless.
First,  the  MBE  provisions  in Fullilove  did  not  involve  only
Congressional  authority  with  respect  to  state  and  local
governments;  on  the  contrary,  the  principal  opinion  in
Fullilove  also  found  that  Congress  had  adequate  authority
under  the  Commerce  Clause  to  impose  these  provisions
on  private  contractors.  Nothing  in  Fullilove  suggests  that
the  authority  of Congress  with  respect  to such  contractors
is  more  limited  than  with  respect  to  state  and  local  gov-
ernments.  If  anything,  the  principal  opinion  in  Fullilove
indicates  that  federalism  concerns  might  impose  greater
limitations  on  Congressional  authority  over  state  and
local  governments.

Second,  as  the  district  court  correctly  determined,  the
DBE  program  at  issue  here  falls  within  Congress’  Section
5 power  to  “remedy  the  effects  of  disabling  discrimina-
tion.”  Pet.  App.  34.  This  Court  has  held  that  “[cJorrectly
viewed,  § 5  is  a  positive  grant  of  legislative  power
authorizing  Congress  to  exercise  its  discretion  in  deter-
mining  whether  and  what  legislation  is needed  to  secure
the  guarantees  of  the  Fourteenth  Amendment.”
Katzenbach  v.  Morgan,  384  U.S.  641,  651  (1966).  In  United
States  v.  Guest,  383  U.S.  745  (1966),  a majority  of the Court
expressed  the  view  that  Congress  has  the  discretion
under  Section  5  to  enact  legislation  proscribing  private
conspiracies  even  where  state  officers  or  others  acting
under  color  of state  law  are  not  involved.  383  U.S.  at  761

—

|

10
(Clark,  J., joined  by Black  and  Fortas,  J.J., concurring);  id.
at  782  (Brennan,  J., joined  by Warren,  C.J.,  and  Douglas,
J.,  concurring).  Thus,  Congress’  Section  5 power  is  not
limited  to  regulation  of  state  action.4  Moreover,  even  if
State  action  were  required,  this  requirement  is  satisfied
here  because  the  purpose  of  the  DBE  Provisions  is  to
remedy  the effects  of society-wide  discrimination,  includ-
ing  discrimination  perpetuated  by state  action.

Finally,  even  if Section  5 were  inapplicable  to  Con-
gress’  attempts  to  redress  the  effects  of prior  discrimina-
tion  in  the  area  of  federal  procurement,  it  is  clear  that

*  Congress  has asserted  the authority,  pursuant  to Section  5
of  the  Fourteenth  Amendment,  to  preclude  discrimination  by
private  parties.  See  Heart  of Atlanta  Motel,  Inc.  v.  United  States,
379  U.S.  241,  249  (1964).  In Heart  of Atlanta  Motel,  a  majority  of
the  Court  did  not  reach  the  issue  of whether  Section  5 provided
adequate  authority  for enactment  of the Civil  Rights Act of 1964,
finding  that  the  commerce  power  was a sufficient  basis  for  the
statute.  Id.  at  250;  see  also  id.  at  279  (“Because  the  Civil  Rights
Act of 1964  as  applied  here  is wholly  valid  under  the Commerce
Clause  and  the Necessary  and  Proper Clause,  there  is no need  to
consider  whether  this  Act  is  also  constitutionally  supportable
under  Section  5 of the  Fourteenth  Amendment...  .  ”) (Black,  J.,
concurring).  Two  members  of the  Court,  however,  would  have
squarely  held  that  Congress  had  authority  under  Section  5, as
well  as  the Commerce  Clause,  to enact  this  legislation.  See id.  at
280  (Douglas,  J., concurring);  id.  at  293  (Goldberg,  J., concur-
ring).
The  present  case  need  not  resolve  the  issue  left  open  in
Heart  of Atlanta  Motel  because  here  Congress  has  not  sought  to
preclude  private  discrimination.  Instead,  Congress  has  merely
sought  to  provide  equal  opportunity  to  minorities  and  other
disadvantaged  persons  to Participate  in the area  of federal  pro-
curement,  which  is funded  in part by the tax revenues  of minor-
ity  citizens.  Congress’  power  to  enforce  equal  protection
guarantees  surely  is  broad  enough  to  ensure  that  federal  pro-
curement  activities  do  not  perpetuate  the  effects  of prior  dis-
crimination  which  has  deprived  minorities  and  other
disadvantaged  persons  of equal  protection  of the  law.

11
Congress  has  equally  broad  remedial  powers  to  achieve
these  objectives  under  other  constitutional  provisions.
For  example,  in  Fullilove  Chief  Justice  Burger  noted  that
the  Court’s  review  of  the  MBE  provisions  must  be  con-
ducted  “with  appropriate  deference  to the Congress,  a co-
equal  branch  charged  by the Constitution  with  the power
to  ‘provide  for  the  .
.  general  Welfare  of  the  United
States,’”  as  well  as  to  enforce  the  Fourteenth  Amend-
ment.  448  U.S.  at  472;  see  also  Metro  Broadcasting  v.  FCC,
497  U.S.  547,  563  (1990)  (Fullilove  held  that  “deference
was  appropriate  in light of Congress’  institutional  compe-
tence  as  the  national  legislature.”).5

.

Fullilove  held  that  the  MBE  set-aside  was  within  Con-
gress’  Section  5 power  not  because  state  or  local  govern-
ments  were  engaging  in  intentional  discrimination  or
other  unlawful  conduct  but  because  Congress  had  rea-
sonably  determined  that  minority  businesses  attempting
to compete  for local  public  works  projects  were  subject  to
“barriers  to  competitive  access  which  had  their  roots  in
racial  and  ethnic  discrimination.”  448  U.S.  at  478.  It
would  be  utterly  illogical,  as  well  as  contrary  to  princi-
ples  of  federalism,  to  hold  that  Congress  somehow  has
less  power  to  eradicate  the  same  effects  of past  discrimi-
nation  as  they  appear  in  the  area  of federal  procurement.

°  The  challenged  DBE  provisions  here  are  also  valid  as  an
exercise  of Congress’  powers  under  the Thirteenth  Amendment.
See  Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at  510  (Powell,  J.,  concurring)  (“[T]he
Thirteenth  and  Fourteenth  Amendments  confer  upon  Congress
the authority  to select  reasonable  remedies  to advance  the com-
pelling state interest  in repairing the effects of discrimination.”).
As  pointed  out  by Justice  Powell  in his  concurrence,  the  Thir-
teenth  Amendment  clearly provides Congress  with  the power  to
enact  legislation  regarding  private  discrimination.  Id.  at  500
(Powell,  J., concurring)  (citing  Runyon  v.  McCrary,  427  U.S.  160,
168-70  (1976)).

12

Any  doubts  as  to  the  applicability  of  Fullilove  here
are  resolved  by this  Court’s  decision  in  Metro  Broadcast-
ing,  which  upheld  the  constitutionality  of  certain  FCC
policies  that  treated  minority  ownership  as  a  favorable
factor  in  decisions  regarding  license  applications.  The
Metro  Broadcasting  Court  specifically  rejected  the  conten-
tion  that  Fullilove  was  applicable  only  to  Congress’  exer-
cise  of  its  authority  pursuant  to  Section  5  of  the
Fourteenth  Amendment.  497  U.S.  563  n.11.  Moreover,  the
Court  also  rejected  the  argument,  raised  again  by  Peti-
tioner  and  its amici  here,  that  Congress’  power  to  redress
racial  and  ethnic  discrimination  is no  broader  than  that  of
state  and  local  governments  under  Richmond  v.  ].A.  Croson
Co.,  488  U.S.  469  (1989).  The  Court  stated:

Croson  cannot  be  read  to  undermine  our  deci-
sion  in  Fullilove.  In  fact,  much  of  the  language
and  reasoning  in Croson  reaffirmed  the  lesson  of
Fullilove  that  race-conscious  classifications
adopted  by Congress  to  address  racial  and  eth-
nic  discrimination  are  subject  to a  different  stan-
dard  than  such  classifications  prescribed  by
state  and  local  governments.
Metro  Broadcasting,  497  U.S.  at  565.6

Met7o  Broadcasting  held  that  all benign  race-conscious
measures  mandated  by Congress,  whether  or  not  reme-
dial,  are  subject  to  intermediate  scrutiny.  NCMB  submits,
however,  that  the  correctness  of  this  holding  is  not  the

©  The  dissent  in Metro  Broadcasting  provides  no  support  for
Petitioner’s  position  here.  The dissent  found  that  the  “consider-
able lattitude”  enjoyed  by Congress  under  Fullilove  was  inappli-
cable  to  the  FCC  minority  preference  policies  since  those
policies  did  not  involve  Congress’  powers  under  § 5 of  the
Fourteenth  Amendment,  nor  did  they  involve  congressional
measures  seeking  to remedy  identified  past discrimination.  See
497 U.S.  at 605-07  (O’Connor,  J., dissenting).  The  present  case  is
like  Fullilove,  and  unlike  Metro  Broadcasting,  in  both  those
respects.

—_—

13
real  issue  before  the  Court  in  the  case  at  bar.  Rather  the
issue  is  whether  Congress,  unlike  state  or  local  govern-
ments,  has  the  power  “to  identify  and  redress  the  effects
of  society-wide  discrimination”  by  using  the  type  of
remedies  upheld  in Fullilove.  See Croson,  448  US.  at 720.  If
the  answer  to  this  question  is  affirmative,  as  it must  be
under  Fullilove,  Croson  and  Metro  Broadcasting,  then  the
challenged  DBE  provisions  here  survive  either  strict  or
intermediate  scrutiny.  Indeed,  in  Fullilove  itself,  Chief
Justice  Burger  noted  that  the  MBE  provisions  of  the
PWEA  (which,  as  shown  below,  raise  more  significant
constitutional  concerns  than  do  the  challenged  DBE  pro-
visions  in this  case)  would  survive  either  type of scrutiny.
See  448  U.S.  at  492.

Il.  THE  EVIDENTIARY  REQUIREMENTS  OF  FULL-

ILOVE  ARE  SATISFIED  HERE
The  principal  opinion  in  Fullilove  contains  an  exten-
sive  review  of  the  legislative  history  not  only  of  the
PWEA,  but  of  other  administrative  and  legislative  pro-
grams  designed  to  promote  minority  opportunities,  par-
ticularly  those  related  to  Section  8(a)  of  the  Small
Business  Act.  See  448  U.S.  at  463  (“The  legislative  objec-
tives  of  the  MBE  provision  must  be  considered  against
the  background  of ongoing  efforts  directed  toward  deliv-
erance  of the century-old  promise  of equality  of economic
opportunity.”).  Based  on  this  review,  the  Chief  Justice
concluded  that  “Congress  had  abundant  evidence  [when
it  enacted  the  PWEA  in  1977]  from  which  it could  con-
clude  that  minority  businesses  have  been  denied  effective
Participation  in  public  contracting  opportunities  by pro-
curement  practices  that  perpetuated  the  effects  of  prior
discrimination.”  Id.  at  477-78.  The  Chief  Justice  further
noted  that  “much  of this  history  related  to the experience
of  minority  businesses  in  the  area  of  federal  procure-
ment.”  Id.  at  478;  see  also  id.  at  506  (“In  light  of  these

14
legislative  materials  and  the  discussion  of legislative  his-
tory  contained  in  THE  CHIEF  JUSTICE’S  opinion,  |
believe  that  a court  must  accept  as  established  the conclu-
sion  that  purposeful  discrimination  contributed  signifi-
cantly  to  the  small  percentage  of  federal  contracting
funds  that  minority  business  enterprises  have  received.”)
(Powell,  J.,  concurring).

Fullilove  thus  clearly  establishes  that  when  in  1978
Congress  first  amended  the  Small  Business  Act  to  require
federal  agencies  to  provide  the  “maximum  practicable
opportunity”  for  DBEs  to  participate  in  the  performance
of  federal  contracts,  it  had  ample  evidence  that  federal
procurement  practices  were  perpetuating  the  effects  of
prior  discrimination.  See  Pub.  L.  No.  95-507,  92  Stat.  1770
(1978).  The  legislative  history  notes  that  “in  many,  but  not
all,  cases  status  as  a  minority  can  be  directly  and
unequivocally  correlated  with  social  disadvantagement
and  this  condition  exists  regardless  of  the  individual,
personal  qualities  of  that  minority  person.”  H.R.  Conf.
Rep.  No.  95-1174,  95th  Cong.,  2nd  Sess.,  reprinted  in  1978
U.S.C.C.A.N.  3882.  It  also  notes  that  “other  Americans
may  also  suffer  from  social  disadvantagement  because  of
cultural  bias.  For  example,  a  poor  Appalachian  white
person  who  has  never  had  the  opportunity  for  a  quality
education  or  the  ability  to  expand  his  or  her  cultural
horizons,  may  similarly  be  found  socially  disadvantaged,
provided  that  the  conditions  leading  to  such  disadvan-
tagement  are  beyond  the  ability  of the  person  to control.”
Id.

This  legislative  history  demonstrates  that  Congress
was  not  engaging  in  racial  stereotyping  or  simply  favor-
ing  certain  racial  groups  over-others.  Rather  Congress
was  attempting  to remedy  the effects  of discrimination  by
identifying  a  “class  of socially  or  economically  disadvan-
taged  persons  [which]  neither  included  all  persons  in the
racial  class  nor  excluded  all  nonmembers  of  the  racial

15
class.  Race  was  used  as  no  more  than a factor  in identify-
ing the  class  of the  disadvantaged.”  Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at
535  n.6  (Stevens,  J.,  dissenting).

The  legislative  history  also  shows  that  Congress  has
not  given  merely  “perfunctory  consideration”  to  the  DBE
classifications  at  issue  here.  See  Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at  550
(Stevens,  J.,  dissenting).  Rather  Congress  has  given
serious  and  continuing  attention  to  the  effects  and  impli-
cations  of the  DBE  provisions  of federal  law.  For example,
the  Report  of the  Senate  Committee  on  Environment  and
Public  Works,  which  accompanied  the  Surface  Transpor-
tation  and  Uniform  Relocation  Assistance  Act  of  1987
(STURAA),  states  that  “[t]he  Committee  has  considered
extensive  testimony  and  evidence  on  the bill’s  DBE  provi-
sions,  and  has  concluded  that  this  provision  is necessary
to  remedy  the  discrimination  faced  by socially  and  eco-
nomically  disadvantaged  persons  attempting  to  compete
in  the  highway  and  mass  transit  construction  industry.”
S. Rep.  No.  100-4,  100th  Cong.,  1st  Sess.,  reprinted  in  1987
U.S.C.C.A.N.  76.  Moreover,  the  Committee  also  consid-
ered  the  views  and  perspectives  of  nonminorities
impacted  by the  DBE  provisions:

The  DBE  program  implemented  by the  Depart-
ment  of  Transportation  following  enactment  of
section  105(f)  operates  primarily  as  a  sub-
contracting  program,  under  which  prime  con-
tractors  must  make  good  faith  efforts  to achieve
DBE  subcontracting  goals.  The  Committee  has
studied  the  materials  presented  by nonminority
subcontractors  regarding  the  program’s  impact
on  their  businesses.  The  increased  funding  for
highway  and  mass  transit  construction  projects
has  increased  the  opportunities  for  subcontrac-
tors  to  compete,  and  the  good  faith  efforts  pro-
vision  of the  program  assures  that  the  program
does  not  operate  as  a  quota.  Also,  since  DBE
firms  compete  in  many  areas  of subcontracting,

16

any  burden  that  might  be  imposed  by this  pro-
gram  is  spread  among  different  groups  of  sub-
contractors.
1987  U.S.C.C.A.N.  76.

Congress  has  held  numerous  and  extensive  oversight
hearings  to  determine  the  impact  of and  continuing  need
for  the  DBE  provisions.”  Congress  has  also  conducted  a
nationwide  survey  of  DBEs  that  had  participated  in  the
Small  Business  Administration  8(a)  program.®  Based  on
testimony  and  evidence  from  these  and  many  other
sources,  including  annual  reports  from  federal  agencies
regarding  the  attainment  of  DBE  contracting  goals,  Con-
gress  has  concluded  that  the  DBE  provisions  remain  nec-
essary  to  meeting  the  objective  of  “promot[ing]  the
business  development  of  small  business  concerns  owned
and  controlled  by  socially  and  economically  disadvan-
taged  individuals  so  that  such  concerns  can  compete  on
an  equal  basis  in  the  American  economy.”  15  U.S.C.
§ 631(f)(2)(A);  see  1987  U.S.C.C.A.N.  76  (despite  progress

”  See,  e.g.,  Hearings  on  Federal  Contracting  Opportunities  for
Minority  and  Women-Owned  Businesses:  An  Examination  of the 8(d)
Subcontracting  Program  Before the Senate  Comm.  on  Small  Business,
98th  Cong.,  dst  Sess.  (1983);  Hearings  on  the  State  of Hispanic
Small  Business  in  America  Before  the  Subcomm.  on  SBA  and  SBIC
Authority,  Minority  Enterprise  and  General  Small  Business
Problems  of the  House  Comm.  on  Small  Business,  99th  Cong.,  1st
Sess.  (1985);  Hearings  on  Minority  Enterprise  and  General  Small
Business  Problems  Before the Subcomm.  on  SBA  and SBIC  Authority,
Minority  Enterprise  and  General  Small  Business  Problems  of the
Comm.  on  Small  Business,  99th  Cong.,  2d  Sess.  (1986).

8  Report  of the  Senate  Committee  on  Small  Business,  Sur-
vey  of the  Graduates  of the  Small  Business  Administration  Sec-
tion  8(a)  Minority  Business  Development  Program  (hereinafter
“8(a)  Survey”),  S.  Rep.  No.  394,  100th  Cong.,  2d  Sess.  App.  A

17
resulting  from  DBE  provisions  “barriers  still  remain,  pre-
venting  minorities  and  women  from  successfully  compet-
ing  in  the  industry”).°

.

Petitioner  asserts  that  any  “pattern  or  practice  of
racial  discrimination  .
.  was  cured  long  ago.”  Pet.  Br.  at
36.  Unfortunately,  there  is  more  than  ample  evidence  to
contradict  this  unsupported  statement.  The  results  of
institutional  discrimination  continue  -  minorities  still
own  a  disproportionately  low  number  of  businesses  in
the  United  States  and  to  receive  a  disproportionately
small  amount  of business  revenue.!°  For example,  in  1987
fewer  than  1%  of the  89,897  American  construction  firms

*  The  Senate’s  1986  Survey  of  8(e)  Program  Graduates
demonstrated  that  “graduated”  firms  (i.e.,  those  that  had  com-
pleted  the  limited  participation  term  in the  8(a)  program)  faced
an  inordinately  high  failure  rate.  8(a) Survey,  supra  note  8. This
is  further  evidence  that  disadvantaged  businesses  continue  to
face  institutional  bias  and  discrimination  as  substantial  barriers
to  their  access  to governmental  and  private  markets,  credit  and
capital.

10  As  one  commentator  notes:

The  black  business  participation  rate  is  only
about  one-quarter  of  the  national  average.  While
blacks  make  up  twelve  percent  of  the  population,
they  own  only  two  percent  of  all  business  firms.  In
1982,  only sixteen  dollars  of every  $10,900  in business
receipts,  less  than  two-tenths  of  one  percent,  came
from  black-owned  firms.  Black  business  receipts
would  have  to  increase  seventy-five-fold  before  they
would  be proportionate  to the black  share  of the total
population.  Perhaps  present  discrimination  against
minority  businesses  explains  the  persistence  of some
of this  disparity.

R. Suggs,  Racial  Discrimination  in Business  Transactions,  42 Hast-
ings  Law  Journal  1257,  1264  (1991)  (footnotes  omitted);  see  also
id.  at  1264  n.41  (noting  that  “[o]ther  minorities  also  are  under-
represented  in business  activity”).

18

19

with  annual  receipts  of $1  million  or  more  were  owned  by
minorities.'!  Partly  as  a  result  of  these  facts,  minority
unemployment  consistently  runs  as  much  as  double  that
of  the  rest  of  the  population,  and  minority  income  and
wealth  lags  far  behind  that  of  the  majority.!?

Minority-owned  businesses  have  made  significant
progress  in  the  area  of  federal  procurement  directly  due
to  Congressional  enactments.  For  example,  in  the  1970s
the  value  of  federal  contracts  awarded  to  minority  firms
amounted  to  less  than  1%  of  the  total.  See  Fullilove,  448
U.S.  at  536  n.7  (Stevens,  J.,  dissenting).  Today,  due  to
these  programs,  minority-owned  businesses  are  awarded
only  4%  of  the  value  of  federal  contracts.'!3  This  limited
progress  is  the  direct  result  of  DBE  provisions  such  as
those  challenged  here;  the  fact  that  these  provisions  are
working  is evidence  that  they  are  still  needed.  Moreover,
even  with  the  DBE  provisions,  the  percentage  of  federal
contracts  awarded  to  minority-owned  businesses  is  still
far  below  the  percentage  of  minorities  in  the  overall

12  The  United  States  Census  Bureau  has  reported  that  white
households  have  more  than  10  times  the  median  wealth  of

income  also  far  exceeds  that  of  African-American  households.

John Jacobs,  the  president  of the  National  Urban  League,  com-
mented  that  African-Americans  are  “not  going  to  move  toward  a
position  of parity  without  increasing  the  wealth  of [African-Ameri-
can]  communiti[es]  and  we  can’t  do that  without  doing  something
about  .

.  business  ownership...  .  ”  Id.  at  A3, col.  3.

.

13  See  Commentary,  In  the Minority:  The  8(a)  Program  Isn't  a

Giveaway,  13  Wash.  Bus.  J. 15  (week  of  Aug.12-18,  1994).

population  (20%),  and  the  percentage  of  minority  busi-
nesses  out  of  total  U.S.  businesses  (9%).

Both  Congress  and  the  Executive  Branch  have  recog-
nized  the  need  for  continuing  efforts  to  assist  minority-
owned  businesses.  For  example,  in  1988  Congress
amended  Section  502  to  strengthen  the  DBE  provisions  by
providing  a  minimum  5%  government-wide  goal  for  con-
tracting  with  DBEs.  Pub.  L.  No.  100-656,  1988
U.S.C.C.A.N.  102  Stat.  3881.  Congress  has  also  enacted  a
number  of  other  statutes,  one  as  recently  as  1992,  to
provide  similar  DBE  goals  for  specific  agencies.'*  Presi-
dent  Clinton,  just  a  few  months  ago,  issued  Executive
Order  No.  12928  which  recognized  the  continuing  vital
importance  of  these  federal  programs  to  assist  DBEs.
These  facts  decisively  refute  Petitioner’s  suggestion  that
Section  502  is  a  relic  of  the  past.

In short,  the  problems  thai  President  Nixon  first  tried
to  address  in  1969  with  the  § 8(a)  program  and  that
congress  has  attempted  to  address  with  numerous  pieces
of  legislation  from  the  1970s  to  the  present  have  not
disappeared.  These  problems  -  such  as  poverty,  high
unemployment,  lack  of  access  to  capital,  and  continuing
job  and  lender  discrimination  -  are  with  us  today.  Peti-
tioner’s  attack  on  Section  502,  if successful,  would  under-
mine  the  vital  federal  programs  designed  to  redress  these
problems  and  reverse  the  progress  that  has  been  made  in
securing  access  by  minorities  and  other  disadvantaged
