3133  (Department  of  Energy);  Pub.  L.  No.  101-167,  1988
U.S.C.C.A.N.  103  Stat.  1249  (Agency  for International  Develop-
ment);  Pub.  L.  No.  99-661,  1987  U.S.C.C.A.N.  101  Stat.  1127
(Department  of  Defense);  Pub.  L.  No.  101-510,  1990
U.S.C.C.A.N.  101  Stat.  1607  (Department  of  Defense);  Pub.  L.
No.  101-507,  1990  U.S.C.C.A.N.  104  Stat.  1374  (Environmental
Protection  Agency);  Pub.  L. No.  101-144,  1989  U.S.C.C.A.N.  103
Stat.  863  (NASA).

20

persons  to  the  free  enterprise  system  in general  and  to  the
federal  procurement  system  in  particular,  and  in  short
order  the  basic  causes  of urban  unrest  (at least  those  that
have  been  ameliorated  by  these  programs)  would  be
greatly  exacerbated.

Congress  is not  required  to  make  findings  of discrim-
ination  by  specific  market,  by  specific  industry  or  by
specific  agency,  as  have  been  variously  suggested  by
Petitioner  and  its  supporting  amici.!5  See  Croson,  448  U.S.
at  504  (“Congress  has  made  national  findings  that  there
has  been  discrimination  in  a  host  of  fields”).  However,
NCMB  notes  that  a  major  study  was  recently  conducted
regarding  discrimination  against  minorities  and  women
in  the  construction  and  professional  design  industries  in
the  six  county  Metropolitan  Statistical  Area  of  Denver,
Colorado.’  This  study  concluded  that  “[t]here  exists
strong  evidence  of  race  and  gender-based  discrimination
in  the  construction  and  professional  design  industries.”!”

‘5  For  example,  one  amicus  argues  that  “[a]t  a  minimum,
the  statistical  evidence  must:  (1) relate  to a  defined  and  relevant
market;  (2) compare  comparably  qualified  and  available  minor-
ity  and  non-minority  firms  within  that  market;  (3)  control  for
such  race-neutral,  market-relevant  variables  as  firm  size  and
experience;  (4)  calculate  the  disparity  ratio  for  each  minority
group  granted  preferential  treatment;  and  (5) identify,  alone  or
in  conjunction  with  anecdotal  evidence,  the  discriminatory
practices  at fault  in the relevant  market.”  Brief  Amicus  Curiae  of
the  Associated  General  Contractors  of America,  Inc.,  in Support
of  Petitioner  at  19.  Such  standards  are  more  reminiscent  of  an
appellate  court’s  review  of a  verdict  in an  antitrust  action  than
of the  deference  due  to  Congress  as  a  co-equal  branch  charged
by the  Constitution  with  the  power  to  provide  for  the  general
welfare  and  to  enforce  the  equal  protection  guarantees  of  the
Fourteenth  Amendment.  See  Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at  472.

16  Denver  City  Council,  Denver  Disparity  Study:  Construc-

tion  and  Professional  Design  Services  (1990).

17  Id.  at  ES-7.

21

This  evidence  included  statistics  showing  that  “[s]ubstan-
tially  fewer  minority  construction  firms  have  been
formed  and  survived  than  would  be  expected  given  the
relative  size  of  the  minority  construction  work  force.” 18
In addition,  the  study  accumulated  considerable  evidence
of specific  acts  of discrimination  as  related  in  testimony,
interviews  and  affidavits,  such  as:

A  minority  was  interviewed  and  supplied  an
affidavit  which  contained  three  very  specific
examples  of race  discrimination  against  his com-
pany.  All  of  the  examples  involved  discrimina-
tion  by City  officials.  One  example  involved  bid
shopping  to  a  majority-owned  firm.  A  second
involved a racial  slur.  The  third  involved  retalia-
tion  for  the  filing  of  a complaint  with  the  Affir-
mative  Action  Office.  Certain  pertinent  facts  set
forth  in  the  affidavit  were  verified  by examina-
tion  of  detailed  company  records.!9
In  the  final  analysis,  however,  Congress’  institutional
competence  and  discretion  to  enact  remedial  legislation
such  as  that  at  issue  here  does  not  depend  on  specific
statistical  studies  or  testimony  regarding  particular  acts
of discrimination.  In  the  first  place,  “Congress,  of course,
may  legislate  without  compiling  the  kind  of  ‘record’
appropriate  with  respect  to judicial  or  administrative  pro-
ceedings.”  Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at  478.  More  fundamentally,
Congress’  objective  here  is  not  simply  to  compensate
individual  victims  of discrimination.  Indeed,  it would  be
impossible  for  Congress,  or  even  a  court,  to  determine
exactly  how  and  to  what  extent  individuals  have  been

18  Id. at V11-2  to V-3; see generally  id. at V1-6  to V1-11;  V11-1

to  V11-11.

19  Id. at V11-2  to V-3; see generally  id. at V1-6  to V1-11;  V11-1

to  V11-11.

22

injured  by  the  type  of  society-wide  discrimination  Con-
gress  is  attempting  to  remedy.”°

Congress’  primary  objectives,  as  expressed  in  the
Small  Business  Act  itself,  look  toward  the  future  benefits
to  all  Americans  of eliminating  the  barriers  to  full  partici-
pation  in  our  free  enterprise  system  by socially  and  eco-
nomically  disadvantaged  persons.  See  15  U.S.C.  § 631
(f)(1)(A).  Achieving  such  full  participation  will  not  only
be  a  major  step  in  ending  the  hateful  and  hurtful  legacy
of  racial  and  ethnic  discrimination  in  this  country,  but
will  improve  the  functioning  of  the  national  economy,
ameliorate  the  economic  conditions  of  disadvantaged
groups,  increase  competition  in  federal  procurement  and
better  equip  this  nation  of  diverse  racial  and  ethnic
groups  to  compete  effectively  in  a  truly  diverse  global
economy.  Any  retreat  from  these  noble  (and  as  yet
unfulfilled)  objectives  would  be  a  disgrace  to  and  a  disas-
ter  for  this  nation.

Ill.  THE  DBE  PROVISIONS  AT  ISSUE  HERE  ARE
MORE  NARROWLY  TAILORED  AND  FLEXIBLE
THAN  THE  STATUTORY  PROVISIONS  UPHELD
IN  FULLILOVE

The  argument  that  the  DBE  provisions  of  the  Small
Business  Act  raise  greater  constitutional  concerns  that  the

20  See  generally,  George  R.  LaNoue  &  John  Sullivan,  “But
for”  Discrimination:  How  Many  Minority-Owned  Businesses  Would
There  Be?,  24  Columbia  Human  Rights  L.  Rev.  293  (Winter
1992-93).  Amici  for  Petitioner  have  cited  this  article  favorably
for  its  criticism  of  disparity  studies  such  as  that  conducted  in
Denver.  However,  the  real  thesis  of this  article  is that  there  is no
way  of  proving  how  many  minority-owned  businesses  would
exist  if  discrimination  had  never  occurred.  The  fact  that  this
cannot  be proved  in a narrow  judicial  sense,  however,  does  not
mean  that  Congress  is without  competence  to redress  the effects
of discrimination.  On  the contrary,  it argues  in favor  of granting
Congress  a  considerable  degree  of  lattitude  in  choosing  the
means  of redress.

23

MBE  provisions  upheld  in  Fullilove  is simply  unsupport-
able.  On  the contrary,  as  Justice  Stevens  pointed  out  in his
Fullilove  dissent,  the  truth  is  the  exact  opposite  -  merely
considering  race  as  a  factor  in determining  disadvantage,
as  do  the  DBE  provisions  here,  presents  significantly
fewer  constitutional  concerns  than  did  the  Fullilove  stat-
ute:

[T]he  SBA’s  class  of  socially  or  economically
disadvantaged  persons  neither  included  all  per-
sons  in  the  racial  class  nor  excluded  all  non-
members  of the  racial  class.  Race  was  used  as  no
more  than a factor  in  identifying  the  class  of the
disadvantaged.  The  difference  between  the  stat-
utory  quota  involved  in  this  case  and  the  SBA’s
1968  description  of those  whose  businesses  were
to  be  assisted  under  § 8(a)  of the  Small  Business
Act  is at  least  as  great  as  the  difference  between
the  University  of  California’s  racial  quota  and
the  Harvard  admissions  system  that  Mr.  Justice
POWELL  regarded  as  critical  in  University  of
California  Regents  v.  Bakke,  483  U.S.  265,  315-318,
98  S.  Ct.  2733,  2761-2763,  57  L.  Ed.  2d  750.

Fullilove,  448  U.S.  at  536  n.6  (Stevens,  J.,  dissenting).

The  10%  set-aside  upheld  in  Fullilove  applied  solely
to  businesses  owned  by specified  racial  minorities.  This
provision  was  attacked  both  as  underinclusive  -  since  its
benefits  were  not  extended  to all businesses  that  might be
disadvantaged  -  and  overinclusive  -  since  its  benefits
might  be  received  by  some  minority-owned  businesses
that  were  not  really  disadvantaged.  See  Fullilove,  448  U.S.
at  485-87.  Chief  Justice  Burger  rejected  the  former  argu-
ment  on  the  ground  that  extending  the  statute  to  all
disadvantaged  persons  was  an  appropriate  function  of
Congress,  not  of  the  courts.  Id.  at  485.  The  Chief  Justice
rejected  the  latter  argument  on  the  ground  that  the
administrative  program  implementing  the  MBE  provi-
sions  contained  sufficient  flexibility  to  ensure  that  bene-
fits  would  not  go  to  “spurious  minority-front  entities”  or

24

other  MBEs  that  were  not  bona  fide.  Id.  at  487-88.  More-
over,  a  state  or  local  government  (although  not  a  private
contractor)  could  obtain  a  partial  or  total  waiver  of  the
10%  MBE  set-aside  if  it  could  demonstrate  that  it  was
unable  to  achieve  compliance  despite  its  best  efforts.  Id.
at  488.

As  the  court  of  appeals  and  district  court  correctly
held,  these  standards  demonstrate  that  the  challenged
DBE  provisions  here  are  narrowly  tailored  to  achieve
Congress’  remedial  objectives.  Pet.  App.  22-23,  35-56.
Indeed,  the  DBE  provisions  are  significantly  better  tai-
lored  and  more  flexible  than  the  statute  upheld  in  Ful-
lilove.

First,  unlike  the  Fullilove  statute,  the  DBE  provisions
permit  nonminority  firms  to  qualify  as  DBEs.?!  13  C.F.R.
§ 124.105(c).  This  is  consistent  with  Congress’  intent.  See
1978  U.S.C.C.A.N.  (92  Stat.)  3882.  Firms  owned  by non-
minorities  (for  example,  white  males  who  are  physically
disabled)  have  in  fact  been  certified  as  DBEs  by the  Small
Business  Administration.?2

.

Moreover,  Petitioner  is  incorrect  when  it asserts  that
.  are  entitled  to  a  presumption
“only  racial  minorities  .
of  ‘disadvantage’.”  Pet.  Br.  at  22.  In  fact,  in  the  House
Conference  report  to  the  Surface  Transportation  and  Uni-
form  Relocation  Assistance  Act  of  1987  (STURAA),  Pub.
L.  No.  100-17,  101  Stat.  132,  Congress  expressed  its  intent
that  women  (including  nonminority  women)  also  be
included  in  the  rebuttable  presumption  of  disadvantage.
H.R.  Conf.  Rep.  No.  100-27,  100th  Cong.,  ist  Sess.,
reprinted  in  1987  U.S.C.C.A.N.  132.  Consistent  with  this

21  The  assertion  by  one  amicus  that  “nonminorities  may
never  qualify  as  DBEs”  is  simply  incorrect.  See  Brief  Amicus
Curiae  of  Pacific  Legal  Foundation  in  Support  of  Petitioner  at
17.

22  See,  e.g.,  Neil  Munro,  8(a)  Program  Accused  of Political

Incorrectness,  9 Wash.  Tech.  1, 16  (Sept.  29,  1994).

bat |

25

intent,  the  Subcontracting  Compensation  Clause  (SCC)  at
issue  in this  case  indicates  that  women-owned  businesses
fall  within  the  rebuttable  presumption.  See  Joint  App.  at
14.

Second,  unlike  the  statute  in  Fullilove,  the  DBE  provi-
sions  here  do  not  extend  benefits  to  all  firms  genuinely
owned  by bona  fide  minorities.  Although  an  individual
who  is a  member  of a  minority  group  is  presumed  to  be
socially  disadvantaged,  this  presumption  is  rebuttable.
See  13  C.F.R.  § 124.105(b)  (“In  the  absence  of evidence  to
the  contrary,  the  following  individuals  are  presumed  to
be  socially  disadvantaged  ....  ”).  If evidence  exists  “to
the  contrary,”  the  ultimate  question  is whether  the  indi-
vidual  meets  the  definition  of  “socially  disadvantaged,”
i.e.,  those  individuals  “who  have  been  subjected  to  racial
or  ethnic  prejudice  or  cultural  bias  because  of their  iden-
tity  as  a  member  of  a  group  without  regard  to  their
individual  qualities.”  15  U.S.C.  § 637(a)(5);  13  C.F.R.
§ 124.105(a).

Even  if  an  individual  is  established  to  be  socially
disadvantaged,  moreover,  his  or  her  firm  does  not  auto-
matically  qualify  as  a  DBE.  In  the  first  place,  the  firm
must  qualify  as  a  small  business  concern  within  the
meaning  of  the  Small  Business  Act.  15  U.S.C.
§ 637(a)(4)(A);  13  C.F.R.  § 124.102.  Thus,  a  large  business
owned  by a  minority  can  never  qualify  as  a  DBE.

In  the  second  place,  the  socially  disadvantaged  indi-
vidual  must  also  be  economically  disadvantaged  in  order
to  meet  the  requirements  of  the  DBE  program.  15  U.S.C.
§ 637(a)(6).  In  determining  whether  an  individual  is eco-
nomically  disadvantaged,  the  Small  Business  Administra-
tion  will  consider:

(1)  the  personal  financial  condition  of the  indi-

vidual  claiming  disadvantage;

(2)  the  financial  condition  of  the  individual's

firm;  and

(3)  a firm’s  access  to  credit,  capital  and  mar-

ets.

13  C.RR.  § 124.106(a)(2).23

26

In  short,  it  is  simply  not  true  to  state,  as  Petitioner
does,  that  “[t]he  Small  Business  Act  not  only  employs
race  as  a  factor,  race  is  the  only  factor.”  Pet.  Reply  at  2.
On  the  contrary,  numerous  factors  are  considered  in  mak-
ing the  determination  of DBE  status.  Minority  status  is no
guarantee  of  certification  as  a  DBE;  on  the  contrary,  the
Small  Business  Administration  rejects  many  minority
applicants  for  its  8(a)  program.

Finally,  the  DBE  requirements  at  issue  here  are  at
least  as  flexible  as  the  Fullilove  statute.  Under  Section  502
of  the  Small  Business  Act,  each  federal  agency  partici-
pates  in  setting  its  own  annual  goal  for  participation  by
DBEs.  15  U.S.C.  § 644(g)(1).  If the  agency  fails  to  meet  its
goal,  it  is  required  only  to  provide  an  “appropriate  justi-
fication”  for  this  failure  in  an  annual  report.  15  U.S.C.
§ 644(h)(1).  There  is no  sanction  for  failing  to  meet  a  goal.
Pet.  App.  9  n.7.  Thus,  in  essence  each  agency  is  free  to
grant  itself  a  waiver  of  its  own  goal.

Moreover,  unlike  Fullilove,  this  case  does  not  involve
any  obligation  at  all  imposed  upon  private  contractors.  In
Fullilove  prime  contractors  were  required  to  meet  MBE
subcontracting  goals  and  did  not  even  have  the  right  to
seek  a  waiver  of these  goals.  In  the  case  at bar  no  require-
ment  at  all  has  been  imposed  upon  the  prime  contractor;
it was  merely  offered  an  additional  financial  inducement
to  subcontract  to  DBEs.  If  the  prime  contractor  did  not
wish  to  do  so,  it would  still  retain  its contract  at  the  price
it  had  bid.

23  Similarly,  under  STURAA  Congress  has directed  states  to
make  DBE  determinations  based  upon  minimum  uniform  crite-
ria  including  on-site  visits,  personal  interviews,  licenses,  anal-
ysis  of  stock  ownership,  listing  of  equipment,  analysis  of
bonding  capacity,  listing  of work  completed,  resume  of princi-
pal owners,  financial  capacity  and  type of work  preferred.  Pub.
L.  No.  100-17,  101  Stat.  146  (1987).

27

Petitioner  argues  that  Section  502  of  the  Small  Busi-
ness  Act  enforces  a  “near  limitless  preference”  because
each  federal  agency  is  directed  to  establish  goals  at  a
“maximum  practicable”  level.  Pet.  Br.  at  40-41.  In  fact,
this  is a  wholly  speculative  concern.  There  is no  sugges-
tion  that  any  agency  has  set  its goal at a level  that  exceeds
or  even  approaches  the  percentage  of  socially  and  eco-
nomically  disadvantaged  individuals  in the  population.  If
the  day  comes  when  it  is  “practicable”  for  an  agency  to
do  so,  then  Congress  may  very  well  decide  that  Section
502  and  similar  provisions  are  no  longer  necessary.  Based
on  historical  data,  however,  such  an  occurrence  is  not
likely  to  occur  anytime  in  the  near  future.

IV.  PRINCIPLES  OF  STARE  DECISIS  MILITATE

AGAINST  OVERRULING  FULLILOVE

Accepting  Petitioner’s  arguments  in  the  case  at  bar
would  mean a radical  reduction  in the  scope  of Congress’
authority  to  engage  in  remedial  race-conscious  measures,
as  the  authority  has  been  understood  by Congress  in  the
wake  of  Fullilove  and  subsequent  decisions.  See  1987
U.S.C.C.A.N.  (101  Stat.)  76  (noting  that  the  DBE  provi-
sions  of  the  1982  Surface  Transportation  Assistance  Act
and  the  1987  STURAA  were  modeled  on  the statute  found
valid  in  Fullilove).  Reversal  of  the  court  of  appeals  here
would  mean  the explicit  or  implicit  overruling  of Fullilove
and  later  decisions  reaffirming  it.

NCMB  submits  that  these  precedents  should  not  be
overruled  not  only  because  they  were  correctly  decided
in  the  first  instance  but  because  settled  principles  of stare
decisis  militate  against  it. In determining  whether  to over-
rule  a  prior  holding,  this  Court’s  “judgment  is  custom-
arily  informed  by  a  series  of  prudential  and  pragmatic
considerations  designed  to  test  the  consistency  of  over-
ruling  a  prior  decision  with  the  ideal  of  the  rule  of  law,
and  to  guage  the  respective  costs  of  reaffirming  and
overruling  a  prior  case.”  Planned  Parenthood  v.  Casey,  112

28

S.  Ct.  2791,  2808  (1992).  These  considerations  require
reaffirming,  not  overruling,  Fullilove.

The  most  important  consideration  here  is  that  of
reliance,  “which  counts  the  cost  of a  rule’s  repudiation  as
it would  fall  on  those  who  have  relied  reasonably  on  the
rule’s  continued  application.”  Planned  Parenthood,  112  S.
Ct.  at  2809.  A  major  purpose  of  statutes  such  as  Section
502  of  the  Small  Business  Act,  the  8(a)  program  and  the
DBE  provisions  of  STURAA  is  to  encourage  socially  dis-
advantaged  persons  to  form  businesses  and  encourage
certified  DBEs  to  make  substantial  investments  of  time
and  money  in  expanding  their  operations.  See,  e.g.,  15
U.S.C.  § 631(f)(1)(G).

In  the  14 years  since  Fullilove  was  decided,  thousands
of  DBEs,  including  many  members  of  NCMB,  have  been
formed.  These  DBE  companies  have  invested  many  mil-
lions  of  dollars  in  their  businesses  in  reliance  upon  the
promises  made  to  DBEs  in  the  Small  Business  Act  and
other  statutes.  If it had  not  been  for  these  statutes,  and  for
this  Court’s  decision  in  Fullilove  upholding  Congress’
authority  to  enact  such  legislation,  many  DBEs  would
never  have  been  created,  and  many  investments  would
never  have  been  made  or  would  have  been  otherwise
directed.

An  example  of  the  type  of  reasonable  reliance
induced  by  these  statutes  is  provided  by  the  8(a)  pro-
gram,  which  is intended  not  only  to  help  small  disadvan-
taged  businesses  obtain  federal  contracts  but  also  to
provide  such  businesses  with  direct  management,  techni-
cal,  and  financial  assistance.  The  8(a)  program  recognizes
that  it  takes  many  years  of  assistance  before  disadvan-
taged  businesses  are  prepared  to  “graduate”  and  compete
without  8(a)  assistance.  Congress  has  provided  that  par-
ticipants  may  remain  in  the  program  for  nine  years.  15
U.S.C.  § 636(j)(15).  This  represents  an  increase  in  the
length  of  the  program,  which  was  previously  five  to
seven  years,  because  Congress  recognized  that  a  high

29
Proportion  of  “graduated”  8(a)  firms  were  failing.  It  is
clear,  therefore,  that  the  precipitous  withdrawal  of assis-
tance  from  8(a)  firms  and  other  DBEs  that  have  relied  on
federal  assistance  can  and  will  have  a  devastating  impact
on  their  economic  survival.

Other  considerations  also  militate  against  overruling
Fullilove.  Fullilove’s  holding  has  in  no  sense  proven
“unworkable.”  See  Planned  Parenthood,  112  S.  Ct.  at  2809.
Far  from  creating  many  difficult  cases,  the  standards
contained  in Fullilove  provide  Congress  with  an  appropri-
ate  measure  of flexibility  and  discretion  in addressing  the
issue  of  past  and  present  racial  discrimination.  The  DBE
provisions  at  issue  here  easily  pass  muster  under  these
standards.

Indeed,  it  would  be  the  overruling  of  Fullilove  that
could  very  well  prove  to  be  unworkable.  Congress  has
wrestled  with  and  continues  to  wrestle  with  the  difficult
policy  issues  concerning  disadvantaged  business  enter-
prises,  including  how  to  define  which  persons  are  suffer-
ing  the  effect  of  discrimination,  how  to  ensure  that
previously  excluded  groups  receive  a  real  opportunity  for
equal  participation  in  the  free  enterprise  system,  how  to
direct  benefits  toward  the  truly  disadvantaged  and  how
to  achieve  all  of this  without  imposing  huge  costs  on  the
government  procurement  system.  Petitioner  and  its  amici
would  replace  Congress’  policy  judgments  on  these
issues  with  rigid  rules  of  evidence  that  would  lead  to
massive  litigation  and  at  the  same  time  defeat  Congress’
remedial  purposes.  It would  not  be  long before  the  chill-
ing  effect  of  overturning  Fullilove  would  result  in  the
reversal  of  more  than  20  years  of  slow  but  steady  pro-
gress  in  securing  inclusion  of  minorities  and  women  in
the  federal  procurement  system.

Another  factor  supporting  reaffirmance  of Fullilove  is
that  no  subsequent  decisions  have  developed  principles
of  law  that  undermine  the  original  holding.  See  Planned
Parenthood,  112  S.  Ct  at  2810.  Petitioner  argues  that  this

30

Court’s  holding  in  Croson  undermines  Fullilove,  but  in
fact  Croson  went  to  great  lengths  to  distinguish  Fullilove,
involving  the  power  of  Congress  to  engage  in  remedial
race-conscious  measures,  from  the  issues  presented  by a
state  or  local  government’s  attempt  to  exercise  such
power.  Moreover,  Metro  Broadcasting  reaffirmed  the  appli-
cability  of  Fullilove,  rather  than  Croson,  to  race-conscious
measures  mandated  by  Congress.

Finally,  the  14  years  since  Fullilove  have  not  seen  a
change  in  the  facts  which  underlay  that  decision.  See
Planned  Parenthood,  110  S.  Ct.  at  2811.  As  already  dis-
cussed,  the  effects  of discrimination  have  not  been  eradi-
cated.  The  overruling  of  Fullilove  would  certainly  create
the  public  perception  that  the  only  “fact”  that  has
changed  is  the  membership  of  the  Court.

CONCLUSION

For  the  reasons  set  forth  above,  the  judgment  of  the
Court  of  Appeals  for  the  Tenth  Circuit  should  be
affirmed.

Respectfully  submitted,

AMICI  CURIAE,  Maryland  Women  Business  Entrepreneurs
Association,  et.  al.,  state  the  following  in  support  of  their
Motion  for Leave  to File Amicus  Brief:

1.  The  parties  granted  Amici  consent  to  file  a  brief  in
support  of Respondents.  The  consents  of both  parties  are  on
file with  the Clerk  of this Court.

2.

Amici  filed  their  brief on  December  6,  1994.  It was
timely  filed  under  Rule  29.2  which  states  that  briefs  may  "be
sent  to the Clerk  by first-class  mail,  postage  prepaid,  and  bear
a  postmark  showing  that  the  document  was  mailed  on  or
(See  Affidavit  of  Service
before  the  last  day  for  filing."
attached  as  Exhibit  1.)

3.  Ina  Scheduling  Order,  the  Court  established a filing
deadline  of December  6, 1994  at  3:00  p.m.  and  suspended  the
Amici  had  no
language  of  Rule  29.2  quoted  above.

knowledge  of the  rule  suspension  for  the  manner  of  filing
amicus  briefs.

4.

Counsel  for  Amici  had  previously  confirmed  with
counsel  for  Respondent  that  the  filing  date  was  December  6,
1994.
Counsel  for  Respondent  failed  to  inform  counsel  for
Amici  of the  suspension  of the  rule  relating  to  the  manner  of
filing.

5.

Amici’s  brief  was  not  received  by the  Court  on  or

before  3:00  p.m.  on  December  6, 1994.

6.  Had  Amici  known  of this rule  suspension,  they would
have  personally  filed  their  brief  with  the  Clerk  on  or  before
3:00  p.m.  on  December  6, 1994.

7.

Amici’s  brief addresses  issues  raised  by Petitioner  in
its Petition  for Writ  of Certiorari  as  they relate  to women  and
(See  copy  of  Amici’s  brief
women-owned  businesses.
attached  as Exhibit  2.)

8.  The  Court's  review  of  Amici’s  brief  will  aid  it  in
understanding  the  statutory  and  regulatory  framework  of the
legislation  in  question  and  in  fully  understanding  how  past
precedents  regarding  gender  discrimination  relate  to  the
questions  presented.

9.  Amici  are not aware  that any other party or  Amici are
addressing  these  issues  relating  to  women-owned  businesses.
Therefore,  the  failure  to  grant  this  motion  may  result  in  an
opinion  that  does  not  fully  and  fairly  address  the  impact  on
women-owned  businesses.

Wherefore,  Amici  Curiae  request  that  this  Court  enter  an

Order  giving leave to Amici  to file their

brief.

The Coalition  for Economic  Equity  is an ad hoc group,
composed  of individuals  and organizations  who joined forces in the
spring of 1983 to address  the problem of access to San Francisco's
contracting  by  minority-owned  and  women-owned  businesses.
Many of  the members  supported  the initial request of two members
of the Board of Supervisors  to have the Human  Rights  Commission
of  the City and County  of San Francisco  conduct  hearings  on
practices  of  City authorities  of seeking  outside  contractors  to
Others
provide  specific  goods  and  services  for  their  agencies.
joined  the Coalition  when the results  of those  hearings  were
announced:  less than 3% of city contracts in the previous  year had
been awarded  to either  minority  or women prime  contractors.

The  Coalition  has  worked  to  inform  the  general  public
about the nature  of the problem  and the proposed  solutions.  The
Coalition  advocates  in other forms  in support  of similar  programs.
Indeed,  the Coalition intervened  in Associated  General  Contractors
of California  v. Coalition  for Economic  Equity,  950  F.2d  1401
(9th  Cir.  1991).

The  Mid-Peninsula  Minority  Contractors  Association
(MPMCA)  is  a  group  of  local  minority  contractors,  bonding
together,  as in times  past, to assist and participate  in the rebuilding
and  redevelopment
communities.
MPMCA  is  dedicated  to the proposition  that its members  should
attempt  to perform  the work  in their community  that has usually
been performed by outsiders.

of  minority  low-income

This  organization  has come  together  to participate  in the
economic  and structural development of its community.  MPMCA
seeks  to  provide  guidance,  construction  education,
project
estimates,  information  sources,  and  quality  assurance  in  local
construction  projects.  MPMCA  fully expects  that the organization
and its members  will  be competing  for contracts  under  § 502 of
the Small  Business  Act; § 106 of the Surface Transportation and
Uniform  Relocation  Assistance  Act of 1987  and  similar  programs.

SUMMARY  OF THE  ARGUMENT

ARGUMENT

Petitioner  is  requesting  that  this  Court  overrule  its

decisions  in Eeiioze_2.  ee 448  U.S. 448 ee  ma

497 U.S. 547 (1990).  em  ae see ae penny  ee
not a doctrine  that should  be lightly  cast aside.  Precedents
of this
Court  have established  factors  to be considered  for determining  the
appropriateness  of overruling  or  reconsidering  earlier  decisions,
and petitioner  has not shown that those factors  are present  in this
instance.  A review  of the factors  this Court has established  as
guideposts  for establishing  the propriety  for the reconsideration  or
overruling  of previous  decisions  indicates  that Fullilove  and Metro
are well outside  the standards  embodied  in the various  factors,  and
therefore  that these cases should  not be overruled.

Fullilove  and Metro  constitute  no departure  from  recent
race-conscious  jurisprudence  established  by this Court starting  with
Regents  of the University  of California  v. Bakke,  438  U.S.  265
(1978).  In addition,  a  reversal  of Fullilove  and  Metro  would
frustrate  substantial  reliance  on these opinions  in the area of race
conscious  relief.  Finally,  there
has been no indication  that  either
of  these decisions  have  been ineffective,  are out of step with  the
times,  or should  be revisited  in light  of new information.

This  legislation  is a valid  exercise  of congressional  power
under  the enforcement  clauses  of the Civil  War  Amendments,  and
thus  calls  for unusual  judicial  deference  upon  review.
The
enforcement  clauses  grant  Congress  broad authority  to address
racial  inequities.  When  doing  so,  Congress  has wide  rein  to
choose  means  with  which to proceed.

A review  of local  contracting  studies  reveals  that in the
absence  of affirmative  action  programs  minority  businesses  are
The  need  for
regularly  underutilized,  given  their  availability.
legislation  to eradicate  discrimination  in government  contracting  is
well-documented,  and is still  very much present.  Programs  such
as this  one are a necessary  and effective  step toward  eradicating
the effects  of racial discrimination.

Finally,  the  delegation  to  state  and  federal  agencies  of
authority  to set the precise  terms  of the preferences  at issue is well
bounds.  Such  delegation  contributes  to
within  constitutional
precision  in tailoring  the program  to the affected  communities.

2

The  statute  and  its application  in this  instance  are  clearly
constitutional  under  the  decisions  of this  Court,  and  there  is no
For  the
compelling  justification  for  overruling  these  decisions.
following  reasons,  the  decision  of the  Tenth  Circuit  in this  case
should  be affirmed.

I.

_V.  ELUTZNICK  AND

THE  NECESSARY  HEAVY  BURDEN  IMPOSED  FOR
THIS  COURT  TO  DISAVOW  ITS  DECISIONS  IN
FULLILOVE
BROADCASTING, INC,  V,  FEDERAL
COMMUNICATIONS  COMMISSION  HAS  NOT
BEEN  MET  IN  THIS  CASE,  AND  THEREFORE,
RECONSIDERATION  OF  THOSE  DECISIONS  IS
AND  UNWARRANTED.
INAPPROPRIATE

This Court  has stated many times that the doctrine  of stare
decisis  is  one that should  not be lightly  cast aside.  See,  e.g.,
Arizona
_v. Rumsey,  467  U.S.  203,  212  (1984);  Vasquez v,
Hillery,  474  U.S.  254, 266 (1986);  Dlinois  Brick Co. v. Illinois,
431  U.S.  720,  736  (1977).

In fact, the doctrine  of stare decisis  was very  recently
defined  by this  Court  in Vasquez,  supra  at 266, as being  "[tjhe
means  by  which  we ensure  that the law will not merely  change
erratically,  but  will  develop  in  a  principled  and  intelligent
manner."  The Court went on to discuss  what was meant  by that
definition  by stating  that the doctrine  thereby  allows  society  to rest
assured  that certain  legal  principles  would  be firmly  established
and not subject  to the changing  whims  of personal  or popular
trends.  Jd.  Thus  the Court reiterated  the fact that legal precedent
is controlling  in the judicial  sphere  of this society  rather  than
people.  Principle  rather than personnel  must be the controlling
factor.  These  statements  must be considered  within  the context  of

the present  case.'

In this case there is nothing  jn constitutional  jurisprudence
that  mandates  a  departure  from  this  doctrine  of stare  decisis.
Indeed,  the issue that has been placed  before  this Court is one that
in no way  fits the traditional  standards  that  this  Court  has
fashioned  for the overruling of decisions.

In Monell v, New York Dept, of Social Services,  436 U.S.
658,  695-701  (1978),  this  Court  articulated  four  factors  to  be
considered  for determining  whether  decisions  should be overruled.
The factors  discussed  in Monell  are  as follows:  whether  the
decision  constituted  a  departure  from  prior  decisions,  whether
overruling  these decisions  would  be inconsistent  with more  recent
expressions  of  congressional  intent,  whether  overruling  these
decisions  would  frustrate  legitimate reliance on these holdings,  and
whether  the decisions  in question  misconstrued  the meaning  of the
Statute  as revealed in the legislative  history.?  A careful  analysis
of these factors  compels  the determination  that reconsideration  and
overruling  of  Fullilove  and  Metro  is  inappropriate  and
unwarranted.

A.

The Decisions  In Fullilove  and Metro  Do Not Constitute
A Departure  From  Recent  Decisions  Of This Court.

Fullilove  has  been  the  seminal  decision  defining  the
appropriate  boundaries  of congressional  power  and duty to attack
and eradicate  all remnants  of slavery  and discriminatory  practices
perpetuated  on various  minority  groups.  Indeed,  Fullilove  has
been  cited repeatedly  as the case that has outlined  and defined  the

'The fact that this case involves  a  constitutional  challenge  does not
alter the fact that the strictures  of stare decisis  are to be observed  absent
special justification.  See, ¢.g., Arizona  v. Rumsey,  467 U.S.  203,  212
(1984);  Planned Parenthood v. Casey, _U.S.__,  __,  112  S.Ct.  2791,
2808-2809,  120  L.Ed.2d  674  (1992);  Payne
v. Tennessee,  501  U.S.  _
»  —»  111  S.Ct.  2597,  2617-2618,  115  L.Ed.2d  (1991);  Welch v. Texas
Dept.  of Highways  and Public  Transportation,
483 U. S.  468,  479
(1987).

"Because

this case involves  issues  of constitutional jurisprudence, this

factor  will not be addressed.

.

B

Bridge

Construct

parameters
of federal attempts  to remedy  discriminatory  practices
and the monument  as to the  limits  on race conscious  relief.
Compare City of Richmond y._1.A. Croson. Co., 488 U.S.  469,
490 (1989);  Harri
981  F.2d 50, 56-58 (2d Cir. 1992).  ilies Sate Git quldapest for
fourteen
and one half years,  and it has been  the basis  for
innumerable  attempts  to  address  problems  of  minority
underrepresentation.  The decision  in Fullilove  certainly  was not
a  casual  or incidental  holding.  Furthermore,
there  has been  an
enormous  opportunity  to reject  the holding  in this decision,  yet
there  have  been no steps taken in that direction  until the granting
of this Petition  for a  Writ  of  Certiorari.  See,  ¢.g.,  Ellis v.
Skinner, 961 F.2d  912  (10th  Cir.),  cert,  denied,  sub nom.  Ellis
y. Card,
U.S.  ___,  113  S.Ct.  374,  121  L.Ed.  2d 286  (1992);
Milwaukee  County  Pavers  Ass’n v, Fiedler,  922 F.2d 419  (7th
Cir.),  cert. denied, 501  U.S.  _,  111  S.Ct.  2261,  114  L.Ed.2d
714  (1991).

Similarly,  Metro was decided  four years ago and Fullilove

provided  a  large  part  of the  doctrinal  basis  for  the  decision.

allow one to opine that ether decision  should  be wrenched  from  its
position  of bedrock  law allowing  Congress  to attempt  to remedy
a rather  egregious  portion  of our history  and its mournful  legacy.

Il.

IS A VALID  EXERCISE  OF
THIS LEGISLATION
CONGRESSIONAL  POWER  UNDER  THE
ENFORCEMENT  PROVISIONS  OF  THE
AND  FOURTEENTH  AMENDMENTS
THIRTEENTH
TO  THE  UNITED  STATES  CONSTITUTION.

The enforcement  clauses  of the Civil  War  Amendments
grant  Congress  broad  power  to address  racial  inequities  in this
country.  Indeed,  it is established  that these amendments  impose
upon Congress  the responsibility
to do so: “Congress  properly  may
—  and  indeed  must  —  address  directly  the  problems  of
discrimination  in  our  society.”
Fullilove  at  499  (Powell,  J.,
concurring).

As discussed  below,  when  Congress  acts  under  the
enforcement  clauses  it employs  the broadest  remedial  power  of any
governmental  organ.
The  well-established  need  for  judicial
deference  to the decisions  of Congress  is correspondingly  strongest
in this context.  The  legislation  at issue in this case  is a  valid
exercise  of congressional  power under the enforcement  clauses  of
the Thirteenth  and Fourteenth  Amendments;  to overturn  it would
be to infringe

upon Congress’s  constitutionally  mandated  role.

A.

The  Enforcement  Clauses  of  the  Civil  War
Amendments  Grant  Broad  Power  to Congress  and
Require  Unusually  Strong  Judicial  Deference.

and Fifteenth  Amendments  to
The Thirteenth,  Fourteenth,
the  United  States  Constitution  were  enacted  in rapid  succession
with  a  common goal:  “the construction
of a penumbra  of legal
commands  ...  designed  to raise the race of freedmen  from  the
status  of inferior  beings  —  a status  imposed  by the  system  of
chattel  slavery  —  to that of free men and women,  equal participants
in the hitherto  white  political  community  consisting  of the  ‘people

of the  United  States’."*  This court has referred to “freedom  of
of the newly-made  freeman  and
the  slave  race  ...  and  protection
citizen  from the oppressions  of those  who had formerly  exercised
dominion  over him" as the “pervading  purpose”  found  in these
Cases,  83 U.S.  (16  Wall.)
three  amendments.  Slaughter-House
36, 71 (1872).  Central  to the fulfillment  of this common  purpose
is  each  amendment’s  explicit  grant  of authority  to Congress  to
enforce  the amendment’s  guarantees  through  legislation.

For  more  than  2  century,  the Supreme  Court  has
consistently  held that the enforcement  provisions  constitute  broad
grants  of  power  to Congress  to fulfill  the purposes  of the Civil

War Amendments.’  The Civil  War Amendments
understood  as effecting  a  dramatic  power shift:  the amendments
broadly  expanded  the  power  of  the  federal  government,  while
Virginia,

Virginia,  100 U.S.  339  (1879)  (the enforcement
provisions  as  a  group);  Katzenbach
v. Morgan,  384  U.S.  641  (1966)
(section  5 of the Fourteenth  Amendment);  Fullilove,  supra  (section  5 of
the Fourteenth  Amendment  (Burger,  C.J.,  joined
by White,  J.  and
Powell,  J.,  plurality  opinion));  Id. at 500-01 (Powell, J.,  concurring);
City of Richmond v, Croson, supra at 488-89 (1989) (section 5 of the

100  U.S.  339,  345  (1879)  (the  Civil  War  Amendments  were

Richmond v.
LA.  Croson,  supra  at  521-22  (1989)  (Scalia,  J.,  concurring).
These  enlargements  of the power  of Congress  authorize  the
legislation  at issue  here,  and  require  unusual  judicial  deference  to
congressional
judgments  about ends and means in the exercise of
this  power.

This  Court  traditionally  is highly deferential  when  passing
on  the validity  of  any act of Congress,  even  when constitutional
values  are  at  issue.
Justice  Holmes  referred  to  constitutionality
review  of acts of the national  legislative  branch as “the gravest and
most  delicate  duty  that  this Court  is called  on to perform.”
Blodgett
vy. Holden,  275 U.S.  142,  148  (1927)  (Holmes,  J., joined
by Brandeis,  J., Sanford,  J., and  Stone,  J., concurring).*  Unlike
when  this  Court is reviewing  the acts of a state or local political
body,  in this  context  a  finding  of unconstitutionality
by the
Supreme  Court  would upset final action  taken by co-equal  branches
of  government.
Congressional  legislation  such  as  the  Small
Business  Act and the Surface  Transportation  Assistance  Act is the
considered  judgment  of  both  houses  of  Congress,  and has been
approved  by  the  President  —  thus  the  long  history  of judicial
deference  and the presumption  of constitutionality  is appropriate  in

First  Amendment  claim, Supreme
421  U.S.  94,  102 (1973) (im evaluating
Court  “must afford  great weight  to the decisions  of Congress.");  Rostker
vy. Goldberg,

C.B.S.);  United States v. National  Dairy Products  Corp., 372 U.S.  29,
32  (1963)  (“strong  presumptive
to an Act of

Congress");  Walters v, National Association  of  Radiation Survivors, 468
U.S.  1323,  1324  (1984)  (*[P]}resumption
of constitutionality  ... attaches
to every Act of Congress"  (Rehnquist, J., as Circuit Justice));  Fullilove,
supra at 472 (quoting  Blodgett  with approval);  Morrison
v. Olson,  487
U.S.  654,  704 (1988)  ("Where  a private  citizen  challenges  action  of the
Government  ...  harmonious  functioning  of the system  demands  that we
ordinarily  give  some  deference,  or a  presumption  of validity,  to the
actions  of the  political  branches  ...."  (Scalia,  J., dissenting)).

this  instance.’

This traditional  deference  is even greater  when  Congress
acts in the area of race relations;  here the Civil  War Amendments’
enforcement  provisions  give  Congress  special  authority  to act,
above and beyond  its normal  powers.  "These  are of course not the
power to enact civil rights laws  ....
only  sources of congressional
the original  grants,  were
But the post-Civil  War  additions,  unlike
born of a special  concern  with racial discrimination;  and the civil
rights  powers  they  confer  are  potentially  the  most  far-reaching."
G.  Gunther,  Constitutional Law, Twelfth  edition,  878-79  (1991).
See also Oregon
v. Mitchell,  400  U.S.  112,  129  (1970)  (“Where
Congress  attempts  to remedy  racial  discrimination  under its
enforcement  powers,  its authority  is enhanced  by the  avowed
intention  of  the framers  of the Thirteenth,  Fourteenth, and
Fifteenth  Amendments.”  (Black,  J.)).

As such, judicial  scrutiny  of  congressional  action  in the
area of race relations  is correspondingly  more  limited  than in other
Justice  O’Connor  has  noted  that  “Congress  has
contexts.
considerable  latitude,  presenting  special  concerns  for judicial

"In addition,  this Court has repeatedly  held that Congress  has broad
power  under  the Spending  Clause to distribute  funds as it sees fit. Again,
judicial  deference  is called  for:  “The  discretion  [in exercise  of  the
spending  power]  ...  is not confided  in the courts.  The discretion  belongs
to Congress,  unless  the choice  is clearly  wrong,  a display  of arbitrary
power,  not an exercise  of judgment.  This is familiar law."  Helvering v.
Davis,  301  U.S.  619,  640  (1937).
Congress’s  choices  may  only  be
overturned  by the courts  if  “by no reasonable  possibility  can  the

to Congress."  Id. at 644.

Congress  is also vested  with a special  responsibility  in spending
matters  to ensure  that its money  does not contribute  to the perpetuation
of racial inequities.  “Simple  justice  requires  that public funds,  to which
all taxpayers  of all races contribute,  not be spent in sny fashion  which
encourages,  entrenches,  subsidizes,  or results  in racial  discrimination.“
Lav _y.
This  Court  has  long
Nichols,  414  U.S.  563,  569  (1974).
recognized  the strong  governmental  interest  in ensuring  that state funds
public  or private.  Sec,  ¢.g.,
do not support  racial discrimination,
Norwood
v. City  of
vy, Harrison,  413  U.S.  455  (1973);  Gilmore

review,  when it exercises  its ‘unique  remedial  powers  ...  under §
5 of the Fourteenth  Amendment."  Metro at 3030,  quoting  Croson
at  488  (emphasis  added).  This  Court’s  posture  when  reviewing
congressional  acts in the area of race relations  is akin to its stance
in another  area of special  deference:  review  of congressional
actions relating  to the military.  See, ¢.g,, Rostker  v, Goldberg,
453  U.S.  57, 68 (1981)  ("In  deciding  the question  before  us we
must be particularly  careful not to substitute  our judgment  of what
is desirable  for that  of Congress,  or our own evaluation  of
evidence  for a  reasonable  evaluation  of the legislative  branch.").

Congress  needs  wide  rein with  which  to further  this
overriding  interest;  judicial  scrutiny  is correspondingly  narrower.
The Court should  keep these  concerns  in mind when it undertakes
its  review  of congressional
action  in this case.

of  Congressional
the  Thirteenth

Under

The  Thirteenth  Amendment  abolished  slavery  in  this
country.”  Of course,  Congress’s  areas of authority  under the
enforcement  provision  of the Thirteenth  Amendment  "extend  far
beyond  the actual imposition  of slavery  or involuntary  servitude.”
Griffin v,
Breckenridge, 403 U.S. 88, 105 (1971).  Since
the Civil
Rights  Cases, supra, it has been clear that Congress  has the power
to pass legislation
to eradicate  all “badges  and incidents
of slavery
..+»”  Jd, at 20. It is Congress,  not any court, that makes the final
determination  as to what constitutes  a badge or incident  of slavery;
this determination  is subject only to rationality  review by courts.
"[Clongress  has the power  under the Thirteenth  Amendment
rationally  to determine  what are the badges  and the incidents  of
Slavery,  and the authority  to translate  that determination  into
effective  legislation.”  Jones
v. Mayer,  392  U.S.  409,  440  (1968);
Griffin,  supra at 105.  The Mayer court went on to uphold  the

"Neither

slavery  nor involuntary  servitude,  except as a punishment
for crime whereof the party shall have been duly convicted,  shall exist
within  the United  States,  or any place  subject  to their  jurisdiction."

congressional  legislation  at  issue  after  applying  a  rational-basis

Congress

that dispensation
could rationally  have  decided
of  government  funds  was an appropriate  area for exercise  of its
Thirteenth  Amendment  powers.  Government
action  such  as
contract  awards  possesses  strong  symbolic  value:  even  decisions
that are small  in dollar  amount  carry  the state’s  imprimatur.
Systematic  underutilization  of available  disadvantaged  business —
usually  minorities
and hurtful
and women  — sends  a destructive
message,  one that resonates  with the history  of societal  devaluation
that  the Civil  War  Amendments  were  aimed  at eradicating.
Congress  could rationally  have  concluded  that it didn’t  want this
message  sent, and employed  its Thirteenth  Amendment  power  to
ensure  that it wouldn't be."

Of course,  it is axiomatic  that there  is no state action
requirement  for Congress  to act under  the Thirteenth  Amendment;
the amendment’s  prohibition  of slavery  obviously  applies to private
individuals.  If Congress,  using  its section  2 power,  can right the
wrongs  of —  and exert control  over —  purely  private  individuals  (as
it  did with  Supreme  Court  approval  in Maver,  supra,  Griffin,
v, McCrary,  427  U.S.  160  (1976),  it must
supra,  and  Runyon
necessarily  be able to do the same with regard to federal  and state
agencies.
Here,  Congress  has taken steps to ensure  that these
agencies  fairly utilize disadvantaged  businesses.  Thus, this statute
is clearly  valid as an exercise  of the broad  grant  of authority
conferred  by section  2 of the Thirteenth  Amendment.

"Note  that Congress  need not explicitly state in the legislation  at issue
that it is acting  under its Civil  War  Amendment  enforcement  powers.
See  EEOC  v. Wyoming, 460 U.S. 226,  243  (1983) (Congress
need not
“recite  the words  ‘section  5’  or ‘Fourteenth  Amendment’  or ‘equal
protection’”  in order to utilize  its section  5 powers).  This comports with
general  principles  of court  review  of congressional  legislation:  “The ...
constitutionality  of action taken  by Congress  does not depend  on recitals
v. Lloyd  W.
of the power  which it undertakes  to exercise."  Woods
Miller  Co., 333  U.S  138,  144  (1948).

The Legislation  at Issue in this Case is a  Valid  Exercise
the  Fourteenth
of  Congressional
Amendment.

Power

Under

Similar

to section  2 of the Thirteenth  Amendment,  section
5  of  the  Fourteenth  Amendment  is a  broad  grant  of  power,
conferring  upon Congress  wide discretion  to determine  both which
areas  require  attention  in order  to fulfill  the  promise  of the
Fourteenth  Amendment,  and the best  means by which to proceed.
"Correctly  viewed,  § 5 is a  positive  grant  of legislative  power
authorizing  Congress  to exercise  its discretion  in determining
whether  and what  legislation  is needed  to secure  the guarantees  of
the  Fourteenth  Amendment."  Katzenbach
v. Morgan,  384  U.S.
641,  651  (1966).  This  statement
of broad  congressional  power
was reaffirmed  in Fullilove, supra at (Burger,  C.J.,  joined  by
White,  J.  and Powell,  J.,  plurality opinion),  and in City of
Richmond v, Croson, supra at 490 (O’Comnor,  J.,  joined  by
Rehnquist,  C.J.  and White,  J., plurality  opinion).

In exercising its section 5 power, Congress is not bound by
judicial  determinations  as to what constitutes  an equal  protection
violation.  Katzenbach,  supra at 648."*  As with the Thirteenth
Amendment,  Congress  is free  to make  its own determinations  as
to which areas need its attention  in order to fulfill  the promise  of
the  Fourteenth  Amendment.  In rejecting  arguments  to the

As  with  the  Thirteenth  Amendment,  courts  review
Congress's  determinations  of this nature  under  a highly  deferential

akin to rationality  review.  “It was for Congress ... to

Congress’s  affirmative  power  under  the  Fourteenth
Amendment  parallels  that granted  by section  2 of the Fifteenth
Amendment,  an analogous  enforcement  provision.”  In City of
Rome vy, United States, 446  U.S.  156,  177  (1980),  this  Court
upheld  congressional  action  under  section  2  of  the  Fifteenth
Amendment  that was not aimed  at remedying  past violations  of
section 1. Jd. at 177.  Similarly,  under section  5 of the Fourteenth
Amendment,  Congress  doesn’t  have to wait for a violation  of the
Amendment's  other provisions  in order to act; the enforcement
clause  grants  power  to take  proactive  steps  to further  the
amendment’s  goals.  This comports  with the original understanding
of the Republican  dominated  Congress  that drafted  and ratified  the
Fourteenth  Amendment,  as demonstrated  by their passage  of the
various  Freedmen’s  Bureau  Acts, providing  affirmative  aid to ex-

"It is important  to note that there is no state action requirement  for
congress  to act under its section  5 power.  Congress’s
role in furthering
the aims of the Fourteenth  Amendment  is not dependent  on the behavior
of states and localities.  On the contrary,  section  5 grants  Congress  the
power  to take  affirmative  steps  to advance  the Amendment’s  goals.
Congressional  power  under  section  5 “may  at times also  include  the
power  ...  to adopt prophylactic  rules to deal with [situations  in which the
‘principles
supra  at 490,
of equality’  are threatened)]."  Croson,
(O’Connor,  J., joined by Rehnquist,  C.J.  and White, J.,  plurality  opinion
(citing with approval  Katzenbech,  supra  at 651)).

slaves."

contracting,
with its well-documented  history  of underutilization of

Amendment.  As noted  above,  Congress  need not explicitly  note

clauses

of the Constitution.  See § I,  B, Ante.
Even if there were a state action  requirement,  it would  be
met in this instance  because  over  eighty  percent  of the funds that
reach private  individuals  under this  legislative  program  first go
of STAA
state  governments.  See,  ¢.g., the application
through
and  STURAA™  at issue in Ellis v. Skinner,  supra (State  of Utah,
as a condition  of receipt  of federal  highway  funds,  complied  with
the  DBE  program  established  by  STAA  and  renewed  by
STURAA).

Although  no state received  or distributed  federal  funds in
petitioners’  scenario  —  funds  went  through  CFLHD  directly  to
Mountain  Gravel  & Construction
Company  — such a situation is
the  exception  rather  than the rule under  this program.  However,
petitioners  have  challenged  this  statute as a whole.  The  role  of
State agencies  in acting  as a  conduit  for federal  funds,  and in
making  the final  contracting  decisions  in most  cases,  clearly
establishes  a level of involvement  that would satisfy  a state action
requirement.  Therefore,  this legislation  is fully  authorized  under
Congress’s  section  5 powers,  and thus calls for a highly  deferential
posture  upon  judicial  review.

14

D.

Congress  Has Chosen  a  Permissible  Means  With  Which
to Act Under  the Enforcement  Clauses
of the Civil  War
Amendments.

Congress  has wide  discretion  as to the means  to be
employed  in remedying  the effects of past discrimination,  and in
enacting  prophylactic  measures  to  prevent  future  discrimination.
Almost  half a  century  ago,  this Court pointed  out that “[iJn  no
matter  should  we  pay more  deference  to the opinion  of  Congress
than in its choice of instrumentalities to perform a function that is
within
its power."  National  Mutual  Insurance  Co, v, Tidewater
Transfer  Co., 337  U.S.  582, 603 (1949)  Jackson,  J., joined  by
Black,  J., and Burton,  J., plurality opinion).  As discussed  above,
judicial  deference  to  congressional
is  particularly
appropriate  when  Congress  is utilizing  its broad grants  of  power
under the enforcement  clauses  of the Civil War Amendments.”
There  is a natural  tension  between  the well-established
deference  to congressional  choices  described  above  and the need
to scrutinize  Congress  for equal  protection  violations.  In Metro,
supra,  this Court resolved  that tension  by applying  intermediate
scrutiny  to non-remedial  congressional  action that classified  on  the
basis of race.  Metro at 3008.  The factors  arguing  for relaxed
scrutiny  are even stronger  here than they  were  in Metro.

choices

First,  this  program  benefits  disadvantaged  businesses,
whether  or not the disadvantaged  business  is woman-  or minority-
owned.  The Court-approved
program  in Metro,  by comparison,
disbursed  governmental  benefits  on race-based  lines.  Second,  the
legislation  at issue in this case  is authorized  by the enforcement
clauses  of the Thirteenth  and Fourteenth  Amendments,  as discussed
above.  The  enforcement  clauses  give  Congress  much  greater
power  than that of the states  as to choice  of means with which  to
vy. Mow Sun Wong,  426  U.S.
remedy  discrimination.  Hampton
88,  100  (1976)  ("[O]verriding  national  interests  ...  may  justify

“See  Metro,  supra  at 3030:  “Congress  has considerable  latitude,
presenting  special  concerns  for judicial  review,  when  it exercises  its
‘unique  remedial  powers  ...  under  § 5 of the Fourteenth  Amendment.”
(O’Connor,  J.,  joined  by Rehnquist,  C.J.  and White,  J.,  plurality  opinion
(quoting  Croson,  supra, at 488)).

selective  legislation that would  be  unacceptable  for  an  individual
state.").

Validity  as an exercise  of enforcement  clause  power  sets
the statutes  apart from the program  at issue  in Metro;  the lack of
a  section  5 justification  for the Metro  program  was  a  key
component
of Justice  O’Connor’s  dissent.  See Metro  at 3030-31.
These  two points  about the  legislation  involved  here  —  its race-
level and its status  as an
neutrality  at the contract-distribution
exercise  of  enforcement  clause  power  —  make  Congress’s  choice
of means  much  less  controversial  than  the Court-approved  choice
in Metro.’”

The extra latitude  granted  Congress  by the enforcement
Clauses  can, in some circumstances,  authorize  even race-conscious
congressional  measures.  “[W]e  reject  the contention
that in the
remedial  context  the Congress  must act in a wholly  ‘color-blind’
matter.”  Fullilove,

Ill.

WITHOUT  MINORITY  BUSINESS  ENTERPRISE
PROGRAMS  AS  EMBODIED  IN  THE  SURFACE
TRANSPORTATION  ASSISTANCE  ACT  OF  1982
AND  THE  SMALL  BUSINESS  ACT,  MBEs  WOULD
CONTINUE  TO OPERATE  AT  A DISADVANTAGE.

Minority  Business  Enterprise  (MBE)  programs  have  had a
significant  positive  impact  on  the  ability  of  minority  owned
businesses  to survive  and,  sometimes,  to thrive  in today’s
economy.  Without  these programs,  many  MBEs  would  be unable
to  compete,  thereby  perpetuating  the  economic  disadvantage
Experience  and  logic
experienced  in  minority  communities.
demonstrate  that barriers  still exist  which exclude  MBEs  from
contracting  opportunities  available  to  similarly  situated  non-
minority  businesses.  As a result,  MBEs  remain  underutilized as
government  contractors.  This,  in turn,  reduces competition
in the
marketplace.

An examination  of programs  which  have been in effect
shows  not only that they have been effective  in raising  minority
participation  in contracting  opportunities,  but that race neutral
policies
are
insufficient  to remedy  the inequality  faced  by MBEs.

to  eliminate  discriminatory

intended

barriers

Existing  MBE  programs  have  been  a  positive  social

"14  Stat.,  c.  200,  at  173-177  (1866).

*See Brief of  the NAACP  Legal Defense  and Educational  Fund, Inc.,
as Amicus  Curiae,  10-53,  in Regents of the University of California v.
Bakke,  supra (No. 76-811,  October  1976  Term).

they  raise  minority  employment  and  open  up
development:
opportunities  previously  denied to a large sector of society  because
of  race  or  ethnicity.  The  SBA  program  (15  U.S.C.  § 644(g))
adopted  by  Congress  as implemented  by the  Federal  Lands
Highway  Program  Transportation  through  the  subcontracting
compensation  clause  program,  is a  desirable,  appropriate,  cost-
effective,
constitutional,  and  necessary  method  to  remedy
discrimination  against  MBEs.

A.

Barriers  To  Equality  In Contracting  Continue  To Exist.

Despite  efforts to eliminate  discrimination  in chis country,
MBEs  continue  to be disadvantaged  as compared  to non-minority
contractors  in obtaining  contracting  opportunities.  Existence of
laws  and regulations  outlawing  discrimination  have  not yet leveled
the playing  field.

Below,  several  utilization  studies  commissioned  by public
entities  in the San Francisco  Bay Area  and across  the country
following  this  Court's  decision  in City of Richmond  v. J.A.
Croson  Co.,  supra  are  examined.”
These  studies  review
governmental  contracting  awards  to assess  the need  for  MBE

Redevelopment  Agency  (SFRA)  study  found  that  a  sizable

they had experienced  discrimination  during  the previous  five years.

Moreover,  evidence  of continuing  barriers  against  MBEs
in the  absence  of  affirmative  action  programs  has  been
demonstrated  in the  studies  analyzed  in this  brief.  MBEs
consistently  report  that  their  only  subcontracting  opportunities
come  with government  programs  requiring  affirmative  action. The
San Francisco  Redevelopment  Agency  reported  that over 75%  of
the  95 M/WBE  construction  firm  owners  who responded  to its
surveys  indicated  that prime  contractors  that use their firms to
meet  M/WBE  requirements  on  public-sector  projects,  seldom  or
never  use their firms  on projects  that  do not  have  M/WBE
in Hayward,  California,  the

study  found that approximately  50% of the survey  respondents  are
seldom  or never  used  by prime contractors  for projects that do not
have  M/WBE  goal requirements.  Hayward  Report,  Table 6.5  An
even  higher  percentage  of those  surveyed  in the Contra  Costa
County  study reported  that they were not used on projects  without
MBE requirements.  Sixty-two  percent  reported  they were  never
used  on  projects  without  MBE requirements  and an additional  23 %
reported  they  were  only  used  infrequently.
Table  6.4  at  219.
Similarly,
in  Milwaukee,  Wisconsin  the  study  found  that
approximately  75  percent  of  black-owned  businesses  and  32
percent  of  Hispanic-owned  businesses  affirmed  the existence  of
racially  motivated  obstacles  within  the  Milwaukee  construction
marketplace.  M.F.  Rice,  Id. at 486.  These  findings  establish  that
without  affirmative  action  programs  many  MBEs  would find it
extremely  difficult  to survive.

These  findings  were  echoed  in  the  Tallahassee,  Florida

minority  construction  firms’  share of the contracts  remained  at less
than one percent  of the total contracts.  In fact,  contract  awards  to
minority  construction  firms  did not exceed  one percent  until  1982
when  the amount awarded was  1.25 percent.  The best year for
awards  to MBEs  was  1987  when  a total of prime and

C.

Race-Neutral  Efforts  To  Provide  Minority  Businesses  a
Fair  Share  of  Government  Contracts  Have  Proven
Insufficient.

examine  race-neutral  attempts
Utilization  studies  typically
to eliminate  discrimination  against  MBEs to determine  whether  a
race-conscious  program  is necessary.  These  studies  universally
reject  race-neutral  means  as  sufficient,  standing  alone,  to  create
equality  between  minority  and non-minority  contractors.

The  City of San Jose provides  a striking  example.  The
City had a race-neutral  small  business  program  in effect for the
entire  period  covered  by its study.  The report  concluded  that

The  fact  that  statistically  significant  disparities
were found  while  these programs  were  in effect
strongly  suggests  that race-neutral  methods  alone
have  provided,  at best,  an  inadequate  means  of
addressing  problems  associated  with discriminatory
practices  against minority  and  female  construction
contractors.

City of San Jose  Study,  Vol.  1 at III-4.  The same conclusion  was
reached  in the City  of Hayward  Study  where  a  variety  of  race-
neutral  activities  and  programs  to  encourage  small  business
participation  had  been  undertaken.
Activities  included  outreach
efforts,  improved  contracting  and  procurement  practices  and
education.  The study concluded:  “Race-neutral  measures  have  not
succeeded  in Hayward  and there is 'imited  evidence  that they have
worked  elsewhere.”  Hayward  Study at xv.

IV.  SECTION  502 OF THE SMALL  BUSINESS  ACT IS A
CONSTITUTIONAL  DELEGATION  OF
CONGRESSIONAL  AUTHORITY.

The  United  States  Constitution  authorizes  Congress  to
“provide  for the  ...  general  Welfare  of the United  States”  and  "to
enforce,  by  appropriate  legislation,”
the  equal  protection
guarantees  of the  Fourteenth  Amendment.
U.S.  Const.  art.  I,
Section  8,  cl.1;  amdt.  14,  Section  5.  By  enacting  the  Small
Business  Act  (SBA),  Congress  merely  exercised  these  powers.
Section  502  of  the  SBA,  15  U.S.C.  § 644  (g),  provides  the
statutory  authorization  for the President  and federal  agencies  to
establish  utilization
goals for disadvantaged  small  businesses.  The
SBA  furnishes  specific guidelines  for the President and federal
agencies,  including the Federal  Lands  Highway  Program  (FLHP),
to  follow  and  ensures  ongoing  congressional  oversight  through  a
mandatory,  detailed  reporting  system  to Congress.”

*The detailed reporting requirements  of SBA Sections 644 (h)(1) and
(2)  require  each federal  agency's annual report to Congress  to include:
the agency's  goals and performance  in attaining the goals; an analysis  of

Because  Congress  had the authority  to enact the SBA  and
because  the legislation  contains inherent  safeguards,  the  SBA is a
permissible  delegation  of  Congress’  authority  to  establish
utilization  goals for disadvantaged  small  businesses.  “If Congress
shall  lay down  by legislative  act  an  intelligible  principle  to  which
the person  or body authorized  to fix such rates is directed  to
conform,  such  legislative  action  is not  a forbidden  delegation  of
legislative  power."  J.W, Hampton,  Jr. & Co. v, United States,
276  U.S.  394,  406,  409  (1928).”

A.

The Cost  to the Federal  Government  of Undertaking
Utilization  Studies  Throughout  the Country  Would  be
Prohibitive.

All  the studies  cited  in this  brief are well  over  200 pages.
They  include  complicated  statistical  analyses  and  extensive  fact
gathering,  including  holding  hearings,  conducting  surveys,  and
research.  The cost of each one of these studies  is extremely  high.
For  example,  the City of San Diego,  California  decided  to fund a
$750,000
disparity  study in 1993.  C.  Van  Housen,  Judge Blocks
ontractors,  San  Diego

Hels

any failure  to achieve  the Government-wide  goals or individual  agency
goals;  actions  planned  by the agency  with respect  to goals in the
succeeding  fiscal year; the number  and dollar value of contracts  awarded
to small  business  concerns  and small  business  concerns  owned  and
controlled  by socially  and economically  disadvantaged  individuals;  and the
number  and dollar  value  of subcontracts
awarded  to small  business
concerns  and small business concerns  owned and controlled  by socially
and economically  disadvantaged  individuals.

*Only twice in this Court’s history has it invalidated  legislation  on
improper-delegation  grounds.  See Schechter Poultry  v. United States,
295 U.S.  495  (1935) and Panama  Refining  Co. v. Ryan, 293 U.S.  38
(1935).  Im the sixty years  subsequent  to these  National  Industrial

doctrine have uniformly  upheld broad delegations  of rulemaking  power
to agencies.  By challenging  the SBA as an impermissible  delegation  of
Congress’  legislative  authority,  Petitioner ignores the vast majority  of the
in this area.
Court’s  precedent

26

27

Business  Journal  (October  4,  1993)  at 70.  In order  for the  federal
government  to  undertake  studies  in each  state  to  determine  state-
wide  (or even  local  geographic  area-wide)  need for MBE programs
would  be staggering.

Given  the  plethora  of  information  in the  public  domain
available  to Congress  and to federal  agencies,  it is a much  more
sensible  approach  to allow  Congress  to delegate  its power to
determine  what  affirmative  action  programs  are  appropriate  to
federal  agencies  which  are  directly  involved  in  the  contracting
process  and aware  of the need to increase  MBE participation  in
government  contracting.  Indeed,  with  regard  to  the  construction
industry,  this  Court  has taken  judicial  notice  of the pervasiveness
of discrimination.  United Steelworkers
of America  v. Weber, 443
U.S.  193,  198  n.1  (1979).

B.

The  Subcontracting  Compensation  Clause  Program
Does  Not Require  the Federal  Agency  to Conduct  a
Croson  Inquiry  Because  Fullilove  Controls.

In order to fulfill its congressional  mandate  under the SBA,
in  1979,  the Department  of Transportation  through  the FLHP,
implemented  the  subcontracting  compensation  clause  program
(SCC).  To qualify  for eligibility
under the SCC program, a small
business  must demonstrate  through  an annual certification  process
that it is socially  and economically  disadvantaged.  The SCC  and
its  eligibility  requirements  comport
with the SBA’s  definitions  of
socially  and economically  disadvantaged  business  concerns  as
defined  by Congress.  As such, the SCC provisions  carry a  heavy
presumption
of validity.  When Congress explicitly  or implicitly
delegates to agencies  the power to elucidate  a specific provision  of
a  statute,  the resulting  agency  action  is entitled  to deference.

467  U.S.  837,  844  (1984).

Petitioner argues that, before the FLHP— a federal  agency
acting pursuant to a congressional  mandate—  may implement  and
administer  the SCC program,  it must conduct the inquiry set forth
in Croson.  As the Court of Appeals for the Tenth Circuit stressed,
“The question of congressional  action  was not before the Court in
Croson.”  Adarand Constructors,
Inc, v, Pefia 16 F.3d 1537,  1544
(10th  Cir.  1994).  Under  Croson,  strict scrutiny  must be applied

28

when  Congress  does not act, and instead,  public state and/or  local
entities  impose  programs  containing  racial classifications.

See  Fullilove,  supra.

as  congressional  programs.

Unlike  Congress,  state  and local  governments  do not
possess affirmative  grants of authority  from Section 5 to enforce
For  this
the  Fourteenth  Amendment.
reason, local and state programs  are not entitled to the same level
of  deference
Race-conscious
affirmative  action  programs  mandated  by Congress  are only subject
to intermediate  scrutiny.  Metro,  supra.  As explained  by Judge
Posner  in  Milwaukee  County  Pavers  Ass'n v. Fielder,  supra
(1991),  the  "joint  lesson  of Fullilove and Croson”  is  “that  the
federal  government  can, by virtue of thee enforcement  clause  of
the Fourteenth  Amendment,  engage  in affirmative  action  with  a
can do."
freer hand than states  and municipalities

government.

Nothing  in  its  implementation

In any case,  the program  at issue here is a  congressional
program.  Not  only was it authorized  by Congress,  but it was
mandated
by Congress.  The  Small  Business  Act  directs  each
federal  agency to establish  goals for small business  participation  in
federal  procurement  contracts.
By  implementing  the  SCC
of Transportation’s  FLHP  was  merely
program,  the Department

program  can only  properly  be characterized  as a  program  of the
federal
or
administration  mystically  transforms  it into  a  state  or  local
program,  subjecting  it  to  Croson’s  findings  requirement  or
of scrutiny.
standard
As with a state,  if Congress  can require  a federal  agency
to comply  and implement  its affirmative  action  legislation,  agency
action
the  means  of  fulfilling  the  legislation’s
requirements

developed  the program,  as opposed  to a state or local  government,

Under the traditional  standards  governing  the delegation  of
authority,  the FLHP was fully  empowered  to implement  the SCC
program.
“Congress...  took  a power  which  [it] possessed  and,
instead  of exercising
to delegate  it.  This is the
process  by which  all federal  regulations  are promulgated  and to
forbid  it would be to necessarily  dismantle  the entire structure  of
the  Executive  Branch."  Hampton
vy, Mow Sun Wong,  supra  at
124  (1976)  (Rehnquist,  J.,  White,  J.,  and  Blackmun,  J.,
dissenting).

it directly,  chose

CONCLUSION

Petitioners  are asking this Court to reconsider  settled  law,
and carry a  heavy  burden  in doing  so.  As detailed  above,  this
Court has set forth the factors  that can lead to a reversal  of prior
decisions,  and none of these factors is present  here.  Furthermore,
a  review  of the various  post-Croson  studies  leads  to a clear
conclusion:  in the absence  of DBE programs,  underutilization  of
available  minority  businesses  is dramatic and persistent.

The Civil  War Amendments
granted  Congress  a special
role in remedying  the effects  of racial discrimination:  it has power
in this area broader than that of any other governmental  entity, and
the duty to use that power to fulfill the goals of the amendments.
The Small Business  Act and the Surface  Transportation  Assistance
Act of 1982 are valid exercises  of that power, entailing  a necessary
delegation  of some authority to agencies so that they can tailor
their actions  to the needs of the affected localities.  To  deny
Congress  the authority  to take this reasonable  measure  would be
an unwarranted judicial intrusion  upon Congress’s  constitutionally-
mandated  prerogative  to address  racial inequities.

For the foregoing reasons,  it is respectfully  submitted  that

the judgment of the court below should  be affirmed.

STATEMENT
OF FACTS

At  the  center  of  this  case

is  a

compensation  clause  ("SCC")  used  by the  Central  Federal
Lands Highway Division  (CFLHD)*  which provides
a financial
incentive  to  a prime contractor  when it subcontracts  with small
businesses owned and controlled by socially and economically
disadvantaged  individuals.’
This  type  of small  business  is
commonly referred  to as a Disadvantaged  Business  Enterprise
("DBE").  The CFLHD  contract
states that:

i

[a] small business concern will be considered  a  DBE
after it has been certified as such by the U.S. Small
Business  Administration  or  any  State  Highway
Agency.  Certification  by other Government  agencies,
counties,  or cities  may be acceptable  on  an  individual
basis
has
determined  that  the  certifying  agency  has  an
acceptable  and  viable  DBE  certification  program.  If
the  Contractor
this
provision,  the  Contractor  shall  furnish  the Engineer
with  acceptable  evidence  of  the  subcontractor(s)

Contracting

provided

payment

requests

Officer

under

the

*  The CFHLD is a division of the Federal Lands Highway Program
("FLHP"),  which  implements  programs  of  the  Federal  Highway

.  The SCC provides  added compensation  of up to 1.5%  of the total
contract  amount to any prime contractor  who subcontracted  10% or more
of the work.  See Joint Appendix
at 24-27.

2

DBE certification  and shall furnish one certified copy
of the executed  subcontract(s).

A  prime  contractor  must use  a certified  DBE  to take
advantage  of  the  SCC.  The  CFLHD  does  not  certify
businesses  as DBEs, but the Small Business  Administration
(SBA) and the U.S.  Department  of Transportation (DOT) do.*
In this case, a  contract  was awarded  to Mountain  Gravel
and Construction  Company  ("Mountain  Gravel") to construct
a  4.7  mile section  of highway  referred  to as the West Dolores
Project.  Adarand  Constructors,  Inc.  submitted  a  bid to
Mountain  Gravel for the guardrail portion of the project as did
Gonzales  Construction  ("Gonzales"),  a  DBE.  Mountain
Gravel  awarded the subcontract  to Gonzales.  Adarand  filed
suit in the District Court for the District of Colorado, claiming
that  although  Gonzales  had  submitted  a  higher  bid  than
Adarand,  Gonzales  was awarded  the contract  because  it was a
DBE  and Mountain  Gravel  received  a compensation  bonus  for
using a  DBE on  the subcontract.  Therefore, Adarand  claimed
it was denied equal protection  under the law.  The District
Court rejected  this argument  and the Tenth Circuit Court of
Appeals  affirmed.  Adarand  filed
a petition for certiorari  which
was accepted
by this Court.

Petitioner  argues that the criteria used by the SBA  for
DBE  certification  is unconstitutional
because  it creates  a
presumption
that favors minorities.  Further,
it claims  that
CFLHD utilized the DBE  requirement  in conjunction  with the
SCC  to comply with the goal requirements  of  § 502 of the
Small  Business  Act.  Therefore,  Petitioner  argues  that  § 502  is
unconstitutional
as applied.

Section  502  of Small  Business  Act  requires government-
wide  goals  for  procurement  with  DBEs.”  One  method  by
which  the  SBA  implements  this requirement  is through  § 8(a)
of  the  Act,  commonly  referred  to  as  the  8(a)  Program.‘
Among  other things,  the Act sets forth five criteria  used  to
certify  a company  as  a DBE.’  The  SBA  has  also  developed  a
presumption  that  certain  minority  and  ethnic  groups  are
socially  disadvantaged,  one  element  of the  necessary  criteria
for  certification."  This  presumption  is  not  contained  in  the
Act?

a  subcontracting

The  SBA  has  created

program,
commonly  referred  to  as  the  8(d)  program.”®  This  section  of
the  Small  Business  Act  provides  contract  language  that  must
be  included  in  a  subcontract  awarded  for  a  federal  project;
"[t]he  contractor  shall  presume  that  socially  and  economically
disadvantaged  individuals  include  Black  Americans,  Hispanic
Americans,  Native  Americans,  Asian  Pacific  Americans,  and
other
be
disadvantaged  by the Administration  pursuant  to  § 8(a) of the
Small  Business  Act.""'

4

Congress  has also passed  legislation  creating  a  DBE  goal
program  for  the  DOT.”  This  legislation  was  implemented
through  regulations
at 49 C.F.R.,  Pt.  23(1988).  Similar to the
two  SBA  programs,  certification  under  the  DOT  process,
requires  compliance  with  five  elements.  Unlike  the  SBA
programs,  however,  the legislation  creating  the DOT  program
contains  a  statutory  rebuttable  presumption,  specifically
providing  that  women,  in addition  to racial  and  ethnic  groups,
shall be presumed  to be socially  and economically
disadvantaged”, satisfying

two of the five elements.

The narrow  issue  involved  in  this case  is  the

constitutionality  of § 502  of the  Act:  "For  purposes  of this
appeal,  both  parties  stipulate  that  the  SCC  program  is
authorized  by § 502  of the Small  Business  Act,  15  U.S.C.  §
644(g)(Supp.  1994),  and  that  the  constitutionality  of STAA

'2  In 1982,  Congress  passed the Surface  Transportation  Assistance
Act ("STAA")  Pub. L. 97-424,  96 Stat. 2100  (1982)  which required state
agencies  to spend  10% of their federal-aid  highway  funds on contracts
with firms participating
in the DBE program.  The DBE program was
comprised  of small businesses  which  were at least  51%  owned  and
controlled  by socially  and economically  disadvantaged  individuals.  STAA
provided  that certain  racial  and ethnic  minorities  were presumed  to be
socially  and  economically  disadvantaged.
In  1987,  Congress  added
through  the Surface  Transportation  and

Despite  the  stipulation,  Petitioner  has  consistently  failed
to  recognize  the distinctions  between  the SBA  programs  and
the DOT  program,  treating  them  as  one.  Hence,  the question
accepted  for  certiorari  by this Court is not limited  merely  to  §
502.  Instead,  it deals with any federal  highway  goals program,
thus  implicating  the  DOT  program."
The  remainder  of
Amici's  brief will  demonstrate  that  this  latter  program  passes
constitutional  muster.

OF ARGUMENT
SUMMARY

Section  502  of  the  Small  Business  Act  is  not  race-
conscious.  Therefore,  this  Court  should  consider  it  to  be
economic  legislation  warranting  only review  under  the rational
basis  standard.  Section  502  was  enacted  to  promote  the
development  of disadvantaged  businesses  to  enable  them  to
compete  on  an  equal basis  in the American  economy.  There  is
ample  evidence  of the  strides  made  by those  disadvantaged
businesses  who  have  benefited  from  § 502  to  conclude  that  it
does not  violate  the Equal Protection  Clause.

The  goals  program  under  § 502 refers  in part to the
STURAA  goals  program.  The latter  program  creates  a
presumption  that  women  are  socially  and  economically
disadvantaged.
applied
intermediate  scrutiny  to  gender-based  legislation  and  should
do so  here.  Otherwise,  application  of strict  scrutiny review  will

have  standing  to  assert  that  STURAA's  gender  presumption  was
constitutionally  impermissible.  Jd. at 1543.  Amici  do not concede by
making  the arguments  related  to DOT that Petitioner  has standing.

decisions  require  review  under  the  intermediate  level  of
