I.  SECTION  502  OF  THE  SMALL  BUSINESS  ACT  IS
NOT  RACE-CONSCIOUS  LEGISLATION  AND,

The  underlying  premise  of Petitioner's  argument  is that
the race of  the business  owner  is the deciding  factor  in the
DBE  certification  process  under  § 502  of the  Act.”®  This
premise  is incorrect  and is fatal  to the remainder  of Petitioner's
argument.

A. Congress  Did  Not  Define _a  Disadvantaged

as a Minority  Business.
Business

When  Congress  enacted  § 502 of the Act, it specifically
required  that  government  agencies  act in such a  manner  so as
to  benefit  certain  small  businesses.  The statute  specifically
defines  the type  of  business  Congress  intended  to benefit:
"[nJotwithstanding  the  Government-wide  goal,  each  agency

open  to all companies  that  can  demonstrate  they can  meet  the
criteria.  Race  can  be a factor  in the  process,  but  Petitioner  is
wrong  when  it asserts  that race  is the only factor.

Economically  disadvantaged  individuals  are  those  socially

In none of these definitions is any group given beneficial
treatment.  Indeed, no racial group is even mentioned.”  On
the contrary,  the carefully  crafted  statute  was designed  to aid
any  business  which  meets  these  elements,  and  specifically
avoids  using race  as a determinative  factor.

disadvantaged  individual  as set forth in the statute  at 15

articulated  the following  policy:  "[tJhis  program  is  not
intended to assist concerns  owned and controlled by socially
disadvantaged  individuals  who  have  accumulated  substantial
wealth, who have unlimited growth potential or  who have not
experienced,  or have overcome,  impediments  to  obtaining
access  to  financing,  markets  and  resources."”’  Furthermore,
once a  DBE is certified and the SBA believes it is  no longer
economically  disadvantaged,  the SBA  will conduct a review of
its status.
Finally,  the  SBA  will  review  any  certification
decision  whenever  a  member  of the  public  submits  credible
evidence  that the SBA was  given false information,  or changes
have occurred  relating  to  the business  or  individual  that no
longer  miake  it, or  him  or  her,  eligible  based  on  economic
disadvantage.  ”

In summary, such a process  is hardly one  where  "race  [is]
the  primary  factor  for  the  awarding  of federal  construction
procurement  contracts  by the  federal  government."
The
statute in  question  is not  race-conscious  and,  therefore,  the
necessity

for strict scrutiny  is obviated.

C.  Section 502 of the Act, in the Absence of the
Rebuttable
Presumptions,
is Constitutional  Under
a Standard
of Rationality,

As amply demonstrated  above,  § 502  of the Act  on  its
face is  racially neutral.  Its design  is to  aid  any  disadvantaged
business  enterprise,  not  just  those  owned  and  controlled  by

7  13 CFR.  § 124.106(a)(ii(1994).
*  15 U.S.C.  § 637(a)(6)(A)(Supp.  1994).
*  13 CFR.  § 124.101(c)(2)(1994).
*  Petitioner's  Brief at 18.

1]

racial  and  ethnic  minorities.  Only the rebuttable  presumptions
created  by regulation  give  the  Petitioner  any  ability  to  argue
that  § 502  of the Act is racially  driven,  and that a heightened
level  of scrutiny  is necessary  in examining  it.

In the absence  of  the presumptions,
§ 502 of the Act
should  be  considered  economic  legislation  designed  to  aid
small  disadvantaged  businesses.  All  applicants,  regardless  of
race,  ethnicity,  or  gender,  would  be required  to  demonstrate
that  they  meet  the specified  criteria  for certification.  No
groups  would  be entitled  to any type of preferential  treatment.
Thus,  the  legislation  would  be  similar  to  the  myriad  of other
statutes  that  this  Court  has  reviewed  and  found  constitutional
The  Court's  analysis  of these  statutes  was
in  the  past.”
accomplished  under  a  standard  of rationality  --  whether  the
legislation  bears  a  reasonable  relation  to  the  purpose  that  it
serves.

This  Court  should  apply  the  rational  basis  standard  to
§ 502  of the  Act.  If this  Court  elects  to  deviate  from  its past
precedents  and  applies  strict  scrutiny,  it should  not  review  the
entire  Act  under  that  standard.  Instead,  strict  scrutiny  should
be applied,  if at all, to  the  rebuttable  presumptions  created  by
since  it  is  only  in  those
the  implementing  regulations,

presumptions  that  race  or  ethnic  origin  is  the  sole
factor.*?  The Act is constitutional.

the DOT  program.  Second,  the rebuttable  presumption  in the
DOT  program  is statutorily  created  while  the presumptions  in
the SBA programs  are  derived  from regulations.

Petitioner  argues  that the proper  standard  of  review  for
the  SBA  programs  is the strict  scrutiny  standard  that this
Court  has employed  when  reviewing  challenges  based on race.
If the Court  adopts  this analysis,  the unintended  result  would
be  to  apply  the strict  scrutiny  standard  to  a  gender
presumption  in the DOT  program  and  sweep  away  23 years  of
settled  law.

For a quarter  of a century,  this Court  has consistently  held
that an intermediate level of scrutiny  is the proper  standard  to
be  applied  when  analyzing  the  constitutionality  of legislation
employing gender-based classifications.  This  standard  was  first
suggested  in  Reed  v.  Reed:*
"A  classification  ‘must  be
reasonable,  not  arbitrary,  and  must  rest  upon  some  ground  of
difference  having  a fair and substantial  relation  to the object of
the legislation,  so that all persons  similarly  circumstanced  shall
standard  of review  has
be treated  alike.”  This intermediate
been  consistently  used  by this Court  in resolving  gender-based
constitutional  challenges.”®

>  404  U.S.  71,  76  (1971)  (citing  Royster  Guano  Co.  v.  Virginia,

253  U.S.  412, 415 (1920)).

©

See  Mississippi  Univ.  for  Women  v.  Hogan,  458  U.S.  718
(1982);  Michael M. v.  Superior
Court  of Sonoma  County,  450  U.S.  464
(1981);  Orr v. Orr,  440  U.S.  283  (1979);  Craig v Boren,  429  U.S.  190
(1976).  See  also  Frontiero  v.  Richardson,  411  U.S.  677  (1973).
In
Frontiero,  several  Justices  were  willing  to apply the strict scrutiny
standard  to  gender-based  discrimination  that  they  had  developed  for
analyzing  racially  based legislation.  However,  this view has never been
accepted  by a  majority-of  this Court,  and subsequent  cases have clarified
that the intermediate  standard  is the proper  one to utilize  in gender
challenge,  previous
discrimination  cases: "(t]o  withstand  constitutional

achievement of those objectives."  Craig, 429 U.S.  at 197.

In reviewing  the  decision  of the  Court  of Appeals,  this
Court  is  bound  by  its  four-year  old  opinion  in  Metro
Broadcasting,  Inc.  v.  Federal  Communications  Commission.**
There,  a  majority  of  this  Court  held  that  an intermediate
standard  of scrutiny was  appropriate  in reviewing  programs  of
the type involved  in this case:

This  was  the  standard  applied  by the  Tenth  Circuit,  and  it is

Petitioner  argues  that  Meiro  is inapplicable  to  this  case
and  attempts  to  distinguish  it on  the  grounds  that  it  dealt
solely  with  First  Amendment  concerns.  Such  is  not  the
circumstance.  Indeed,  a  review  of the  Court's  two  previous
decisions  regarding  affirmative  action  contracting  programs
demonstrates  that Metro  is the logical result  of these cases.

Fullilove  v.  Klutznick”  is  the  first  instance  where  the
Court  wrestled  with the issue  of  a  government  contracting
program  designed  to  remediate  past discrimination.  There, a
provision  of the  federal  Public  Works  Employment  Act  of
1977  required  ten  percent  of the  federal  funds  granted  for
local  public  works  projects  to  be  used  by  state  and  local
or  supplies  from  minority
grantees  to  obtain  services
businesses.
This  Court  upheld  the  validity  of the  provision,
although  the  various  opinions  did  not  rely  upon  the
pronouncement of a specific  standard  of review.  At least  five
Justices  adopted  either  a  standard  of intermediate  scrutiny,’
or  a  two-step  approach  focusing  first on  the objective  of the

%  Id. at 564-65.

©  448 U.S.  448 (1980).
“'  Jd.  (Marshall,  J., concurring).

16

congressional legislation, and, second, on whether the means
selected  were specifically  tailored  to meet that objective.”

Several years later, the Court  was faced  with  a similar
public  works  provision  established  by the  City  of Richmond.
In City of Richmond v. J.A.  Croson  Co.,® this Court rejected
the City's  program  as unconstitutional.  The Court,  however,
recognized  the  distinction  between  a  remediation  program
established  by Congress  in Fullilove,  and one established  by a
state or  political subdivision  as was involved  in Croson.“  The
former  implicates  a grant of authority to Congress  in § 5 of the
14th  Amendment,  while the latter entails a  constraint  of power
on  the states under § 1 of that Amendment.®  Yet even  in
Croson,  the Court  continued  to adhere  to the two-step
analysis  of Chief Justice  Burger used  in Fullilove  with  respect
to  congressional  programs.

in
Thus,  the  lessons  of Fullilove  and  Croson  are  clear:
reviewing  state  action  taken  to remedy past discrimination,  the
Constitution  may  require  that  strict  scrutiny  be  applied  to
ensure  that the 14th Amendment is not  violated.  When  the

©  Id.  (Burger,  C.J.,  concurring).  Compare  Craig,  429  U.S.  190
(1976),  in which the majority  imposed  an intermediate  level of scrutiny
cases.
in *ender  classification

488 U.S.  469 (1989).

“  Justice  O'Connor,  writing  for the majority,  stated:  "[tJhus,  our
treatment  of an exercise  of congressional  power  in Fullilove  cannot  be
dispositive  here." Jd. at 491.

“  Petitioner  fails  to  recognize  that  this  unique  grant  of
congressional  power  to remedy  past  discrimination  —  present  in both
Fullilove  and the present  case  -- was absent  in Croson  and Metro.
Indeed,  Croson  was decided,  in part, on this basis, as was the dissent's
attempts  in Metro to impose  a stricter standard  of scrutiny.  Metro,  497
U.S. at 605-606  (O'Connor,

to a single case where strict scrutiny  was applied,  or even adopted  by a

IV.  THE  STURAA  GENDER  PRESUMPTION  SERVES

AN  IMPORTANT  GOVERNMENTAL  O
AND
IS  SUBSTANTIALLY
ACHIEVING  THAT  OBJECTIVE.

|

RELATED

TO

This  Court  is  in  agreement  that  whatever  the
formulation  employed,  remedying  past  or  present
racial  discrimination  by a  state  actor  is a  sufficiently
weighty  state  interest  to warrant  the remedial  use  of a
carefully  constructed  affirmative  action  program.
This  remedial  purpose  need  not  be  accompanied  by
contemporaneous  findings  of actual  discrimination  to
be accepted  as legitimate  as long as the public  actor
has a firm  basis  for believing  that  remedial  action  is
required.*’

“  Metro goes beyond  Fullilove  in holding  that such a  standard is
applicable  even if the measures  are not remedial,  per se.  This portion  of
the holding  in  Metro  is not at issue in the present  case, since the program
in question,  like the one in Fulliiove,  is specifically
designed  to remedy

good  faith  efforts  to  achieve  DBE  subcontracting
goals.  The  Committee  has  studied  the  materials
presented  by non  minority  subcontractors  regarding

construction  projects  has increased  the  opportunities
for  subcontractors  to  compete,  and  the  good  faith

program  does  not  operate  as  a  quota.  Also,  since
DBE  firms  compete  in many  areas  of subcontracting,
any  burden  that  might  be imposed  by this program  is
spread  among  different  groups  of subcontractors.“

“  Numerous  other  congressional  committees  and  members  of
Congress  have  acknowledged  the  exclusion  of women  from  the  federal
procurement  market  and expressed  the need  to solve  this problem.  See,

Business  Enterprise  Program  of the Federal-Aid  Highway Act:  Hearings
before  the  Subcomm.  on Transportation  of the Senate  Comm.  on
Environment  and  Public  Works,  99th  Cong.  Ist  Sess.  (1985);  Women

Equity Act of 1989:  Hearing Before Subcomm.  on  Procurement,  Tourism,
and Rural  Development,  House  Comm.  on  Small Business  (Testimony  of
Ike Skelton,  "Women  entrepreneurs

20

Thus,  the congressional  record  is replete  with  evidence  of
the discrimination  faced  by women  business  owners.  Congress
treats this  as a serious  national  problem  and seeks  through
STURAA  and  other  measures  to assist  the  economic
development  of women-owned  businesses.  The  legislation  has
accomplished  this  objective  to  a  limited  degree.  Women-
owned  businesses  have been awarded  a greater  percentage  of
subcontracts  each year since  STURAA's  enactment.  “

Because  an important  governmental  objective  has been,
and will be, substantially  furthered,  this gender  presumption
passes  scrutiny  under  the Equal  Protection  Clause.  Craig  v.
Boren,  429  U.S.  190  (1976).  There  can  be no  question  that
Congress  had a firm basis  upon  which  to  enact STURAA in an
attempt  to  remedy  this  pervasive  discrimination
against
women.

STURAA  is  substantially

to  achieving  the
objectives  set  out  by  Congress  in  that  it  is  neither  over
inclusive  or  under  inclusive.*’  First,  the  program  is not  under
inclusive  because  all  small  businesses  which  satisfy  the
eligibility  requirements  for  the  program  can  be  certified  and
participate.**  No business  is excluded  by the racial  and gender
presumptions.”  The Fullilove  Court  foresaw  and  approved  of
this type of program.**  Second,  STURAA  is not  over  inclusive
because  even  though  minority-  and  women-owned  businesses
are  presumed  to  be  socially  and  economically  disadvantaged,
to  be certified  they  must  show  they  can  meet  the other
and  control.
criteria:**
Therefore,  not  all companies  owned  by women  and  minorities
can  participate.  Additionally,  it is not  over  inclusive  because  a
waiver  can  be employed  in the event  of a lack of capacity.”

22

Importantly,  the  presumption  of  social  and  economic
disadvantage  is rebuttable.’  Any credible evidence that the
applicant  is  not  socially  and  economically  disadvantaged,
rebuts the presumption  and places the burden on the applicant
to  affirmatively
prove  he or she qualifies.’  Anyone  can
challenge  the presumption  as applied  to an applicant,  including
the governmental  agency  that is implementing  the program.  In
fact, the guidance  given  to the agencies  implementing  the
certification
the
presumption  should  not be applied  in the face of conflicting
data, even if a third party does not challenge  the presumption:
"the existence  of  the presumption  does  not  mean  that the
certifying  agency  must  ignore  evidence  that  any  applicant  for
certification  is  not  socially  or  economically  disadvantaged

CONCLUSION

The narrow  issue  before  the Court  is  whether  the goals
and  certification  process  promulgated  by  the  SBA  to
implement  § 502  of the  Small  Business  Act violates  the Equal
Amici  request  that  this  Court  hold  that
Protection  Clause.

oversight  for STURAA  stated  its belief that the Secretary’s  decision  to
approve  or disapprove  a state’s  request  for a reduced  goal should  be based
on, but not limited  to, consideration  of the following  factors:
(1)  the
number  of DBE/WBE  firms  in the state or region;  (2) historical  data
relating  to DBE/WBE  participation  in Federal-Aid  highway  projects  in
the state or region;  and (3) the minority  population  in the state or region.

§ 502  is constitutional,  and  affirm  the  decision  of the  Tenth

and

Amici do  not  believe that it is  necessary to  go  beyond a
review  of  § 502.
However,  if the  Court  elects  to  review
STURAA,  including  its  statutory  presumption  in  favor  of
women,  Amici  urge that an intermediate  level of scrutiny  be
applied  to  the  presumption.  This  Court  has  consistently
applied  this level  of scrutiny to classifications  based  on  gender.
It should  not  deviate  from  this  standard  here.  Applying  this
standard  renders  STURAA  constitutional.
